Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems

by Galileo Galilei

1632 2026

This is an AI modernization of Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems into contemporary English. The original is available from Wikisource (Italian).

To the Grand Duke

DIALOGUE ON THE TWO CHIEF WORLD SYSTEMS

A Dialogue by Galileo Galilei, member of the Lincean Academy, Extraordinary Mathematician of the University of Pisa, and Chief Philosopher and Mathematician to the Most Serene Grand Duke of Tuscany.

In which, over the course of four days of conversation, the two chief systems of the world — the Ptolemaic and the Copernican — are discussed, and the philosophical and natural arguments for each side are set out without a verdict.

With privileges. Printed in Florence by Giovanni Battista Landini, 1632. With the license of the authorities.

Let it be printed, if it so appears to the Most Reverend Father, Master of the Sacred Apostolic Palace. A., Bishop of Bellicastro, Vicegerent.

Let it be printed. Fra Niccolò Riccardi, Master of the Sacred Apostolic Palace.

Let it be printed in Florence, the customary requirements being observed. September 11, 1630. Pietro Niccolini, Vicar General of Florence.

Let it be printed. September 11, 1630. Fra Clemente Egidi, Inquisitor General of Florence.

Let it be printed. September 12, 1630. Niccolò dell'Antella.

The difference between human beings and the other animals is enormous; and yet anyone who claimed that a difference nearly as great exists among human beings themselves would not be talking nonsense. What is the ratio of one to a thousand? And still the proverb is on everyone's lips: one man may be worth a thousand, where a thousand are not worth one. A difference like that comes down to the varying powers of our minds, and for me it reduces to this — being or not being a philosopher. For philosophy is the proper food of such minds, and whoever can feed on it is set apart, in fact, from the common run of humanity, to a greater or lesser degree of worth depending on how rich that nourishment is. Whoever looks higher stands out more highly; and turning to the great book of nature — which is the true object of philosophy — is the way to lift one's eyes. In that book, everything we read, being the work of an all-powerful Craftsman, is for that reason perfectly proportioned; but the reading is swiftest and worthiest where the work and the artistry appear, to our sight, the greatest. Among all the natural things our minds can grasp, the constitution of the universe deserves, I believe, first place: for as it contains everything and surpasses everything else in size, so, as the rule and sustainer of everything, it must surpass everything else in nobility as well. Therefore, if it ever fell to any men to stand out in intellect above the rest of humanity, it was Ptolemy and Copernicus, who read so loftily in the constitution of the world, fixed their gaze on it, and philosophized about it. Since these Dialogues of mine turn chiefly on the works of those two, it seemed they should be dedicated to no one but Your Highness; for their doctrine rests on those two men, whom I judge the greatest minds ever to have left us their works on such speculations, and so, to spare them any loss of rank, I had to place them under the favor of the one who is, in my eyes, greatest of all, from whom they may receive both glory and protection. And if those two have brought so much light to my understanding that this work of mine may largely be called theirs, it may just as well be called Your Highness's; for by your generous magnificence I was given not only the leisure and peace to write it, but also, through your effective help — never weary of honoring me — it has at last been brought into the light. Accept it, then, Your Highness, with your customary kindness; and if lovers of truth find in it anything that yields them fuller knowledge or real benefit, count it as your very own, accustomed as you are to doing good — so much so that in your happy dominion no one feels any of the universal hardships of this world troubling him. Praying for your prosperity, and that you may grow ever greater in this pious and magnanimous habit, I offer you my most humble reverence.

Your most humble and most devoted servant and vassal, Galileo Galilei.

To the Discerning Reader

Some years ago a salutary edict was issued in Rome which, to head off the dangerous scandals of the present age, imposed a timely silence on the Pythagorean opinion that the Earth moves. There was no shortage of people who rashly asserted that this decree was the offspring not of judicious examination but of poorly informed passion; and complaints were heard that consultants wholly inexperienced in astronomical observation ought not, with a sudden prohibition, to clip the wings of speculative minds. My zeal could not keep silent when I heard the recklessness of such complaints. Being fully informed about that most prudent determination, I judged it right to appear publicly on the stage of the world as a witness of the honest truth. I happened to be in Rome at the time; I received not only audiences but applause from the most eminent prelates of that court; nor was the decree published without my having had some advance notice of it. Therefore it is my purpose in the present work to show foreign nations that as much is known of this matter in Italy — and particularly in Rome — as ultramontane diligence could ever have imagined; and, by gathering together all my own speculations on the Copernican system, to make it known that awareness of every one of them preceded the Roman censure, and that from this climate come not only dogmas for the salvation of the soul, but ingenious discoveries for the delight of the mind as well.

To this end I have taken the Copernican side in the discussion, proceeding upon a pure mathematical hypothesis, and striving by every artful means to present it as superior — not to the doctrine of the Earth's stillness absolutely, but to that doctrine as it is defended by certain men who profess to be Peripatetics yet keep only the name, content, without ever taking a walk, to worship shadows, philosophizing not with due attention but from the mere memory of four ill-understood principles.

Three main topics will be treated. First, I shall try to show that all experiments that can be performed on the Earth are insufficient means for proving its mobility, since they can be fitted indifferently to a moving Earth or to one at rest; and I hope that in this connection many observations unknown to the ancients will come to light. Second, the celestial phenomena will be examined, reinforcing the Copernican hypothesis as if it must emerge absolutely victorious, and adding new speculations — which, however, serve for convenience in astronomy, not as necessities of nature. In the third place I shall propose an ingenious fancy. I found myself saying, many years ago, that the unsolved problem of the tides might receive some light if the Earth's motion were granted. This remark of mine, flying from mouth to mouth, had found charitable fathers ready to adopt it as the child of their own wit. Now, so that no foreigner may ever appear who, arming himself with our own weapons, reproaches us for inattention to so important a matter, I have judged it best to set out the probable arguments that would make it persuasive, granted that the Earth moves. I hope that from these considerations the world will learn that if other nations have navigated more, we have speculated no less; and that to submit to asserting the Earth's stillness, and to take the contrary as nothing more than a mathematical caprice, comes not from ignorance of what others have thought about it, but — if from nothing else — from those reasons supplied by piety, by religion, by the knowledge of divine omnipotence, and by the consciousness of the weakness of the human mind.

I have thought it fitting, moreover, to unfold these ideas in the form of a dialogue, which, not being bound to the rigorous observance of mathematical laws, leaves room for digressions too — at times no less interesting than the main argument.

Many years ago I often found myself in the marvelous city of Venice in conversation with Signor Giovan Francesco Sagredo, a man of most illustrious birth and most penetrating mind. From Florence came Signor Filippo Salviati, the least of whose splendors were the purity of his blood and the magnificence of his wealth: a sublime intellect that fed on no delicacy more avidly than on exquisite speculations. With these two I often found myself discussing these matters, in the presence of a Peripatetic philosopher — one for whom nothing seemed to stand more in the way of understanding the truth than the fame he had acquired as an interpreter of Aristotle.

Now, since a most bitter death has, in the very prime of their years, deprived Venice and Florence of those two great lights, I have resolved to prolong their fame, as far as my feeble powers allow, upon these pages of mine, by bringing them in as speakers in the present controversy. Nor will the good Peripatetic lack his place: because of his excessive fondness for the commentaries of Simplicius, it seemed fitting, without revealing his name, to leave him the name of that revered author. May those two great souls, forever venerable to my heart, receive this public monument of my undying love; and may the memory of their eloquence help me to set forth to posterity the speculations here promised.

It happened (as it will) that various scattered discussions had arisen among these gentlemen, which had inflamed rather than quenched the thirst for learning in their minds; so they made the wise resolution to spend a few days together in which, with every other business set aside, they would devote themselves to contemplating, in more orderly speculations, the wonders of God in the heavens and on the earth. They gathered in the palace of the most illustrious Sagredo; and there, after the customary but brief compliments, Signor Salviati began as follows.

The First Day

Salviati: Yesterday we ended with an agreement: today we would discuss, as clearly and in as much detail as we can manage, the natural arguments — and their real strength — that have so far been offered on either side, by the champions of the Aristotelian and Ptolemaic position and by the followers of the Copernican system. And since Copernicus, by placing the Earth among the moving bodies of the heavens, makes it a globe like a planet, the right place to begin our considerations is by examining just what force and energy there is in the arguments of the Peripatetics — the Aristotelian school — when they set out to demonstrate that this assumption is flatly impossible; given that, on their view, nature requires two substances utterly different from each other, the celestial and the elemental, the one impervious to change and immortal, the other alterable and perishable. Aristotle handles this argument in his books On the Heavens, first working up to it through reasoning that depends on certain general assumptions, and then confirming it with experience and with particular demonstrations. I'll follow his same order: I'll lay the case out, and then say freely what I think of it — submitting myself to your judgment, and especially to that of Simplicio, so vigorous a champion and defender of the Aristotelian doctrine.

The first step in the Peripatetic case is the one where Aristotle proves the completeness and perfection of the world by pointing out that it is not a mere line, nor a bare surface, but a body furnished with length, breadth, and depth; and since there are no dimensions beyond these three, in having them the world has them all, and having all, it is perfect. Now, that from simple length there is constituted the magnitude we call a line; that adding breadth gives us a surface; that adding height or depth on top of that yields a body; and that after these three dimensions there is no passing on to any other, so that in these three alone completeness — totality, so to speak — is exhausted: all this I could well have wished Aristotle had proved to me by strict necessity, above all since it can be done quite clearly and quickly.

Simplicio: What about the beautiful demonstrations in the second, third, and fourth texts, right after the definition of the continuum? Don't you have it there, first of all, that there is no dimension beyond the three because three is everything — three is in every direction? And isn't this confirmed by the authority and doctrine of the Pythagoreans, who say that all things are determined by three — beginning, middle, and end — which is the number of the whole? And where do you leave the other argument: that this number is used, as if by a law of nature, in the sacrifices to the gods? And that nature herself dictates that we give the title "all" to things that come in threes, and to nothing fewer? Of two things we say "both," not "all" — but of three we say it readily. All this doctrine you have in the second text. Then in the third, ad pleniorem scientiam — for fuller understanding — we read that "everything," "the whole," and "the perfect" are formally one and the same; and that this is why, among magnitudes, only body is perfect, because only body is determined by three, which is the whole, and being divisible in three ways, it is divisible in every direction — whereas of the others, one is divisible in one way and another in two, since each has its division and its continuity according to the number allotted to it: one is continuous in one direction, another in two, but body alone in all of them. What's more, in the fourth text, after some further doctrines, doesn't he prove the same point with yet another demonstration — namely, that a transition is made only where something is lacking (thus we pass from the line to the surface, because the line lacks breadth), but it is impossible for the perfect to lack anything, since it is complete in every direction, and therefore there is no passing from body to any further magnitude? Now from all these places, doesn't it seem to you sufficiently proved that beyond the three dimensions — length, breadth, and depth — there is no passage to any other, and that body, which has all three, is therefore perfect?

Salviati: To tell you the truth, in all these discourses the only thing I've felt compelled to concede is that whatever has a beginning, a middle, and an end may and should be called perfect. But that because beginning, middle, and end make three, the number three is therefore a perfect number, with the power to confer perfection on whatever possesses it — there I feel nothing that moves me to agree. I don't understand, and don't believe, that where legs are concerned, for example, the number three is any more perfect than four or two; nor am I aware that the number four does the elements any disservice, or that they'd be more perfect if there were three of them. Better, then, to have left these pretty conceits to the orators, and to have proved his point by necessary demonstration — for that is how one is to proceed in the demonstrative sciences.

Simplicio: You seem to treat these arguments as a joke. Yet all of this is the doctrine of the Pythagoreans, who attributed so much to numbers; and you, who are a mathematician — and, I believe, a Pythagorean philosopher in many of your opinions — now appear to scorn their mysteries.

Salviati: That the Pythagoreans held the science of numbers in the highest esteem, and that Plato himself admired the human intellect and thought it shared in divinity simply because it could understand the nature of numbers — this I know very well, and I'd be inclined to make the same judgment myself. But that the mysteries which led Pythagoras and his sect to hold the science of numbers in such veneration are the silly stuff that circulates in the mouths and on the pages of the crowd — that I don't believe for a moment. On the contrary: I know that they, so that marvelous things would not be exposed to the sneers and contempt of the mob, condemned as sacrilege the publishing of the most hidden properties of numbers, and of the incommensurable and irrational quantities they had investigated, and preached that whoever revealed them would be tormented in the next world. So I think one of them, to throw the crowd a scrap and buy himself free of its questions, told them that their numerical mysteries were precisely those trifles which afterwards spread among the common people — a piece of cunning and shrewdness much like that of the clever young man who, to rid himself of the pestering of his mother, or his inquisitive wife, I forget which, who besieged him to share the secrets of the senate, made up that famous tale which left her, and many other women with her, thoroughly mocked — to the great laughter of that same senate.

Simplicio: I have no wish to be counted among those too curious about the mysteries of the Pythagoreans. But to keep to our subject: I reply that the arguments Aristotle produces to prove that the dimensions are not, and cannot be, more than three seem conclusive to me; and I believe that if a more rigorous demonstration had existed, Aristotle would not have left it out.

Sagredo: Add at least: if he had known it, or if it had occurred to him. But Salviati, you'd be doing me a great kindness by giving me some clear argument of your own — if you have one plain enough for me to grasp.

Salviati: Not only for you, but for Simplicio too; and not merely one you can grasp, but one you already know — though perhaps without ever having noticed it. And to make it easier to follow, let's take paper and pen, which I see lying ready here for just such occasions, and draw ourselves a little figure. First we'll mark these two points, A and B, and draw from one to the other the curved lines ACB and ADB, and the straight line AB. Now I ask you: which of these, in your mind, is the one that determines the distance between the endpoints A and B — and why?

Sagredo: I'd say the straight line, and not the curves. Partly because the straight one is the shortest; partly because it is one — single and definite — while the others are infinitely many, unequal, and longer; and it seems to me the determination ought to be taken from what is one and certain.

Salviati: So we have the straight line, then, as the determiner of length between two endpoints. Now let's add another straight line, parallel to AB — call it CD — so that a surface lies enclosed between them; and I'd like you to assign me that surface's breadth. So, starting from the endpoint A, tell me where and by what route you'd go to land on the line CD, so as to assign me the breadth contained between the two lines. Will you determine it by the length of the curve AE, or by the straight line AF, or...

Simplicio: By the straight line AF, and not by the curve — curves having already been ruled out for such a use.

Sagredo: But I wouldn't use either of them, seeing that the straight line AF runs at a slant. I'd draw a line square to CD, because that one, it seems to me, would be the shortest — and the single one among the infinitely many longer, mutually unequal lines that can be drawn from the endpoint A to one point after another of the opposite line CD.

Salviati: Your choice, and the reason you give for it, strike me as perfect. So far, then, we have this: the first dimension is determined by a straight line; the second — breadth — by another line, also straight, and not merely straight, but at right angles to the one that determined length. And with that we have defined the two dimensions of a surface: length and breadth. But suppose you had to determine a height — say, how high this ceiling stands above the floor we have beneath our feet. From any point on the ceiling one can draw infinitely many lines, curved and straight, all of different lengths, to infinitely many points on the floor below. Which of all those lines would you use?

Sagredo: I'd fasten a string to the ceiling with a lead plumb hanging from it, and let it pay out freely until it came down almost to the floor. The length of that string — being the straight line, and the shortest of all the lines that could be drawn from that same point to the floor — I'd say is the true height of this room.

Salviati: Excellent. And if from the point marked on the floor by this hanging string (taking the floor to be level, not sloping) you set two more straight lines running, one along the length and the other along the breadth of the floor's surface, what angles would they make with the string?

Sagredo: Right angles, certainly — since the string falls plumb and the floor is good and flat and level.

Salviati: Then if you fix some point as the origin and starting place of your measurements, and from it draw one straight line as the determiner of the first measure, namely the length, then of necessity the line that is to define the breadth must set out at a right angle to the first; and the line that is to mark the height, the third dimension, setting out from that same point, must form with the other two angles that are not oblique but right. And so from the three perpendiculars — as from three lines that are each single, certain, and shortest — you'll have the three dimensions determined: AB length, AC breadth, AD height. And since it is clear that no further line can meet at that same point so as to make right angles with these, and since the dimensions must be determined by straight lines alone that make right angles with one another, it follows that the dimensions are no more than three. And whatever has the three has them all; and whatever has them all is divisible in every direction; and whatever is that, is perfect — and so on.

Simplicio: And who says other lines can't be drawn? Why can't I bring another line up from below to the point A, square to all the others?

Salviati: You most certainly cannot make more than three straight lines meet at one and the same point so as to form right angles with one another.

Sagredo: Yes — because the line Simplicio means would be, it seems to me, simply DA itself extended downward. In that fashion one could draw two more; but they would be the same first three lines, differing only in this: where they now merely touch at the point, they would then cut through it. They'd bring in no new dimensions.

Simplicio: I won't say this argument of yours can't be conclusive. But I will say, with Aristotle, that in the things of nature one need not always demand the necessity of mathematical demonstration.

Sagredo: Perhaps not — where it can't be had. But if here it can, why don't you want it used? Still, we'd best not spend more words on this point, because I believe Salviati would have granted Aristotle — and you — without any further demonstrations, that the world is a body, and perfect, and supremely perfect, as the greatest work of God.

Salviati: So he would, truly. So let's leave the general contemplation of the whole and come to the consideration of the parts, which Aristotle in his first division makes two, very different from each other and in a certain way contrary: the celestial and the elemental. The celestial: ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, impervious to change, and so on. The elemental: exposed to continual alteration, mutation, and the rest. This difference he derives, as from its original principle, from the diversity of local motions, and he proceeds along this path.

Leaving the world of the senses, so to speak, and withdrawing into the ideal world, he begins, architect-fashion, by considering that since nature is the principle of motion, it is fitting that natural bodies be movable with local motion. He then declares that local motions come in three kinds: circular, straight, and a mixture of straight and circular. The first two he calls simple, because of all lines only the circular and the straight are simple. Narrowing things down a bit, he then defines afresh: of the simple motions, one is the circular — that made around the center — and the straight is upward and downward; upward being the motion that departs from the center, downward the motion that goes toward the center. From this he infers that of necessity all simple motions are confined to these three kinds: toward the center, away from the center, and around the center. And this, he says, answers with a certain lovely proportion to what was said above about body — that it too is perfected in three things, and so likewise is its motion. With these motions established, he goes on to say that since some natural bodies are simple and others are compounds of them (and he calls simple those bodies that have from nature a principle of motion, like fire and earth), it is fitting that the simple motions belong to the simple bodies, and the mixed motions to the compounds — with this proviso, that the compounds follow the motion of whichever part predominates in their composition.

Sagredo: Please, Salviati, stop there a moment, because in this progression I feel doubts sprouting up on so many sides that I must either speak them now, if I'm to listen attentively to what you'll add next, or else withdraw my attention from what's to be said, if I'm to hold the doubts in memory.

Salviati: I'll stop very gladly, because I'm running the same risk myself, and at every step I'm on the point of losing my way — having to sail among reefs and waters so broken that they make me, as the saying goes, lose my compass. So before the pile grows any higher, set out your difficulties.

Sagredo: You began, together with Aristotle, by drawing me a little way out of the sensible world, to show me the architecture by which it must have been built; and, to my pleasure, you started by telling me that a natural body is by nature movable — nature having been defined elsewhere as the principle of motion. But here a small doubt was born in me: why didn't Aristotle say that some natural bodies are movable by nature and others immobile? For in the definition, nature is said to be the principle of motion and of rest. If natural bodies all have a principle of movement, then either there was no need to put rest into the definition of nature, or no need to bring such a definition in at this point.

Next, as to his explaining what he understands by simple motions, and how he determines them from the spaces they cover — calling simple those made along simple lines, which are only the circular and the straight — I accept that quietly enough; nor do I care to press the fine-grained objection of the helix wound around a cylinder, which, being in every part similar to itself, could seemingly be counted among the simple lines. But I do bristle a little at hearing him narrow this down — while seeming, in other words, merely to repeat the same definitions — to calling the one "motion around the center," and the other sursum et deorsum, that is, up and down: terms that have no use outside the built world, and that suppose it not only built but already inhabited by us. Now, if straight motion is simple by the simplicity of the straight line, and if simple motion is natural, then let it be made in any direction whatever — I mean up, down, forward, backward, to the right, to the left, and any other way you can imagine — so long as it's straight, it ought to belong to some simple natural body; or, if not, Aristotle's supposition is defective. One sees, moreover, that Aristotle hints there is only one circular motion in the world, and consequently only one center, to which alone the straight motions up and down refer — every sign that he means to switch the cards in our hand, and to fit the architecture to the building rather than build the building according to the precepts of architecture. For if I say that in the universe of nature there can be a thousand circular motions, and consequently a thousand centers, there will then also be a thousand motions up and a thousand motions down.

Moreover, he posits, as I said, simple motions and mixed motion — calling the circular and the straight simple, and the compound of the two mixed. Of natural bodies he calls some simple (namely, those that have a natural principle of simple motion) and others compound; and the simple motions he assigns to the simple bodies, and the compound motion to the compounds. But by compound motion he no longer means the mixture of straight and circular — which can exist in the world; he brings in a mixed motion as impossible as it is impossible to mix opposite motions along one and the same straight line, so as to produce from them a motion that is partly upward and partly downward. And to soften so great an awkwardness and impossibility, he falls back on saying that such mixed bodies move according to the simple part that predominates — which in the end forces one to say that even motion made along the same straight line is sometimes simple and sometimes compound; so that the simplicity of a motion no longer depends solely on the simplicity of its line.

Simplicio: Oh, doesn't it seem to you difference enough, if the simple and absolute motion is a great deal swifter than the motion that comes from predominance? How much faster does a piece of pure earth fall than a scrap of wood?

Sagredo: Fine, Simplicio. But if simplicity has to change on that account, then besides there being a hundred thousand mixed motions, you'll never be able to point out the simple one for me. Worse: if greater and lesser speed can alter the simplicity of a motion, then no simple body will ever move with simple motion — since in all natural straight motions the speed keeps forever increasing, and so keeps forever changing the simplicity, which, to be simplicity, must be unchangeable. And, what matters more, you'll be charging Aristotle with a fresh fault: in his definition of compound motion he made no mention of slowness or of speed, which you now lay down as a necessary and essential condition. Besides, you'll harvest no fruit at all from such a rule; for there will be mixed bodies, and not a few, of which some move more slowly and others more swiftly than the simple one — lead and wood, for example, compared with earth. So among these motions, which will you call the simple, and which the compound?

Simplicio: The simple motion will be the one performed by a simple body, and the mixed motion the one performed by a compound body.

Sagredo: Very fine indeed. But what's this, Simplicio? A moment ago you wanted simple and mixed motion to teach me which bodies are simple and which are compound; and now you want me to learn, from the simple bodies and the compound ones, which motion is simple and which is mixed. An excellent rule for never coming to know either the motions or the bodies. And besides, you're now declaring that greater speed no longer satisfies you either — you're after a third condition for defining simple motion, where Aristotle contented himself with just one, namely the simplicity of the path. But now, on your account, the simple motion will be the one performed along a simple line, at a certain determinate speed, by a simple movable body. Well, have it however you like, and let's get back to Aristotle — who defined mixed motion for me as the one compounded of the straight and the circular, but then never found me a single body that naturally moves with such a motion.

Salviati: Then I return to Aristotle. He began his discourse very well and methodically; but since his eye was fixed more on reaching and striking a target he had set up in his mind beforehand than on where the argument was straightforwardly leading him, he breaks the thread and comes at us sideways, offering as a known and obvious fact that as for the straight motions up and down, these naturally belong to fire and to earth — and that therefore, besides these bodies here around us, there must be another body in nature to which circular motion belongs, a body all the more excellent as circular motion is more perfect than straight motion. And just how much more perfect the one is than the other, he determines from the perfection of the circular line over the straight one, calling the circle perfect and the straight line imperfect: imperfect because, if it is infinite, it lacks an end and a boundary; and if it is finite, there is something outside it into which it can be extended.

This is the first stone — the base and foundation of the entire fabric of the Aristotelian world — on which all the other properties lean: neither heavy nor light, ingenerable, incorruptible, exempt from every change except change of place, and so on. All these attributes, he asserts, are proper to the simple body that moves with circular motion; and the contrary conditions — heaviness, lightness, corruptibility, and the rest — he assigns to the bodies naturally movable with straight motions. So whenever a flaw turns up in what has been established this far, we may reasonably doubt everything else that gets built on top of it. I don't deny that what Aristotle has introduced so far by a general discourse, resting on universal first principles, is later reconfirmed as he goes along with particular reasons and with experiences — all of which will have to be considered and weighed one by one. But since what has been said so far already presents many difficulties, and not small ones (and first principles and foundations really ought to be secure, firm, and stable, so that one can build on them with full confidence), it may be no bad idea, before we pile the doubts any higher, to see whether by chance — as I suspect — striking out along another road might set us on a straighter and surer path, and whether, with better-considered rules of architecture, we might lay the first foundations properly. So, suspending Aristotle's line of argument for now — we'll take it up again in due course and examine it point by point — I say that of the things he has said so far, I agree with him, and I grant that the world is a body endowed with all the dimensions, and therefore most perfect. And I add that as such it is necessarily supremely ordered — that is, made of parts arranged among themselves in the highest and most perfect order. This assumption I don't believe will be denied by you or by anyone else.

Simplicio: And who would you expect to deny it? First of all, it is Aristotle's own; and besides, the world's very name — cosmos — seems to be drawn from nothing else than the order it so perfectly contains.

Salviati: With that principle established, then, we can immediately conclude that if the integral bodies of the world are to be by nature movable, it is impossible for their motions to be straight, or anything other than circular. The reason is easy and plain enough. Whatever moves with straight motion changes place; and as it keeps moving, it gets farther and farther from the point it started from and from every place it successively passes through. Now if that motion belongs to it naturally, then at the beginning it was not in its natural place — and so the parts of the world were not arranged in perfect order. But we are supposing them to be perfectly ordered; as such, then, it is impossible that they should have from nature a tendency to change place, and consequently impossible that they move with straight motion.

Moreover, since straight motion is by its nature infinite — because the straight line is infinite and indeterminate — it is impossible for any movable body to have from nature a principle of moving along a straight line, that is, toward a place it is impossible to reach, there being no fixed end to arrive at; and nature, as Aristotle himself rightly says, does not undertake to do what cannot be done, nor undertake to move toward a place it is impossible to arrive at. And if someone objects that although the straight line, and consequently motion along it, can be extended to infinity — has no built-in end — nature has nevertheless, so to speak, arbitrarily assigned certain end-points to it, and given her natural bodies natural instincts to move toward them, I will answer that this might perhaps be told as a fable of the first chaos, where indistinct materials wandered about confusedly and in disorder. To set them in order, nature might very suitably have used straight motions — for just as such motions disorder well-arranged bodies when they move them, so they are suited to putting badly arranged ones into order. But after the best possible distribution and placement, it is impossible that any natural inclination to keep moving in a straight line should remain in those bodies; because from straight motion, now, nothing would follow but their removal from their own natural place — that is, their disordering. We can say, then, that straight motion serves to carry the materials for building the work; but once the work is built, it either stays motionless or, if it moves at all, moves only circularly.

Unless we would rather say, with Plato, that the world's bodies too, after they were made and fully established, were moved by their Maker for some time in straight motion, and that afterwards, on arriving at certain determinate places, they were turned one by one into circuits, passing from straight motion to circular — in which they have been maintained ever since, and are preserved still. A lofty thought, and truly worthy of Plato; and I remember hearing our mutual friend, the Academician, discourse on it. If I remember rightly, his discourse went like this. Every body constituted, for whatever cause, in a state of rest, but movable by its nature, will move when set at liberty — provided, that is, that it has by nature an inclination toward some particular place; for if it were indifferent to every place, it would stay at rest, having no more reason to move this way than that. And from its having such an inclination, it necessarily follows that in its motion it will keep continually accelerating. Beginning with the slowest possible motion, it will not acquire any degree of speed without first passing through all the lesser degrees of speed — or, if you prefer, the greater degrees of slowness. For setting out from the state of rest — which is the degree of infinite slowness of motion — there is no reason whatever why it should enter into one particular degree of speed before entering into a smaller one, and into another still smaller before that. On the contrary, it seems entirely reasonable that it should pass first through the degrees nearest to the one it starts from, and from those to the more remote; and the degree from which the body takes up its motion is that of extreme slowness, namely rest. Now, this acceleration of motion happens only as the moving body gains something by its moving; and its gain is nothing other than drawing nearer to the place it desires — the place its natural inclination pulls it toward; and it will make its way there by the shortest route, that is, by the straight line. So we may reasonably say that nature, in order to confer some determinate speed on a movable body first constituted at rest, makes use of having it move, for some time and through some space, with straight motion.

Granting this discourse, let us imagine that God created the body of, say, Jupiter, and had determined to confer on it a certain speed, which it must then keep perpetually uniform. We can say with Plato that He gave it at the beginning a straight and accelerated motion, and then, once it had reached that particular degree of speed, converted its straight motion into circular motion — whose speed is then naturally suited to remain uniform.

Sagredo: I hear this discourse with great pleasure, and I believe the pleasure will be greater still once you've removed one difficulty for me. It is this: I can't quite see how it must necessarily be that a movable body, setting out from rest and entering a motion toward which it has a natural inclination, passes through all the prior degrees of slowness lying between any assigned degree of speed and the state of rest — and those degrees are infinite. Couldn't nature, then, have given the body of Jupiter its circular motion, at just such-and-such a speed, the instant it was created?

Salviati: I did not say, nor would I dare to say, that it was impossible for nature or for God to confer that speed you speak of immediately. But I will say this: de facto — in actual fact — nature does not do it; so that doing it would be an operation outside the natural course of things, and therefore miraculous.

Sagredo: Then you believe that a stone, leaving rest and entering into its natural motion toward the center of the Earth, passes through every degree of slowness below any given degree of speed?

Salviati: I believe it — no, I am sure of it, and sure with such certainty that I can make you sure of it too.

Sagredo: If in the whole of today's reasoning I gained nothing but that one piece of knowledge, I'd count it a handsome return.

Salviati: As far as I can gather from the way you talk, a great part of your difficulty lies in this: that the body has to pass, in some stretch of time — even a very short one — through those infinite degrees of slowness that come before whatever speed it acquires in that time. So before going any further, I'll try to remove that scruple; and it should be an easy job, once I reply that the body does pass through all those degrees, but the passage is made without dwelling in any of them. Since the passage requires no more than a single instant of time, and any little stretch of time whatever contains infinite instants, we will never run short of instants to assign one to each of the infinite degrees of slowness — however brief the time may be.

Sagredo: So far I follow. Still, it seems an astonishing thing to me that a cannonball — for that's how I picture the falling body — which we see come down so headlong that in less than ten pulse-beats it will pass more than two hundred braccia — arm-lengths — of height, should at some point in its motion have been at so small a degree of speed that, had it kept moving at that rate without accelerating any further, it would not have covered that distance in a whole day.

Salviati: Say rather in a whole year — or in ten, or in a thousand; as I will try to persuade you, and perhaps even without your objecting, by a few quite simple questions I'll put to you. So tell me whether you have any difficulty granting that the ball, in coming down, keeps acquiring greater impetus — greater built-up drive — and greater speed.

Sagredo: Of that I am completely sure.

Salviati: And if I say that the impetus acquired at any point whatever of its motion is enough to carry it back up to the height it started from — would you grant me that?

Sagredo: I'd grant it without objection, provided the ball could apply its whole impetus, unimpeded, to that single operation of carrying itself, or another ball equal to it, back to that same height. So, for example, if the Earth were bored through the center, and the ball were let fall from a hundred or a thousand braccia above it, I firmly believe it would pass beyond the center, rising as far as it had fallen. And experience shows me the same thing happening with a weight hanging from a cord: pulled away from the vertical — which is its state of rest — and then set free, it swings down to that vertical and passes beyond it by just as much space, or falls short only by as much as the resistance of the air and the cord and other such accidents impede it. Water shows me the same: descending through a siphon, it climbs back up as far as it came down.

Salviati: You reason perfectly. And since I know you have no doubt about granting that the gaining of impetus comes from moving away from the point where the body starts and drawing nearer to the center toward which its motion tends — will you have any difficulty in granting that two equal movable bodies, even though they descend along different lines, acquire equal impetus, so long as their approach to the center is equal and nothing at all impedes them?

Sagredo: I don't quite understand the question.

Salviati: I'll explain it better by drawing a little figure. So: I'll mark this line AB, parallel to the horizon; on the point B I'll raise the perpendicular BC; and then I'll join the two with the slanting line CA. Now, take the line CA to be an inclined plane, exquisitely polished and hard, down which a perfectly round ball of the hardest material rolls, and let a similar ball fall freely down the perpendicular CB. I ask whether you would grant that the impetus of the one descending along the plane CA, once it has reached the endpoint A, could be equal to the impetus acquired by the other at the point B after its fall down the perpendicular CB.

Sagredo: I resolutely believe it could; because in fact both have approached the center equally, and, by what I granted just now, their impetuses would be equally sufficient to carry them back up to the same height.

Salviati: Now tell me what you believe that same ball would do if it were set on the horizontal plane AB.

Sagredo: It would stay still, that plane having no slope at all.

Salviati: But on the inclined plane CA it would descend — though with a slower motion than down the perpendicular CB.

Sagredo: I was about to answer a flat yes, since it seems necessary to me that the motion along the perpendicular CB must be faster than along the incline CA. And yet, if that's so, how can the body falling along the incline, when it reaches the point A, have as much impetus — that is, as great a degree of speed — as the body falling along the perpendicular has at the point B? These two propositions seem to contradict each other.

Salviati: Then it will seem far more false to you if I say that, taken absolutely, the speeds of the bodies falling along the perpendicular and along the incline are equal. And yet this is a perfectly true proposition — just as it is also true to say that the falling body moves faster along the perpendicular than along the incline.

Sagredo: To my ear those sound like contradictory propositions. And to yours, Simplicio?

Simplicio: They seem the same to me.

Salviati: I think you're teasing me, pretending not to grasp what you understand better than I do. So tell me, Simplicio: when you imagine one movable body being faster than another, what concept do you picture in your mind?

Simplicio: I picture one of them passing a greater space than the other in the same time — or else passing an equal space, but in less time.

Salviati: Very good. And for bodies that are equally fast, what concept do you picture?

Simplicio: I picture them passing equal spaces in equal times.

Salviati: And no other concept than that?

Simplicio: That, it seems to me, is the proper definition of equal motions.

Sagredo: Let's add one more to it, though: let the speeds also be called equal when the spaces passed have the same proportion as the times in which they are passed. That will be a more universal definition.

Salviati: So it is; because it covers equal spaces passed in equal times, and also unequal spaces passed in unequal times — provided those times are proportional to the spaces. Now take up the same figure again, and applying the concept you've formed of the faster motion, tell me why it seems to you that the speed of the body falling along CB is greater than the speed of the one descending along CA.

Simplicio: It seems so to me because, in the time the falling body passes the whole of CB, the descending one will pass, on CA, a part smaller than CB.

Salviati: Just so; and that is how it's verified that the body moves faster along the perpendicular than along the incline. Now consider whether, in this same figure, the other concept might be verified in some way — whether we might find the moving bodies to be equally fast along both the lines CA and CB.

Simplicio: I can see no such thing; in fact it seems to me a contradiction of what's just been said.

Salviati: And what do you say, Sagredo? I certainly wouldn't want to teach you what you already know — the very thing you brought me a moment ago as a definition.

Sagredo: The definition I offered was that movable bodies may be called equally fast when the spaces they pass have the same proportion as the times in which they pass them. So for the definition to apply in the present case, the time of the descent along CA would have to bear the same proportion to the time of the fall along CB as the line CA itself bears to the line CB; but I cannot understand how that can be, given that the motion along CB is faster than along CA.

Salviati: And yet you must understand it. Tell me now: don't these motions keep continually accelerating?

Sagredo: They do — but more in the perpendicular than in the incline.

Salviati: But is this acceleration in the perpendicular such, compared with the acceleration in the incline, that if we took two equal stretches anywhere at all on those two lines, the perpendicular and the incline, the motion in the stretch of the perpendicular would always be faster than in the stretch of the incline?

Sagredo: No indeed. On the contrary, I could take a stretch on the incline in which the speed is far greater than in an equal stretch taken on the perpendicular — and this will happen if the stretch on the perpendicular is taken near the end C, and the one on the incline far away from it.

Salviati: You see, then, that the proposition "motion along the perpendicular is faster than along the incline" does not hold universally, except for motions that begin from the first endpoint — that is, from rest. Without that condition, the proposition would be so defective that even its flat contradictory could be true: namely, that motion on the incline is faster than on the perpendicular; for it is true that on the incline we can take a stretch which the body passes in less time than it takes to pass an equal stretch on the perpendicular. Now, since motion on the incline is in some places faster, and in others slower, than on the perpendicular, it follows that along some places of the incline, the time of the body's motion will bear a greater proportion to the time of the body's motion along certain places of the perpendicular than the space passed bears to the space passed; and in other places, the proportion of time to time will be smaller than the proportion of space to space. For example: let two bodies set out from rest — that is, from the point C — one along the perpendicular CB and the other along the incline CA. In the time in which the body on the perpendicular will have passed the whole of CB, the other will have passed CT, which is smaller. And therefore the time along CT will bear a greater proportion to the time along CB — the two times being equal — than the line TC bears to the line CB, since the same magnitude bears a greater proportion to a smaller one than to a larger. And conversely: if on CA, prolonged as far as need be, we took a stretch equal to CB, but passed in a shorter time, then the time on the incline would bear a smaller proportion to the time on the perpendicular than the space bears to the space. If, then, on the incline and on the perpendicular we can conceive of spaces and speeds such that the proportions between the spaces come out both smaller and greater than the proportions between the times, we may very reasonably grant that there are also spaces along which the times of the motions retain the same proportion as the spaces.

Sagredo: That takes care of my biggest scruple: I now see that what looked to me like a contradiction is not only possible but — I'll go further — necessary. What I still don't see, though, is that one of these possible or necessary cases is the one we actually need right now. Is it really true that the time of descent along CA stands to the time of fall along CB in the same proportion as the line CA stands to the line CB, so that we can say, without contradiction, that the speeds along the incline CA and along the vertical CB are equal?

Salviati: Be content, for now, that I've cleared away your disbelief; the science itself you must wait for until another occasion — namely, when you see what our Academician has demonstrated about local motions. There you'll find it proved that in the time the falling body drops through the whole of CB, the other body descends along CA only as far as the point T, where the perpendicular drawn down from B cuts it. And to find where the body falling down the vertical would be at the moment the other one reaches A, draw the perpendicular to CA from A itself, extending it and CB until they meet: that intersection is the point you're looking for. In the meantime, notice how this shows that the motion along CB really is faster than along the incline CA (taking C as the starting point of the two motions we're comparing): the line CB is longer than CT, and the line from C down to where CB's extension meets the perpendicular drawn from A is longer than CA, and so the motion along it is faster than along CA. But when we compare the motion through the whole of CA not with the whole motion made in that same time along the extended vertical, but with the motion made in a part of that time through the part CB alone, then there's nothing absurd in this: the body on CA, continuing its descent past T, can reach A in a time that stands to the other time in exactly the proportion the line CA has to the line CB.

Now let's take up our original point again, which was to show that a heavy body, starting from rest, passes in its descent through every degree of slowness before reaching any degree of speed it acquires. Going back to the same figure, remember what we had agreed: that the body falling down the vertical CB and the one descending along the incline CA arrive at B and A having acquired equal degrees of speed. Moving on from there, I don't think you'll have the least difficulty granting that on another plane tilted less steeply than AC — say, DA — the descending motion would be slower still than on the plane CA. So there's no doubt whatever that planes can be marked out so slightly raised above the horizontal AB that the moving body — our same ball — would take as long as you please to reach the point A; after all, to get there along the plane BA itself, even infinite time isn't enough, and the motion grows ever slower as the slope grows smaller. So you must necessarily admit that we can take a point above B, so close to B, that if we drew a plane from it to A, the ball wouldn't cross it even in a year.

Now here is what you need to know: the impetus — that is, the degree of speed — that the ball has acquired by the time it arrives at point A is such that, if it kept moving at that same degree uniformly, neither speeding up nor slowing down, then in a second interval equal to the time of its descent it would cover a distance twice the length of the inclined plane. For example: if the ball had taken an hour to travel the plane DA, then, continuing uniformly at the speed it had on reaching A, it would in one hour cover a distance twice the length of DA. And since (as we were saying) the degrees of speed acquired at B and at A — by bodies that set out from any point you please on the vertical CB, one descending along the inclined plane and the other straight down the vertical — are always equal, it follows that the falling body can start from a point so close to B that the degree of speed acquired at B would not be enough (kept forever constant) to carry it through a distance twice the length of the inclined plane in a year, or in ten years, or in a hundred.

So we can conclude as follows. Suppose it's true that, in the ordinary course of nature, a body — with all external and accidental hindrances removed — moves down inclined planes more and more slowly as the tilt gets smaller, until finally the slowness becomes infinite, which happens when the tilt runs out altogether and we arrive at the horizontal plane. And suppose it's equally true that the degree of speed acquired at some point on the inclined plane is equal to the degree of speed of the body falling down the vertical, at the point cut off by a horizontal line drawn through that same point of the incline. Then we are forced to admit that a falling body, starting from rest, passes through all the infinite degrees of slowness; and consequently, that to acquire any given degree of speed it must first move in a straight line, descending through a short or a long distance according as the speed to be acquired is smaller or greater, and according as the plane of descent is gently or steeply tilted. So there can be a plane with so little tilt that, to acquire a given degree of speed on it, the body would first have to move through an immense distance, over an immense time. On the horizontal plane, then, no speed at all will ever be acquired naturally, since there the body will never move in the first place. But motion along a horizontal line, which slopes neither down nor up, is circular motion around the center. Therefore circular motion is never acquired naturally without straight motion coming first; but once it has been acquired, it will continue perpetually at uniform speed.

I could explain these same truths to you, and even demonstrate them, by other lines of argument; but I don't want to interrupt our main discussion with such long digressions. I'll come back to them on some other occasion — all the more since we got onto this point not to use it as a necessary demonstration, but to dress up a Platonic conceit. And to that conceit I want to add one further particular observation, again our Academician's, and it has something marvelous about it. Let's imagine that among the decrees of the divine Architect was the thought of creating in the world these globes we see continually moving in circles; of establishing the center of their revolutions, and placing there the Sun, immovable; and then of fashioning all those globes in one and the same place, and giving them from there an inclination to move, descending toward the center, until they had acquired those degrees of speed that seemed right to the divine Mind itself — whereupon, the speeds acquired, they were turned into circulation, each in its own circle, keeping the speed it had already gained. The question is: at what height, at what distance from the Sun, was the place where those globes were first created — and could they all have been created in the same place? To carry out the investigation, you take from the most expert astronomers the sizes of the circles in which the planets revolve, and likewise the times of their revolutions. From those two pieces of knowledge you work out how much faster, say, Jupiter's motion is than Saturn's; and having found (as is in fact the case) that Jupiter moves faster, it must be that, both having set out from the same height, Jupiter descended farther than Saturn — as we know is really true, his orbit lying below Saturn's. But pressing further: from the proportion between the speeds of Jupiter and Saturn, from the distance between their orbits, and from the ratio of acceleration in natural motion, one can work out at what height, at what distance from the center of their revolutions, lay the place they set out from. Once that's found and fixed, we ask whether Mars, descending from there down to his own orbit, checks out — and we find that the size of his orbit and the speed of his motion agree with what the calculation gives us. The same is done for the Earth, for Venus, and for Mercury; and the sizes of their circles and the speeds of their motions come so close to what the computations yield that it is a thing of wonder.

Sagredo: I've listened to this idea with the keenest pleasure; and if I didn't think that carrying out those calculations precisely would be a long and laborious undertaking — and perhaps too hard for me to follow — I'd insist you do it here and now.

Salviati: The operation really is long and difficult, and besides, I wouldn't trust myself to reconstruct it on the spot; so we'll save it for another time. For now let's return to our original subject, picking up right where we digressed. If I remember correctly, we were in the middle of establishing that motion in a straight line can be of no use whatever among the parts of a world in good order. And we were going on to say that it's just the opposite with circular motions: the one a body makes turning on itself keeps it always in the same place, while the one that carries a body around the circumference of a circle, about its own stable and fixed center, puts neither the body nor its neighbors into any disorder. For such motion, in the first place, is finite and bounded — indeed, not just finite and bounded, but there is no point on the circumference that isn't at once the first and the last terminus of the circuit; and continuing around its assigned circumference, the body leaves everything else, inside that circle and out, free for the needs of others, without ever hindering or disturbing them. Now, this being a motion in which the moving body is forever departing from its terminus and forever arriving at it, circular motion alone, in the first place, can be uniform. For acceleration happens in a body when it's moving toward a terminus it feels an inclination for, and slowing happens through its reluctance to leave that terminus and move away; and since in circular motion the body is always departing from its natural terminus and always moving toward that very same terminus, the reluctance and the inclination in it are always of equal strength. From that equality comes a speed neither slowed nor quickened — that is, uniform motion. And from this uniformity, and from the motion's being bounded, perpetual continuation can follow, the circuits repeating forever — which can never be found naturally on an unbounded line, or in a motion that is continually slowing or accelerating. And I say naturally, because straight motion that slows down is violent motion — forced motion — which cannot be perpetual; and accelerated straight motion necessarily arrives at its terminus, if there is one; and if there isn't one, there can't be any motion toward it either, for nature does not set out for a place it is impossible to reach. I conclude, therefore, that circular motion alone can naturally suit the natural bodies that make up the universe and stand in their best arrangement; and that straight motion, at the very most, is assigned by nature to bodies and their parts only when they find themselves outside their proper places, stuck in a bad arrangement, and therefore in need of getting back to their natural state by the shortest road. From this it seems to me we can quite reasonably conclude that, to maintain perfect order among the parts of the world, the movable ones must be movable only in circles; and if there are any that do not move circularly, they must of necessity be immovable — for nothing except rest and circular motion is fit for the preservation of order. And I'm more than a little surprised that Aristotle, who after all held that the terrestrial globe is located at the center of the world and stays there immovably, didn't say that among natural bodies some are movable by nature and others immovable — especially since he had already defined nature as the principle of motion and of rest.

Simplicio: Aristotle, being a man who never promised himself more from his own genius — piercing as it was — than is fitting, held in his philosophizing that the experiences of the senses must be set above any argument fabricated by human ingenuity; and he said that those who would deny the senses deserved to be punished by being deprived of the sense in question. Now, who is so blind that he cannot see the parts of earth and of water moving, as heavy things do, naturally downward — that is, toward the center of the universe, appointed by nature herself as the goal and terminus of straight motion deorsum — downward? And who cannot see, just as plainly, fire and air moving straight up toward the concave arch of the lunar orb, as toward the natural terminus of motion sursum — upward? And this being so manifestly seen, and since we are certain that eadem est ratio totius et partium — the same reasoning holds for the whole as for its parts — why shouldn't we call it a true and manifest proposition that the natural motion of earth is straight motion ad medium — toward the center — and that of fire, straight motion a medio — away from it?

Salviati: On the strength of this argument of yours, the very most you could claim to have won is this: that just as the parts of the earth, when removed from their whole — that is, from the place where they naturally reside, and so, in the end, put into a bad and disordered arrangement — return to their place spontaneously, and therefore naturally, in straight motion, so (granting that eadem sit ratio totius et partium — that whole and parts follow the same rule) one might infer that if the terrestrial globe were forcibly removed from the place nature assigned it, it would go back there in a straight line. That, as I say, is the most that could be conceded to you, and only after granting you every sort of accommodation along the way. But anyone who wanted to audit these accounts rigorously would first deny that the parts of the earth, in returning to their whole, move in a straight line at all, rather than in a circular one, or in some mixture of the two; and you would certainly have your work cut out demonstrating the contrary, as you'll see clearly in the replies to the particular reasons and experiments brought forward by Ptolemy and Aristotle. Secondly, if someone told you that the parts of the earth move not in order to go to the center of the world but to go and reunite with their whole, and that this is why their natural inclination is toward the center of the terrestrial globe — an inclination by which they conspire to form it and preserve it — then what other whole, what other center, would you find in the world for the entire terrestrial globe to seek to return to, if it were removed, so that the reasoning about the whole would still match the reasoning about the parts? Add to this that neither Aristotle nor you will ever prove that the Earth is de facto — in actual fact — at the center of the universe; if any center can be assigned to the universe at all, we shall find it is rather the Sun that sits there, as you'll come to understand as we proceed.

Now, just as from the concordant conspiring of all the parts of the earth to form their whole it follows that they converge on it from every side with equal inclination, and, to unite as closely as possible, fit themselves together into a sphere — why shouldn't we believe that the Moon, the Sun, and the other bodies of the world are likewise round for no other reason than the concordant instinct and natural convergence of all their component parts? And if at some time one of those parts were separated from its whole by some violence, isn't it reasonable to believe it would return there spontaneously, by natural instinct? And in this way to conclude that straight motion belongs equally to all the bodies of the world?

Simplicio: There isn't the least doubt that if you're prepared to deny not only the first principles of the sciences but manifest experience and the senses themselves, you can never be convinced or budged from any opinion you've conceived; and I will hold my peace, rather because contra negantes principia non est disputandum — with those who deny first principles there is no arguing — than because your reasons have persuaded me. But staying with what you've just now pronounced — since you throw doubt on whether even the motion of heavy bodies is straight — how can you ever reasonably deny that the parts of earth, that is, the heaviest of materials, descend toward the center in straight motion? When they're dropped from a very tall tower, with walls dead straight and built plumb, they come down grazing the wall, so to speak, and strike the ground at exactly — to a hair — the point where a plumb bob would land, hung from a cord fixed above at the very spot the stone was released. Isn't that a more than evident argument that this motion is straight, and toward the center? In the second place, you call into doubt whether the parts of the earth move, as Aristotle affirms, toward the center of the world — as though he hadn't demonstrated it conclusively from contrary motions, arguing like this: the motion of heavy things is contrary to that of light things; but the motion of light things is seen to be straight upward, that is, toward the circumference of the world; therefore the motion of heavy things is straight toward the center of the world, and it happens per accidens — merely incidentally — that it is toward the center of the Earth, since the one center happens to coincide with the other. As for asking what a piece of the lunar globe or of the Sun would do if it were separated from its whole — that's an idle question, because it asks what would follow from an impossibility; for, as Aristotle also demonstrates, celestial bodies are impervious to change, impenetrable, and unbreakable, so the case can never arise. And even if it did arise, and the separated piece returned to its whole, it wouldn't return as a heavy body or a light one — for the same Aristotle proves that celestial bodies are neither heavy nor light.

Salviati: How reasonable my doubt is, about whether heavy bodies move in a straight and plumb line, you'll hear when I come to examine that particular argument, as I said just now. As for the second point, I'm amazed you should need Aristotle's paralogism — his flawed reasoning — exposed for you, when it's so obvious on its face, and that you don't notice Aristotle is assuming the very thing in question. So observe...

Simplicio: Please, Salviati — speak of Aristotle with more respect. To whom could you ever sell the idea that a man who was the first, the only, the admirable expounder of the syllogistic form, of demonstration, of refutations, of the ways of detecting sophisms and paralogisms — in short, of the whole of logic — should then blunder so gravely as to assume as known the very thing in question? Gentlemen, first understand him perfectly; then try your hand at refuting him.

Salviati: Simplicio, we're here talking among ourselves, familiarly, to investigate some truth. I'll never take it badly that you point out my errors; and whenever I've failed to grasp Aristotle's meaning, correct me freely — I'll thank you for it. In the meantime, allow me to lay out my difficulties, and to answer a word or two of what you just said. Logic, as you know perfectly well, is the organ with which we philosophize — the instrument of the trade. But just as a craftsman can be excellent at building organs and yet untrained in playing them, so a man can be a great logician and yet little practiced in actually using logic — just as there are many who know the whole art of poetry by heart and then are hopeless at composing four lines of verse, and others who possess every one of Leonardo's precepts and couldn't paint a footstool. Playing the organ isn't learned from those who know how to build organs, but from those who know how to play them; poetry is learned from constant reading of the poets; painting is picked up by constant drawing and painting; and demonstration is learned from reading books full of demonstrations — and those are the books of the mathematicians alone, not of the logicians. Now, back to our subject. What Aristotle sees, concerning the motion of light bodies, is fire departing from any point on the surface of the terrestrial globe and moving straight away from it, rising upward. That truly is moving toward a circumference larger than the Earth's — indeed, Aristotle himself has it move up to the concave arch of the Moon's orb. But that this larger circumference is the circumference of the world, or concentric with it, so that moving toward the one is also moving toward the other — that cannot be affirmed unless you first suppose that the center of the Earth, the point we watch ascending light bodies move away from, is the same as the center of the world. And that is as much as to say that the terrestrial globe sits at the center of the world — which is precisely what we are doubting, and precisely what Aristotle set out to prove. And will you tell me this isn't a manifest paralogism?

Sagredo: This argument of Aristotle's had struck me as deficient and inconclusive in another respect as well, even if we grant him that the circumference toward which fire moves in a straight line is the one that encloses the world. For take, inside a circle, not just the center but any other point you please: every moving body that sets out from it along a straight line, in any direction whatever, will without any doubt be heading toward the circumference, and if it keeps moving it will get there too — so it's perfectly true to say that it moves toward the circumference. But it will not be true that a body moving along those same lines with the contrary motion is heading toward the center — not unless the point we chose was the center itself, or the motion ran along that one single line which, drawn from the chosen point, passes through the center. So the argument "Fire, moving in a straight line, goes toward the circumference of the world; therefore the parts of earth, which move along the same lines with the contrary motion, go toward the center of the world" concludes nothing at all — unless we first assume that fire's lines, extended, pass through the center of the world. And since we know for certain that those lines pass through the center of the terrestrial globe (being perpendicular to its surface, not slanted), then to make the conclusion work we must assume that the center of the Earth is the same as the center of the world — or at least that the parts of fire and earth rise and fall only along a single line that passes through the center of the world. And that is false, and it contradicts experience, which shows us that the parts of fire ascend not along one single line but along the infinitely many lines drawn from the center of the Earth toward every part of the world — always rising perpendicular to the surface of the terrestrial globe.

Salviati: Sagredo, you very ingeniously drive Aristotle into the same difficulty by exposing the obvious equivocation; but add one more awkwardness to it. We see that the Earth is spherical, so we're certain it has a center. We see that all its parts move toward that center — we're forced to say so, since their motions are all perpendicular to the Earth's surface. We understand that in moving toward the center of the Earth, they move toward their whole, toward their universal mother. And then we're so obliging that we let ourselves be talked into believing their natural instinct is not to head for the center of the Earth but for the center of the universe — which we don't know where it is, or whether it exists at all; and which, even if it does exist, is nothing but an imaginary point, a nothing, with no power to do anything.

As for Simplicio's last remark — that debating whether parts of the Sun or the Moon or some other celestial body, if separated from their whole, would naturally return to it is a waste of breath, because the case is impossible, it being evident from Aristotle's demonstrations that celestial bodies are impervious to change, impenetrable, indivisible, and so on — I answer this: not one of the conditions by which Aristotle makes celestial bodies differ from elemental ones has any footing except what he deduces from the difference between their natural motions. So that once we deny that circular motion belongs to celestial bodies alone, and affirm that it suits all naturally movable bodies, one of two conclusions necessarily follows: either the attributes of generable or ingenerable, alterable or inalterable, divisible or indivisible, and so on, belong equally and in common to all the bodies of the universe — celestial and elemental alike — or else Aristotle went badly and mistakenly wrong in deducing from circular motion the attributes he assigned to celestial bodies.

Simplicio: This way of philosophizing tends toward the subversion of all natural philosophy — toward throwing heaven and Earth and the whole universe into disorder and wreckage. But I believe the foundations of the Peripatetics are solid enough that there's no fear of new sciences being built on their ruins.

Salviati: Don't worry yourself about heaven or the Earth, and don't fear their subversion — nor philosophy's either. As for heaven, it's pointless for you to fear for the very thing you yourself hold to be inalterable and impervious to change. As for the Earth, we're trying to ennoble and perfect it: we're working to make it like the celestial bodies and, in a certain way, to set it almost in heaven — the place your philosophers banished it from. And philosophy itself can only profit from our disputes: if our thoughts turn out to be true, new ground will have been gained; if false, then in refuting them the original doctrines will stand all the more firmly confirmed. Worry instead about certain philosophers, and see how you might help them and prop them up — because the science itself cannot help but advance. And now, back to our subject: bring forward freely whatever occurs to you in support of the total difference Aristotle sets up between celestial bodies and the elemental region, making the former ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, and so on, and the latter corruptible, alterable, and the rest.

Simplicio: I don't yet see that Aristotle needs any rescuing. He is still on his feet, firm and strong — indeed, he hasn't yet even been attacked by you, let alone knocked down. And what will your shield be against this first assault? Aristotle writes: What is generated comes to be from a contrary, in some subject; and likewise it is corrupted, in some subject, from a contrary into a contrary — so that (mark this well) corruption and generation occur only among contraries. But the motions of contraries are contrary to each other; so if no contrary can be assigned to the celestial body — since no other motion is contrary to circular motion — then nature did very well indeed to exempt from contraries the thing that was meant to be ingenerable and incorruptible. With this first foundation laid, it follows immediately that the celestial body cannot grow, cannot alter, is impervious to change, and finally is eternal — a dwelling fit for the immortal gods, in agreement with the opinion of every person who has any conception of the gods at all. He then confirms the same thing by the senses: in all past time, according to tradition and record, nothing is seen to have changed, either in the whole of the outermost heaven or in any one of its proper parts. And that no other motion is contrary to circular motion, Aristotle proves in many ways; but without rehearsing them all, it stands demonstrated plainly enough by this: the simple motions are only three — toward the center, away from the center, and around the center; of these, the two straight ones, sursum et deorsum — upward and downward — are manifestly contrary; and since one thing has only one contrary, there remains no other motion that could be contrary to the circular.
