There you have Aristotle's argument, as sharp and as conclusive as could be, by which the incorruptibility of heaven is proved.

Salviati: That is nothing more than the bare course of Aristotle's argument, which I sketched for you already — and the moment I deny that the motion you attribute to celestial bodies is off-limits to the Earth, his inference comes to nothing. So I tell you plainly: that circular motion you assign to celestial bodies suits the Earth as well. From which — granting that the rest of your argument is conclusive — one of three things will follow, as I said a little while ago and now repeat: either the Earth is itself ingenerable and incorruptible, just like the celestial bodies; or the celestial bodies are, like the elemental ones, generable, alterable, and so on; or else this difference of motions has nothing to do with generation and corruption.

Aristotle's argument — and yours — contains many propositions that shouldn't be granted lightly, and to examine it better it will be worth reducing it to as clean and distinct a form as possible. Sagredo will forgive me if he finds it tedious to hear the same things said over more than once — let him imagine he's listening to the arguments being rehearsed again in a public disputation. You say: "Generation and corruption occur only where there are contraries; contraries exist only among simple natural bodies, movable with contrary motions; contrary motions are only those made along straight lines between contrary endpoints; and of these there are only two, namely away from the center and toward the center; and such motions belong to no natural bodies except earth, fire, and the other two elements. Therefore generation and corruption exist only among the elements. And since the third simple motion — the circular motion around the center — has no contrary (for the other two are each other's contraries, and one thing has only one contrary), the natural body to which that motion belongs lacks a contrary; and having no contrary, it remains ingenerable and incorruptible, and so on, because where there is no contrariety there is no generation or corruption. But that motion belongs only to celestial bodies; therefore they alone are ingenerable, incorruptible, and so forth."

Now, first of all: it strikes me as a far easier thing to make sure whether the Earth — a vast body, and, being so near us, by far the easiest to handle — moves with an enormous motion, such as turning on itself once every twenty-four hours, than it is to understand and make sure whether generation and corruption arise from contraries — indeed, whether corruption and generation and contraries exist in nature at all. And if you, Simplicio, could tell me nature's method of operation when she generates, in the briefest time, a hundred thousand gnats out of a little vapor rising off fermenting grape must — showing me what the contraries are in that case, what thing is corrupted, and how — I would think even more highly of you than I already do, because I understand none of it. Furthermore, I would dearly love to know how and why these corrupting contraries are so gentle with crows and so fierce with doves, so tolerant of stags and so impatient with horses, granting the former more years of life — which is to say, of incorruptibility — than they grant the latter weeks. Peach trees and olive trees put their roots into the same soils; they stand exposed to the same cold spells, the same heat, the same rains and winds — in short, to the same contrarieties — and yet the one is destroyed in a short time while the other lives many hundreds of years. What's more, I have never managed to feel at ease with this substantial transmutation (keeping strictly within purely natural terms) by which a piece of matter is supposed to be so transformed that we must say it has been utterly destroyed, with nothing of its first being remaining in it, and that another body, completely different from it, has been produced out of it. When a body presents itself to me under one aspect and, a little later, under another very different one, I don't consider it impossible that this comes about by a simple rearrangement of parts, without anything being corrupted or anything new generated — we see metamorphoses of that kind every day of our lives. So I say it once more: if you mean to persuade me that the Earth cannot move in a circle by way of an argument from corruptibility and generability, you will have far more work on your hands than I will — for I'll prove you the contrary with arguments a good deal harder, but no less conclusive.

Sagredo: Salviati, forgive me for interrupting your reasoning. It delights me greatly — I find myself tangled in these same difficulties — but I suspect there's no getting to the bottom of it without abandoning our principal subject altogether. So if the original discussion can be carried forward, I'd judge it best to set this question of generation and corruption aside for a separate, complete conversation of its own — and the same, if it suits you and Simplicio, for the other particular questions the course of our talks brings up. I'll keep a separate note of them, so we can raise them another day and examine them in detail. As for the present one: since you say that once Aristotle is denied the claim that circular motion belongs to the celestial bodies but not to the Earth, it follows that whatever holds of the Earth — being generable, alterable, and so on — holds of the heavens too, let's leave aside whether generation and corruption exist in nature or not, and get back to investigating what the terrestrial globe actually does.

Simplicio: I cannot bring my ears to listen to anyone doubting whether generation and corruption exist in nature — a thing we have continually before our eyes, and about which Aristotle wrote two entire books. Once you start denying the principles of the sciences and casting doubt on the most manifest things, who doesn't know that you can prove whatever you please and defend any paradox whatsoever? If you don't see grasses, plants, and animals generated and corrupted every day, then what do you see? How can you fail to see the contrarieties perpetually jousting against one another — earth changing into water, water converting into air, air into fire, and air again condensing into clouds, rains, hail, and storms?

Sagredo: Of course we see all these things; and so, as far as this part goes — generation and corruption produced by contraries — we're happy to grant you Aristotle's argument. But if, using those very propositions you've granted to Aristotle, I prove to you that the celestial bodies are themselves every bit as generable and corruptible as the elemental ones, what will you say then?

Simplicio: I'll say you've done the impossible.

Sagredo: Tell me something, Simplicio: aren't these properties contrary to one another?

Simplicio: Which ones?

Sagredo: These: alterable and inalterable, subject to change and impervious to change, generable and ingenerable, corruptible and incorruptible?

Simplicio: As contrary as can be.

Sagredo: Well then — if that's so, and if it's also true that celestial bodies are ingenerable and incorruptible, I'll prove to you that of necessity the celestial bodies must be generable and corruptible.

Simplicio: That can only be a sophism.

Sagredo: Hear the argument first; then give it its name and refute it. The celestial bodies, because they are ingenerable and incorruptible, have contraries in nature — namely, the generable and corruptible bodies. But where there is contrariety, there is generation and corruption. Therefore the celestial bodies are generable and corruptible.

Simplicio: Didn't I tell you it could be nothing but a sophism? This is one of those horned arguments they call sorites — like the one about the Cretan who said that all Cretans were liars. Being a Cretan, he was telling a lie when he said Cretans were liars; therefore Cretans must be truthful, and consequently he, being a Cretan, was truthful; and so, in saying Cretans were liars, he spoke the truth; and since he was included among the Cretans, he must have been a liar after all. With sophisms of this sort you could keep spinning in circles for all eternity without ever concluding anything.

Sagredo: So far you've only given it its name. It remains for you to solve it — to show where the fallacy lies.

Simplicio: As for solving it and exposing its fallacy — don't you see, first of all, the open contradiction? The celestial bodies are ingenerable and incorruptible; therefore the celestial bodies are generable and corruptible? And besides, the contrariety isn't among the celestial bodies at all; it's among the elements, which have the contrariety of the motions sursum et deorsum and of lightness and heaviness. But the heavens, which move circularly — a motion to which no other is contrary — lack all contrariety, and are therefore incorruptible, and so on.

Sagredo: Gently, Simplicio. This contrariety, by which you say certain simple bodies are corruptible — does it reside in the very body that gets corrupted, or does it stand in relation to some other body? I mean: the moisture, say, by which a portion of earth is corrupted — does it reside in that earth itself, or in another body, such as the air or the water? I believe you'll say that just as the motions up and down, and heaviness and lightness — which you make the primary contraries — cannot exist in one and the same subject, so neither can wet and dry, or hot and cold. So you're forced to say that when a body is corrupted, this happens through a quality residing in another body, contrary to its own. Therefore, to make the celestial body corruptible, it's enough that there exist in nature bodies that have a contrariety to the celestial body — and the elements are exactly such bodies, if it's true that corruptibility is contrary to incorruptibility.

Simplicio: That is not enough, my dear sir. The elements alter and corrupt because they touch and mingle with one another, and so can bring their contrarieties to bear; but the celestial bodies are separate from the elements — the elements don't even touch them, though they touch the elements. If you want to prove generation and corruption in the celestial bodies, you must show that the contrarieties reside among those bodies themselves.

Sagredo: Then watch me find them there. The first source from which you draw the contrarieties of the elements is the contrariety of their motions up and down; therefore the principles those motions depend on must be contraries as well. And since one body moves upward by lightness and the other downward by heaviness, lightness and heaviness must be contrary to each other; and no less must we believe that those further principles are contraries which cause one body to be heavy and another light. But by your own account, lightness and heaviness follow from rarity and density; therefore density and rarity are contraries. And these conditions are found so abundantly in the celestial bodies that you yourselves hold the stars to be nothing but denser parts of their own heaven; and if that's so, the density of the stars must exceed the density of the rest of heaven by an almost infinite margin — which is obvious from heaven's being supremely transparent and the stars supremely opaque, and from the fact that up there no other qualities are to be found, beyond the more and less dense or rare, that could serve as principles of greater and lesser transparency. Since such contrarieties exist among the celestial bodies, then, it necessarily follows either that they too are generable and corruptible in the very same way the elemental bodies are — or else that contrariety is not the cause of corruptibility, and so on.

Simplicio: Neither one is necessary — because density and rarity in the celestial bodies are not contraries to each other the way they are in elemental bodies. They don't depend on the primary qualities, hot and cold, which are contraries, but on there being much or little matter in proportion to the bulk. Now "much" and "little" express only a relative opposition — the weakest kind of opposition there is — and that has nothing to do with generation and corruption.

Sagredo: So then: for density and rarity to do their job among the elements — to be the cause of heaviness and lightness, which in turn can be causes of the contrary motions sursum et deorsum, on which in turn the contrarieties for generation and corruption depend — it isn't enough for them to be the kind of dense and rare that contain much or little matter under the same bulk; they must be dense and rare thanks to the primary qualities, cold and hot. Otherwise nothing gets accomplished. But if that's so, Aristotle has cheated us: he should have said so from the start, and left it in writing, that the generable and corruptible bodies are those simple bodies which are movable with the simple motions up and down, depending on lightness and heaviness, caused by rarity and density, made by much or little matter, by virtue of heat and cold — and not have stopped at simple upward and downward motion. Because I assure you that as far as making bodies heavy or light — so that they can then move with contrary motions — any density and rarity whatever will do the job, whether it comes from hot and cold or from anything else you please; hot and cold have nothing to do with the operation. You can see for yourself that a piece of iron glowing red — which surely may be called hot — weighs just the same, and moves in just the same way, as when it's cold. But leaving even that aside: how do you know that celestial density and rarity don't depend on cold and heat?

Simplicio: I do know: it's because such qualities have no place among the celestial bodies, which are neither hot nor cold.

Salviati: I can see we're about to plunge back into a boundless sea with no way out — because this is sailing without a compass, without stars, without oars, without a rudder, so that we'd be forced to grope from reef to reef, or run aground, or sail on forever lost. So if we mean to press ahead toward our main subject, as you advise, we must for now set aside this general question of whether straight-line motion is necessary in nature and belongs to certain bodies, and come down to the particular demonstrations, observations, and experiments. First we'll lay out everything that Aristotle, Ptolemy, and others have offered up to now as proof of the Earth's stability; second, we'll try to answer it; and last, we'll bring forward the arguments by which a person might be persuaded that the Earth, no less than the Moon or any other planet, deserves to be counted among the natural bodies that move in circles.

Sagredo: I'll hold to that plan all the more gladly because your grand architectural argument satisfies me far more than Aristotle's does. Yours puts my mind at rest without a single snag, while the other trips me over some stumbling block at every step. And I don't see how Simplicio wasn't instantly persuaded by the reason you gave to prove that straight-line motion can have no place in nature — provided we assume the parts of the universe are arranged in their best constitution and perfectly ordered.

Salviati: One moment, please, Sagredo — for it has just occurred to me how I might satisfy Simplicio too, so long as he isn't so bound to Aristotle's every word that he counts it sacrilege to depart from any of them. There's no doubt that, to maintain the perfect arrangement and order of the universe's parts as far as position is concerned, nothing will serve but circular motion and rest. As for motion in a straight line, I don't see that it can be good for anything except restoring to its natural constitution some particle of one of the integral world-bodies that had been separated from its whole by some accident, as we said earlier. Now let's consider the whole terrestrial globe, and see what can become of it, given that it and the other world-bodies must keep themselves in their best and natural arrangement. One of three things must be said: either it remains and keeps itself perpetually motionless in its place; or, staying always in the same place, it spins upon itself; or it goes around a center, traveling along the circumference of a circle. Of these possibilities, Aristotle and Ptolemy and all their followers say that it has always observed, and will maintain forever, the first — perpetual rest in the same place. Well then, why in heaven's name shouldn't they say that its natural tendency is to remain motionless, rather than making its nature the motion downward — a motion it has never once moved with, and never will? As for straight-line motion, let nature use it to restore to their wholes those particles of earth, of water, of air, of fire, and of every other integral world-body, whenever one of them happens to find itself separated, and so transported to a place out of order — unless it should turn out that even for making this restitution some circular motion is better suited. This primary position, it seems to me, answers all the other consequences far better — even keeping to Aristotle's own way of thinking — than attributing straight-line motions to the elements as an intrinsic and natural principle. And that's plain to see. For if I ask the Peripatetic whether, holding as he does that the celestial bodies are incorruptible and eternal, he believes the terrestrial globe is not so — believes it corruptible and mortal, so that a time must come when, while the Sun and the Moon and the other stars carry on their existence and their operations, the Earth is no longer to be found in the world but has been destroyed along with the rest of the elements and gone to nothing — I'm certain he will answer no. Therefore generation and corruption belong to the parts, not to the whole — and to the very smallest and most superficial parts at that, which are as good as imperceptible compared with the whole mass. And since Aristotle infers generation and corruption from the contrariety of straight-line motions, let's leave such motions to the parts, which alone alter and decay, and assign to the whole globe and the whole sphere of the elements either circular motion or a perpetual staying in their own place — the only conditions fit for perpetuation and for keeping the perfect order. What we say here of earth can be said with the same reasoning of fire and of the greater part of the air: the Peripatetics have been reduced to assigning those elements, as their intrinsic and natural motion, one with which they have never moved and never will, and to calling foreign to their nature the very motion with which they move now, have always moved, and will move perpetually. I say this because they assign to air and fire the motion upward, with which neither of those elements as a whole has ever moved — only some particle of them, and that for no other purpose than to return to its perfect constitution after finding itself outside its natural place. And on the other hand they call contrary to their nature the circular motion with which they move incessantly — forgetting, it would seem, what Aristotle has said again and again: that nothing violent can last for long.

Simplicio: To all of this we have the most fitting replies, which I'll set aside for now in order to come to the more particular reasons and to the evidence of the senses — which in the end must be given precedence, as Aristotle rightly says, over anything human reasoning can supply.

Sagredo: Then let everything said so far serve to help us weigh which of the two general accounts is the more probable. I mean Aristotle's, which would persuade us that the sublunar bodies — everything below the Moon — are by nature generable and corruptible and so on, and therefore utterly different in essence from the celestial bodies, those being impervious to change, ingenerable, incorruptible, and the rest, a conclusion drawn from the difference of their simple motions; or this account of Salviati's, which, supposing the integral parts of the world to be arranged in their best constitution, necessarily excludes straight-line motions from the simple natural bodies as being of no use in nature, and takes the Earth to be itself one of the celestial bodies, adorned with every privilege that belongs to them. So far his account rings far truer to me than the other. So let Simplicio be good enough to produce all the particular reasons, experiments, and observations, natural and astronomical alike, by which one might be persuaded that the Earth is different from the celestial bodies, motionless, and set at the center of the world — and whatever else there is that would rule out its moving like a planet, like Jupiter or the Moon and so on. And Salviati, out of his courtesy, will kindly answer them point by point.

Simplicio: Here you are, then, to begin with: two very powerful demonstrations proving that the Earth is completely different from the celestial bodies. First: bodies that are generable, corruptible, alterable, and so on are utterly different from bodies that are ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, and so on. The Earth is generable, corruptible, alterable, and so on; the celestial bodies are ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, and so on. Therefore the Earth is utterly different from the celestial bodies.

Sagredo: For your first argument, you're bringing back to the table the same dish that has been sitting on it all day and was only just now cleared away.

Simplicio: Gently, sir; hear the rest, and you'll see how different it is. Before, the minor premise was proved a priori — from first principles; now I want to prove it to you a posteriori — from experience. Judge for yourself whether that's the same thing. I prove the minor premise, then, since the major is perfectly obvious. The experience of the senses shows us that on Earth continual generations, corruptions, alterations, and so on take place — and not one of these, whether by our own senses or by the traditions and records of our ancestors, has ever been seen in heaven. Therefore heaven is inalterable and so on, and the Earth alterable and so on, and therefore different from heaven. The second argument I take from a principal and essential attribute, and it's this: a body that is by nature dark and devoid of light is different from bodies that are luminous and shining. The Earth is dark and without light; the celestial bodies are splendid and full of light; therefore, and so on. Answer these first, so we don't pile up too great a heap, and then I'll bring out others.

Salviati: As for the first, whose force you draw from experience, I'd like you to set out for me more precisely the alterations you see happening on Earth and not in heaven, on account of which you call the Earth alterable and heaven not.

Simplicio: On Earth I see grasses, plants, and animals continually generated and decaying; winds, rains, tempests, and storms stirred up; in short, this face of the Earth in perpetual metamorphosis. And not one of these changes can be made out in the celestial bodies, whose constitution and configuration agree to the letter with every record we possess — with nothing new ever generated there, and nothing old destroyed.

Salviati: But if you're going to rest your case on these visible — or rather, these actually seen — experiences, then you must count China and America as celestial bodies, because you've certainly never seen in them the alterations you see here in Italy; so as far as your own perception goes, they are inalterable.

Simplicio: Even if I haven't witnessed those alterations in those places with my own senses, there are reliable reports of them; besides which, cum eadem sit ratio totius et partium — the same reasoning holding for whole and parts — those countries, being parts of the Earth just like ours, must be alterable just as ours are.

Salviati: And why haven't you observed and seen these things for yourself, with your own eyes, instead of having to fall back on other people's reports?

Simplicio: Because those countries, besides not being exposed to our eyes, are so remote that our sight could never reach far enough to make out such changes.

Salviati: Now see how you have stumbled, all on your own, onto the flaw in your argument. If you say that the alterations seen on Earth near at hand couldn't be made out in America because of the great distance, much less could you see them on the Moon, hundreds of times farther away. And if you believe in the changes in Mexico on the strength of news arriving from there, what dispatches have reached you from the Moon to inform you that there is no alteration in it? So from your not seeing alterations in heaven — where, even if there were any, you couldn't see them for the distance, and from which no news is to be had — you cannot conclude that there are none, the way you rightly conclude, from seeing and hearing of them, that there are alterations on Earth.

Simplicio: Then I'll find you changes that have taken place on Earth so vast that, if any such had happened on the Moon, they could perfectly well have been observed from down here. We have it from the most ancient records that once, at the strait of Gibraltar, the headlands Abila and Calpe were joined together, along with lesser mountains that held the Ocean back; but when those mountains were parted — whatever the cause — and the opening let in the waters of the sea, the flood ran so far inland that it formed the whole Mediterranean Sea. If we consider its vastness, and the difference in appearance that the surface of water and the surface of land must present when seen from far away, there's no doubt that such a change could easily have been made out by anyone standing on the Moon — just as we inhabitants of the Earth would be able to make out alterations of that size on the Moon. But there is no record that any such thing was ever seen. So there's no foothold left for saying that any of the celestial bodies is alterable, and so forth.

Salviati: That changes so vast have taken place on the Moon, I wouldn't dare to say; but neither am I at all certain that they couldn't have. And since a change of that kind could show itself to us only as some variation between the brighter and the darker regions of the Moon, I don't know that we have had, here on Earth, any curious selenographers — Moon-mappers — who over a long run of years have kept us supplied with lunar maps so exact that we could be sure no such change has ever taken place in the face of the Moon. For a detailed description of its features, I find nothing more precise than one person saying it looks like a human face, another that it's like a lion's muzzle, and another that it's Cain with a bundle of thorns on his back. So to say "Heaven is inalterable, because on the Moon or in some other celestial body we don't see the alterations we make out on Earth" has no force to conclude anything at all.

Sagredo: And for my part there's another scruple still nagging me about this first argument of Simplicio's, which I'd like removed. So I ask him: was the Earth generable and corruptible before the Mediterranean flood, or did it only begin to be so then?

Simplicio: It was without doubt generable and corruptible before as well; but that was a change so vast that it could have been observed even from the Moon.

Sagredo: Well then: if the Earth was generable and corruptible even before that flood, why can't the Moon be so equally, without any such change? Why is something required of the Moon that counted for nothing in the case of the Earth?

Salviati: A very sharp thrust. But I begin to suspect that Simplicio is bending a little the meaning of the texts of Aristotle and the other Peripatetics. They say they hold heaven inalterable because no star has ever been seen generated or destroyed in it — and a star is perhaps a smaller part of heaven than a city is of the Earth; yet countless cities have been destroyed so thoroughly that not even their traces remain.

Sagredo: I truly thought otherwise: I believed Simplicio was keeping quiet about this reading of the text to spare the Master and his disciples a charge even uglier than the first. What foolishness it is to say "the celestial region is inalterable because stars are not generated and destroyed in it"! Is there anyone who has seen one terrestrial globe decay and another regenerated in its place? And isn't it accepted by all philosophers that very few stars in heaven are smaller than the Earth, while a very great many are much, much bigger? So for a star in heaven to decay is no smaller a thing than for the whole terrestrial globe to be destroyed. If, then, in order to introduce generation and corruption into the universe with any truth, it's required that bodies as vast as a star be destroyed and regenerated, you may as well strike the whole idea out — for I assure you that no one will ever see the terrestrial globe, or any other integral body of the world, decay in such a way that, after standing in view for centuries past, it dissolves without leaving a trace of itself behind.

Salviati: But to give Simplicio satisfaction in overflowing measure, and to free him from error if that can be done, I say that in our own age we have new events and observations of such a kind that, if Aristotle were alive today, I have no doubt at all he would change his opinion. That follows plainly from his own way of philosophizing. When he writes that he considers the heavens inalterable and so on because nothing new has been seen generated there and nothing old dissolved, he implicitly lets us understand that if he had seen any such event, he would have judged the contrary — and would have put the experience of the senses ahead of natural reasoning, as is proper. For if he had meant to give no weight to the senses, he would not have argued immutability from the fact that no change was ever sensibly seen.

Simplicio: Aristotle laid his principal foundation on a priori reasoning, showing the necessity of the heavens' inalterability by his natural principles, manifest and clear; and he then established the same conclusion a posteriori, by the senses and by the traditions of the ancients.

Salviati: What you describe is the method by which he wrote out his doctrine, but I don't believe it's the method by which he investigated it. I take it as certain that he first worked to assure himself of the conclusion as far as possible by way of the senses, experiments, and observations, and afterward went hunting for the means to demonstrate it — because that's how things are mostly done in the demonstrative sciences. And the reason is this: when the conclusion is true, then working backward from it — the method of resolution — you readily come upon some proposition already demonstrated, or arrive at some principle known in its own right; but if the conclusion is false, you can go on forever without ever meeting a single known truth — unless you happen to run into some impossibility or manifest absurdity along the way. And have no doubt that Pythagoras, long before he found the demonstration for which he offered the hecatomb — that sacrifice of a hundred oxen — had already made sure that in a right triangle the square on the side opposite the right angle is equal to the squares on the other two sides; and certainty about the conclusion helps not a little in the search for the demonstration — speaking always of the demonstrative sciences. But however Aristotle actually proceeded, whether the a priori reasoning came before the a posteriori evidence of the senses or the other way around, it's enough that this same Aristotle puts sensory experience ahead of all reasonings, as has been said more than once; and besides, as for the a priori arguments, we've already examined how much force they carry. Now, coming back to our subject, I say that what has been discovered in the heavens in our own times is such, and has been such, as can give complete satisfaction to all philosophers. In particular bodies and in the universal expanse of heaven alike, phenomena have been seen — and are being seen still — exactly like those we call generations and corruptions here among ourselves. Excellent astronomers have observed many comets generated and dissolved in regions higher than the Moon's orbit, besides the two new stars of 1572 and 1604, indisputably far above all the planets; and on the face of the Sun itself, thanks to the telescope, dense dark materials are seen being produced and dissolved, looking very like the clouds that surround the Earth — and many of them are so vast that they far exceed not only the Mediterranean basin but all of Africa and Asia besides. Now, if Aristotle saw these things, what do you think he would say and do, Simplicio?

Simplicio: I don't know what Aristotle would do or say, for he was the master of the sciences; but I know in part what his followers do and say — and what they must do and say, if they're not to be left without a guide, without an escort, and without a chief in philosophy. As for the comets: weren't those modern astronomers who wanted to make them celestial defeated by the Anti-Tycho, the book written against Tycho — and defeated with their own weapons, I mean by way of parallaxes — those apparent shifts in position — and calculations turned round and round in a hundred ways, concluding at last, in Aristotle's favor, that the comets are all elemental? And with that torn up — and it was the whole foundation the lovers of novelty had — what do they have left to stand on?

Salviati: Gently, Simplicio. What does this modern author of yours say about the new stars of 1572 and 1604, and about the sunspots? As for the comets, I myself would make little difficulty about placing their birth below the Moon or above it; I've never put much stock in Tycho's long-windedness, and I feel no reluctance at all about believing their matter is elemental stuff, free to rise as high as it pleases without meeting any obstacle in the impenetrability of the Peripatetic heaven — which I take to be far thinner, more yielding, and more subtle than our own air. As for the parallax calculations, there is first the doubt whether comets are even subject to that effect, and then the inconstancy of the observations the computations are built on; together these make me equally suspicious of the one set of opinions and the other — all the more because it seems to me the Anti-Tycho sometimes bends the observations to fit his own design, or writes off as faulty the ones that stand against it.

Simplicio: As for the new stars, the Anti-Tycho makes short work of them in four words: such modern new stars, he says, are no certain parts of the celestial bodies, and if his adversaries want to prove that there is alteration and generation up there, they must demonstrate changes in the stars that were charted ages ago — the ones nobody doubts are celestial things — and that they can never do by any means. As for the matter some claim is generated and dissolved on the face of the Sun, he makes no mention of it at all; from which I gather he takes it for a fable, or for illusions of the telescope, or at most for little effects produced in the air — in short, for anything in the world except celestial matter.

Salviati: But you, Simplicio — what answer have you worked out to the objection of these troublesome spots that have come along to muddy the heavens, and worse still, the Peripatetic philosophy? As that philosophy's intrepid defender, you must have found some remedy, some solution — and you mustn't cheat us out of it.

Simplicio: I have heard various opinions on this particular point. "Some say they are stars which, like Venus and Mercury, revolve around the Sun in orbits of their own, and show themselves dark to us when they pass beneath it; and since they are very numerous, it often happens that some of them cluster together and afterwards separate again. Others believe them to be impressions formed in the air; others, illusions of the lenses; and others, other things still. But I lean strongly toward believing — indeed I hold it for certain — that they are an aggregate of many and various opaque bodies, running together almost at random: which is why we often see, within a single spot, ten or more of these tiny bodies that can be counted, irregular in shape, looking to us like flakes of snow, or tufts of wool, or hovering flies. They shift position among themselves, now scattering, now clustering, and above all beneath the Sun, around which, as around their center, they keep moving. But it does not therefore follow that they are generated and corrupted; rather, at times they hide behind the body of the Sun, and at other times, though well clear of it, they cannot be seen because of the nearness of its overwhelming light. For within the Sun's eccentric orb there is built up a kind of onion, composed of many layers one inside another, each of them studded with certain small spots, and each in motion; and although their movement at first seemed inconstant and irregular, nevertheless it is said to have been observed lately that within fixed periods the very same spots return exactly." This seems to me the most serviceable remedy found so far for accounting for the appearance while still preserving the incorruptibility and ingenerability of the heavens; and if it should fall short, there will be no lack of loftier minds to find better ones.

Salviati: If what we are disputing were some point of law, or one of the other human studies, where there is neither truth nor falsehood, then one could rely a great deal on subtlety of wit, fluency of speech, and wider reading in the authors, and hope that whoever excelled in those things would make his case appear, and be judged, the stronger. But in the natural sciences, whose conclusions are true and necessary and where human preference counts for nothing, a man had better take care not to plant himself in defense of the false — because a thousand Demostheneses and a thousand Aristotles will be left unhorsed by any average mind that has had the luck to seize on the truth. So, Simplicio, put out of your head this thought and this hope of yours, that there could ever be men so much more learned, well-read, and steeped in books than the rest of us that they can make the false become true in defiance of nature. And since, of all the opinions produced so far about the essence of these sunspots, the one you have just laid out strikes you as the true one, it follows — if that is so — that all the others are false. And to free you from this one as well, which is an utterly false chimera, I will pass over the thousand other improbabilities in it and bring you just two observations against it. The first is that many of these spots are seen to be born in the middle of the solar disk, and many likewise to dissolve and vanish far from the Sun's edge — a necessary argument that they are generated and dissolved; for if they appeared there by mere local motion, without generation and corruption, they would all be seen to enter and exit across the outermost rim. The other observation, for anyone not stuck in the lowest grade of ignorance about perspective: from the change in their apparent shapes, and the apparent change in their speed of motion, it follows necessarily that the spots lie right against the body of the Sun — that they touch its surface and move with it or upon it, and in no way circle around it in orbits at a distance. The motion proves it: toward the rim of the solar disk it appears extremely slow, and toward the middle, faster. The shapes prove it too: toward the rim they appear extremely narrow compared with how they show in the middle regions, and this is because in the middle they are seen full-face, as they really are, while toward the rim, where the globe's surface curves away, they show foreshortened. And both diminutions, of shape and of motion, for anyone who has known how to observe and calculate them carefully, answer precisely to what ought to appear if the spots lie against the Sun — and clash inexcusably with their moving in circles at any distance from the solar body, however small — as our mutual friend has demonstrated at length in his Letters on Sunspots, addressed to Mark Welser. From the same change of shape it also follows that none of them is a star, or any other body of spherical shape; for among all shapes the sphere alone is never seen foreshortened, and can never present itself as anything but perfectly round. So if any single spot were a round body, as all stars are held to be, it would show the same roundness in the middle of the solar disk as near the edge; whereas their foreshortening so much, showing so slender near the rim and broad and spacious toward the middle, makes us certain that they are flakes of little depth or thickness compared with their length and breadth. And as for its having "lately been observed" that the spots come back exactly the same after their fixed periods — don't you believe it, Simplicio; whoever told you that means to deceive you. Notice that he kept quiet about the ones that are generated and dissolved on the face of the Sun far from the rim; nor did he say a word about that foreshortening, which is a necessary argument that they lie against the Sun. What truth there is about the same spots returning is nothing more than what may already be read in the Letters I mentioned: namely, that some of them may last so long that they do not break up within a single revolution around the Sun — a circuit that is completed in less than a month.

Simplicio: To tell the truth, I have not made observations long or careful enough to make me master of the quod est — the plain fact — of this matter. But I certainly mean to make them, and then to try for myself whether I can bring what experience offers us into accord with what Aristotle demonstrates — for it is obvious that two truths cannot contradict each other.

Salviati: Whenever you want to square what your senses show you with the soundest doctrines of Aristotle, you'll have no trouble at all. Doesn't Aristotle say that, because of their great distance, the things of the heavens cannot be treated with much certainty?

Simplicio: He says so plainly.

Salviati: And doesn't he likewise affirm that what experience and the senses show us must be preferred to any argument, however well-founded it may seem? And doesn't he say this flatly, without the least hesitation?

Simplicio: He does.

Salviati: Then of these two propositions, both of them Aristotle's own doctrine, the second — that the senses must be put before argument — is a much firmer and more resolute doctrine than the other, which holds the heavens unalterable. And so you will be philosophizing more Aristotelianly if you say, "The heavens are alterable, because my senses tell me so," than if you say, "The heavens are unalterable, because reasoning persuaded Aristotle it was so." Add to this that we can reason about the things of the heavens far better than Aristotle could; for in confessing that such knowledge was difficult for him because of their distance from the senses, he concedes that anyone to whom the senses could present those things better would be able to philosophize about them with greater assurance. Now we, thanks to the telescope, have brought the heavens thirty and forty times closer than they were to Aristotle, so that we can make out a hundred things in them that he could not see — among others, these spots on the Sun, which were absolutely invisible to him. Therefore we can treat of the heavens and the Sun with more assurance than Aristotle.

Sagredo: I can see straight into Simplicio's heart, and I can tell he is deeply shaken by the force of these all-too-conclusive arguments. But on the other side, when he sees the great authority Aristotle has won with the whole world, considers the number of famous interpreters who have labored to explain his meaning, and sees the other sciences, so useful and so necessary to the public, resting a great part of their esteem and reputation on Aristotle's credit — all this confounds him and frightens him badly. I can almost hear him saying: "Once Aristotle is pulled down from his seat, who is to settle our controversies? What other author are we to follow in the schools, the academies, the universities? What philosopher has written out every part of natural philosophy, and so methodically, without leaving behind even a single particular conclusion? Must we lay waste the great building that shelters so many travelers? Must we destroy that refuge, that Prytaneum — that civic hall — where so many students find such comfortable lodging, where, without ever exposing yourself to the weather, merely by turning a few pages you acquire complete knowledge of nature? Must we flatten the very fortress in which we live secure against every enemy assault?" I pity him — no less than I would pity some lord who, over long years, at immense expense, with the labor of hundreds upon hundreds of craftsmen, built himself a most noble palace, and then sees it threatening to collapse because the foundations were badly laid; and who, rather than watch, heartbroken, while the walls hung with so many beautiful paintings are pulled down, the columns that hold up the splendid galleries topple, the gilded ceilings fall, and the doorframes, the pediments, the marble cornices installed at such cost all come to ruin — tries to stave off the collapse with chains, props, braces, buttresses, and shores.

Salviati: Oh, Simplicio needn't fear any such collapse just yet; I would undertake to insure him against the damage at far smaller expense. There is no danger that so great a multitude of clever, sharp-eyed philosophers will let themselves be overrun by one or two men making a bit of noise; without so much as turning the points of their pens against them, by silence alone they will sink them into contempt and derision with the whole world. It is a perfectly idle notion to think anyone could establish a new philosophy just by refuting this author or that one. One would first have to learn how to remake men's brains — to render them fit to tell the true from the false — and that is something only God can do. But from one line of talk to the next, where have we wandered off to? I couldn't find my way back to the trail without your memory as my guide.

Simplicio: I remember it perfectly well. We were on the Anti-Tycho's answers to the objections against the immutability of the heavens; among those objections you inserted this one about the sunspots, which he never touches, and I believe you meant to consider his answer to the challenge of the new stars.

Salviati: Now the rest of it comes back to me. To carry on with the matter: it seems to me the Anti-Tycho's answer contains several things that deserve to be called out. First of all, if the two new stars — which he cannot avoid placing in the highest parts of the heavens, and which lasted a long while and finally vanished — give him no trouble in maintaining the inalterability of the heavens, simply because they were not certain parts of it, nor changes in the ancient stars, then what is the point of campaigning with so much anxiety and sweat against the comets, to banish them from the celestial regions at any cost? Wasn't it enough to say of them exactly what he said of the new stars — that since they are not certain parts of the heavens, nor alterations in any of its stars, they do no damage either to the heavens or to the doctrine of Aristotle? Second, I cannot quite make out what is really going on in his mind. He grants that alterations in the stars would be destructive of the heavens' prerogatives — their incorruptibility and the rest — and this because the stars are celestial things, as everyone unanimously agrees; and yet it does not disturb him in the least if those same alterations take place elsewhere, outside the stars, in the rest of the celestial expanse. Does he perhaps think the heavens are not a celestial thing? For my part, I always supposed the stars were called celestial things because they are in the heavens, or made of the heavens' own matter — so that the heavens should be more celestial than the stars, just as nothing can be said to be more earthly than earth itself, or more fiery than fire. And then his making no mention at all of the sunspots — which have been conclusively demonstrated to be produced and dissolved, to lie next to the body of the Sun, and to turn with it or around it — gives me a strong hint that this author may be writing more to please others than to satisfy himself. I say this because he shows himself competent in mathematics, and it is impossible that a competent man should not be persuaded by the demonstrations that these materials are necessarily in contact with the body of the Sun, and that they are generations and corruptions so vast that nothing so great ever happens on Earth. And if changes of such size, in such numbers, and so frequent take place on the very globe of the Sun — which may reasonably be reckoned among the noblest parts of the heavens — what argument is left strong enough to persuade us that others cannot take place on the other globes?

Sagredo: I cannot hear it without great astonishment — I will say more, without great resistance from my own intellect — when this quality of being impervious to change, immutable, unalterable, and so on, is held up as a great nobility and perfection in the natural bodies that make up the universe, while conversely being alterable, generable, mutable is counted a great imperfection. For my part, I hold the Earth most noble and admirable precisely because of the many and diverse alterations, mutations, and generations that go on in it without pause. If it were not subject to any change at all — if it were one vast solitude of sand, or a mass of jasper, or if at the time of the Flood the waters covering it had frozen and it had remained one immense globe of crystal, where nothing was ever born or altered or changed — I would count it a great useless carcass in the world, idle through and through, and, in a word, superfluous, as if it did not exist in nature at all; and I would draw the same distinction for it that there is between a living animal and a dead one. And I say the very same of the Moon, of Jupiter, and of all the other globes of the world. The deeper I go into the vanity of popular reasoning on this point, the more shallow and foolish I find it. What greater silliness can be imagined than to call gems, silver, and gold precious, and earth and mud worthless? Doesn't it occur to these people that if earth were as scarce as jewels or the most prized metals, there is not a prince alive who wouldn't gladly pay a load of diamonds and rubies and four cartloads of gold just to have enough earth to plant a jasmine in a little pot, or to sow a China orange and watch it sprout, grow, and put out such lovely leaves, such fragrant flowers, such delicate fruit? So it is scarcity and plenty that raise or sink the price of things with the crowd — the same crowd that will call a diamond magnificent because it resembles pure water, and then would not trade it for ten barrels of the real thing. These people who so exalt incorruptibility, inalterability, and the rest are driven to say such things, I believe, by their great longing to keep on living and by the terror they have of death; and they fail to consider that if men were immortal, their own turn to come into the world would never have arrived. They deserve to run into a Medusa's head and be transmuted into statues of jasper or diamond, so as to become more perfect than they are.

Salviati: And perhaps such a metamorphosis would even work to their advantage; for it is better, I think, not to reason at all than to reason backwards.

Simplicio: There is no doubt whatever that the Earth is far more perfect being, as it is, alterable, mutable, and so on, than it would be as a mass of stone — even if it were one whole diamond, supremely hard and impervious to change. But however much nobility these conditions confer on the Earth, they would make the celestial bodies just that much more imperfect, since in them such qualities would be superfluous: the celestial bodies — that is, the Sun, the Moon, and the other stars, which are ordered to no other use than the service of the Earth — need nothing beyond motion and light to achieve their end.

Sagredo: So nature has produced and set in order all those vast, consummate, most noble celestial bodies — impervious to change, immortal, divine — for no other use than the service of the Earth: a thing changeable, transient, and mortal? For the service of what you yourselves call the dregs of the world, the sink of every filth? To what purpose make the celestial bodies immortal and all the rest, in order to serve something perishable and all the rest? Take away this one use, the service of the Earth, and the innumerable host of celestial bodies is left entirely useless and superfluous, since they have not, and cannot have, any mutual interaction among themselves — all of them being unalterable, immutable, impervious to change. If the Moon, for example, is impervious to change, what do you expect the Sun or any other star to work upon her? The effect would surely be far feebler than that of a man trying to melt down a great mass of gold with a glance or a thought. Besides, it seems to me that if the celestial bodies really do cooperate in the generations and alterations on Earth, then they themselves must be alterable too; otherwise I cannot see how the Moon's or the Sun's being brought to bear upon the Earth to produce generation would be anything more than laying a marble statue beside the bride and expecting children from that union.

Simplicio: Corruptibility, alteration, change, and the rest are not in the terrestrial globe as a whole. As far as its integrity goes, the Earth is no less eternal than the Sun or the Moon; it is generable and corruptible only in its outer parts. But it's quite true that in those parts generation and corruption are perpetual, and as perpetual they require the eternal operations of the heavens; and therefore the celestial bodies must be eternal.

Sagredo: That all runs along nicely. But if the corruptibility of the surface parts does no harm whatever to the eternity of the whole terrestrial globe — if, in fact, being generable, corruptible, alterable, and the rest brings it great ornament and perfection — then why can't you, why shouldn't you, admit alterations, generations, and so on in the outer parts of the celestial globes as well? They would add ornament without diminishing perfection or robbing those globes of their actions; rather they'd increase them, by having them all act not only upon the Earth but mutually upon one another, and the Earth upon them in turn.

Simplicio: That cannot be, because the generations, changes, and so forth that took place, say, on the Moon would be useless and vain, and natura nihil frustra facit — nature does nothing in vain.

Sagredo: And why would they be useless and vain?

Simplicio: Because we plainly see, and touch with our own hands, that all the generations, changes, and so on that occur on Earth are directed — all of them, directly or indirectly — to the use, the convenience, and the benefit of man. Horses are born for men's convenience; for the horses' nourishment the Earth produces hay, and the clouds water it. For men's convenience and nourishment grow the herbs, the grains, the fruits, the beasts, the birds, the fish; in short, if we go through all these things and examine them carefully, we'll find that the end to which they are all directed is the need, the use, the convenience, and the delight of men. Now of what possible use to the human race could generations taking place on the Moon or on some other planet ever be? Unless you meant to say that there are men on the Moon as well, enjoying its fruits — a notion either fabulous or impious.

Sagredo: Whether on the Moon or on any other planet there grow herbs or plants or animals like ours, whether rains and winds and thunderstorms occur there as they do around the Earth — I don't know it and I don't believe it, and still less that the Moon is inhabited by men. But I don't at all see how it follows that, because nothing like our things is generated there, no alteration can take place there at all — that there can be no other things there that change, come to be, and dissolve: things not merely different from ours, but utterly remote from our imagination, and in a word wholly beyond our power to conceive. I am certain that a man born and raised in some immense forest, among beasts and birds, with no knowledge whatever of the element of water, could never so much as imagine that there exists in nature another world quite unlike the land, full of animals that travel swiftly without legs and without wings — and not only along the surface, as beasts do on land, but through the whole depth of it; and that they not only travel, but stop motionless wherever they please, which the birds cannot do in the air; and that men live there too, and build palaces and cities there, and find travel so convenient that without any effort at all they go off to the most distant countries with their whole family, house and all — with entire cities, even. As I say, I'm certain that such a man, however keen his imagination, could never picture to himself the fish, the ocean, the ships, the fleets, the navies. So too, and much more easily, it may be that on the Moon — removed from us by so vast an interval, and made, perhaps, of material very different from the Earth's — there are substances, and operations going on, that are not merely remote from all our imaginings but entirely outside them: things bearing no resemblance whatever to ours, and for that reason wholly inconceivable to us. For whatever we imagine has to be either something already seen, or a composite of things, or of parts of things, seen at some other time — which is exactly what sphinxes, sirens, chimeras, centaurs, and the rest are.

Salviati: I've often let my fancy roam over these matters, and in the end I think I can indeed pick out some of the things that are not and cannot be on the Moon — but none of the things that I believe are and can be there, except in the very broadest generality: things that adorn her, acting and moving and living, and perhaps — in a manner utterly different from ours — beholding and admiring the greatness and beauty of the world and of its Maker and Ruler, and singing His glory with continual praise; doing, in short (and this is my whole meaning), what the sacred writers so often affirm: all creatures perpetually occupied in praising God.

Sagredo: Those are among the things that, speaking in the most general terms, may well be there. But I'd gladly hear you name the ones you believe are not and cannot be there — those, surely, can be named more particularly.

Salviati: Take note, Sagredo: this will be the third time that, step by step and without noticing it, we've drifted away from our main plan; and at this rate of digression we'll be a long while getting to the end of our reasonings. So perhaps it would be well to defer this discussion, along with the others we've agreed to save for a session of their own.

Sagredo: Please — since we're on the Moon, let's finish the business that belongs to her, so that we don't have to make such a long journey a second time.

Salviati: As you like. And to begin with the most general points: I believe the lunar globe is very different from the terrestrial, though in some respects certain resemblances can be seen. I'll give the resemblances first, and then the differences. The Moon certainly conforms to the Earth in shape, which is beyond doubt spherical, as follows necessarily from our seeing her disk as a perfect circle, and from the way she receives the light of the Sun. If her surface were flat, the whole of it would be clothed in light at one and the same instant, and then likewise stripped of light all at once in a single moment — not the parts facing the Sun first and the following parts in succession, so that only when she reaches opposition — the point directly opposite the Sun — and not before, does the whole apparent disk stand illuminated. And the exact contrary would happen if her visible surface were concave: the illumination would begin from the parts turned away from the Sun. Second, she is, like the Earth, dark and opaque in herself, and it is this opacity that fits her to receive and throw back the Sun's light — which she could not do if she were otherwise. Third, I hold her material to be every bit as dense and solid as the Earth's; my clearest evidence for this is that her surface is for the most part uneven, full of the many prominences and hollows that the telescope reveals. Of these prominences many are in every respect like our most rugged and precipitous mountains, and some can be seen stretched out in continuous chains hundreds of miles long; others come in more compact clusters; and there are also many detached, solitary crags, very steep and sheer. But what occurs most frequently of all are certain raised banks (I'll use that word, since no other comes to mind that pictures them better), quite elevated, which enclose and encircle plains of various sizes, forming various figures, though most of them circular; many of these have a considerably raised mountain in the middle, and a few are filled with somewhat dark material — like that of the great spots seen with the naked eye — and these are the largest of the enclosures; of the smaller and smaller ones the number is enormous, and still almost all of them circular. Fourth, just as the surface of our globe is divided into two chief parts, the land and the water, so in the lunar disk we see a grand distinction between certain great fields that shine more brightly and others less. And I believe the Earth would present much the same appearance to anyone who could see her lit by the Sun from the Moon, or from some similar distance: the surface of the sea would appear darker, and that of the land brighter. Fifth, just as from the Earth we see the Moon now all luminous, now half, now more, now less, sometimes a sickle, and sometimes wholly invisible — namely when she lies beneath the Sun's rays, so that the part facing the Earth is left in darkness — just so, from the Moon, one would see the Sun's illumination of the face of the Earth, with precisely the same period, to the letter, and under the same changes of shape. Sixth —

Sagredo: Gently a moment, Salviati. That the illumination of the Earth, as far as the variety of shapes goes, would look to someone on the Moon exactly like what we observe in the Moon — that I understand perfectly well. But I don't yet see how it would show itself with the same period, seeing that what the Sun's illumination does upon the lunar surface in a month, it does upon the terrestrial in twenty-four hours.

Salviati: It's true that the Sun's effect, so far as lighting these two bodies and sweeping their entire surfaces with its splendor, is accomplished on the Earth in one natural day and on the Moon in a month. But the variation of the shapes under which the illuminated parts of the terrestrial surface would be seen from the Moon doesn't depend on that alone; it depends on the changing aspects the Moon keeps taking up with respect to the Sun. So that if, for example, the Moon followed the Sun's motion exactly and happened to stand always directly in line between it and the Earth — in the aspect we call conjunction — then, since she would always be seeing the same hemisphere of the Earth that the Sun sees, she would see it perpetually all bright. If, on the contrary, she stood always at opposition to the Sun, she would never see the Earth at all, since the Earth's dark part would be continuously turned toward the Moon, and therefore invisible. But when the Moon is at quadrature with the Sun — a quarter-circle away from it — then, of the terrestrial hemisphere exposed to the Moon's view, the half toward the Sun is luminous and the other half, turned away from the Sun, is dark; and so the illuminated part of the Earth would present itself to the Moon in the shape of a half circle.

Sagredo: I'm entirely satisfied; and now I understand it perfectly. As the Moon departs from opposition to the Sun, where she saw nothing of the illuminated part of the terrestrial surface, and comes day by day toward the Sun, she begins little by little to uncover some small portion of the Earth's illuminated face, and she sees it in the shape of a thin sickle, the Earth being round; and as her motion brings her day by day closer to the Sun, she keeps uncovering more and more of the illuminated terrestrial hemisphere, so that at quadrature she uncovers exactly half of it, just as we see the same amount of her; then, continuing on toward conjunction, she uncovers successively more of the lit surface, and finally, at conjunction, sees the entire hemisphere all luminous. In short, I understand very well that what happens to the inhabitants of the Earth in watching the Moon's changes would happen to anyone on the Moon watching the Earth, but in the reverse order: when the Moon is full for us, at opposition to the Sun, for them the Earth would be at conjunction with the Sun, wholly dark and invisible; and conversely, the configuration that for us is conjunction of Moon with Sun — the Moon silent, as they say, and unseen — would there be opposition of Earth to Sun: a full Earth, so to speak, all luminous. And finally, however much of the illuminated lunar surface shows itself to us at a given time, just so much of the Earth, at that same time, would be seen from the Moon to be dark; and however much of the Moon is left without light for us, just so much of the Earth is illuminated for the Moon. Only at the quadratures, then, do we see a half circle of the Moon luminous, and they just as much of the Earth. In one thing, though, these reciprocal operations seem to me to differ: granting, for argument's sake and not conceding it, that there were someone on the Moon who could gaze at the Earth from up there, he would see the whole terrestrial surface every single day, thanks to the Moon's motion around the Earth in twenty-four or twenty-five hours; whereas we never see anything but half of the Moon, since she does not revolve upon herself, as she would have to do to be able to show us all of her.

Salviati: Provided it isn't the other way around — that it's precisely her turning upon herself that keeps us from ever seeing the other half; for that is how it would have to be, if she rode on the epicycle — that small secondary circle the astronomers assign her. But you're leaving out another difference, to set against the one you've just pointed out.

Sagredo: And which is that? No other comes to my mind at the moment.

Salviati: It's this: if the Earth (as you've rightly noted) sees no more than half of the Moon, while from the Moon the whole Earth is seen, then, on the other hand, all the Earth sees the Moon, but only half the Moon sees the Earth. For the inhabitants, so to speak, of the upper hemisphere of the Moon — the one invisible to us — are deprived of the sight of the Earth; and these, perhaps, are the antichthones, the "counter-dwellers" the ancients talked of. But here I'm reminded of a particular phenomenon, newly observed in the Moon by our mutual friend, the Academician, from which two necessary consequences follow: one, that we actually see somewhat more than half of the Moon; the other, that the Moon's motion is related exactly to the center of the Earth. And the phenomenon and the observation are these. If the Moon has a correspondence and a natural sympathy with the Earth, toward which she keeps a certain fixed part of herself turned, then the straight line joining their two centers must always pass through the same point of the Moon's surface — so that anyone gazing at her from the center of the Earth would always see the same lunar disk, bounded precisely by one and the same circumference. But for someone stationed on the Earth's surface, the ray running from his eye to the center of the lunar globe would not pass through that same point of her surface through which the line drawn from the Earth's center to the Moon's passes — except when she stood directly overhead. With the Moon placed in the east or in the west, however, the point where the visual ray strikes lies above the point of the line joining the centers; and so some part of the lunar hemisphere toward the upper circumference comes into view, and just as much is hidden on the lower side — comes into view and is hidden, I mean, with respect to the hemisphere that would be seen from the Earth's true center. And since the part of the Moon's circumference that is uppermost at her rising is lowermost at her setting, the difference in the appearance of those upper and lower parts should be quite noticeable, with spots or other marked features in those regions now showing themselves, now hiding. A similar variation should also be seen toward the northern and southern edges of the same disk, according as the Moon stands in one or the other belly of her "dragon" — the old astronomers' name for the two arcs of her path that swing north and south: when she is to the north, some of her northern parts are hidden from us and some southern parts come into view, and the reverse when she is to the south. Now, that these consequences are in fact borne out, the telescope makes us certain. For there are on the Moon two particular spots, one of which, when the Moon is on the meridian, faces toward the northwest, while the other lies almost diametrically opposite; and the first is visible even without the telescope, but not the second. The northwesterly one is a small oval spot, separate from the other very large ones; the opposite one is smaller, likewise detached from the great spots, and set in a quite bright field. In both of these the variations I've described are observed very plainly: they are seen — each moving contrary to the other — now near the rim of the lunar disk, now drawn back from it, and with such a difference that the interval between the northwesterly spot and the circumference of the disk is at one time more than double what it is at another; and as for the other spot, since it lies closer to the circumference, its shift amounts to more than triple from one time to the next. From all this it is plain that the Moon, as if drawn by a magnetic power, constantly faces the terrestrial globe with one and the same face, and never turns away from it.

Sagredo: And when will there be an end to the new observations and discoveries of this admirable instrument?

Salviati: If its progress follows the course of other great inventions, we may hope that with the passage of time we'll come to see things we can't now so much as imagine. But to return to our original discussion: I say, as the sixth point of resemblance between the Moon and the Earth, that just as the Moon, a great part of the time, makes up for the absence of the Sun's light and renders our nights quite bright by the reflection of her own, so the Earth repays her in kind when her need is greatest, reflecting the solar rays back at her with a very vigorous illumination — greater, in my judgment, than the light that comes to us from her, in the same proportion as the Earth's surface is greater than the Moon's.

Sagredo: No more, no more, Salviati! Leave me the pleasure of showing you how, at this first hint, I've penetrated the cause of a phenomenon I've thought about a thousand times without ever getting to the bottom of it. You mean that a certain pale glow we see in the Moon, above all when she's a sickle, comes from the reflection of the Sun's light off the surface of the land and the sea; and that this light shows the more clearly, the thinner the crescent is — because then the luminous part of the Earth seen from the Moon is larger, in keeping with what we concluded a moment ago: that the luminous part of the Earth showing itself to the Moon is always exactly as large as the dark part of the Moon that faces the Earth. So when the Moon is a thin sickle, and her dark portion consequently great, great too is the illuminated part of the Earth as seen from the Moon, and so much the more powerful its reflection of light.

Salviati: That is exactly what I meant to say. Truly, it's a great sweetness to talk with people of judgment and quick understanding — above all when one is strolling and discoursing among truths. I've run into brains so thick that, though I repeated to them a thousand times this very thing you've just penetrated on your own, they never once managed to grasp it.

Simplicio: If you mean that you couldn't persuade them to the point of understanding it, I'm greatly amazed, and I'm confident that if they didn't understand it from your explanation, they'll hardly understand it from anyone else's — your way of putting it seems very clear to me. But if you mean you didn't persuade them to the point of believing it, that doesn't amaze me in the least; for I confess that I myself am one of those who understand your discourses without being set at rest by them. Indeed, on this and on parts of the other six points of resemblance, many difficulties remain with me — and I'll bring them forward once you've finished recounting them all.

Salviati: My desire to get at some truth — a pursuit in which the objections of intelligent men like you two can help me a great deal — will make me very brief in dispatching what's left. Let the seventh point of resemblance, then, be this: that the Earth and the Moon answer each other in injuries no less than in favors. The Moon, quite often at the very peak of her illumination, is robbed of light and eclipsed when the Earth steps between her and the Sun; and she in her turn, by way of payback, places herself between the Earth and the Sun, and with her shadow darkens the Earth. True, the revenge doesn't measure up to the offense: the Moon often remains plunged entirely in the Earth's shadow, and for a good long while, whereas the whole Earth is never darkened by the Moon, nor for any long stretch of time. Still, considering how small her body is compared to the size of the Earth's, you can only say that her fighting spirit, in a certain way, is very great. So much for the resemblances. What would come next is a discussion of the disparities; but since Simplicio means to favor us with his doubts about the resemblances, we'd better hear them and weigh them before moving on.

Sagredo: Yes — since it's a fair bet that Simplicio will have no objections to the disparities and differences between the Earth and the Moon, seeing that he holds their substances to be utterly different.

Simplicio: Of the resemblances you recited in drawing your parallel between the Earth and the Moon, I find I can admit without objection only the first and two others. I admit the first — the spherical shape — though even there there's a catch, since I take the Moon's sphere to be highly polished and smooth as a mirror, whereas this Earth of ours we can touch with our own hands and feel how rough and rugged it is. But that point, concerning the unevenness of the surface, belongs under another of the resemblances you brought forward, so I'll save what I have to say about it for the consideration of that one.

That the Moon is, as you claim in your second resemblance, opaque and dark in herself like the Earth — of that I admit only the first attribute, the opacity, of which solar eclipses assure me: for if the Moon were transparent, the air during a total eclipse of the Sun would not go as dark as it does; a refracted light would come through the transparency of the lunar body, as we see happen through the densest clouds. But as for the darkness, I don't believe the Moon is wholly without light of her own, as the Earth is. On the contrary, that faint brightness we see across the rest of her disc, beyond the slender horns lit by the Sun, I take to be her own proper and natural light, and not a reflection from the Earth — which I judge incapable, by its extreme roughness and darkness, of reflecting the Sun's rays.

In the third parallel I agree with you in one part and dissent in the other. I agree in judging the body of the Moon utterly solid and hard, like the Earth — indeed much more so; for if we take from Aristotle that the heavens are of impenetrable hardness, and the stars are the denser parts of the heavens, then they must necessarily be perfectly firm and perfectly impenetrable.

Sagredo: What beautiful material the stuff of the heavens would be for building palaces, if only one could get hold of it — so hard, and so transparent!

Salviati: Terrible material, rather: being, by its utter transparency, completely invisible, you couldn't walk through the rooms without grave danger of banging into the doorposts and cracking your head.

Sagredo: There'd be no such danger, if it's true, as some Peripatetics say, that the stuff is intangible; and if it can't be touched, still less can it be bumped into.

Salviati: That would be no comfort at all. For even if celestial matter can't be touched, since it lacks the tangible qualities, it can perfectly well touch elemental bodies; and as far as hurting us goes, it comes to the same thing whether it crashes into us — or worse — or we crash into it. But let's leave these palaces, or better, these castles in the air, and not hold up Simplicio.

Simplicio: The question you've raised in passing is one of the hard ones treated in philosophy, and I have some very fine thoughts on it from a great professor who held a chair at Padua; but this isn't the moment to go into them. So, returning to our subject: I say again that I take the Moon to be far more solid than the Earth — but I argue it not, as you do, from the roughness and ruggedness of her surface, but from the opposite: from her being fit to take (as we see among ourselves in the hardest gems) a polish and luster beyond that of the smoothest mirror; for her surface must be like that, to send us so vivid a reflection of the Sun's rays. As for those appearances you speak of — mountains, crags, ridges, valleys, and so on — they're all illusions. I've been present at public disputations where it was stoutly maintained, against these introducers of novelties, that such appearances come from nothing but parts unequally opaque and transparent, of which the Moon is composed inside and out — the kind of thing we often see happen in crystal, in amber, and in many perfectly polished precious stones, where, from the opacity of some parts and the transparency of others, all sorts of hollows and bulges seem to appear in them.

In the fourth resemblance I concede that the surface of the terrestrial globe, seen from far away, would present two different appearances, one brighter and one darker; but I think those differences would fall out the reverse of what you say. That is, I believe the surface of the water would appear bright, because it's smooth and transparent, and the surface of the land would stay dark, because of its opacity and roughness, poorly suited to throwing back the light of the Sun.
