On the fifth comparison I admit everything, and I'm satisfied that if the Earth shone as the Moon does, it would show itself, to anyone gazing at it from up there, in shapes matching the phases we see in the Moon. I understand, too, how the period of its illumination and its change of shapes would be a month, even though the Sun sweeps over the whole of it in twenty-four hours; and finally I have no difficulty admitting that only half the Moon sees all the Earth, and all the Earth sees only half the Moon.

The sixth I hold to be utterly false — that the Moon could receive light from the Earth, which is pitch-dark, opaque, and thoroughly unfit to reflect the Sun's light the way the Moon reflects it to us. As I've said, I hold that the light we see on the rest of the Moon's face, beyond the horns blazing with the Sun's illumination, is the Moon's own proper and natural light — and it would take a great deal to make me believe otherwise.

The seventh, on the mutual eclipses, can also be admitted — though what you insist on calling an eclipse of the Earth is properly and customarily called an eclipse of the Sun. And that is all I need say to you for now in contradiction of the seven resemblances. If you'd like to reply to any of these objections, I'll listen gladly.

Salviati: If I've rightly taken in what you've answered, it seems to me that certain conditions I had made common to the Moon and the Earth still remain in dispute between you and us — and they are these. You take the Moon to be smooth and polished like a mirror, and as such fit to reflect the Sun's light to us, and the Earth, on the contrary, too rough to make any such reflection. You grant that the Moon is solid and hard, and you argue this from her being polished and smooth, not from her being mountainous; and for her mountainous appearance you assign as the cause her being made of parts more and less opaque and transparent. And finally, you hold that secondary light to be the Moon's own, and not a reflection from the Earth — though you don't seem to deny the sea some reflection, its surface being smooth. As for undeceiving you on this error — that the Moon's reflection cannot work as from a mirror — I have little hope, when I see that what's written on exactly this subject in The Assayer and in the Letters on Sunspots of our mutual friend has gained no ground at all in your mind — if, that is, you've read attentively what's written there on the matter.

Simplicio: I've skimmed them, only superficially, in what little idle time my more solid studies leave me. So if, by repeating some of those arguments or bringing up others, you think you can resolve my difficulties, I'll listen more attentively.

Salviati: I'll say what comes to mind at the moment, and it may well be a mixture of my own thoughts and what I once read in those books — from which, I remember well, I came away entirely persuaded, though at first sight the conclusions struck me as great paradoxes. What we're asking, Simplicio, is whether, to make a reflection of light like the one that comes to us from the Moon, it's necessary that the surface it comes from be as smooth and polished as a mirror, or whether a surface that is not smooth and not polished — rough and badly finished — is better suited to it. Now, suppose two reflections reached us, one brighter and one less so, from two surfaces facing us. I ask you: which of the two surfaces do you think would appear brighter to our eyes, and which darker?

Simplicio: I believe, without any doubt, that the one reflecting the light to me more vividly would show itself brighter in appearance, and the other darker.

Salviati: Now be so kind as to take that mirror hanging on the wall there, and let's step out into the courtyard. Come, Sagredo. Hang the mirror there, on that wall where the sun strikes; now let's move away and pull back here into the shade. There you have two surfaces struck by the sun — the wall and the mirror. Tell me now: which looks brighter to you, the wall's surface or the mirror's? You don't answer?

Sagredo: I leave the answering to Simplicio; the difficulty is his. As for me, this little beginning of an experiment has already persuaded me that the Moon must, of necessity, have a very badly polished surface.

Salviati: Tell me, Simplicio: if you had to paint a picture of that wall with the mirror hanging on it, where would you use the darker colors — in painting the wall, or in painting the mirror?

Simplicio: Much darker in painting the mirror.

Salviati: Then if the more powerful reflection of light comes from the surface that appears brighter, the wall will reflect the Sun's rays to us more vividly than the mirror.

Simplicio: Very good, my dear sir — is that the best experiment you have? You've stationed us where the mirror's reverberation doesn't strike. But come with me a little this way. No — come along.

Sagredo: Are you looking for the spot where the mirror throws its reflection?

Simplicio: I am.

Sagredo: Well, there it is — on the opposite wall, exactly as big as the mirror, and hardly less bright than if the Sun were striking there directly.

Simplicio: Come over here, then, and look from this spot at the surface of the mirror, and tell me whether it looks darker than the wall's.

Sagredo: Look at it yourself; I'm not ready to blind myself just yet. I know perfectly well, without looking, that it shows as vivid and bright as the Sun itself, or very little less.

Simplicio: So what do you say now — that the reflection of a mirror is less powerful than that of a wall? I see that on this opposite wall, where the reflection from the other sunlit wall arrives together with the one from the mirror, the mirror's is far brighter; and I see likewise that from here the mirror itself looks much brighter than the wall.

Salviati: You've gotten ahead of me with your sharpness, because this is exactly the observation I needed in order to explain the rest. You see, then, the difference between the two reflections made by the two surfaces of the wall and the mirror, struck in precisely the same way by the Sun's rays. And you see how the reflection coming from the wall spreads out toward all the parts facing it, while the mirror's goes toward one single place, not a bit bigger than the mirror itself. You see likewise how the surface of the wall, looked at from any spot whatever, always shows equally bright, and everywhere much brighter than the mirror's — except for that one small place alone where the mirror's reflection strikes, for from there the mirror appears much brighter than the wall. From these experiences, which we can see and all but touch, it seems to me we can very quickly come to know whether the reflection that reaches us from the Moon comes as from a mirror or as from a wall — that is, from a smooth surface or a rough one.

Sagredo: If I were standing on the Moon herself, I don't believe I could touch the roughness of her surface with my hand more plainly than I grasp it right now by following the argument. The Moon, seen in any position whatever with respect to the Sun and to us, shows us the surface the Sun touches as always equally bright — an effect that matches the wall's to a hair, since the wall, viewed from anywhere, appears equally bright; and it clashes with the mirror, which shows luminous from one spot only and dark from all the others. What's more, the light that reaches me from the wall's reflection is bearable and weak compared with the mirror's, which is ferociously strong, and hardly less punishing to the eyes than the primary, direct light of the Sun. And that's why we can gaze comfortably at the face of the Moon: if she were like a mirror — showing herself, because she's so near, as large as the Sun itself — her blaze would be absolutely unbearable, and it would seem to us that we were staring at almost a second Sun.

Salviati: Please, Sagredo, don't credit my demonstration with more than it's worth. Let me raise a counter-objection against you, and I'm not sure it's an easy knot to untie. You count it a great difference between the Moon and the mirror that she sends her reflection toward all parts equally, as the wall does, while the mirror sends it to one single determinate place; and from this you conclude that the Moon is like the wall, and not like the mirror. But I tell you: that mirror sends its reflection to one place alone because its surface is flat, and since reflected rays must leave at angles equal to those of the incoming rays, rays leaving a flat surface must depart together toward the same place. But the Moon's surface is not flat — it's spherical; and rays falling on such a surface find slopes from which to reflect, at angles equal to their angles of incidence, toward every direction, thanks to the infinity of inclinations that make up a spherical surface. So the Moon can send her reflection everywhere, and isn't forced to send it to one place alone, like that mirror, which is flat.

Simplicio: That is precisely one of the objections I wanted to raise against him.

Sagredo: If that's one, you must have others; so out with them — though as for this first one, I think it's going to turn out to count against you rather than in your favor.

Simplicio: You pronounced it as an obvious thing that the reflection from that wall is as bright and illuminating as the one that comes to us from the Moon, and I count it as nothing by comparison. For "in this business of illumination one must pay attention to, and distinguish, the sphere of activity — the reach of a body's power; and who doubts that celestial bodies have a greater sphere of activity than these elemental, frail, and mortal things of ours? And that wall, when all is said — what is it but a bit of earth, dark and unfit to give light?"

Sagredo: Here too, I believe, you're very much deceived. But let me come to the first objection raised by Salviati. I observe that for an object to appear luminous to us, it isn't enough that the rays of the illuminating body fall upon it; the reflected rays have to come to our eye. That's plain in the example of that mirror: no doubt the Sun's luminous rays are landing on it, and yet it doesn't show itself bright and lit up except when we put our eye in that one particular place where the reflection goes. Now let's consider what would happen if the mirror's surface were spherical. We'd find, without question, that of the reflection made by the whole illuminated surface, only the tiniest part reaches the eye of any one particular observer — because the portion of the whole spherical surface whose inclination bounces the ray to the particular spot where the eye is, is a most minute particle. So only the smallest part of the spherical surface can show itself shining to the eye, and all the rest appears dark. If the Moon, then, were polished like a mirror, only the tiniest part of her would show itself to any one person's eyes as lit by the Sun, even though a whole hemisphere lay exposed to the Sun's rays; all the rest would remain, to the observer's eye, as if unilluminated, and therefore invisible — and finally the Moon herself would be entirely invisible, since that little particle sending the reflection would be lost through its smallness and its great distance. And just as she would remain invisible to the eye, so her illumination would amount to nothing; for it's simply impossible that a luminous body should drive away our darkness with its splendor while we fail to see it.

Salviati: Hold on a moment, please, Sagredo, for I see certain movements in Simplicio's face and bearing that tell me he isn't fully convinced or satisfied by what you've said with such complete clarity and absolute truth. And it's just occurred to me that I can remove his every scruple with another experiment. I've seen, in a room upstairs, a large spherical mirror: let's have it brought down here; and while it's on its way, let Simplicio go back and consider how great the brightness is that the flat mirror's reflection throws on the wall here under the loggia.

Simplicio: I see that it's hardly less bright than if the Sun were striking it directly.

Salviati: So it is. Now tell me: if we take away that little flat mirror and set the big spherical one in the same place, what effect do you think its reflection will have on that same wall?

Simplicio: I believe it will throw a much greater and much broader light onto it.

Salviati: But if the illumination turns out to be nothing, or so small you can barely make it out — what will you say then?

Simplicio: When I've seen the effect, I'll think about my answer.

Salviati: Here's the mirror, and I want it set right next to the other one. But first let's go over there, near the reflection thrown by the flat one, and look closely at its brightness. See how bright it is here where it strikes, and how distinctly you can make out all these little details of the wall.

Simplicio: I've looked and observed it perfectly well. Have the other mirror set beside the first.

Salviati: There it is. It was put there the moment you started studying the little details, and you never noticed — that's how great an increase of light it made on the rest of the wall. Now let the flat mirror be taken away. Look: every reflection is gone, even though the big convex mirror is still there. Take that one away too, and put it back as many times as you please — you'll see no change of light at all across the whole wall. So there you have it, shown to your very senses: the reflection of the Sun made in a spherical convex mirror does not perceptibly light up the places around it. Now — what do you answer to this experiment?

Simplicio: I'm afraid there's some sleight of hand at work here. I do see, when I look at that mirror, a great blaze of light coming off it that nearly blinds me; and — what matters more — I see it from every spot I look at it from, and I see it shift position across the mirror's surface as I move to view it from this place or that. Which is a conclusive sign that the light is reflected vividly toward every side, and so strikes that whole wall as powerfully as it strikes my eye.

Salviati: Now you see how careful and reserved we need to be about granting our assent to what reasoning alone sets before us. No doubt what you say has plenty of surface plausibility; and yet you can see how the experience of the senses shows the contrary.

Simplicio: Then how does this business work?

Salviati: I'll tell you what I make of it, though I don't know how far it will satisfy you. First: that brilliant splash of light you see on the mirror, which seems to you to take up a good part of it, isn't anywhere near that large — in fact it's very, very small. But its intensity produces in your eye — by way of the reflection made in the moisture at the rims of your eyelids, which spreads across the pupil — a borrowed radiance, like the fringe of fine hairs we seem to see around the flame of a candle set some distance away; or, if you prefer, compare it to the adventitious sparkle of a star. If you set the tiny little body of, say, the Dog Star, seen by day through the telescope, when it shows without any radiance, beside the same star seen at night by the naked eye, you'll grasp beyond any doubt that with its rays it looks more than a thousand times bigger than the bare, real little body. And the Sun's image that you see in that mirror undergoes a similar enlargement, or a greater one — greater, I say, because it's more vivid than the star, as is obvious from the fact that you can stare at the star with far less injury to your eyes than at this reflection from the mirror. So the reverberation that has to be shared out over this whole wall comes from a small part of that mirror; whereas a moment ago, what came from the whole flat mirror was delivered to — and confined within — a very small part of the same wall. What wonder, then, that the first reflection illuminates very vividly, while this other remains almost imperceptible?

Simplicio: I find myself more tangled up than ever, and now the other difficulty comes over me: how can it be that that wall, made of such dark material, with a surface so badly polished, throws back light more powerful and vivid than a mirror that's been beautifully smoothed and cleaned?

Salviati: More vivid, no — but more universal, yes. As for intensity, you can see for yourself: the reflection from that little flat mirror, where it strikes over there under the loggia, illuminates powerfully; and the rest of the wall, which receives the reflection from the wall the mirror hangs on, is nowhere near as brightly lit as the small patch the mirror's reflection lands on. And if you want to understand the whole of this business, consider that the roughness of that wall's surface amounts to the same thing as its being composed of countless tiny little surfaces, set at countless different inclinations — among which, of necessity, many happen to be placed so as to send the rays they reflect to one particular spot, and many others to another; in short, there is no place that doesn't receive a great many rays, reflected from a great many little surfaces scattered over the entire face of the rough body the luminous rays fall on. From which it necessarily follows that reflected rays — and with them illumination — reach every part of any surface whatever that faces the one receiving the primary incident rays. It follows, too, that the body the illuminating rays fall on shows itself wholly lit and bright when viewed from anywhere at all. And that is why the Moon, having a rough rather than a polished surface, sends the Sun's light back in every direction and shows equally bright to every observer. Whereas if its surface, being spherical, were also as smooth as a mirror, it would be completely invisible — since that very tiny part of it that could reflect the Sun's image to the eye of any one person would, across so great a distance, remain invisible, as we've already said.

Simplicio: I follow your reasoning quite well; still, I think I can dispose of it with very little effort, and maintain perfectly well that the Moon is round and highly polished and reflects the Sun's light to us the way a mirror does — nor does it follow that the Sun's image must be seen in the middle of it. For "it is not by the image of the Sun itself that the Sun's small figure can be made out at so great a distance; rather, what we perceive is the illumination of the whole lunar body, produced by the Sun's light. We may see the like in a gilded and well-burnished plate, which, when struck by a luminous body, shows itself all resplendent to one who views it from afar; only from close up does one make out, at its center, the small image of the luminous body."

Salviati: Frankly confessing my own incapacity, I'll say that of this speech of yours I understand nothing except the part about the gilded plate. And if you'll permit me to speak freely, I strongly suspect you don't understand it either, but memorized those words — written by somebody out of the itch to contradict and to show himself cleverer than his opponent; to show himself, that is, to the sort of people who, wanting to look clever themselves, applaud what they don't understand, and think the more highly of a writer the less they understand him. Provided the writer himself isn't (as many are) one of those who write what they themselves don't understand — which is why what they write is understood by nobody. So, leaving the rest aside, here is my answer about the gilded plate. If it's flat and not very large, it can indeed appear all ablaze from a distance while a strong light strikes it — but only when the eye lies on one determinate line, namely the line of the reflected rays; and it will look more fiery than if it were, say, silver, because it's colored, and because the extreme density of the metal lets it take an absolutely perfect polish. And if its surface, though beautifully polished, were not exactly flat but carried various inclinations, then its blaze would be visible from more positions — from as many, that is, as the different reflections made by the different surfaces reached. That's why diamonds are cut with many facets: so their delightful sparkle can be seen from many places. But if the plate were very large, it would not show all ablaze from a distance even if it were perfectly flat. Let me put it more precisely: imagine a huge flat gilded plate set out in the Sun. To a distant eye, the Sun's image will appear to occupy only one part of that plate — the part the reflection of the incident solar rays comes from. It's true that, because of the light's intensity, the image will appear garlanded with a great many rays, and so will seem to occupy a considerably larger portion of the plate than it really does. And to test that this is true: note the particular spot on the plate the reflection comes from, mark out likewise how large the blazing patch looks to you, then cover the greater part of that space, leaving only the region around its middle exposed. The apparent blaze won't shrink one bit for the distant viewer — on the contrary, you'll see it spread wide over the cloth or whatever else you covered it with. So if anyone, from seeing a small gilded plate all ablaze from a distance, has convinced himself the same must happen with plates as big as the Moon, he has fooled himself no less than if he believed the Moon to be no bigger than the bottom of a wine vat. And if the plate's surface were spherical, the powerful reflection would be seen in only a single small patch of it — though, thanks to its intensity, that patch would indeed show wreathed in many quivering rays; the rest of the ball would appear merely tinted, and even that only if it were not polished to the highest degree — for if it were perfectly burnished, it would appear dark. We have an example of this before our eyes every day in silver vessels: so long as they've only been boiled in the whitening bath, they're all white as snow and return no images at all; but burnish any part of them, and that part immediately turns dark, and from it images come back as from a mirror. That darkening comes from nothing else than the flattening of the very fine grain that made the silver's surface rough — rough in just the way that reflected light toward every side, so that from every position it showed equally illuminated. When burnishing then planes those minute inequalities exquisitely smooth, so that the reflection of the incident rays is all directed to one determinate place, the burnished part shows, from that place, far brighter and shinier than the rest, which is merely whitened; but from every other position it looks quite dark. And it's well known that the change of viewpoint in looking at burnished surfaces produces such differences of appearance that painters, to imitate a burnished breastplate, say, have to set pure blacks and pure whites side by side on parts of the armor where the light falls equally.

Sagredo: So then: if these philosopher gentlemen would content themselves with granting that the Moon, Venus, and the other planets have surfaces not quite so glossy and polished as a mirror — a hair's-breadth less, that is: like a silver plate that has been whitened but not burnished — would that be enough to make it visible, and fit to bounce the Sun's light back to us?

Salviati: It would be enough in part; but it wouldn't give a light as powerful as it does by being mountainous — full, in short, of great heights and hollows. But these philosopher gentlemen will never grant it to be less polished than a mirror; they'll have it far more so, if more can be imagined, because, holding that the most perfect shapes belong to the most perfect bodies, they need the sphericity of those celestial globes to be absolute. Besides, if they granted me any unevenness at all, however tiny, I'd help myself without the least scruple to a much bigger one — for since a perfection of that kind consists in indivisibles, a hair spoils it as thoroughly as a mountain does.

Sagredo: Here two questions occur to me: one is to understand why a greater unevenness of surface should make a more powerful reflection of light; the other, why these Peripatetic gentlemen insist on this exactness of shape.

Salviati: I'll answer the first, and leave to Simplicio the job of answering the second. You should note, then, that the same surfaces are more or less illuminated by the same light according as the illuminating rays fall on them more or less obliquely; the greatest illumination is where the rays are perpendicular. And look — I'll show it to your senses. I bend this sheet of paper so that one part makes an angle with the other; and holding it up to the light reflected from that wall opposite, you see how this face, which receives the rays obliquely, is less bright than this other, where the reflection arrives at right angles; and notice how, as I turn it to catch them more and more obliquely, the illumination grows weaker.

Sagredo: I see the effect, but I don't grasp the cause.

Salviati: If you thought about it for a hundredth of an hour, you'd find it; but so as not to waste time, here's a quick demonstration of it in this figure.

Sagredo: The mere sight of the figure has cleared the whole thing up for me — go on.

Simplicio: Kindly spell out the rest for me; I'm not so quick on the uptake.

Salviati: Take all the parallel lines you see setting out from the points A and B to be the rays that arrive at the line CD at right angles. Now tilt CD so that it leans like DO: don't you see that a good number of the rays that were striking CD now pass by without touching DO? So if DO is lit by fewer rays, it stands to reason that the light it receives is weaker. Now back to the Moon. Being spherical in shape, if its surface were as smooth as this paper, the parts of its Sun-lit hemisphere toward the rim would receive vastly less light than the parts in the middle, since the rays fall on the former at the most extreme slant and on the latter at right angles. So at full Moon, when we see nearly the whole hemisphere illuminated, the parts toward the middle ought to show more brilliant to us than the parts toward the circumference — which is not what we see. Now picture the Moon's face full of good high mountains: don't you see how their slopes and ridges, rising above the curve of the perfect spherical surface, stand exposed to the Sun's view, placed to receive its rays far less obliquely — and so to show as brightly lit as the rest?

Sagredo: All well and good. But if such mountains are there, then it's true the Sun will strike them far more squarely than it would the slant of a smooth surface; but it's also true that all the valleys between those mountains would stay dark, because of the enormous shadows the mountains would cast at that time; whereas the middle regions, though full of valleys and mountains, would be free of shadow, the Sun standing high above them — and so a great deal brighter than the outer parts, which would be sown with as much shadow as light. And yet we see no such difference.

Simplicio: A similar difficulty was winding its way through my own imagination.

Salviati: How much readier Simplicio is at penetrating the difficulties that favor Aristotle's opinions than at penetrating their solutions! And I have a suspicion that at times he deliberately keeps quiet about those, too; in the present case, since he was able to spot the objection on his own — and it's a genuinely ingenious one — I can't believe he hasn't also noticed the answer. So I'll try to draw it out of his mouth, as they say. Tell me, then, Simplicio: do you believe there can be shadow anywhere the Sun's rays strike?

Simplicio: I believe — no, I'm certain — that there cannot; because the Sun, being the greatest of luminaries, driving out the darkness with its rays, it's impossible that any place it reaches should stay dark. Besides, we have the definition: tenebrae sunt privatio luminis — darkness is the absence of light.

Salviati: So the Sun, gazing at the Earth or the Moon or any other opaque body, never sees any of their shadowed parts — since it has no other eyes to see with than its own rays, the bearers of light. And consequently someone standing on the Sun would never see anything in shadow at all, because his visual rays would always travel in company with the Sun's illuminating rays.

Simplicio: Perfectly true — no contradiction possible.

Salviati: Now: when the Moon stands at opposition to the Sun, what difference is there between the path traveled by the rays of your sight and the path traveled by the rays of the Sun?

Simplicio: Now I've got it. You mean that since the rays of sight and the rays of the Sun travel along the same lines, we can't make out any of the Moon's shadowed valleys. And please — rid yourself of this notion that I'm a faker or a dissembler. I swear to you on my honor as a gentleman that I had not penetrated to that answer, and might never have found it without your help, or without long thinking.

Sagredo: The solution the two of you have produced between you for this last difficulty has genuinely satisfied me as well. But at the same time, this point about the rays of sight traveling with the rays of the Sun has stirred up another scruple in me about the earlier part — though I don't know whether I'll manage to explain it, because it's only just been born and I haven't yet got it into order to my own liking. But let's see, among us, if we can bring it into the clear. There's no doubt at all that the parts toward the rim of a smooth but unburnished hemisphere lit by the Sun, receiving the rays obliquely, receive far fewer of them than the middle parts, which receive them head-on. It may well be that a band, say, twenty degrees wide out toward the edge of the hemisphere receives no more rays than another band toward the middle only four degrees wide; so the first will certainly be much darker than the second, and will look so to anyone who views them both face-on — full-face, as we say. But if the observer's eye were positioned so that the twenty-degree width of the dark band presented itself looking no longer than a four-degree band set at the middle of the hemisphere, then I don't hold it impossible that it might show him just as bright and luminous as the other — because, in the end, within two equal angles of four degrees each, the eye receives the reflections of two equal multitudes of rays: those reflected from the middle band four degrees wide, and those reflected from the other band of twenty degrees but seen foreshortened, compressed into the span of four. And the eye will get itself such a position when it stands between that hemisphere and the body illuminating it, because then the sight and the rays travel along the same lines. So it doesn't seem impossible that the Moon should have a perfectly even surface and nonetheless, at full Moon, show itself no less luminous at the rim than in the middle.

Salviati: The doubt is an ingenious one and deserves consideration; and since it came to you just now, unrehearsed, I'll likewise answer with whatever falls into my mind unrehearsed — and it may well be that with more thought a better answer would occur to me. But before I bring anything else into it, we'd do well to make sure by experiment that your objection holds up in fact the way it seems to conclude in appearance. So, taking the same sheet of paper again and bending a small part of it so that it tilts against the rest, let's test whether, when we hold it to the light so that the rays fall directly on the smaller part and obliquely on the other, the part receiving the direct rays shows brighter. And there — the experiment already settles it: it's noticeably more luminous. Now, if your objection is conclusive, then when we lower the eye far enough that, viewing that other, larger, less illuminated part foreshortened, it appears no wider than the brighter part — so that we see it under no greater an angle — then, I say, its light will have to increase enough to look as brilliant to us as the other. There: I'm looking at it, viewing it so obliquely that it appears narrower than the other; and yet its darkness doesn't lighten for me in the least. See now whether the same happens for you.

Sagredo: I've looked; and however far I lower my eye, I don't see that surface pick up any light or brighten in the slightest — if anything, it seems to me to darken.

Salviati: So far, then, we can be sure the objection has no force. As for the solution: I believe that since this paper's surface falls only a little short of smooth, very few rays get reflected back toward the incident ones, compared with the multitude reflected toward the opposite side; and of those few, more and more are lost the closer the visual rays draw to the incident luminous rays. And since it's not the incident rays but the rays reflected to the eye that make an object look luminous, in lowering the eye you lose more than you gain — just as you said yourself, seeing the sheet grow darker.

Sagredo: Experience and reason are enough for me. It now remains for Simplicio to answer my other question, and tell me what it is that moves the Peripatetics to insist on this roundness in the celestial bodies being so exact.

Simplicio: The celestial bodies' being ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, impervious to change, immortal, and so on, makes them absolutely perfect; and their being absolutely perfect carries the consequence that every kind of perfection is in them — and therefore that their shape, too, is perfect: that is, spherical, and absolutely and perfectly spherical, not rough and irregular.

Salviati: And this incorruptibility — from what do you derive it?

Simplicio: Immediately, from their having no contraries; mediately, from their simple circular motion.

Salviati: So then, as far as I gather from your account, in constituting the essence of the celestial bodies as incorruptible, inalterable, and the rest, roundness doesn't enter into it as a cause or a necessary requirement. For if roundness were what produced inalterability, then we could make wood, wax, and other elemental materials incorruptible whenever we pleased, simply by working them into a spherical shape.

Simplicio: And isn't it obvious that a wooden ball will keep better and longer than a spire, or any other angular shape, made from the same amount of the same wood?

Salviati: That is perfectly true; but it won't thereby pass from corruptible to incorruptible. It stays corruptible — merely longer-lasting. Note this, then: the corruptible admits of more and less. We can say, "This is less corruptible than that" — jasper, for instance, is less corruptible than soft gray sandstone. But the incorruptible admits no more and no less; you cannot say, "This is more incorruptible than that other," if both are incorruptible and eternal. Difference of shape, therefore, can operate only on materials capable of lasting a longer or a shorter time; but in eternal things, which can only be equally eternal, the operation of shape ceases. And so, since celestial matter is incorruptible not through its shape but through something else, there is no need to be so anxious about this perfect sphericity; for once the matter is incorruptible, let it have whatever shape you like — it will always remain so.

Sagredo: But I'm turning over something further, and I say this: grant that the spherical shape had the power to confer incorruptibility, and then all bodies, of whatever shape, would be eternal and incorruptible. For since the round body is incorruptible, corruptibility would have to reside in those parts that spoil the perfect roundness. Take a die, for example: inside it there is a perfectly round ball, and as such incorruptible; so what remains corruptible must be those corners that cover and hide the roundness. The very most that could happen, then, is that those corners — those excrescences, so to speak — should decay. But if we look deeper still, within those parts toward the corners there lie other, smaller balls of the same material, and they too, being round, are incorruptible; and in the remnants surrounding these eight smaller little spheres one can conceive still others; so that at last, resolving the whole die into innumerable balls, you will have to confess it incorruptible. And this same argument, with a similar resolution, can be run on every other shape.

Salviati: The argument runs beautifully. So that if, say, a sphere of crystal owed its incorruptibility to its shape — that is, the power to resist every internal and external alteration — then it's hard to see why adding more crystal to it and reworking it into, say, a cube should alter it within or without, or leave it less able to resist a new surrounding made of its very own material than it was against a former one of different material. Especially if it's true that corruption is worked by contraries, as Aristotle says: for what can you wrap around that ball of crystal that is less contrary to it than crystal itself? But we're not noticing how the hours are slipping away, and we'll come late to the end of our discussions if we make such long speeches over every particular; besides which, my memory gets so tangled in the multitude of things that I can barely recall the propositions Simplicio laid out for us, in order, to be considered.

Simplicio: I remember them very well. And on this particular point of the Moon's mountainousness, the cause I brought forward for that appearance still stands: it can perfectly well be accounted for by saying it is an illusion arising from the parts of the Moon being unequally opaque and transparent.

Sagredo: A little while ago, when Simplicio was attributing the Moon's apparent unevenness — following the opinion of a certain Peripatetic friend of his — to its parts being variously opaque and transparent, on the model of the similar illusions one sees in crystals and gems of many kinds, I thought of a material far better suited to produce such effects, one I feel certain that philosopher would pay any price for: mother-of-pearl. It is worked into various shapes, and even when brought to an extreme polish it looks to the eye so variously hollowed and swelling from place to place that the touch itself can scarcely be trusted to vouch for its smoothness.

Salviati: A truly beautiful thought; and what hasn't been done yet may well be done another time — if other gems and crystals have been brought forward that have nothing to do with the illusions of mother-of-pearl, these will surely be brought forward too. In the meantime, so as not to cut off anyone's opportunity, I'll keep quiet about the answer that would meet it, and for now try only to satisfy the objections Simplicio has raised. I say, then, that this reason of yours is far too general; and unless you apply it, one by one, to all the appearances that are seen in the Moon — the ones that have moved me and others to hold it mountainous — I don't believe you'll find anyone content with such a doctrine. Nor do I believe that you yourself, or the author himself, find any more rest in it than in anything else remote from the point. Of the many, many varied appearances that are seen evening after evening through one lunar month, you could not imitate so much as one by building a ball to your own specification out of parts more and less opaque and transparent, with a polished surface. Whereas, on the other side, from any solid, non-transparent material whatever, one can make balls which — by nothing but ridges and hollows, and by catching the light in different ways — will reproduce exactly, to a hair, the same sights and changes that are seen in the Moon from hour to hour. On them you will see the crests of the ridges facing the Sun's light show quite bright, and behind them the cast shadows lying very dark; you will see those shadows greater and smaller, according as the ridges stand nearer to or farther from the boundary dividing the lit part of the Moon from the dark; you will see that same dividing line drawn not evenly, as it would be if the ball were smooth, but winding and notched like battlements; you will see, beyond that line, in the dark part, many illuminated summits standing detached from the region already luminous; you will see the shadows I've described shrink as the illumination climbs, until they vanish altogether, not one of them left once the whole hemisphere is lit; and then, as the light passes over toward the other lunar hemisphere, you will recognize the same ridges you observed before, and see the projections of their shadows thrown the opposite way and growing. Of all these things — I say it again — you cannot show me even one with your opaque-and-transparent.

Sagredo: One of them, in fact, will be imitated: the full moon, when everything is lit and no shadow is seen any longer, nor anything else that takes any variation from ridges and hollows. But please, Salviati, lose no more time on this particular; for anyone who had the patience to make the observations through one or two lunations and still could not take in this truth that the senses themselves deliver might fairly be sentenced as wholly devoid of judgment — and with such people, why spend time and words for nothing?

Simplicio: I honestly haven't made such observations, because I've never had that curiosity, nor an instrument fit to make them with; but I fully mean to. And meanwhile we can leave this question hanging and pass to the point that follows: your producing the grounds on which you hold that the Earth can reflect the Sun's light no less vigorously than the Moon. For to me it looks so dark and opaque that such an effect strikes me as utterly impossible.

Salviati: The cause that makes you consider the Earth unfit for illumination is not at all what you think, Simplicio. And wouldn't it be a fine thing if I understood your own reasoning better than you do yourself?

Simplicio: Whether I reason well or badly, you may perhaps know better than I do; but that you can see into my reasoning better than I can myself — that I will never believe.

Salviati: Then I'll make you believe it right now. Tell me a little: when the Moon is nearly full, so that it can be seen by day and at midnight too, when does it look more brilliant to you — by day, or at night?

Simplicio: At night, beyond all comparison. It seems to me the Moon is like that pillar of cloud and fire that went before the children of Israel: in the Sun's presence it showed like a little cloud, but at night it was most splendid. So I have sometimes observed the Moon by day among small clouds, looking exactly like one of them, whitish; but then at night it shines magnificently.

Salviati: So that if you had never happened to see the Moon except by day, you would never have judged it brighter than one of those little clouds.

Simplicio: I firmly believe I wouldn't have.

Salviati: Now tell me: do you believe the Moon is really more luminous at night than by day, or does it only show that way through some accident?

Simplicio: I believe it shines in itself just as much by day as by night, but that its light looks greater at night because we see it against the dark field of the sky; whereas by day, the whole surrounding air being quite bright, the Moon exceeds it in light by only a little, and so appears to us much less luminous.

Salviati: Now tell me this: have you ever, at midnight, seen the terrestrial globe illuminated by the Sun?

Simplicio: That strikes me as a question to be asked only as a joke — or else of someone known to be an outright fool.

Salviati: No, no: I hold you a most sensible man, and I ask the question in earnest. So answer anyway; and then, if my talk seems beside the point, I'm content to be the fool myself — for the one who asks foolishly is a bigger fool than the one who's asked.

Simplicio: If, then, you don't take me for a complete simpleton, consider that I've answered you, and said this: it is impossible for anyone standing on the Earth, as we do, to see by night that part of the Earth where it is day — the part the Sun is striking.

Salviati: So it has never fallen to you to see the Earth illuminated except by day; whereas the Moon you see shining in the sky even in the deepest night. And that, Simplicio, is the reason you believe the Earth doesn't shine like the Moon. If you could see the Earth illuminated while you stood in a place as dark as our night is, you would see it more resplendent than the Moon. Now, if the comparison is to be run fairly, we must set the Earth's light against the light of the Moon seen by day — not against the nighttime Moon — since we never get to see the Earth illuminated except by day. Isn't that so?

Simplicio: So it must be.

Salviati: And since you yourself have already confessed to having seen the Moon by day among whitish little clouds, looking in appearance exactly like one of them, you have thereby already granted, to begin with, that those little clouds — which after all are elemental matter — are as fit to receive illumination as the Moon is; more fit, in fact, if you will call back to mind having sometimes seen certain enormous clouds white as snow. And there can be no doubt that if such a cloud could stay that luminous through the deepest night, it would light the surrounding country more than a hundred Moons. If we were certain, then, that the Earth is lit by the Sun as much as one of those clouds, no doubt would remain that it shines no less than the Moon. And on this point every doubt vanishes when we see those same clouds, in the Sun's absence, stay as dark by night as the Earth does; and — what's more — there is not one of us who hasn't many times seen certain low, distant clouds and stood in doubt whether they were clouds or mountains: clear evidence that mountains are no less luminous than those clouds.

Sagredo: But why go on arguing? Look — there's the Moon up there, more than half full; and there's that high wall the Sun is beating on. Step back this way, so the Moon shows beside the wall. Now look: which seems brighter to you? Don't you see that if either has the advantage, it's the wall? The Sun strikes that wall; from there the light bounces onto the walls of the hall; from those it is reflected into that room beyond, so that it arrives there at the third reflection — and even so, I am certain there is more light in that room than if the Moon's light reached it directly.

Simplicio: Oh, that I don't believe. The Moon's light — above all when it's full — is a great illumination.

Sagredo: It seems great because of the darkness of the shadowy places around it, but in absolute terms it isn't much; it's less than the twilight half an hour after sunset. That's plain, because it's only around then that the shadows of bodies lit by the Moon begin to be distinguishable on the ground. As for whether that third reflection lights that room better than the Moon's first — that can be settled by going in there and reading a book, and then trying again tonight, by moonlight, whether it reads more easily or less. Less, I have no doubt at all.

Salviati: Now, Simplicio — if you are satisfied, that is — you can see how you yourself truly knew all along that the Earth shines no less than the Moon, and that merely being reminded of things you knew on your own, and were never taught by me, is what has made you certain of it. For I did not teach you that the Moon shows more resplendent by night than by day — you knew it yourself; just as you knew on your own that a little cloud shows as bright as the Moon. You knew likewise that the Earth's illumination cannot be seen at night; in short, you knew everything, without knowing that you knew it. From here, then, it should not reasonably be hard for you to grant that the Earth's reflection can light the dark part of the Moon with a light no weaker than that with which the Moon lights the darkness of our night — indeed, so much the stronger, inasmuch as the Earth is forty times larger than the Moon.

Simplicio: Truly, I believed that secondary light — that dim glow on the dark part — was the Moon's own.

Salviati: And this too you know on your own, and don't notice that you know it. Tell me: haven't you known, all by yourself, that the Moon shows far more luminous by night than by day, on account of the darkness of the surrounding field? And consequently, don't you know in general that every shining body shows brighter, the darker its surroundings?

Simplicio: That I know perfectly well.

Salviati: When the Moon is a thin crescent and shows you that secondary light quite plainly, isn't it always near the Sun, and consequently in the glow of twilight?

Simplicio: It is; and many times I've wished the air would grow darker so I could see that light more clearly — but the Moon always set before full dark.

Salviati: So you know very well that in deep night that light would show more?

Simplicio: Yes indeed — and more still, if one could take away the great light of the horns the Sun touches, whose presence badly dims the other, lesser light.

Salviati: Well then — doesn't it sometimes happen that one can see the Moon's whole disk on the darkest of nights, without its being lit by the Sun at all?

Simplicio: I don't know that that ever happens, except in total eclipses of the Moon.

Salviati: Then at those moments this light of its own ought to show at its most vivid, standing as it does in a pitch-dark field, undimmed by the brightness of the luminous horns. In that state, how bright have you seen it?

Simplicio: I've seen it sometimes the color of copper, faintly whitish; but at other times it has stayed so dark that I lost sight of it entirely.

Salviati: How, then, can that light be the Moon's own — this light you see so clearly in the pale of twilight, despite the interference of the great splendor of the horns right beside it, and which then, in the darkest night, with every other light removed, doesn't appear at all?

Simplicio: I understand there have been some who believed such light comes to the Moon as a share from the other stars, and in particular from Venus, its neighbor.

Salviati: And that likewise is an empty notion; because at the time of its total darkening it ought then to show more brilliant than ever — for no one can say the Earth's shadow blocks its view of Venus or of the other stars. But in fact the Moon is then wholly deprived of that light, because the terrestrial hemisphere facing the Moon at that time is the one where it is night — that is, a total privation of the Sun's light. And if you go observing carefully, you will see with your own senses that just as the Moon, when it is a thin crescent, gives very little light to the Earth, and as the Sun-lit part of it grows, the splendor reflected down to us grows along with it — so too the Moon, while it is a thin crescent and, standing between the Sun and the Earth, looks out over a very great part of the illuminated terrestrial hemisphere, shows quite bright; and as it draws away from the Sun toward quadrature — the half-moon position — that light is seen to languish; and past quadrature it shows quite feeble, because it keeps losing more and more of its view of the luminous part of the Earth. Yet exactly the contrary ought to happen if that light were its own, or lent to it by the stars — for then we could see it in deep night, against very dark surroundings.

Simplicio: Stop, please — for just now I remember reading, in a recent little book of theses, one full of novelties, "that this secondary light is not caused by the stars, nor is it the Moon's own, and least of all is it lent to her by the Earth; rather it derives from the Sun's own illumination, which — the substance of the lunar globe being somewhat transparent — penetrates through the whole of its body; but it lights most vividly the surface of the hemisphere exposed to the Sun's rays, while the depths beneath, drinking in that light and, so to speak, soaking themselves with it after the manner of a cloud or a crystal, transmit it and become visibly luminous." And this (if I remember rightly) he proves by authority, by experience, and by reason, citing Cleomedes, Witelo, Macrobius, and some other modern author; and he adds that experience shows the Moon very bright in the days near conjunction — that is, when it is a crescent — and shining above all around its rim; and further, he writes that in solar eclipses, when the Moon stands beneath the Sun's disk, it can be seen glowing through, especially around its outermost circle. As for the reasons, I think he says that since this light can come neither from the Earth nor from the stars nor from the Moon itself, it necessarily remains that it comes from the Sun; and besides, once this supposition is granted, tidy explanations can be given for every particular that arises. That the secondary light shows livelier around the outer rim has for its cause the shortness of the space the Sun's rays have to penetrate there — since, of the lines crossing a circle, the greatest is the one through the center, and of the others, those farther from it are always shorter than those nearer. From the same principle, he says, it follows that this light diminishes very little. And finally, on this account one can assign the cause of why that brighter circle around the Moon's outer margin is seen, in a solar eclipse, in the part standing under the Sun's disk, but not in the part outside it; this comes about because the Sun's rays pass straight through the parts of the Moon lying under the disk and on to our eye, while from the parts outside the disk they fall wide of the eye.

Salviati: If this philosopher had been the first author of that opinion, I wouldn't be surprised that he was so fond of it he took it for true. But since he received it from others, I can't find any excuse good enough for his failure to see through its fallacies — above all after he had heard the true cause of the effect, and could have assured himself, by a thousand trials and plain confirmations, that it comes from the reflection of the Earth and from nothing else. And as much as that knowledge makes us wish for a little more discernment in this author, and in everyone else who withholds assent, so much does the fact that it was never understood — never even occurred to them — excuse the older writers, who, I'm quite certain, would admit it now without the slightest resistance if they understood it. And if I'm to tell you my honest thought: I can't believe this modern author doesn't privately accept it. I suspect, rather, that not being able to claim it as his own invention goads him a little into trying to suppress it, or at least to discredit it in the eyes of the simple — whose numbers, we know, are enormous. And there are plenty of men who enjoy the loud applause of the crowd far more than the assent of the discerning few.

Sagredo: Hold on a moment, Salviati — I don't think you're going straight to the real point. These men who spread their nets for the public also know how to pass themselves off as the authors of other people's inventions, provided the inventions aren't so old, so broadcast from the lecture halls and the piazzas, that everyone already knows them by heart.

Salviati: Oh, I'm more wicked than you are. Why talk about "public" or "well known"? Isn't it all the same whether the opinions and inventions are new to the men, or the men are new to them? If you'd settle for the admiration of beginners in the sciences — and a fresh crop comes up every season — you could set yourself up as the inventor of the alphabet itself and be their marvel. And if in time your little game were discovered, it would hardly hurt your purpose, since new recruits step in to keep the ranks of your admirers full.

But let's get back to showing Simplicio how feeble his modern author's arguments are — arguments containing falsehoods, non-sequiturs, and sheer implausibilities. First of all, it is false that this secondary light is brighter around the outer rim than in the middle, forming a sort of ring or circle more brilliant than the rest of the field. It's true that when you look at the Moon in the twilight, at its first appearance, such a circle does show — but it's an illusion, born of the different borders that bound the lunar disc bathed in this secondary light. On the side toward the Sun, it borders the dazzling horns of the Moon; on the other side, its neighbor is the dark field of the twilight sky, and by that contrast the pale glow of the lunar disc looks brighter to us, while on the opposite side the same glow is drowned out by the greater splendor of the horns. If our modern author had tried placing an obstacle between his eye and the primary brilliance — the roof of some house, or any other screen — so that only the stretch of the Moon outside the horns stayed visible, he would have seen it all equally luminous.

Simplicio: I do seem to remember that he writes of having used exactly that kind of trick to hide the bright sickle from himself.

Salviati: Oh — then what I was charitably calling an oversight becomes a lie, and a reckless one at that, since anyone can repeat the test as often as he likes. As for seeing the Moon's disc during an eclipse of the Sun in any way except as a dark absence, I doubt it very much — especially when the eclipse isn't total, as the ones this author observed must necessarily have been. But even if the disc did show as luminous then, that wouldn't contradict our opinion; it would favor it, because at that moment the entire terrestrial hemisphere lit by the Sun is facing the Moon, and though the Moon's shadow darkens a part of it, that part is tiny compared with what stays illuminated.

What he adds — that in this case the part of the rim lying under the Sun shows quite bright, but not the part outside it, because the Sun's rays reach our eye directly through the one part and not the other — is truly one of those fables that give away all the teller's other fictions. If the Sun's rays have to come straight to our eye for the lunar disc to be visible by secondary light, doesn't the poor man see that we would then never see that secondary light except during a solar eclipse? And if a part of the Moon standing less than half a degree away from the Sun's disc can deflect the solar rays enough to keep them from our eye, what happens when the Moon stands twenty or thirty degrees away, as it does at its first appearance? How are the Sun's rays, which are supposed to pass clean through the body of the Moon, going to find our eye then?

This man goes along, step by step, imagining things as they would need to be to serve his purpose, instead of adjusting his purpose, step by step, to things as they are. Watch: to let the Sun's splendor penetrate the Moon's substance, he makes that substance partly transparent — transparent, say, the way a cloud or a crystal is. But I don't know what he'd make of that transparency once the Sun's rays had to penetrate a depth of cloud more than two thousand miles thick. Suppose he answers boldly that this can perfectly well happen in celestial bodies, which are quite another affair from these impure and dreggy elemental bodies of ours; then let's convict him of his error by means that admit no reply — or rather, no subterfuge. If he wants to maintain that the Moon's substance is transparent, he'll have to say it is transparent when the Sun's rays must pass through its whole depth — more than two thousand miles of it — and yet, when faced with barely a single mile, it blocks them as stubbornly as one of our mountains would.

Sagredo: You remind me of a man who wanted to sell me a secret: how to speak with someone two or three thousand miles away, by means of a certain sympathy between magnetized needles. I told him I'd gladly buy it, but that I wanted to see a trial first, and that it would satisfy me to make it with me standing in one room of my house and him in another. He answered that at so short a distance the operation couldn't be seen properly. So I showed him the door, telling him I didn't feel like traveling to Cairo or to Muscovy just then to watch the demonstration — but if he wanted to go himself, I would hold up the other end from Venice. But let's hear where the author's own logic lands him, and how he's forced to admit that the Moon's material is supremely permeable to the Sun's rays through a depth of two thousand miles, yet more opaque than one of our mountains across the thickness of a single mile.

Salviati: The Moon's own mountains are the witnesses. Struck by the Sun on one side, they cast pitch-black shadows on the other — sharper-edged and more cleanly cut, by far, than the shadows of ours. If they were transparent, we could never have detected the least roughness in the Moon's surface, nor seen those luminous peaks standing detached beyond the line that divides the lit part from the dark. Still less would we see that line itself so distinctly, if it were true that the Sun's light sank deep into the Moon. On the author's own account, in fact, the passage and border between the part the Sun sees and the part it doesn't would have to look thoroughly blurred, a muddle of light and darkness — because material that lets the Sun's rays through two thousand miles of depth must be so transparent that it would scarcely resist them across a hundredth of that thickness or less. Yet the line separating the illuminated part from the dark one is as clean and distinct as the line between white and black — above all where the cut crosses the naturally brighter and rougher part of the Moon. Where it slices across the ancient spots, which are plains, the edge isn't so sharp — those plains curve away with the sphere, so they catch the Sun's rays very obliquely, and the fainter illumination softens the line.

Finally, what he says about the secondary light neither shrinking nor dimming as the Moon waxes, but keeping the same strength throughout, is utterly false. It's barely visible at quadrature — the half-Moon — precisely when it ought to show at its liveliest, since it can then be seen clear of twilight, in the dead of night. Let us conclude, therefore, that the Earth's reflection is very powerful upon the Moon. And — this is what you should prize most — draw from it one more beautiful correspondence: if it's true that the planets act upon the Earth by their motion and their light, then perhaps the Earth is no less able to act back upon them with the same light, and, for all we know, with motion too. And even if the Earth didn't move, the same action could remain; for, as we've seen, the action of the light is exactly the same — reflected sunlight — and motion does nothing but vary the aspects, which change in exactly the same way whether you make the Earth move and the Sun stand still, or the other way around.

Simplicio: You will find no philosopher who has said that these lower bodies act on the celestial ones — and Aristotle says plainly the contrary.

Salviati: Aristotle and the others who didn't know that the Earth and the Moon light each other in turn deserve to be excused. But they would richly deserve blame if, while demanding that we grant and believe on their word that the Moon acts on the Earth by its light, they then turned around and denied — to us, who have taught them that the Earth illuminates the Moon — any action of the Earth upon the Moon.

Simplicio: The plain truth is, I feel an extreme reluctance in myself to admit this fellowship you want to talk me into between the Earth and the Moon — enrolling the Earth, as the saying goes, in the company of the stars. If nothing else, the sheer separation and distance between it and the celestial bodies seems to me to force the conclusion that their natures are enormously dissimilar.

Salviati: See, Simplicio, what an inveterate attachment and a deep-rooted opinion can do. It is strong enough to make the very things you produce against yourself look like points in your favor. For if separation and distance are valid grounds for persuading you of a great diversity of natures, then nearness and contact must, by the same token, imply similarity — and how much nearer is the Moon to the Earth than to any other celestial globe? Confess, then, by your own concession — and you'll have other philosophers for company — that there is a very great affinity between the Earth and the Moon. Now let's push on: tell us whether anything remains to consider among the difficulties you raised against the correspondences between these two bodies.

Simplicio: There would remain something about the solidity of the Moon — which I argued from its being supremely polished and smooth, and you from its being mountainous. And another difficulty occurred to me, from my belief that the sea's reflection ought to be stronger than the land's, because of the evenness of its surface, while the land's surface is so rough and dull.

Salviati: As to the first doubt: just as among the parts of the Earth — all of which, by their heaviness, conspire to press as close as they can to the center — some nonetheless remain farther out than others, the mountains more than the plains, and this because of their solidity and hardness (if they were made of fluid matter, they would level out); so, in the same way, seeing some parts of the Moon standing raised above the sphere of the lower parts tells us they are hard. For it's credible that the Moon's material gathers itself into a spherical shape by the concordant conspiring of all its parts toward the same center.

As for the other doubt: from what we've already observed happening with mirrors, we can understand perfectly well that the reflection of light coming from the sea is far weaker than the one coming from land — speaking, that is, of the universal reflection. As for the particular reflection that a surface of calm water sends toward one determinate place, there's no doubt that whoever stands in that place will see a very powerful reflection in the water; but from every other spot the water's surface will look darker than the land's. And to show it to your senses, let's step into the hall here and pour a little water on the floor. Now tell me: doesn't this wet brick show much darker than the dry ones? Certainly it does — and it will look that way from any point you view it, with one single exception: the spot where the reflection of the light coming in at that window lands. So back away, slowly.

Simplicio: From here I see the wet part brighter than the rest of the floor, and I see that it happens because the reflection of the light coming in at the window is heading toward me.

Salviati: All the wetting has done is fill in the little hollows in the brick and reduce its surface to an exquisite flatness, so that the reflected rays leave it united, all toward the same place. The rest of the floor, dry, keeps its roughness — an innumerable variety of tilts among its tiniest particles — so the reflections of the light go off in every direction, but weaker than if they all traveled united together. That's why its look changes little or nothing as you view it from different sides; it shows the same from everywhere, but far less bright than the reflection from the wet part. I conclude, therefore, that the surface of the sea, seen from the Moon, would look extremely even — islands and reefs aside — but less bright than the surface of the land, mountainous and uneven as it is.

And if I weren't afraid of seeming, as they say, to want too much, I'd tell you that I have observed that secondary light on the Moon — the light I say comes to it from the reflection of the terrestrial globe — to be noticeably brighter two or three days before conjunction than after it: that is, brighter when we see the Moon in the east before dawn than when it shows in the west in the evening, after sunset. The cause of the difference is that the terrestrial hemisphere facing the eastern Moon has little sea and a very great deal of land — all of Asia — whereas when the Moon is in the west, it looks down on immense seas: the whole Atlantic Ocean, clear to the Americas. A rather probable argument that the surface of water shows itself less splendid than the surface of land.

Simplicio: But do you actually believe, then, that those great spots we see in the face of the Moon are seas, and the brighter remainder land, or something of that sort?

Salviati: What you're asking is the beginning of the disparities that I hold to exist between the Moon and the Earth — and it's time we worked our way through those, for we have lingered on this Moon quite long enough. I say, then, that if nature had only one way of making two sunlit surfaces appear one brighter than the other — namely, by one being land and the other water — then we would necessarily have to say the Moon's surface is part earthy and part watery. But because we know of several ways to produce the same effect, and there may be still others unknown to us, I would not dare affirm that this one rather than that exists on the Moon.

We saw above how a plate of whitened silver, touched with the burnisher, goes from bright white to dark. The wet part of the Earth shows darker than the dry. On mountain ridges, the wooded stretches appear much duskier than the bare and barren ones, because among the trees a great quantity of shadow falls, while the open ground lies fully lit by the Sun. And this mingling of shadows works so strongly that in figured velvet the cut silk shows much darker than the uncut, thanks to the shadows scattered between thread and thread — and plain velvet, likewise, much darker than a smooth taffeta made of the very same silk. So if there were things on the Moon that resembled immense forests, their aspect could well present us with the spots we see; the spots would show a like difference if they were seas; and, finally, nothing rules out those spots really being of a darker color than the rest — after all, that is how snow makes mountains look brighter.

What is plainly seen on the Moon is this: the darker parts are all plains, with few reefs and ridges inside them, though there are some; the brighter remainder is all full of reefs, mountains, little rounded ridges, and other shapes; and in particular, around the spots run enormous chains of mountains. That the spots are flat surfaces we can be sure of, from seeing that the line dividing the lit part from the dark cuts evenly where it crosses the spots, while in the bright parts it shows jagged and toothed everywhere. But whether that evenness of surface is enough, by itself, to account for the darkness — that I don't know; I rather think not.

Beyond all this, I judge the Moon to be profoundly different from the Earth. Though I imagine those regions are not idle and dead, I still would not assert that motion and life exist there — much less that plants, animals, or other things like ours are generated there; and if anything is, it would be utterly different, remote from everything we can imagine. I'm moved to think so, first, because I judge that the material of the lunar globe is not earth and water, and that alone is enough to rule out generations and alterations like ours. But even granting that there were water and earth up there, plants and animals like ours still would not arise, and for two principal reasons.

The first is that the varying aspects of the Sun are so necessary to our generations that without them everything would fail — and the Sun's dispositions toward the Earth are very different from its dispositions toward the Moon. As for daily illumination, we, over most of the Earth, get part day and part night in every twenty-four hours; on the Moon, that effect takes a month. And the yearly dipping and climbing by which the Sun brings us the changing seasons and the inequality of days and nights is likewise finished, on the Moon, in a month. And whereas for us the Sun rises and sinks so far that between its greatest and least altitude there runs a difference of about forty-seven degrees — the distance from one tropic to the other — on the Moon it comes to no more than ten degrees or a little over, which is as far as the greatest latitudes of the dragon, the Moon's tilted path, stray on either side of the ecliptic. Now consider what the Sun's action would be within the torrid zone if it went on striking it with its rays for fifteen days without a break: you will grasp at once that all the plants and grasses and animals would be destroyed; and if generations did occur there, they would be of grasses, plants, and animals utterly different from the present ones.

Second, I hold it for certain that there are no rains on the Moon; because if clouds gathered anywhere on it, as they do around the Earth, they would hide from us some of the things we see on the Moon with the telescope — in short, they would change the view in one particular or another. And that is an effect I have never once seen in long and careful observation: I have always found there a uniform, perfectly pure serenity.

Sagredo: To that one might answer either that there are enormous dews there, or that it rains during their night — that is, when the Sun isn't shining on it.

Salviati: If other evidence gave us signs that things are generated there the way they are here, and rain were the only ingredient missing, we could come up with this or some other arrangement to stand in for it — the way the flooding of the Nile stands in for rain in Egypt. But since we haven't found one single feature that matches ours, out of the many that would be needed to produce similar effects up there, there's no point straining to introduce even one — and that one not because we have any solid observation of it, but merely because nothing rules it out. Besides, if I were asked what my first impression and plain natural reasoning tell me about whether the things produced there are like ours or different, I would always answer: utterly different, and entirely beyond our imagining — for that, it seems to me, is what the richness of nature and the omnipotence of her Creator and Governor demand.

Sagredo: It has always seemed to me the height of rashness when people make human capacity the measure of what nature can do and knows how to do — when in fact the opposite is true: there is not a single effect in nature, however small, that the most speculative minds can ever completely understand. That vain presumption of understanding everything can have no other root than never having understood anything at all; for anyone who had experienced, even once, the perfect understanding of one single thing, and had truly tasted what knowledge is made of, would recognize that of the infinity of other conclusions he understands nothing.

Salviati: Your argument is airtight. And confirming it we have the experience of those who understand something, or once did: the wiser they are, the more clearly they recognize — and the more freely they confess — how little they know. And the wisest man in Greece, pronounced so by the oracles themselves, said openly that he knew he knew nothing.

Simplicio: Then we'd have to say that either the oracle or Socrates himself was lying — the one proclaiming him the wisest of men, the other declaring that he knew himself to be utterly ignorant.

Salviati: Neither follows, because both pronouncements can be true. The oracle judges Socrates wisest above other men, whose wisdom is limited; Socrates knows that he knows nothing when measured against absolute wisdom, which is infinite. And since, against infinity, much is just as small a portion as little, or as nothing — to reach an infinite number, piling up thousands gets you no closer than piling up tens, or zeros — Socrates saw very well that his finite wisdom was nothing next to the infinite wisdom he lacked. But since there is nonetheless some knowledge to be found among men, and it isn't shared out equally to all, Socrates could hold a larger portion of it than anyone else — and so the oracle's answer came true.

Sagredo: I think I understand this point very well. Among men, Simplicio, there is the power to act, but not shared equally by all: no one doubts that an emperor's power is far greater than a private person's, yet both are nothing compared with divine omnipotence. Among men there are some who understand farming better than many others; but what does knowing how to plant a vine cutting in a trench have to do with knowing how to make it take root, draw in nourishment, and sort out from that nourishment the part that's good for making leaves, this other part for the tendrils, that one for the clusters, another for the grapes, still another for the skins — all of which are the works of supremely wise nature? This is one single, particular operation among the countless ones nature performs, and in it alone we recognize an infinite wisdom; so we may conclude that divine knowledge is infinitely infinite.

Salviati: Here's another example. Wouldn't we say that the power to discover a beautiful statue inside a block of marble lifted Michelangelo's genius far, far above the ordinary minds of other men? And yet that work is nothing but the imitation of a single posture — one outward, surface arrangement of the limbs of a motionless man. What is it compared to a man made by nature, composed of so many parts outside and in, so many muscles, tendons, nerves, and bones, serving so many different movements? And what shall we say of the senses, of the powers of the soul, and finally of understanding itself? Can't we say, with good reason, that the making of a statue falls infinitely short of the forming of a living man — indeed, of the forming of the lowliest worm?

Sagredo: And what difference do we suppose there was between Archytas' dove — that ancient mechanical bird — and one of nature's own?

Simplicio: Either I'm not one of these men who understand, or there is an open contradiction in this talk of yours. Among the greatest tributes you pay to man, made by nature — indeed as the greatest of them all — you count this power of understanding; and a moment ago you were saying, with Socrates, that his understanding was nothing. So it would follow that not even nature has understood how to make an intellect that understands.

Salviati: A very sharp objection. To answer it, we have to fall back on a philosophical distinction, and say that understanding can be taken in two ways: intensive — in depth — or extensive — in range. Taken extensive, that is, with respect to the multitude of things there are to be understood, which are infinite, human understanding is as nothing, even if it understood a thousand propositions — because a thousand, next to infinity, is like zero. But taking understanding intensive — insofar as that term means grasping some proposition intensively, that is, perfectly — I say that the human intellect does understand some propositions as perfectly, and holds them with as absolute a certainty, as nature herself has. Such are the pure mathematical sciences, geometry and arithmetic. Of these the divine intellect knows infinitely more propositions, to be sure, since it knows them all; but of those few the human intellect does grasp, I believe its knowledge equals the divine in objective certainty — for it reaches the point of comprehending their necessity, and beyond necessity there does not seem to be any greater sureness.

Simplicio: This strikes me as very bold and daring talk.
