Salviati: These are commonplace propositions, far from any shadow of rashness or daring, and they take nothing away from the majesty of divine wisdom — just as saying that God cannot make what has been done be undone diminishes His omnipotence not at all. But I suspect, Simplicio, that you've taken alarm because my words reached you with a certain ambiguity in them. So, to explain myself better: as for the truth that mathematical demonstrations give us knowledge of, it is the same truth that divine wisdom knows. But I will gladly grant you that the way God knows the infinitely many propositions, of which we know a few, is supremely more excellent than ours. Ours proceeds by reasoning, by passages from one conclusion to the next; His is a single, simple intuition. Where we, for example, to win the knowledge of a few of the properties of the circle — which has infinitely many — begin from one of the simplest, take it as the definition, and pass by reasoning to a second, and from that to a third, then a fourth, and so on, the divine intellect, by the simple apprehension of the circle's essence, comprehends the whole infinity of its properties without any reasoning through time. And in fact all those properties are virtually contained in the definitions of all things anyway — and being infinite, perhaps they are, in their essence and in the divine Mind, one single property. Nor is any of this entirely unknown to the human intellect either; it is only veiled by a deep, dense fog, which thins and clears in part whenever we have made ourselves masters of some conclusions firmly demonstrated and possessed so readily that we can run swiftly among them. For, in the end, what is it for the square on the side opposite the right angle of a triangle to be equal to the squares on the other two sides, if not for parallelograms on a common base between parallel lines to be equal to each other? And isn't that, finally, the same as two surfaces being equal that, when fitted together, do not spill over each other but close within the same boundary? Now these passages, which our intellect makes in time, moving step by step, the divine intellect runs through in an instant, like light — which is the same as saying that they are all always present to it. So I conclude that our understanding — both in the manner and in the number of things understood — is surpassed by the divine at an infinite distance. But I do not for all that debase it so far as to count it absolutely nothing. No: when I go considering how many and how marvelous are the things men have understood, investigated, and accomplished, I recognize and understand all too clearly that the human mind is a work of God, and one of the most excellent.

Sagredo: Many times, along the lines of what you're saying now, I have turned over in my own mind how great the sharpness of human ingenuity is. And when I run through the many marvelous inventions men have found, in the arts as in letters, and then reflect on my own knowledge — so far from promising me the discovery of anything new that I can hardly hope even to learn what has already been found — I am confounded with wonder and afflicted with despair, and count myself little short of miserable. If I look at one of the excellent statues, I say to myself: "And when would you know how to strip the excess away from a block of marble, and uncover so beautiful a figure hidden inside it? When would you know how to mix and spread different colors over a canvas or a wall, and with them represent every visible object, like a Michelangelo, a Raphael, a Titian?" If I look at what men have discovered in dividing up the musical intervals, in laying down precepts and rules for handling them to the marvelous delight of the ear — when will I ever be done being amazed? What shall I say of all the many, and so different, instruments? What wonder does the reading of the excellent poets fill us with, for anyone who attends closely to the invention of their ideas and the unfolding of them? What shall we say of architecture? What of the art of navigation? But above all stupendous inventions, what towering mind was his who imagined finding a way to communicate his most hidden thoughts to any other person whatsoever, however distant in place and in time? To speak with those who are in the Indies; to speak to those who are not yet born, and will not be born for a thousand or ten thousand years? And with what ease? — with the various arrangements of twenty little characters on a page. Let this be the seal of all humanity's admirable inventions, and the close of our reasonings for today. And now that the hottest hours are past, I think Salviati will enjoy going out to taste the cool of the evening on our boat; and tomorrow I shall be expecting you both, to carry on with the discussions we have begun.

The Second Day

Salviati: Yesterday's digressions, which pulled us off the straight thread of our main discussions, were so many and so varied that I don't know whether I can find the trail again without your help, so as to move forward.

Sagredo: I'm not surprised that you, with your imagination filled and cluttered both with what's been said and with what remains to be said, should find yourself in some confusion. But I, being a simple listener, carry nothing in my head but the things I've heard; so perhaps, by recalling them in summary, I can set the reasoning back on its thread. As far as my memory serves, then, the sum of yesterday's discussions was an examination, from their very foundations, of which of two opinions is the more probable and reasonable: the one which holds that the substance of the celestial bodies is ingenerable, incorruptible, inalterable, impervious to change — in short, exempt from every kind of alteration except change of place, and therefore a fifth essence utterly different from this substance of our elemental bodies, which are generable, corruptible, alterable, and so on; or else the other opinion, which strips this lopsidedness out of the world and holds that the Earth enjoys the very same perfections as the other integral bodies of the universe — that it is, in short, a globe that moves and wanders no less than the Moon, Jupiter, Venus, or any other planet. In the end many particular parallels were drawn between this Earth of ours and the Moon — more with the Moon than with any other planet, perhaps because we have greater and more solid knowledge of her, thanks to her lesser distance. And having finally concluded that this second opinion has more of the likely about it than the first, it seems to me the natural next step was to begin examining whether the Earth should be judged immobile, as most people have believed up to now, or mobile, as certain ancient philosophers believed and as others have come to think in more recent times — and, if mobile, what its motion might be.

Salviati: I already recognize the landmarks of our road. But before we set out any further, I have to say a word about those last remarks of yours — that we concluded the opinion which grants the Earth the same conditions as the celestial bodies to be more likely than its contrary. I concluded no such thing, just as I don't intend to reach a verdict on any other of the disputed propositions. My intention was only to produce, for each side equally, the reasons and replies, the objections and solutions, that others have thought of up to now, along with a few more that have dropped into my mind after long thinking about it — and then to leave the decision to the judgment of others.

Sagredo: I let myself be carried away by my own feeling; and believing that what I felt in myself must be what others felt too, I made universal a conclusion I should have kept particular. I truly was wrong, especially since I didn't know the view of Simplicio, here present.

Simplicio: I'll confess to you that all last night I was ruminating over yesterday's matters, and truly I find many fine, new, and vigorous considerations in them. All the same, I feel myself gripped far more strongly by the authority of so many great writers, and in particular... You're shaking your head, Sagredo, and smirking, as though I'd said something wildly out of line.

Sagredo: I'm only smirking — but believe me, I'm about to burst with the effort of holding back a much bigger laugh. You've reminded me of a wonderful incident I witnessed not many years ago, together with some noble friends of mine whom I could still name for you.

Salviati: Then you'd better tell it, so that Simplicio doesn't go on believing it was he who set you laughing.

Sagredo: Gladly. One day I was at the house of a highly esteemed physician in Venice, where people gathered from time to time — some for their studies, others out of curiosity — to watch a dissection at the hands of an anatomist who was truly as learned as he was careful and skilled. It happened that day that the search was on for the origin and root of the nerves, the subject of a famous controversy between the Galenist physicians and the Peripatetics. The anatomist showed how the great trunk of nerves sets out from the brain, passes through the nape of the neck, then stretches down along the spine and branches out through the whole body, while only a single strand, fine as sewing thread, arrives at the heart. Then he turned to a gentleman he knew to be a Peripatetic philosopher — for whose benefit he had uncovered and demonstrated the whole thing with extraordinary care — and asked whether he was now satisfied and certain that the nerves originate in the brain and not in the heart. And the philosopher, after standing lost in thought for a while, answered: "You have made me see this matter so plainly and palpably that if Aristotle's text did not stand against it — for it says openly that the nerves are born in the heart — I would be forced to confess it true."

Simplicio: Gentlemen, I want you to know that this dispute over the origin of the nerves is by no means as digested and settled as some people may persuade themselves.

Sagredo: Nor will it ever be, safe to say, so long as it has opponents of that sort. But what you say doesn't shave one bit off the extravagance of the Peripatetic's reply: against such palpable evidence of the senses he produced no other experiments or arguments of Aristotle's — only bare authority, the pure Ipse dixit: he himself said it.

Simplicio: Aristotle did not acquire so great an authority except by the force of his demonstrations and the depth of his reasoning. But you have to understand him — and not merely understand him, but have such long practice in his books that you've formed a complete and perfect idea of them, so that every saying of his is always present to your mind. He did not write for the crowd, nor did he bind himself to string out his syllogisms in the ordinary schoolroom order. Instead, using a scattered method, he sometimes placed the proof of a proposition among texts that seem to deal with anything but. So you must hold that whole grand scheme in your head, and know how to combine this passage with that one, to piece this text together with another far removed from it. There's no doubt that whoever has this practice will be able to draw from his books the demonstrations of everything that can be known — because everything is in them.

Sagredo: My dear Simplicio: since having things scattered here and there doesn't bother you, and since you believe that by piecing together and combining various little scraps you can squeeze out their juice — well, what you and the other brave philosophers do with the texts of Aristotle, I will do with the verses of Virgil or Ovid, stitching them into centos — patchwork poems — and using them to explain all the affairs of men and the secrets of nature. But why do I even mention Virgil, or any other poet? I have a little book much shorter than Aristotle or Ovid, which contains all the sciences, and with very little study anyone can form a most perfect idea of it: the alphabet. There's no doubt that whoever knows how to couple and arrange this vowel and that one with those consonants or these others will draw from them the truest answers to every doubt, and the teachings of every science and every art — in exactly the way a painter, from simple colors laid out separately on his palette, goes about combining a little of this with a little of that and a little of the other, and shapes men, plants, buildings, birds, fish; in short, imitates every visible object, though there are no eyes on his palette, no feathers, no scales, no leaves, no stones. In fact it's necessary that none of the things to be imitated, nor any part of them, be actually present among the colors, if you want those colors to be capable of representing everything; if there were feathers among them, say, they'd serve to paint nothing but birds and plumes.

Salviati: There are gentlemen still alive and well who were present when a doctor lecturing in a famous university, hearing the telescope described — he hadn't yet seen it — announced that the invention was lifted from Aristotle. He had a text brought to him, found a certain passage where Aristotle gives the reason why, from the bottom of a very deep well, one can see stars in the sky during the day, and said to the company: "Here you have the well, which stands for the tube; here the thick vapors, from which the invention of the lenses was taken; and here, finally, the strengthening of vision as the rays pass through a transparent medium that is denser and darker."

Sagredo: That's a way of containing everything knowable which is very like the way a block of marble contains within itself one beautiful statue — no, a thousand beautiful statues; the trick is knowing how to uncover them. Or we might say it's like the prophecies of Abbot Joachim, or the answers of the pagan oracles, which are understood only after the prophesied events have happened.

Salviati: And where do you leave the predictions of the horoscope-casters, which after the outcome can be seen so clearly in the birth-chart — or, as they say, the celestial figure?

Sagredo: It's in just this fashion that the alchemists, led on by their melancholy humor, discover that all the loftiest geniuses of the world never really wrote about anything except how to make gold — only, so as to say it without revealing it to the crowd, each went fancifully hiding it under a different cover. And it's the most delightful thing to hear their commentaries on the ancient poets, uncovering the weighty mysteries concealed under the fables: what the loves of the Moon really mean, and her coming down to Earth for Endymion, and her fury at Actaeon; and when Jupiter turns himself into a shower of gold, and when into burning flames; and what great secrets of the art lie in that Mercury the interpreter, in those abductions by Pluto, in those golden boughs.

Simplicio: I believe — indeed in part I know — that the world doesn't lack for thoroughly extravagant brains; but their follies shouldn't redound to the discredit of Aristotle, of whom it seems to me you sometimes speak with too little respect. His antiquity alone, and the great name he has won in the minds of so many distinguished men, should be enough to make him honorable in the eyes of all the learned.

Salviati: That's not how the matter runs, Simplicio. It is certain of his followers, too fainthearted by half, who give occasion — or rather, who would give occasion — for thinking less of him, if we chose to applaud their frivolities. Tell me, honestly: are you really so naive that you don't see that if Aristotle had been there to hear the doctor who wanted to make him the inventor of the telescope, he'd have been far angrier with him than with the people laughing at the doctor and his interpretations? Do you have any doubt that if Aristotle could see the new discoveries in the heavens, he would change his opinion, amend his books, move toward the doctrines that agree with the senses, and drive away from himself those pitifully small brains who too timidly let themselves be drawn into defending his every word — never grasping that if Aristotle were the man they imagine, he'd be an intractable brain, an obstinate mind, a spirit full of barbarity, a tyrant's will, someone who reckoned everyone else to be witless sheep and wanted his own decrees set above the senses, above experience, above nature herself? It is his followers who have given Aristotle his authority; he never usurped it or took it for himself. And since it's easier to hide behind another man's shield than to show one's own face in the open, they're afraid — they don't dare step one single pace away from him; and rather than admit any alteration in Aristotle's heaven, they insolently deny the alterations they can see with their own eyes in nature's heaven.

Sagredo: These people put me in mind of that sculptor who carved a great block of marble into the image of — Hercules, I think it was, or a thundering Jupiter — and gave it, by his marvelous craft, such vividness and ferocity that it frightened everyone who looked at it; and then he began to be afraid of it himself, though all its spirit and menace was the work of his own hands. The terror got such a grip on him that he no longer dared face it with his chisels and mallet.

Salviati: I have often marveled at how these strict upholders of Aristotle's every word can fail to notice what damage they do to his reputation and credit — how much, in trying to swell his authority, they diminish it. When I see them obstinately defending propositions that I can touch with my hand to be manifestly false, and trying to persuade me that this is what a true philosopher does, and that Aristotle himself would do the same, my confidence sinks that he reasoned rightly about the other conclusions that lie beyond my reach. Whereas if I saw them yield and change their minds before manifest truths, I'd be ready to believe that where they held firm, they might have solid demonstrations I hadn't understood or hadn't heard.

Sagredo: Or else — if they felt they'd be staking too much of their own reputation, and Aristotle's, by confessing they hadn't known some conclusion discovered by someone else — wouldn't it be the lesser evil to go find it among his texts, by piecing several of them together, according to the practice Simplicio has described to us? For if everything knowable is in there, then it must follow that everything can be found in there.

Salviati: Sagredo, don't make fun of this stratagem — I can see you propose it as a joke, but it's no laughing matter. Not long ago a philosopher of great name composed a book on the soul, in which, reporting Aristotle's opinion on whether or not it is immortal, he adduced many texts — not the ones cited by Alexander, because in those, he said, Aristotle wasn't even treating that subject, let alone settling anything about it — but others he had dug up himself in various out-of-the-way places, which leaned toward the dangerous reading. Warned that he'd have trouble getting the book licensed, he wrote back to his friend not to let that stop the approval going forward: if no other obstacle turned up, he'd have no difficulty at all in changing Aristotle's doctrine, and with different expositions and different texts he'd defend the contrary opinion — and it would still agree with the mind of Aristotle.

Sagredo: Oh, now this doctor can give me orders any day! He won't let Aristotle bamboozle him — he means to lead Aristotle by the nose and make him talk to order! See how much it matters to know how to seize the right moment. You don't go negotiating with Hercules while he's raging and in a fury, but while he's spinning tales among the Maeonian serving-girls. Ah, the unheard-of cowardice of servile minds! To make yourself a slave of your own free will; to accept another man's decrees as inviolable; to bind yourself to declare yourself persuaded and convinced by arguments so effective, so clearly conclusive, that the defenders themselves cannot decide whether they were even written on that subject, or whether they prove that conclusion at all! And here is the greater madness still: among themselves they are still in doubt whether the author himself held the affirmative side or the negative. What is this but making a wooden statue their oracle — running to it for answers, fearing it, revering it, adoring it?

Simplicio: But if we abandon Aristotle, who is to be our escort in philosophy? Name some author, if you can.

Salviati: You need an escort in unknown and savage country; in open, level places only the blind need a guide. Whoever is blind would do best to stay home; but whoever has eyes in his head and in his mind — those are the escort he should use. Not that I'm saying we shouldn't listen to Aristotle; on the contrary, I applaud reading him and studying him diligently. What I blame is handing yourself over to him as his property, so that you blindly countersign his every word and, without looking for any further reason, are bound to hold it as an inviolable decree. That is an abuse, and it drags another extreme disorder behind it: people stop trying to understand the force of his demonstrations at all. And what is more shameful than to hear, in public disputations about demonstrable conclusions, someone cut in from the sidelines with a text — often written on some entirely different subject — and shut his opponent's mouth with it? If you insist on going on studying this way, then put down the name of philosophers and call yourselves historians, or doctors of memory; for it isn't right that people who never philosophize should usurp the honorable title of philosopher. But we'd better pull back to shore, or we'll drift into an endless sea we couldn't get out of all day. So, Simplicio: come forward, please, with reasons and demonstrations — your own or Aristotle's — and not with texts and bare authorities, because our discussions are to be about the sensible world, and not about a world on paper. Yesterday's discourse hauled the Earth up out of the darkness and set it out under the open sky, showing that the wish to count it among what we call the celestial bodies is not a proposition so beaten and flattened that no breath of life is left in it. It follows, then, that we go on to examine how much probability there is in holding the Earth fixed and wholly immobile — I mean as to its entire globe — and how much likelihood in making it mobile with some motion, and of what kind. And since I'm undecided on this question, while Simplicio, along with Aristotle, stands resolute on the side of immobility, he shall bring out, step by step, the motives for their opinion; I'll bring the replies and the arguments for the contrary side; and Sagredo shall tell us the motions of his own mind, and which way he feels himself pulled.

Sagredo: That suits me very well — provided I also keep the freedom to bring up whatever plain natural common sense may dictate to me now and then.

Salviati: Indeed, that's the very thing I beg of you in particular. For of the easier — the material, so to speak — considerations, I think few have been left out by the writers on the subject, so that only some of the subtler and more remote ones can still be wanting or missing; and for hunting those down, what subtlety could be better suited than the wit of Sagredo, sharp and penetrating as it is?

Sagredo: I am whatever Salviati pleases. But please, let's not slide into yet another kind of digression — the ceremonial kind — because right now I'm a philosopher, and in the schoolroom, not at the Broglio, trading compliments.

Salviati: Then let our contemplation begin with this consideration: whatever motion may be attributed to the Earth, it is necessary that for us — as inhabitants of the Earth and therefore sharers in that motion — it remain completely imperceptible, as if it didn't exist, so long as we look only at terrestrial things; but on the other hand, it is just as necessary that this same motion appear to us as a motion shared in common by all the other bodies and visible objects which, being separated from the Earth, lack it. So the true method for investigating whether any motion can be attributed to the Earth, and if so, what it is, is to consider and observe whether in the bodies separated from the Earth we can detect some appearance of motion that belongs equally to all of them. Because a motion that showed up only in the Moon, say, and had nothing to do with Venus or Jupiter or the other stars, could not possibly belong to the Earth — or to anything but the Moon itself. Now, there is one motion that is the most general and the greatest of all: the one by which the Sun, the Moon, the other planets, and the fixed stars — in short, the whole universe, the Earth alone excepted — appear to us to move together from east to west in the space of twenty-four hours. And this motion, as far as this first appearance goes, might just as well belong to the Earth alone as to all the rest of the world with the Earth left out; for the same appearances would be seen in the one arrangement as in the other. That's why Aristotle and Ptolemy, who had grasped this point thoroughly, argue against no other motion than this daily one when they set out to prove the Earth immobile — except that Aristotle does touch briefly on a certain something against another motion attributed to the Earth by one of the ancients, which we'll speak of in its proper place.

Sagredo: I'm fully persuaded by the necessity with which your argument reaches its conclusion, but a doubt is stirring in me that I can't shake off. It's this: Copernicus assigns the Earth another motion besides the daily one. By the rule you've just laid down, that motion ought to be imperceptible to us here on the Earth, as far as appearances go, but visible in all the rest of the world. So it seems to me I can draw one necessary conclusion or the other: either Copernicus plainly blundered in assigning the Earth a motion whose general correspondence doesn't show up anywhere in the sky, or else, if the correspondence is there, then Ptolemy was just as much at fault for failing to refute this motion the way he refuted the other.

Salviati: A very reasonable doubt; and when we come to deal with that other motion, you'll see by how wide a margin Copernicus outstripped Ptolemy in sharpness and penetration of mind — for he saw what Ptolemy did not see, I mean the marvelous correspondence with which that motion is reflected in all the rest of the celestial bodies. But for now let's set that part aside and come back to our first consideration. Beginning with the most general points, I'll go on proposing the reasons that seem to favor the Earth's mobility, and then we'll hear the contrary ones from Simplicio. First: if we consider nothing but the immense bulk of the starry sphere, compared with the smallness of the terrestrial globe — which it contains many millions of times over — and then think of the speed of a motion that has to complete one entire revolution in a day and a night, I can't persuade myself that anyone could be found who thought it the more reasonable and credible thing that it's the celestial sphere doing the turning while the terrestrial globe stands still.

Sagredo: If, for the whole universe of effects that can depend in nature on motions like these, all the very same consequences followed, to a hair, from the one arrangement as from the other, then — as my first, general impression — I'd judge that anyone who considered it more reasonable to set the entire universe moving in order to keep the Earth still is more unreasonable than a man who climbed to the top of your great dome for no other purpose than to take in a view of the city and the countryside around it, and then demanded that the whole landscape be spun around him so he could be spared the trouble of turning his head. The conveniences to be drawn from that arrangement and not from this one would have to be many and great indeed to balance out, in my mind, and outweigh this absurdity, and so make the first more believable to me than the second. But perhaps Aristotle, Ptolemy, and Simplicio must find some advantages in it — and if there are any, it would be well to have them laid before us too; or else let it be shown to me how and why there are none, and can be none.

Salviati: For all the thought I've given it, I haven't been able to find any difference; and what's more, I think I've found that no difference can exist — so that I judge any further search for one to be in vain. So mark this: motion is motion, and does its work as motion, only in relation to things that lack it; among things that all share it equally, it does nothing — it's as if it weren't there at all. The goods a ship is loaded with move in the sense that, leaving Venice, they pass by Corfu, by Candia, by Cyprus, and go on to Aleppo — Venice, Corfu, Candia, and the rest staying behind, not moving with the ship. But for the bales, the crates, and the other bundles the ship is loaded and stowed with, and with respect to the ship itself, the motion from Venice to Syria is as good as nothing: it doesn't alter the relation among them in the least. And that's because the motion is common to them all, and shared equally by all. If, of all the cargo aboard, one bale should shift a single finger's breadth away from a crate, that alone would be, for the bale, a greater motion in relation to the crate than the two-thousand-mile voyage they made together.

Simplicio: That is good, solid doctrine, and Peripatetic through and through.

Salviati: I take it to be older than that; and I suspect that Aristotle, in taking it over from some good school, didn't penetrate it fully, and that having written it down in altered form, he became a source of confusion — thanks to those who insist on defending his every word. When he wrote that everything that moves, moves upon something immobile, I suspect he was garbling the statement that everything that moves, moves with respect to something immobile. That proposition suffers no difficulty at all; the other suffers many.

Sagredo: Please, let's not break the thread — carry on with the argument you began.

Salviati: It's clear, then, that a motion common to many moving bodies is idle, as good as null, with respect to the relation of those bodies among themselves, since among them nothing changes; it does its work only in the relation those bodies have with others that lack the motion — for with those, their arrangement does change. Now, we've divided the universe into two parts, one of which is necessarily movable and the other motionless. So as far as anything that can depend on such a motion is concerned, moving the Earth alone comes to exactly the same as moving all the rest of the world, since the whole operation of the motion lies in nothing but the relation between the celestial bodies and the Earth — and that relation alone is what changes. Now, if to obtain the very same effect ad unguem — to the last detail — it makes no difference whether the Earth alone moves while all the rest of the universe stays still, or the Earth alone stays still while the whole universe moves with one and the same motion, who is going to believe that nature — which, by common consent, does not employ many things to bring about what can be managed with few — chose to set an immense number of enormous bodies moving, at an inestimable speed, to achieve what could be had by the moderate motion of one single body turning around its own center?

Simplicio: I don't quite see how this vast motion can count as nothing for the Sun, for the Moon, for the other planets, and for the innumerable host of the fixed stars. How can you call it nothing that the Sun passes from one meridian to the next, climbs above this horizon, sinks below that one, brings now the day and now the night — and that the Moon, the other planets, and the fixed stars too go through the same variations?

Salviati: All these variations you list are nothing except in relation to the Earth. To see that this is true, just remove the Earth in your imagination: there is no longer any rising or setting of Sun or Moon left in the world, no horizons, no meridians, no days, no nights — in short, from that motion there never arises any change at all between the Moon and the Sun or any other stars you please, fixed or wandering. All the changes are relative to the Earth; and all of them, when you come down to it, amount to nothing more than the Sun showing itself first to China, then to Persia, afterwards to Egypt, to Greece, to France, to Spain, to America, and so on — with the Moon and the rest of the celestial bodies doing the same. And it all comes out in exactly the same way if, without dragging in so great a part of the universe, you simply let the terrestrial globe turn in place. But let's double the difficulty with another, enormous one. If you attribute this great motion to the heavens, you're forced of necessity to make it contrary to the particular motions of all the planetary orbs — each of which, beyond any dispute, has its own proper motion from west to east, and a gentle, moderate motion at that — and then to have them swept off the opposite way, from east to west, by this furiously rapid daily motion. Whereas if the Earth is made to turn on itself, the contrariety of motions vanishes, and the single motion from west to east accommodates every appearance and satisfies them all completely.

Simplicio: As for the contrariety of motions, that would matter little, because Aristotle demonstrates that circular motions are not contrary to one another, and that theirs cannot be called true contrariety.

Salviati: Does Aristotle demonstrate it, or does he merely say it because it suited a certain design of his? If contraries are, as he himself declares, things that destroy one another, I fail to see how two moving bodies that collide on a circular line should do each other any less harm than two that collide on a straight one.

Sagredo: Hold on a moment, please. Tell me, Simplicio: when two knights run at each other jousting in the open field, or when two whole squadrons, or two fleets at sea, drive into one another and smash and sink each other — would you call encounters like those contrary to one another?

Simplicio: Let's call them contrary.

Sagredo: Then how is it there's no contrariety in circular motions? These encounters, taking place on the surface of the earth or the water — which, as you know, are spherical — turn out to be circular motions. Do you know, Simplicio, which circular motions really aren't contrary to each other? Those of two circles that touch from the outside: turn one of them, and it naturally makes the other move the opposite way. But if one circle is inside the other, it's impossible for their motions, made in different directions, not to fight each other.

Salviati: Contrary or not contrary, these are quarrels over words; and I know that in fact it's a far simpler and more natural thing to be able to save everything with a single motion than to introduce two — and if you don't want to call them contrary, call them opposite. Nor am I putting this forward as something impossible, or claiming to draw a necessary demonstration from it — only a greater probability. And the improbability is compounded a third time by the way this arrangement disrupts, out of all proportion, the order that we see with certainty holding among those celestial bodies whose circling is not in doubt but absolutely settled. The order is this: the bigger the orb, the longer it takes to complete its revolution, and the smaller, the quicker. Thus Saturn, tracing a circle greater than any other planet's, completes it in thirty years; Jupiter goes around its smaller one in twelve years; Mars in two; the Moon runs through its far smaller circle in a single month. And just as plainly we see that of the Medicean Stars — Jupiter's four little moons — the one nearest Jupiter makes its revolution in a very short time, about forty-two hours; the next in three and a half days; the third in seven days; and the most remote in sixteen. And this thoroughly consistent pattern isn't altered in the least so long as the twenty-four-hour motion is assigned to the terrestrial globe turning on itself. But if you insist on keeping the Earth motionless, then after passing from the Moon's very brief period to the successively greater ones — to Mars's two years, from there to the greater sphere of Jupiter with its twelve, and from that to the still greater sphere of Saturn, whose period is thirty years — you must then leap across to another sphere incomparably bigger yet, and make it finish an entire revolution in twenty-four hours. And even that is the least of the disorder you'd be introducing; for if anyone wanted to pass from Saturn's sphere to the starry sphere, and make it as much bigger than Saturn's as its extremely slow motion of many thousands of years would proportionally require, then the leap from that one to a still bigger sphere — while making it swing clear around in twenty-four hours — would be out of proportion by far more.

And there's a fourth difficulty, which you must necessarily accept the moment the starry sphere is made movable: the immense disparity among the motions of the stars themselves. Some of them would be moving at tremendous speed in vast circles, others extremely slowly in the tiniest circles, according as each lies farther from or nearer to the poles. That has its own awkwardness — both because we see all the bodies whose motion is beyond doubt moving in great circles, and because it seems a poorly judged arrangement to station bodies that are meant to move circularly at immense distances from the center, and then have them move in the smallest circles of all.

And not only will the sizes of the circles, and consequently the speeds of motion, of these stars be utterly different from the circles and motions of those others, but — and this will be the fifth inconvenience — the very same stars keep changing their circles and their speeds. For the stars that two thousand years ago sat on the celestial equator, and so traced great circles with their motion, are found in our own day many degrees away from it; so they must have grown slower in motion and been reduced to moving in smaller circles. And it isn't even far from possible that a time will come when one of them — a star that till then had always moved — will reach the pole, join it, and stand still; and then, after resting for some while, will take up its motion again. Whereas the other stars, the ones whose motion is certain, all trace, as I said, the great circle of their orb and keep to it without change.

The improbability grows again — let it be the sixth inconvenience — for anyone who reasons carefully, in that no one can even conceive what the solidity of that vast sphere would have to be, in whose depths so many stars are riveted so firmly that, without shifting position among themselves in the slightest, they are carried around in concert despite so enormous a disparity of motions. Or, if the heavens are fluid — as it's far more reasonable to believe — so that each star goes wandering through them on its own, then what law will govern their motions, and to what end, so that, watched from the Earth, they appear to be moved by one single sphere? To accomplish that, it seems to me as much easier and more convenient to fix them motionless than to send them roaming, as it is easier to keep in their places the many stones set into a piazza's pavement than the swarms of boys who run around on top of them.

And finally, for the seventh objection: if we attribute the daily rotation to the highest heaven, we have to make it of such force and power that it carries along with it the innumerable multitude of the fixed stars — every one of them a vast body, far bigger than the Earth — and all the planetary spheres besides, even though both stars and planets by their own nature move the other way. And beyond that, we're forced to concede that the element of fire and the greater part of the air are swept along too, and that only the little globe of the Earth stands defiant and resistant to so much power. That, to me, seems very hard to swallow; nor could I understand how the Earth — a body hanging suspended and balanced on its center, indifferent to motion and to rest, placed in and surrounded by a fluid medium — should not yield as well and be carried around with everything else. But we meet no such obstacles in making the Earth move — a tiny body, imperceptible in comparison with the universe, and for that very reason incapable of doing it any violence.

Sagredo: I can feel certain notions whirling around in my imagination, stirred up in a jumble by the arguments we've been hearing; and if I'm going to attend properly to what's still to be said, I'd better see whether I can put them in order and draw out whatever substance is in them — if there turns out to be any. Perhaps proceeding by questions will help me explain myself more easily. So I ask Simplicio, first: does he believe that different motions can naturally belong to one and the same simple movable body, or that only one suits it — its own proper and natural motion?

Simplicio: For a simple movable body there can be only one motion that suits it naturally, and no more; all the others belong to it by accident and by participation. Just as, for a man strolling about a ship, his own proper motion is that of his walking, while by participation he has the motion that carries him into port — where he would never have arrived by strolling, if the ship's motion hadn't brought him there.

Sagredo: Tell me, second: that motion which is communicated by participation to some movable body, while the body itself is moving with another motion of its own, different from the participated one — must that motion reside in some subject of its own, or can it exist in nature with no other support?

Simplicio: Aristotle answers all these questions for you. He tells you that just as of one movable body there is one motion, so of one motion there is one movable body; and consequently that without inhering in its subject, no motion can exist or even be imagined.

Sagredo: Third, I'd like you to tell me whether you believe the Moon and the other planets and celestial bodies have motions of their own — and what those motions are.

Simplicio: They have them; and they are the motions by which they run through the zodiac — the Moon in a month, the Sun in a year, Mars in two, the starry sphere in all those thousands of years. These are their own proper and natural motions.

Sagredo: But that motion by which I see the fixed stars, and with them all the planets, travel together from east to west and return to the east in twenty-four hours — in what way does that belong to them?

Simplicio: They have it by participation.

Sagredo: Then it does not reside in them; and since it doesn't reside in them, and cannot exist without some subject in which it does reside, it must be made the proper and natural motion of some other sphere.

Simplicio: On this account the astronomers and philosophers have found another, highest sphere, without any stars, to which the daily rotation naturally belongs; and they have called it the primum mobile — the "first movable" — which then sweeps all the lower spheres along with it, contributing its own motion to them and letting them share in it.

Sagredo: But when — without introducing other unknown and enormous spheres, without other motions or sweepings-along by participation, leaving to every sphere its own single, simple motion, without mixing contrary motions, but having them all run the same way, as they must if they all depend on a single principle — everything proceeds and answers in the most perfect harmony, why refuse that option, and give your assent to such strange and labored conditions?

Simplicio: The whole point lies in finding this way of yours, so simple and so ready.

Sagredo: The way looks to me found already. Make the Earth the primum mobile — that is, make it turn on itself in twenty-four hours, and in the same direction as all the other spheres — and, without this motion being shared with any other planet or star, they will all have their risings, their settings, and in short every other appearance.

Simplicio: The trick is being able to move it without a thousand inconveniences.

Salviati: All the inconveniences will be removed as fast as you propose them. What's been said so far amounts only to the first and most general motives for holding it not altogether improbable that the daily rotation belongs to the Earth rather than to all the rest of the universe. I don't offer them to you as unbreakable laws, but as motives that carry some appearance of reason. And since I understand perfectly well that a single experiment or one conclusive demonstration to the contrary would be enough to knock these and a hundred thousand other probable arguments flat, we mustn't stop here, but press on and hear what Simplicio answers, and what better probabilities or firmer reasons he brings against us.

Simplicio: First I'll say something in general about all these considerations taken together, and then I'll come to some particulars. It seems to me that you build everything on the greater simplicity and ease of producing the same effects: you judge that, as far as causing them goes, moving the Earth alone comes to the same as moving all the rest of the world except the Earth, but that as far as the doing of it goes, you count the first far easier than the second. To that I answer that it seems the same to me too — so long as I measure by my own power, which is not merely finite but utterly feeble. But measured against the power of the Mover, which is infinite, it is no less easy to move the universe than the Earth — or a straw, for that matter. And if the power is infinite, why should not a great part of it be exercised rather than a tiny one? So it seems to me that the argument, in its general form, has no force.

Salviati: If I had ever said that the universe does not move for lack of power in its Mover, I would have been wrong, and your correction would be well aimed; and I grant you that for an infinite power it is as easy to move a hundred thousand bodies as to move one. But what I said had no reference to the Mover — only to the moving bodies, and in them not just to their resistance, which is without doubt smaller in the Earth than in the whole universe, but to the many other particulars we were considering just now. As for the claim that it is better for an infinite power to exercise a great part of itself rather than a tiny one, I answer that no part of the infinite is greater than another, so long as both are finite; nor can one say that a hundred thousand is a greater part of an infinite number than two is, even though a hundred thousand is fifty thousand times bigger than two. And if moving the universe required a finite power — however enormous compared with what would suffice to move the Earth alone — the part of the infinite employed would be no greater for that, nor would what remained idle be any less than infinite. So applying a little more or a little less power to one particular effect matters not at all; besides which, the operation of such a power does not stop and end at the daily rotation alone — there are plenty of other motions in the world that we know of, and there may be many more unknown to us. Looking, then, at the moving bodies; not doubting that it is a shorter and readier operation to move the Earth than the universe; and keeping an eye, moreover, on the many other savings and simplifications that this one choice secures — a very true axiom of Aristotle, which teaches us that frustra fit per plura quod potest fieri per pauciora — it is pointless to do with more what can be done with less — makes it more probable that the daily rotation belongs to the Earth alone than to the whole universe minus the Earth.

Simplicio: In citing the axiom you have left out a clause that is the whole point, especially in the present case. The phrase omitted is æque bene — "equally well." So we must examine whether the two assumptions can satisfy everything equally well.

Salviati: Whether both positions satisfy equally well will emerge from the particular examination of the appearances they have to satisfy; for so far we have argued, and shall go on arguing, ex hypothesi — as a supposition — assuming that as far as satisfying the appearances goes, the two positions are equally serviceable. As for the phrase you say I left out, I rather suspect you have superfluously added it. "Equally well" is a relation, and a relation necessarily requires at least two terms — a thing cannot stand in relation to itself; one doesn't say, for example, that rest is equally as good as rest. And when we say "it is done in vain by more means what can be done by fewer," we understand that the thing to be done must be the same thing, not two different things; and since the same thing cannot be said to be done equally as well as its very self, the addition of the phrase "equally well" is superfluous — a relation with only one term.

Sagredo: If we don't want the same thing to happen to us as yesterday, please, let's get back to the matter at hand; and let Simplicio begin producing the difficulties that seem to him to tell against this new arrangement of the world.

Simplicio: The arrangement is not new — on the contrary, it is very ancient; and the proof is that Aristotle refutes it, and his refutations are these. "First: if the Earth moved — whether in itself, staying at the center, or in a circle, being outside the center — that motion would necessarily be violent, for it is not her natural motion; if it were hers, every one of her particles would share it, but each of them moves in a straight line toward the center. The motion being therefore violent and preternatural, it could not be everlasting; but the order of the world is everlasting; therefore, and so on. Second: all the other bodies that move circularly appear to lag behind and to move with more than one motion — excepting only the primum mobile, the outermost sphere that carries all the rest. On that account the Earth too would have to move with two motions; and if that were so, of necessity changes would have to show up in the fixed stars — which is not seen; rather, without any variation, the same stars always rise from the same places and set in the same. Third: the motion of the parts and of the whole is naturally toward the center of the universe, and for that reason too the whole rests there." He then raises the question whether the motion of the parts is aimed naturally at the center of the universe or at the center of the Earth, and concludes that its own proper instinct is to go toward the center of the universe, and only per accidens toward the center of the Earth — a doubt we discussed at length yesterday. Finally he confirms the same point with a fourth argument, drawn from the experience of heavy bodies: falling from high to low, they come down plumb onto the surface of the Earth; and likewise projectiles thrown plumb upward return plumb down along the same lines, however immense the height to which they were thrown — arguments that conclude of necessity that their motion is toward the center of the Earth, which, without moving at all, waits for them and receives them. Last of all he notes that the astronomers produce further reasons in confirmation of the same conclusions — I mean, that the Earth is at the center of the universe and immobile — and he cites just one of them: that all the appearances seen in the movements of the stars answer to the Earth's position at the center, a correspondence that would fail if she were not there. The other reasons, produced by Ptolemy and by other astronomers, I can bring forward now, if you like, or after you've said whatever occurs to you in reply to these of Aristotle's.

Salviati: The arguments produced in this matter are of two kinds: some have regard to earthly happenings, without any relation to the stars, and others are drawn from the appearances and observations of things celestial. Aristotle's arguments are mostly drawn from the things around us, and he leaves the others to the astronomers; so it will be well, if you agree, to examine the ones taken from the Earth's own experiences, and then come to the other kind. And since Ptolemy, Tycho, and other astronomers and philosophers — besides taking up, confirming, and fortifying Aristotle's arguments — have produced others of their own, we may as well gather them all together, so as not to have to give the same or similar answers twice. So, Simplicio: would you like to recite them, or shall I relieve you of the trouble? I am happy to oblige either way.

Simplicio: Better that you present them: having studied them more, you'll have them readier to hand, and in greater number too.

Salviati: As the strongest reason of all, everyone produces the argument of heavy bodies: falling from high to low, they come down along a straight line perpendicular to the surface of the Earth — an argument held to be irrefutable proof that the Earth stands still. For if she had the daily rotation, then a tower from whose top a stone was dropped would be carried along by the Earth's spin, and in the time the stone consumed in its fall, the tower would race many hundreds of braccia toward the east — and the stone ought to strike the ground that far from the tower's base. They back this effect up with another experiment: dropping a lead ball from the top of the mast of a ship standing still, and marking where it hits — which is near the foot of the mast; but if the same ball is dropped from the same place while the ship is under way, its impact will land as far from the first mark as the ship has run forward during the time of the lead's fall — and this for no other reason than that the natural motion of the ball, once set free, is in a straight line toward the center of the Earth. They fortify the argument with the experience of a projectile launched upward to a very great height — say, a ball fired from a cannon aimed plumb vertical above the horizon. In its climb and return the ball consumes so much time that, at our latitude, the cannon and we along with it would be carried many miles eastward by the Earth, so that the ball, falling, could never come back near the gun, but would land as far to the west as the Earth had run on ahead. They add, further, a third and very effective experience, which is this: if you fire a culverin — a long cannon — at full range toward the east, and then another, with an equal charge and at the same elevation, toward the west, the westward shot would carry vastly farther than the eastward one. For while the ball travels west and the gun, carried by the Earth, travels east, the ball would strike the ground at a distance from the gun equal to the sum of the two journeys — the one made by the ball itself toward the west, the other by the gun, carried by the Earth, toward the east; and conversely, from the journey of the ball fired eastward you would have to subtract the journey the gun made in following it. Suppose, for example, that the ball's own journey is five miles, and that at that latitude the Earth runs three miles during the ball's flight: in the westward shot, the ball would fall to earth eight miles from the gun — its own five to the west plus the gun's three to the east; but the eastward shot would carry no farther than two miles, which is what remains when you subtract the gun's three miles of travel in the same direction from the shot's five. But experience shows the shots to be equal; therefore the gun stands immobile, and consequently so does the Earth. And no less than these, shots toward the south or the north likewise confirm the Earth's stability: you would never hit the mark you had aimed at, for the target, carried by the Earth toward the east while the ball is in the air, would slide away, and the shots would always land slanting off to the west. Nor is it only shots along the meridian lines: even those fired east or west would fail to fly true — the eastward ones would run high and the westward ones low, whenever you fired point-blank, dead level. For since the ball's journey in both shots is made along the tangent — that is, along a line parallel to the horizon — and since, if the daily rotation belongs to the Earth, the horizon keeps dropping away toward the east and rising in the west (which is why the eastern stars appear to us to climb and the western ones to sink), the eastern target would keep dropping below the line of the shot, so the shot would run high; and the rising of the western target would make the westward shot run low. So you could never fire true in any direction; and since experience says the opposite, one is forced to say the Earth stands still.

Simplicio: Oh, now these are real arguments — arguments to which it is impossible to find any answer worth giving.

Salviati: They come to you as new, perhaps?

Simplicio: Truly they do. And now I see with how many beautiful experiments nature has been kind enough to help us come to knowledge of the truth. Oh, how well one truth chimes with another, and how they all conspire together to make themselves unassailable!

Sagredo: What a pity there was no artillery in Aristotle's day! He'd have stormed ignorance with it, and spoken of the things of this world without the slightest hesitation.

Salviati: I'm very glad these arguments have come to you as new — so that you won't remain of the opinion of most of the Peripatetics, who believe that if anyone departs from Aristotle's doctrine, it must be because he never understood or properly penetrated his demonstrations. But you will certainly hear other novelties yet: you'll hear the followers of the new system produce, against their own case, observations, experiments, and arguments of far greater force than those produced by Aristotle and Ptolemy and the other assailants of the same conclusions; and so you'll come to be certain that it was not out of ignorance or inexperience that they were led to follow this opinion.

Sagredo: This is the moment for me to tell you a few things that have happened to me since I first began hearing this opinion talked about. When I was quite a young man, having only just finished my course of philosophy — which I then set aside, having applied myself to other occupations — it happened that a certain northerner from Rostock, whose name I believe was Christian Wursteisen, a follower of Copernicus's opinion, turned up in these parts and gave two or three lectures on the subject at an academy, with a crowd in attendance — drawn, I think, more by the novelty of the subject than by anything else. I didn't go, having formed the fixed impression that this opinion could be nothing but solemn madness. When I later questioned some who had been there, I heard them all making fun of it — except one, who told me the business was not entirely ridiculous. And since I considered him a thoroughly intelligent and very cautious man, I regretted not having gone; and from that time on, whenever I ran into anyone who held the Copernican opinion, I would ask him whether he had always been of that view. Among all the many I've questioned, I have not found a single one who didn't tell me he had long been of the contrary opinion, and had come over to this one moved by the force of the reasons that argue for it. Then, testing them one by one to see how well they had mastered the reasons of the other side, I found every one of them with those reasons at his fingertips — so I truly cannot say they threw themselves into this opinion out of ignorance, or vanity, or to play, as they say, the clever wit. On the other hand, of all the Peripatetics and Ptolemaics I have asked — and out of curiosity I've asked a good many — what study they had made of Copernicus's book, I found very few who had so much as seen it, and of those I believe actually understood it, none. And among the followers of the Peripatetic doctrine, I've likewise tried to learn whether any of them ever held the other opinion — and likewise found none. Whereupon, considering that everyone who follows Copernicus's opinion first held the contrary one and is perfectly well informed of the reasons of Aristotle and Ptolemy, while on the other hand not one follower of Ptolemy and Aristotle ever formerly held Copernicus's opinion and abandoned it to come over to Aristotle's — considering these things, I say, I began to believe that a man who leaves an opinion drunk in with his mother's milk and followed by countless others, to come over to one held by a bare handful, denied by all the schools, and looking, truly, like an enormous paradox, must of necessity have been moved — not to say forced — by more powerful reasons. And so I have become curious in the extreme to get, as they say, to the bottom of this business; and I count it my great good fortune to have met the two of you, from whom, without any labor of my own, I can hear everything that has been said — and perhaps everything that can be said — on this matter, confident that by the power of your reasonings I shall be pulled out of doubt and set in a state of certainty.

Simplicio: Provided the opinion and the hope don't fail you, and you don't find yourself, at the end, more confused than before.

Sagredo: I feel quite sure that cannot happen in any way whatsoever.

Simplicio: And why not? I am a good witness in my own case: the further we go, the more confused I get.

Sagredo: That is a sign that the reasons which until now seemed conclusive to you, and kept you secure in the truth of your opinion, are beginning to change their look in your mind, and to let you slip little by little — if not all the way over to the contrary opinion, at least into leaning toward it. But I, who am and have been until now indifferent, have great confidence that I shall come to rest and to certainty; and you yourself won't deny it, if you'll hear what persuades me to hope so.

Simplicio: I'll hear it gladly — and I'd be no less pleased if it worked the same effect on me.

Sagredo: Then favor me by answering my questions. First, tell me, Simplicio: isn't the conclusion we are trying to settle precisely this — whether we should hold, with Aristotle and Ptolemy, that the Earth alone stands still at the center of the universe while all the celestial bodies move; or whether, the starry sphere standing still with the Sun at its center, the Earth stands outside it, and the motions that appear to belong to the Sun and the fixed stars are really hers?

Simplicio: These are the conclusions in dispute.

Sagredo: And aren't these two conclusions such that, of necessity, one must be true and the other false?

Simplicio: Just so. We stand in a dilemma, one side of which must of necessity be true and the other false; for between motion and rest, which are contradictories, there is no third possibility — no one can say, "The Earth neither moves nor stands still; the Sun and the stars neither move nor stand still."

Sagredo: The Earth, the Sun, and the stars — what are they in nature? Trifling things, or considerable ones?

Simplicio: They are bodies of the very first rank — most noble, integral parts of the universe, vast, and considerable in the highest degree.

Sagredo: And motion and rest — what sort of conditions are they in nature?

Simplicio: So great and so fundamental that nature herself is defined by them.

Sagredo: So that to move eternally, and to be wholly immobile, are two very considerable conditions in nature, pointing to an enormous difference — above all when attributed to the principal bodies of the universe — from which only the most dissimilar consequences can follow.

Simplicio: Certainly so.

Sagredo: Now answer me on another point. Do you believe that in dialectic, in rhetoric, in physics, in metaphysics, in mathematics — in the whole universe of reasoned argument, in short — there are arguments powerful enough to persuade and demonstrate false conclusions no less than true ones?

Simplicio: No, sir. On the contrary, I hold it firm and certain that for the proof of a true and necessary conclusion there exist in nature not one but many most powerful demonstrations; that one can argue around it and turn it over with thousands upon thousands of cross-checks without ever running into any inconsistency; and that the more some sophist tried to muddy it, the clearer its certainty would always become. And that, on the opposite side, to make a false proposition look true and to persuade people of it, nothing can be produced but fallacies, sophisms, paralogisms, equivocations, and arguments that are empty, incoherent, and riddled with inconsistencies and contradictions.

Sagredo: Now then: if eternal motion and eternal rest are such fundamental conditions in nature, and so different that only the most different consequences can depend on them — above all when applied to the Sun and the Earth, bodies so vast and so eminent in the universe; and if, moreover, it is impossible that one of two contradictory propositions should fail to be true and the other false; and if for proofs of the false one nothing can be produced but fallacies, while the true one can be argued by every kind of conclusive and demonstrative reason — then how can you suppose that whichever of you takes up the defense of the true proposition will fail to persuade me? I would have to be dull of wit, warped in judgment, thick of mind and intellect, and blind in reasoning, not to tell light from darkness, gems from coals, the true from the false.

Simplicio: I tell you, as I have told you before: the greatest master for teaching us to recognize sophisms, paralogisms, and every other fallacy was Aristotle, who in this department can never be deceived.

Sagredo: There you go again with Aristotle, who cannot speak; and I tell you that if Aristotle were here, he would either be persuaded by us, or he would untie our arguments and persuade us with better ones. But come — when you heard the artillery experiments recited, didn't you yourself recognize them, admire them, and confess them more conclusive than Aristotle's? And yet I don't hear Salviati, who produced them and who has surely examined them and sounded them to the bottom with the utmost care, confessing himself persuaded by them — nor even by others of still greater force that he hints he is going to let us hear. And I don't know on what grounds you would scold nature, as though she had grown senile with age and forgotten how to produce speculative minds, and could turn out none anymore except the sort who make themselves Aristotle's slaves, thinking with his brain and sensing with his senses. But let us hear the rest of the arguments favorable to his opinion, so that we can then bring them to the test — refining them in the crucible and weighing them on the assayer's balance.

Salviati: Before we go any further, I have to tell Sagredo that in these discussions of ours I'm playing the Copernican — wearing him, almost, as an actor wears a mask. But as for what these arguments I seem to produce in his favor have actually worked in me, inwardly, I don't want you to judge that from my talk while we're in the heat of performing the play, but after I've taken off the costume — for you may find me a different man from the one you see on stage. Now let's move ahead. Ptolemy and his followers produce another experience, similar to the one about projectiles, drawn from the things that hang in the air a long while, detached from the Earth — the clouds, and birds on the wing. Since one can't say the clouds are carried along by the Earth, not being attached to her, it doesn't seem possible that they could keep up with her speed; rather, it ought to look to us as if they were all racing westward at tremendous velocity. And if we, carried by the Earth, run through our whole parallel in twenty-four hours — and that's at least sixteen thousand miles — how could the birds keep pace with such a course? Whereas in fact we see them fly east just as readily as west, or in any direction whatever, without the least perceptible difference. Besides that: if, when we're galloping on horseback, we feel the air strike our faces pretty briskly, what a wind we ought to feel blowing perpetually out of the east, carried as we'd be on so rapid a course against the air! And yet nothing of the kind is felt at all. Then there's another very ingenious argument, taken from a certain experience, and it goes like this. Circular motion has the power to extrude — to fling off, scatter, and drive away from its center the parts of the moving body, whenever the motion is not quite slow or the parts are not fastened together very solidly. So if, for example, we set one of those great wheels spinning at top speed — the kind inside which one or two men walk in order to move enormous weights, like the massive stones of a mangle-press, or loaded boats that get dragged overland from one waterway to another — then unless the parts of that rapidly whirling wheel were bound together more than solidly, they would all fly apart; and however tenaciously stones or other heavy stuff might be fastened to its outer surface, they couldn't withstand the impetus, which would hurl them with great violence in every direction, far from the wheel and, in consequence, from its center. If the Earth, then, were spinning with so much greater a speed — so very much greater — what weight, what tenacity of mortar or cement, would hold the rocks, the buildings, whole cities, and keep so headlong a whirl from launching them into the sky? And men and beasts, who aren't attached to the Earth at all — how would they resist so great an impetus? Whereas, on the contrary, we see them, and far feebler resistances too — little stones, sand, leaves — resting on the Earth in perfect quiet, and settling back down onto her when they fall, even with the slowest of motions. There, Simplicio, are the most powerful arguments taken, so to speak, from terrestrial things. There remain those of the other kind — the ones that concern celestial appearances — arguments which really aim more at proving that the Earth is at the center of the universe, and hence at stripping her of the annual motion around it that Copernicus assigns her. Being of somewhat different material, they can be brought out after we've weighed the strength of the ones proposed so far.

Sagredo: What do you say, Simplicio? Does it seem to you that Salviati possesses the Ptolemaic and Aristotelian arguments, and knows how to lay them out? Do you think any Peripatetic is as much a master of the Copernican demonstrations?

Simplicio: If it weren't for the high opinion I've formed, from our conversations so far, of Salviati's solid learning and Sagredo's sharpness of wit, I would — with their kind leave — take myself off without hearing another word, since it seems impossible to me that such palpable experiences can be contradicted; and I'd want to rest in my old opinion without listening further, because it seems to me that even if that opinion were false, being propped on such plausible reasons would make it excusable. And if these are fallacies, what true demonstrations were ever so beautiful?

Sagredo: All the more reason, then, that we should hear Salviati's replies. If they are true, they must be more beautiful still — infinitely more beautiful — and those arguments must be ugly, ugly in the extreme, if that metaphysical proposition holds which says the true and the beautiful are one and the same thing, as are the false and the ugly. So, Salviati, let's lose no more time.

Salviati: The first argument Simplicio produced, if I remember rightly, was this: the Earth cannot move in a circle, because such motion would be violent for her, and therefore not perpetual; and the reason it would be violent was that if it were natural, her parts would also move naturally in a circle — which is impossible, because the natural motion of the parts is straight downward. To this I answer that I'd have been glad if Aristotle had made himself a little clearer when he said "the parts would also move circularly," because this moving-in-a-circle can be understood in two ways. One is that every particle separated from its whole would move circularly around its own center, tracing out its own little circlets; the other is that as the whole globe turns around its center in twenty-four hours, the parts also circle that same center in twenty-four hours. The first would be an absurdity no smaller than saying that every part of a circle's circumference must itself be a circle, or that because the Earth is spherical, every piece of earth must be a ball — because that's what the axiom eadem est ratio totius et partium — whole and parts follow the same rule — supposedly demands. But if he meant it the other way — that the parts, imitating the whole, would naturally move around the center of the whole globe in twenty-four hours — then I say that is exactly what they do; and it will fall to you, standing in for Aristotle, to prove that they don't.

Simplicio: Aristotle proves it in that same passage, when he says that the natural motion of the parts is the straight motion toward the center of the universe, so that circular motion cannot naturally belong to them.

Salviati: But don't you see that those very words contain the refutation of this answer too?

Simplicio: How? And where?

Salviati: Doesn't he say that circular motion would be violent for the Earth, and therefore not eternal? And that this is absurd, because the order of the world is eternal?

Simplicio: He says so.

Salviati: But if what is violent cannot be eternal, then, turning it around, what cannot be eternal cannot be natural. Now the Earth's downward motion cannot possibly be eternal; therefore still less can it be natural — nor can any motion be natural to her that isn't also eternal. But if we make the Earth mobile with circular motion, that motion can be eternal for her and for her parts, and therefore natural.

Simplicio: Straight motion is supremely natural to the parts of the Earth, and it is eternal; it will never happen that they fail to move with straight motion — always understanding, of course, that impediments are removed.

Salviati: You're equivocating, Simplicio, and I want to see if I can free you from the equivocation. So tell me: do you believe that a ship sailing from the Strait of Gibraltar toward Palestine could sail toward that shore eternally, moving always at the same steady pace?

Simplicio: Certainly not.

Salviati: And why not?

Simplicio: Because that voyage is confined and bounded between the Pillars of Hercules and the coast of Palestine; and the distance being bounded, it's crossed in a finite time — unless someone chose to turn back with a contrary motion and then repeat the same trip over again; but that would be an interrupted motion, not a continuous one.

Salviati: A perfectly true answer. But the voyage from the Strait of Magellan across the Pacific, through the Moluccas, around the Cape of Good Hope, and from there through the same strait again and across the Pacific once more, and so on — do you believe that voyage could be kept up forever?

Simplicio: It could; for this being a circuit that returns into itself, by repeating it infinitely many times it could be perpetuated without any interruption.

Salviati: So on this route a ship could keep on sailing eternally.

Simplicio: It could, provided the ship were incorruptible; but with the ship falling apart, the voyage would of necessity come to an end.

Salviati: But in the Mediterranean, even if the ship were incorruptible, it still couldn't move perpetually toward Palestine, since that voyage is bounded. So two things are required for a moving body to move eternally without pause: one, that the motion be, by its own nature, unbounded and infinite; the other, that the moving body be likewise incorruptible and eternal.

Simplicio: All of that is necessary.

Salviati: Then you've already confessed, out of your own mouth, that it's impossible for any moving body to move eternally in a straight line — since straight motion, whether you take it upward or downward, is by your own account bounded by the circumference and by the center. So even if the moving body — the Earth — is eternal, still, since straight motion is not by its nature eternal but bounded through and through, it cannot naturally belong to the Earth. Indeed, as was said just yesterday, Aristotle himself is forced to make the terrestrial globe eternally stationary. And when you say that the Earth's parts will always move downward, impediments being removed, you equivocate mightily; because, on the contrary, they have to be impeded, opposed, and forced if you want them to move at all. Once they've fallen, they must be violently thrown back up in order to fall a second time. And as for impediments, these only prevent their reaching the center; for if there were a well passing clear beyond the center, even then no clod of earth would move past that point — except insofar as, carried by its impetus, it overshot it, only to come back and finally settle there. So as for maintaining that motion along a straight line belongs, or can belong, naturally either to the Earth or to any other moving body while the universe keeps its perfect order — give that up entirely, and (unless you're willing to grant her circular motion) put all your strength into maintaining and defending her immobility.

Simplicio: As for the immobility, it seems to me that Aristotle's arguments, and even more the others you've produced, establish it necessarily so far; and it will take great things, in my judgment, to refute them.

Salviati: Then let's come to the second argument, which was this: those bodies of which we're certain that they move circularly all have more than one motion — except the primum mobile, the first and outermost sphere. So if the Earth moved circularly, she would have to move with two motions, from which changes in the risings and settings of the fixed stars would have to follow; but no such thing is seen to follow; therefore, and so on. The simplest and most fitting answer to this objection lies in the argument itself; Aristotle himself puts it into our mouths — and it cannot be, Simplicio, that you haven't seen it.

Simplicio: I haven't seen it, and I still don't.

Salviati: That can't be, because it's too plainly there.

Simplicio: With your permission, I'd like to take a look at the text.

Sagredo: We'll have the text brought in this instant.

Simplicio: I always carry it in my pocket. Here it is — and I know the exact place: the second book of On the Heavens, chapter 14. Here we are, text 97: Praeterea, omnia quae feruntur latione circulari, subdeficere videntur — "Moreover, all bodies carried with circular motion are seen to lag behind, and to move with more than one motion, except the first sphere; hence the Earth too, whether she moves around the center or is placed at the center, must move with two motions. But if that happened, there would necessarily be shifts and turnings among the fixed stars — which is not seen to happen; rather, they always rise and set at the same places." Now here I see no fallacy whatever, and the argument strikes me as absolutely conclusive.
