Salviati: And to me this fresh reading has confirmed the fallacy in the reasoning — and uncovered another falsehood besides. So pay attention. There are two positions, or let's say two conclusions, that Aristotle wants to attack. One belongs to those who, placing the Earth at the center, make her turn upon herself, around her own center; the other to those who, setting her far from the center, make her travel with circular motion around that center. And he attacks both of these positions jointly, with the same argument. Now I say he errs in each attack: his error against the first position is an equivocation, a paralogism, and against the second it's a false consequence. Let's come to the first position — the one that puts the Earth at the center and makes her turn upon herself around her own center — and confront it with Aristotle's objection, which runs: all movable bodies that move circularly seem to lag behind, and move with more than one motion, except the first sphere (that is, the primum mobile); therefore the Earth, moving around her own center while placed at the center, must move with two motions and must lag behind; but if that were so, the risings and settings of the fixed stars would have to vary, which is not seen to happen; therefore the Earth does not move, and so on. Here is the paralogism; and to expose it, I argue with Aristotle like this. You say, Aristotle, that the Earth placed at the center cannot move upon herself, because one would then have to assign her two motions. So if it were not necessary to assign her more than a single motion, you would not hold it impossible for her to move with that single one — since it would have been beside the point to locate the impossibility in the plurality of motions if she couldn't move even with one alone. Now of all the moving bodies in the world, you make exactly one move with a single motion, and all the rest with more than one; and you declare that this body is the first sphere — the one by which all the stars, fixed and wandering alike, appear to us to move in concert from east to west. Then if the Earth could be that first sphere, moving with a single motion that made the stars appear to move from east to west, you wouldn't deny it to her. But whoever says that the Earth, placed at the center, turns upon herself assigns her no motion other than the very one by which all the stars appear to move from east to west; and so she turns out to be that very first sphere which you yourself concede moves with a single motion. So, Aristotle, if you want to conclude anything, you must demonstrate that the Earth placed at the center cannot move even with a single motion — or else that not even the first sphere can have a single movement. Otherwise you commit the fallacy inside your own syllogism and put it on open display, denying and granting the same thing at once. I come now to the second position, that of those who, placing the Earth far from the center, make her mobile around it — who make her, that is, a planet, a wandering star. Against this position the argument does have force, and as to its form it is conclusive; but it fails in its matter. For grant that the Earth does move in that fashion, and moves with two motions: it still does not follow of necessity that changes must then occur in the risings and settings of the fixed stars, as I'll explain in its proper place. And here I'm quite willing to excuse Aristotle's error — indeed, I want to praise him for having brought forward the subtlest argument against Copernicus's position that can be brought. And if the objection is acute and, in appearance, utterly conclusive, you'll find the solution that much more subtle and ingenious — one that could not have been found by a mind less acute than Copernicus's; and from the difficulty of understanding it you may gauge the far greater difficulty of finding it in the first place. For now let's leave the answer hanging; you'll hear it at its proper place and time, after we've restated this same objection of Aristotle's and, what's more, fortified it considerably in his favor. Now let's pass to the third argument, also Aristotle's, about which there's no need to say anything further, since it was answered well enough between yesterday and today: he repeats that the motion of heavy bodies is naturally along a straight line toward the center, then inquires whether toward the center of the Earth or the center of the universe, and concludes that it is naturally toward the center of the universe, and per accidens toward that of the Earth. So we can pass to the fourth, on which we'll need to dwell a good while, because it's founded on the very experience from which most of the remaining arguments then draw their force. Aristotle says, then, that a most certain proof of the Earth's immobility is that we see projectiles shot straight up return along the same line to the same place they were fired from — and this even when the motion reaches a very great height. This could not happen if the Earth moved: for during the time the projectile is moving up and down, separated from the Earth, the place where its motion began would slide a long stretch eastward, thanks to the Earth's turning, and in falling the projectile would strike the ground that far away from the said place. Here, then, we can fit in the argument of the ball fired straight up from a cannon, and likewise the other one, used by Aristotle and by Ptolemy, of seeing heavy bodies fall from great heights along a straight line, perpendicular to the Earth's surface. Now, to begin untying these knots, I ask Simplicio: if someone denied to Ptolemy and Aristotle that heavy bodies, falling freely from a height, come down by a straight and perpendicular line — that is, one aimed at the center — by what means would they prove it?

Simplicio: By means of the senses: which assure us that that tower is straight and plumb, and show us the stone, as it falls, grazing along it without leaning a hair's breadth to one side or the other, and striking at the tower's foot exactly below the spot it was dropped from.

Salviati: But if it happened that the terrestrial globe were turning — and consequently carrying the tower along with it — and the stone in falling were still seen to graze the tower's edge, what would its motion then have to be?

Simplicio: In that case one would rather have to say "its motions": for there would be one by which it came down from top to bottom, and it would need another in order to follow the tower's course.

Salviati: Its motion, then, would be a compound of two: the one by which it measures the tower, and the other by which it follows it. And from this compound it would result that the stone no longer traces that simple straight and perpendicular line, but a slanting one — and perhaps not straight at all.

Simplicio: Whether it wouldn't be straight, I can't say; but I understand well that of necessity it would be slanting, and different from the other, straight perpendicular line it traced when the Earth stood still.

Salviati: Therefore, from merely seeing the falling stone graze the tower, you cannot affirm with certainty that it traces a straight and perpendicular line — unless you first suppose that the Earth stands still.

Simplicio: Just so; because if the Earth were moving, the stone's motion would be slanting, not perpendicular.

Salviati: There, then, is the paralogism of Aristotle and Ptolemy, plain and evident — and uncovered by you yourself: the very thing to be demonstrated is assumed as already known.

Simplicio: How so? To me it looks like a syllogism in proper form, not a begging of the question.

Salviati: Here's how. Tell me: in a demonstration, isn't the conclusion set down as unknown?

Simplicio: Unknown — otherwise demonstrating it would be superfluous.

Salviati: But the middle term — mustn't that be known?

Simplicio: Necessarily; otherwise it would be an attempt to prove ignotum per aeque ignotum — the unknown by the equally unknown.

Salviati: Our conclusion to be proved, the unknown one — isn't that the stability of the Earth?

Simplicio: It is.

Salviati: And the middle term, which has to be known — isn't that the straight and perpendicular fall of the stone?

Simplicio: That is the middle term.

Salviati: But wasn't it concluded only a moment ago that we can have no knowledge that the fall is straight and perpendicular unless we first know that the Earth stands still? So in your syllogism the certainty of the middle term is drawn from the uncertainty of the conclusion. You see, then, what the paralogism is — and how big.

Sagredo: For Simplicio's sake I'd like to defend Aristotle, if it can be done — or at least come away with a better grasp of the force of your inference. You say: seeing the stone graze the tower isn't enough to assure us that its motion is vertical — which is the middle term of the syllogism — unless one assumes the Earth stands still, which is the very conclusion to be proved; because if the tower were moving along with the Earth while the stone grazed it, the stone's motion would be slanting, not vertical. But I'll answer this way: if the tower were moving, it would be impossible for the stone to fall grazing it; and therefore from its falling along the tower one infers the stability of the Earth.

Simplicio: Just so. For the stone to come down grazing the tower while the tower was being carried along by the Earth, the stone would have to have two natural motions — the straight one toward the center and the circular one around the center — and that is impossible.

Salviati: So Aristotle's defense comes down to this: that it is impossible — or at least that he judged it impossible — for the stone to move with a motion mixed of straight and circular. For if he had not held it impossible for the stone to move toward the center and around the center at the same time, he would have understood that the falling stone could come down grazing the tower whether the tower was moving or standing still, and consequently he would have realized that from this grazing nothing whatever can be inferred about the motion or rest of the Earth. But that in no way excuses Aristotle — not only because, if he had had such a notion, he ought to have said so, it being so central a point in his argument, but even more because it cannot be said either that the effect is impossible or that Aristotle judged it impossible. The first cannot be said, because in a moment I'll show that it is not merely possible but necessary; nor can the second, because Aristotle himself grants that fire goes upward naturally in a straight line, and also moves in a circle with the daily rotation, which the heavens share with the whole element of fire and with the greater part of the air. If, then, he doesn't hold it impossible to mix the straight-upward motion with the circular one communicated to fire and air by the hollow underside of the Moon's sphere, much less should he count it impossible to mix the stone's straight-downward motion with a circular motion that was natural to the whole terrestrial globe, of which the stone is a part.

Simplicio: That doesn't look right to me. If the element of fire goes around together with the air, it is a very easy — indeed a necessary — thing that a little particle of fire, climbing up from the Earth, should receive that same movement as it passes through the moving air, being a body so thin and light and so very easily moved. But that a very heavy stone, or a cannonball dropping down from on high and already left to its own devices, should let itself be carried along by air or anything else — that is utterly unthinkable. Besides, there is that experiment so exactly to the point: the stone dropped from the top of a ship's mast, which falls at the foot of the mast while the ship stands still, but when the ship is under way falls as far from that same spot as the ship has run ahead during the time of the stone's fall — and that is no few braccia, when the ship's course is swift.

Salviati: There's a great disparity between the case of the ship and that of the Earth, if the terrestrial globe has the daily rotation. For it's perfectly obvious that the ship's motion, just as it is not natural to her, is likewise incidental to everything aboard her; so it's no wonder that the stone which was held at the masthead, once set free, comes down without any obligation to follow the ship's motion. But the daily rotation is attributed to the terrestrial globe as its own natural motion, and consequently to all its parts; and being stamped in them by nature, it is indelible in them. So the stone at the top of the tower has, as its primary instinct, the going around the center of its whole once in twenty-four hours, and this natural tendency it exercises eternally, whatever state you put it in. To be persuaded of this you need only revise one old impression stamped on your mind, and say: "Just as until now — having held it a property of the terrestrial globe to stand motionless about its center — I have never had the least difficulty or reluctance in grasping that each of its particles likewise remains naturally in that same rest, so it stands to reason that if it were the natural instinct of the terrestrial globe to go around in twenty-four hours, then every one of its parts would also have an intrinsic and natural inclination not to stand still, but to follow that same course." And so, without running into any awkwardness at all, one may conclude that since the motion conferred on the ship by the force of the oars — and through her on everything in her — is not natural but foreign, it's only right that the stone, once separated from the ship, should return to its natural state and go back to exercising its pure and simple natural tendency. Add to this that at least the part of the air that lies below the highest mountaintops must be seized and carried around by the roughness of the Earth's surface — or else, mixed as it is with so many earthly vapors and exhalations, must naturally follow the daily rotation; and none of that is true of the air around the ship driven by the oars. So arguing from the ship to the tower carries no inferential force: the stone that falls from the masthead enters a medium that does not share the ship's motion, but the one that leaves the top of the tower finds itself in a medium that has the same motion as the whole terrestrial globe — so that, far from being hindered by the air, it is rather helped by the air's motion, and can follow the universal course of the Earth.

Simplicio: I still can't see how the air could impress upon an enormous stone, or a great ball of iron or lead weighing, say, two hundred pounds, the motion with which it moves itself — a motion it may well communicate to feathers, snow, and other featherweight things. In fact I observe that a weight of that sort, exposed to the most furious wind you please, isn't shifted from its place by so much as a finger's breadth. Now imagine whether the air will carry it along.

Salviati: There's a great disparity between your experience and our case. You have the wind come upon a stone at rest; we are placing into air that is already moving a stone that is moving too, and at the same speed — so the air doesn't have to confer any new motion on it, but only maintain, or rather not impede, the motion it has already received. You want to drive the stone with a motion foreign to it and outside its nature; we want to conserve it in its natural one. If you wanted to produce a better-fitted experiment, you should have said: let someone observe, if not with the eye in his forehead then at least with the mind's eye, what would happen if an eagle, carried on the rush of the wind, let a stone drop from its talons. Since that stone was already flying with the wind when it left the claws, and after leaving them enters a medium moving at the same speed, I strongly suspect it would not be seen to drop straight down, but rather, following the wind's course and adding to it the pull of its own weight, would move on a slant.

Simplicio: One would have to be able actually to perform such an experiment, and then judge by the outcome. Meanwhile the result on the ship has, so far, come out applauding our opinion.

Salviati: "So far" — well said; for perhaps in a moment things will change their look. And so as not to keep you dangling any longer, as they say, tell me, Simplicio: do you honestly feel that the ship experiment fits our purpose so well that we may reasonably believe that whatever is seen to happen on the ship must also happen on the terrestrial globe?

Simplicio: So far, yes; and though you've brought up a few small discrepancies, they don't seem to me weighty enough to shake my opinion.

Salviati: On the contrary, I want you to keep holding it — to hold firm that the effect on the Earth must answer to the one on the ship — provided that, when that turns out to damage your case, you don't take a fancy to change your mind. You say: "Since, when the ship stands still, the stone falls at the foot of the mast, and when she is under way it falls far from the foot, then conversely, from the stone's falling at the foot one infers that the ship stands still, and from its falling far away one argues that the ship is moving; and since what holds for the ship must equally hold for the Earth, from the stone's falling at the foot of the tower one infers of necessity the immobility of the terrestrial globe." Isn't that your argument?

Simplicio: That's it exactly, boiled down to a brevity that makes it very easy to grasp.

Salviati: Now tell me: if the stone dropped from the masthead while the ship was running at great speed were to fall in precisely the same spot on the ship where it falls when she stands still, what service would those falls render you toward determining whether the vessel is standing still or under way?

Simplicio: Absolutely none — just as from the beating of the pulse, for instance, you can't tell whether someone is asleep or awake, since the pulse beats the same way in sleepers as in the waking.

Salviati: Very good. Now, have you ever performed the ship experiment?

Simplicio: I haven't; but I well believe that the authors who cite it have observed it carefully. Besides, the cause of the disparity is so plainly understood that it leaves no room for doubt.

Salviati: That those authors may cite it without ever having performed it, you yourself are a fine witness: without having performed it, you offer it as certain and take it on good faith at their word — just as it is not only possible but necessary that they did the same, I mean relied on their own predecessors, without anyone ever reaching back to someone who had actually done it. For whoever does perform it will find that the experiment shows the exact opposite of what is written: it will show that the stone always falls in the same place on the ship, whether she stands still or moves at any speed you please. And since the same reasoning holds for the Earth as for the ship, from the stone's falling always plumb at the foot of the tower nothing can be inferred about the motion or rest of the Earth.

Simplicio: If you refer me to any means other than experience, I truly believe our disputes won't be finished in a hurry; for this strikes me as a thing so remote from all human reasoning that it leaves not the smallest room for belief or even probability.

Salviati: And yet it has found room in me.

Simplicio: So you haven't made a hundred trials of it — no, not even one — and yet you assert it so freely as certain? I go straight back to my incredulity, and to my conviction that the experiment was performed by the principal authors who make use of it, and that it shows what they affirm.

Salviati: Even without the experiment I am certain the effect will follow as I tell you, because it must follow; and I'll add further that you yourself already know it cannot follow otherwise — however much you feign, or pretend to feign, not to know it. But I'm a good enough breaker-in of minds that I'll wring the confession out of you by sheer force. — But Sagredo sits there very quiet; I thought I saw you stir just now, as if to say something.

Sagredo: I did in fact mean to say something or other; but the curiosity stirred in me by hearing you threaten such violence against Simplicio — forcing him to disclose the knowledge he wants to keep hidden from us — has driven out every other wish. So I beg you to make good the boast.

Salviati: Provided Simplicio is willing to answer my questions, I won't fail to.

Simplicio: I'll answer what I know, confident it will give me little trouble; for about the things I hold to be false, I don't believe I can know anything at all — since knowledge is of truths, not of falsehoods.

Salviati: I don't want you to say or answer anything except what you know for certain. So tell me: suppose you had a flat surface, polished like a mirror and made of a material hard as steel, and not parallel to the horizon but somewhat tilted; and suppose that on it you set a perfectly spherical ball of some heavy, very hard material — bronze, say. Left to itself, what do you think it would do? Don't you believe — as I do — that it would stay still?

Simplicio: If that surface were tilted?

Salviati: Yes — that's what I supposed.

Simplicio: I don't believe it would stay put at all; on the contrary, I'm certain it would move down the slope of its own accord.

Salviati: Careful what you say, Simplicio, because I'm certain it would stay still wherever you set it down.

Simplicio: As long as you help yourself to suppositions of that sort, Salviati, I'll stop being surprised that you arrive at utterly false conclusions.

Salviati: So you're absolutely sure it would move down the slope of its own accord?

Simplicio: What doubt is there?

Salviati: And you hold this firmly not because I taught it to you — I was trying to persuade you of the opposite — but by yourself, by your own natural judgment.

Simplicio: Now I see your game: you said that to test me — to trip me up, as the common phrase has it — not because you really believed it.

Salviati: Just so. And how long would that ball keep moving, and how fast? Bear in mind that I specified a perfectly round ball and an exquisitely polished plane, precisely to remove all external and accidental impediments. In the same way, I want you to set aside the impediment of the air — its resistance to being parted — and any other incidental obstacles there might be.

Simplicio: I've understood the whole thing perfectly well. To your question I answer that it would continue moving indefinitely, as long as the slope of the plane lasted, and with a continually accelerating motion — for such is the nature of heavy moving bodies, that vires acquirant eundo, they gather strength as they go. And the steeper the slope, the greater the speed.

Salviati: But if someone wanted the ball to move upward along that same surface, do you think it would go?

Simplicio: Of its own accord, no; but dragged up, or hurled up by force, yes.

Salviati: And if it were driven by some impetus violently impressed on it, what sort of motion would it have, and how great?

Simplicio: The motion would keep languishing and slowing, being against nature, and it would last a longer or shorter time according to the greater or lesser impulse and the greater or lesser steepness of the climb.

Salviati: So far, then, you've explained to me how a moving body behaves on two different planes: on the downward slope the heavy body descends of its own accord, continually gaining speed, and force is needed to hold it at rest; on the upward slope force is needed to push it, and even to stop it, and the motion impressed on it keeps dwindling until at last it dies away entirely. You say further that in both cases a difference arises from the slope being steeper or gentler, so that a steeper descent brings greater speed, while conversely, on a rising plane, the same body driven by the same force travels a greater distance the smaller the elevation. Now tell me: what would happen to that same body on a surface that sloped neither up nor down?

Simplicio: Here I need to think a moment about my answer. With no downward slope there can be no natural inclination to motion, and with no upward slope there can be no resistance to being moved; so the body would stand indifferent between propensity and resistance to motion. It seems to me, then, that it ought naturally to stay put. But I'm being forgetful — it wasn't long ago that Sagredo gave me to understand that this is what would happen.

Salviati: So I believe, provided someone set it down at rest. But if it were given an impetus toward some direction, what would follow?

Simplicio: It would follow that it moves in that direction.

Salviati: But with what sort of motion? Continually accelerated, as on the downward slope, or progressively retarded, as on the upward one?

Simplicio: I can see no cause for acceleration or for retardation, there being neither descent nor climb.

Salviati: Right. But if there is no cause for retardation, still less should there be any for coming to rest. How long, then, would you have the body keep moving?

Simplicio: As long as the length of that surface — neither rising nor falling — lasted.

Salviati: Then if such a space were unbounded, the motion in it would likewise be without bound — that is, perpetual?

Simplicio: It seems so to me, provided the body were of material durable enough to last.

Salviati: That has already been assumed, since we said that all accidental and external impediments were to be removed — and the brittleness of the moving body is, in this business, one of the accidental impediments. Now tell me: what do you take to be the cause of that ball's moving of its own accord on the downward slope, and only under violence on the rising one?

Simplicio: Because the inclination of heavy bodies is to move toward the center of the Earth, and only by violence upward toward the circumference; and the sloping surface is the one that gains nearness to the center, while the rising one leads away from it.

Salviati: Then a surface that was to be neither downhill nor uphill would have to be, in all its parts, equally distant from the center. Is there any such surface anywhere in the world?

Simplicio: There's no lack of them: there is the surface of our terrestrial globe, if only it were quite smooth, and not, as it actually is, rough and mountainous; and there is the surface of the water, when it lies placid and tranquil.

Salviati: Then a ship moving across a calm sea is one of those bodies running along a surface that is neither downhill nor uphill, and is therefore disposed — if every accidental and external obstacle were removed — to move, with the impulse once received, incessantly and uniformly.

Simplicio: It seems it must be so.

Salviati: And the stone at the top of the mast — doesn't it too move, carried by the ship, along the circumference of a circle about the center, and consequently with a motion that is indelible in it, once the external impediments are removed? And isn't this motion as fast as the ship's?

Simplicio: So far everything runs smoothly. But the rest?

Salviati: Then draw the final consequence yourself, and welcome — if by yourself you've known all the premises.

Simplicio: You mean, as the final conclusion, that the stone, moving with a motion indelibly impressed on it, is not about to abandon the ship but to follow her, and in the end to fall in the very same place where it falls when the ship stands still. And I too say this would follow, if there were no external impediments to disturb the stone's motion once it is set free. But there are two such impediments: one is that the moving body is powerless to cleave the air by its own impetus alone, once it has lost the impetus of the oars' force, which it shared in, as a part of the ship, while it was up on the mast; the other is the new motion of falling downward, which must surely be an impediment to the forward one.

Salviati: As for the impediment of the air, I don't deny it to you; and if the falling body were of light material — a feather, or a tuft of wool — the retardation would be very great; but in a heavy stone it is very small indeed. You yourself said a moment ago that the force of the most furious wind doesn't suffice to budge a big stone from its place; now imagine what the still air will do when met by a stone moving no faster than the whole ship. Nevertheless, as I said, I concede you this small effect that may depend on such an impediment — just as I know you will concede to me that if the air were moving at the same speed as the ship and the stone, the impediment would be absolutely nil. As for the other point, the supervening downward motion: first of all, it is obvious that these two motions — the circular around the center and the straight toward the center — are not contraries, are not destructive of one another, are not incompatible; for on the side of the moving body there is no repugnance at all to such motion. You yourself have already granted that its repugnance is against the motion that carries it away from the center, and its inclination is toward the motion that brings it nearer the center; from which it follows necessarily that toward a motion which neither approaches nor recedes from the center, the body has neither repugnance nor propensity — nor, in consequence, any cause for the faculty impressed on it to be diminished. And since the moving cause is not a single one, which might be weakened by the new operation, but two distinct ones — of which gravity attends solely to drawing the body toward the center, while the impressed force attends to carrying it around the center — there remains no occasion whatever for impediment.

Simplicio: The argument really is quite plausible on the surface, but at its core it's troubled by a snag that's not so easy to get past. Through the whole course of it you've made an assumption that the Peripatetic school will not grant you lightly, since it flatly contradicts Aristotle: namely, taking it as obvious and well established that the projectile, once separated from the thrower, continues its motion by a force impressed on it by that same thrower. Now this impressed force is as odious to Peripatetic philosophy as the passage of any accident from one subject into another; in that philosophy the position is — as I believe you know — that the projectile is carried by the medium, which in our case is the air. So if that stone released from the top of the mast were to follow the ship's motion, the effect would have to be attributed to the air, not to any force impressed on it; but you assume the air does not follow the ship's motion, and is calm. What's more, the man who lets it fall is not supposed to hurl it or give it impetus with his arm — he simply opens his hand and releases it. And so the stone can follow the ship's motion neither by a force impressed on it by the thrower nor by any courtesy of the air; and therefore it will be left behind.

Salviati: What I seem to gather from your speech, then, is that since the stone isn't driven out by the man's arm, it doesn't count as a projection at all.

Simplicio: Properly speaking, it cannot be called projectile motion.

Salviati: Then what Aristotle says about the motion, the moving body, and the mover of projectiles has nothing to do with our question; and if it has nothing to do with it, why do you bring it up?

Simplicio: I bring it up for the sake of that impressed force you named and introduced — which, since it doesn't exist in the world, can accomplish nothing, for non entium nullae sunt operationes — what doesn't exist can't act. And therefore the moving cause not only of projectile motion, but of every other motion that isn't natural, must be attributed to the medium, which hasn't been given its due consideration; and so everything said up to now stands ineffective.

Salviati: Fine — all in good time. But tell me: since your objection rests entirely on the nonexistence of the impressed force, if I demonstrate to you that the medium has nothing to do with continuing the motion of projectiles once they've separated from the thrower, will you let the impressed force stand — or will you mount some fresh assault to destroy it?

Simplicio: Once the action of the medium is removed, I don't see what one could fall back on other than the faculty impressed by the mover.

Salviati: Then it will be well, to cut off as far as possible any endless back-and-forth of objections, for you to explain as distinctly as you can just what the medium's operation is in keeping the projectile in motion.

Simplicio: The thrower has the stone in his hand; he moves his arm with speed and force, and with that motion he moves not just the stone but the surrounding air as well; so the stone, at the moment the hand abandons it, finds itself in air that is already moving with impetus, and it is carried along by that air. If the air weren't doing the work, the stone would drop from the hand to the thrower's feet.

Salviati: And you've been so credulous as to let yourself be talked into these absurdities, when you had your own senses right there to refute them and to learn the truth? Tell me, then: that great stone, and that artillery ball, which merely resting on a table stayed immovable against any wind however fierce, as you affirmed a little while ago — if it had been a ball of cork, or the same bulk of cotton wool, do you think the wind would have shifted it?

Simplicio: On the contrary, I know for certain the wind would have carried it clean away — and the faster, the lighter the material. That's exactly why we see the clouds borne along at a speed equal to that of the very wind that drives them.

Salviati: And what is wind?

Simplicio: Wind is defined as nothing other than moving air.

Salviati: So moving air carries the lightest materials much faster and much farther than the heaviest ones?

Simplicio: Certainly.

Salviati: But if you had to throw with your arm first a stone and then a wisp of cotton, which would move faster and farther?

Simplicio: The stone, by a wide margin — the cotton would just drop at my feet.

Salviati: Well then: if what moves the projectile, after the hand releases it, is nothing but the air set moving by the arm, and moving air pushes light materials more easily than heavy ones, how is it that the cotton projectile doesn't fly farther and faster than the stone one? Something must remain in the stone besides the motion of the air. And furthermore: suppose two strings of equal length hung from that beam, with a lead ball tied to the end of one and a cotton ball to the end of the other; draw both aside equally far from the vertical, and then set them free. No doubt both will swing toward the vertical and, driven by their own impetus, will pass beyond it a certain distance and then return. But which of these two pendulums do you think will keep moving longer before it comes to rest hanging plumb?

Simplicio: The lead ball will swing back and forth a thousand times; the cotton one, two or three at most.

Salviati: So that impetus and that mobility, whatever their cause may be, are conserved longer in heavy materials than in light ones. Now I come to another point, and I ask you: why doesn't the air carry off that citron sitting on the table right now?

Simplicio: Because the air itself isn't moving.

Salviati: Then the thrower must confer the motion on the air, and the air must then use it to move the projectile. But if such a force cannot be impressed — since an accident cannot be made to pass from one subject into another — how does it pass from the arm into the air? Isn't the air a subject distinct from the arm?

Simplicio: The answer given is that the air, being neither heavy nor light in its own region, is disposed to receive every impulse with the greatest ease, and to conserve it as well.

Salviati: But the pendulums have just now shown us that the less heaviness a moving body partakes of, the less fit it is to conserve motion. How then can it be that the air, which in air has no heaviness at all, is the one thing that conserves the motion it has received? I believe — and I know that by now you believe it too — that the arm is no sooner stopped than the air around it stops as well. Let's go into a room and stir the air as hard as we can with a towel; then, the cloth held still, let a small lighted candle be brought into the room, or let a leaf of gold foil be set drifting loose in it: from the quiet wandering of either one you'll see that the air has been brought back to calm immediately. I could bring you a thousand experiments, but if one of these weren't enough, the case had better be given up as altogether hopeless.

Sagredo: When an arrow is shot against the wind, what an incredible thing it is that the little thread of air pushed by the bowstring should travel along with the arrow in defiance of the gale! But there's another particular point I'd like to learn from Aristotle, and I beg Simplicio to favor me with an answer. Suppose two arrows were shot from the same bow, one point-first in the usual way and the other sideways — that is, laid lengthwise along the string and shot in that flat position: I'd like to know which of them would go farther. Do me the kindness of an answer, even though the question may strike you as more ridiculous than not; and forgive me, because I — being, as you can see, rather on the thick side — can't reach any higher with my speculating.

Simplicio: I've never seen arrows shot sideways; still, I believe that shot crosswise one wouldn't go even a twentieth of the distance it goes point-first.

Sagredo: And since I've believed the very same thing, that's what gave me occasion to raise a doubt between Aristotle's pronouncement and experience. Because as far as experience goes, if I set two arrows on that table when a strong wind is blowing — one laid along the line of the wind, the other crosswise to it — the wind will quickly carry off the crosswise one and leave the other lying there. And the same thing, it seems, ought to happen with the two shot from the bow, if Aristotle's doctrine were true: for the crosswise arrow would be driven by a great quantity of air set moving by the string — as much air, that is, as the arrow's whole length — whereas the other arrow receives its push from no more air than the tiny little circle of its thickness. And I can't imagine the cause of such a difference, and I'd dearly like to know it.

Simplicio: The cause seems obvious enough to me: it's because the arrow shot point-first has only a small quantity of air to penetrate, while the other has to cleave as much air as its whole length.

Sagredo: So arrows, when shot, have to penetrate the air? But if the air goes along with them — indeed, if the air is what carries them — what penetration can there be? Don't you see that on this account the arrow would have to be moving faster than the air? And who confers this greater speed on the arrow? Will you say the air gives it a speed greater than its own? Understand, then, Simplicio, that the business works exactly backwards from what Aristotle says: it is as false that the medium confers motion on the projectile as it is true that the medium is the one thing that hinders it. And once you've grasped that, you'll understand without any difficulty that when the air really is moving, it carries the arrow along much better crosswise than straight on, because there's a great deal of air pushing it in that posture and very little in the other; but when the shots come from the bow, since the air is standing still, the crosswise arrow, striking against a great deal of air, is greatly impeded, while the point-first one overcomes with the greatest ease the obstacle of the tiny quantity of air that opposes it.

Salviati: How many propositions I've noted in Aristotle — I mean always in natural philosophy — that are not merely false, but false in such a way that their diametrical opposite is true, as happens with this one! But to keep to our purpose: I believe Simplicio is now persuaded that from seeing the stone always fall in the same place, nothing can be inferred about the ship's motion or its stillness. And if everything said so far weren't enough for him, there is the experiment ready at hand, which can settle the matter for him completely: in that experiment, the most he could ever see would be the falling body left behind if it were made of very light material and the air did not follow the ship's motion; but where the air moves with equal speed, no imaginable difference would be found in this or in any other experiment whatsoever, as I'm shortly going to tell you. Now if, in this case, no difference at all shows itself, what could anyone claim to see in the stone falling from the top of the tower — where the circular motion is not adventitious and accidental to the stone but natural and eternal, and where the air follows the tower's motion exactly, and the tower follows that of the terrestrial globe? Have you anything further to say, Simplicio, on this particular point?

Simplicio: Nothing further — except that so far I don't see the Earth's mobility proved.

Salviati: Nor have I claimed to prove it. My only claim was to show that nothing can be extracted from the experiment our adversaries put forward as an argument for the Earth's being fixed — just as I believe I'll show of the other arguments.

Sagredo: Please, Salviati, before you pass on to anything else, allow me to bring up a certain difficulty that's been turning round in my imagination while you were so patiently grinding this ship experiment down fine for Simplicio.

Salviati: We're here to discuss, and it's good for each of us to raise the difficulties that occur to him — that's the road to knowledge of the truth. So speak.

Sagredo: If it's true that the impetus with which the ship moves remains indelibly impressed in the stone after it has separated from the mast, and if it's furthermore true that this motion brings no hindrance or slowing to the straight downward motion natural to the stone, then a marvelous effect must follow in nature. Let the ship stand still, and let the fall of a stone from the top of the mast take two beats of the pulse. Then let the ship be under way, and let the same stone be dropped from the same place: by what we've said, it will still take the time of two pulse-beats to arrive at the bottom, and in that time the ship will have run, say, twenty braccia — so that the stone's true motion will have been a slanting line, considerably longer than the first straight and vertical one, which is simply the length of the mast; and yet the ball will have traversed it in the same time. Suppose next that the ship's motion is speeded up considerably more, so that the falling stone must pass along a slanting line even longer than the last; and in short, however much you increase the ship's speed, the falling stone will trace out slanting paths longer and longer still, and yet will traverse every one of them in those same two pulse-beats. On the same pattern: if a culverin — a long cannon — were mounted level at the top of a tower and fired point-blank, that is, parallel to the horizon, then whatever charge, small or large, were given the piece, so that the ball fell to earth now a thousand braccia away, now four thousand, now six thousand, now ten thousand, and so on, all these shots would be completed in times equal to one another, and each equal to the time the ball would take to go from the muzzle of the piece to the ground if it were simply allowed to fall straight down, with no other impulse. Now it seems a marvelous thing that in the same short time of a plumb fall to the ground from a height of, say, a hundred braccia, the same ball, driven out by the powder, can pass through now four hundred, now a thousand, now four thousand, now ten thousand braccia — so that in all point-blank shots the ball stays in the air for equal times.

Salviati: The observation is beautiful for its novelty, and if the effect is real, it is marvelous — and of its truth I have no doubt. Setting aside the accidental impediment of the air, I hold it for certain that if, at the instant the ball left the gun, another ball were let fall straight down from the same height, both would reach the ground at the same instant, even though one had traveled ten thousand braccia of distance and the other only a hundred — assuming, that is, that the surface of the Earth were level; and to be safe, the shot could be fired over some lake. The impediment that might come from the air would consist in slowing down the extremely rapid motion of the shot. Now, if you please, let's come to the solutions of the other arguments, since Simplicio is — as far as I can judge — well satisfied of the nullity of this first one, drawn from bodies falling from high to low.

Simplicio: I don't feel all my scruples removed; but perhaps the fault is mine, for not being as quick and nimble of understanding as Sagredo. And it seems to me that if this motion the stone shared in while it was on the ship's mast were, as you say, conserved in it indelibly even after it has separated from the ship, then it should likewise follow that when someone riding a galloping horse lets a ball drop from his hand, that ball, having landed, would continue its motion and follow the horse's course without falling behind. And I don't believe that effect is ever seen — except when the rider throws the ball forcefully in the direction of his run; short of that, I believe it will stay on the ground right where it strikes.

Salviati: I believe you're very much mistaken, and I'm sure experience will show you the contrary: the ball, once it has reached the ground, will run along with the horse, and won't fall behind it except insofar as the roughness and unevenness of the road hinders it. And the reason seems clear enough to me. For if you, standing still, threw that same ball along the ground, wouldn't it continue its motion even after leaving your hand — and for a longer stretch, the smoother the surface, so that on ice, for instance, it would travel a very long way?

Simplicio: No doubt of that, so long as I give it impetus with my arm; but in the other case, we're assuming the man on the horse merely lets it drop.

Salviati: That's exactly what I want to happen. When you throw it with your arm, what remains with the ball, once it has left your hand, except the motion received from your arm — which, conserved in it, keeps carrying it forward? And what does it matter whether that impetus is conferred on the ball by your arm or by the horse? While you're on horseback, doesn't your hand — and consequently the ball — travel as fast as the horse itself? Certainly it does. So at the mere opening of your hand, the ball departs with the motion it has already received — not from your arm by any private motion of your own, but from the motion that depends on the horse itself, which is communicated to you, to your arm, to your hand, and finally to the ball. And I'll tell you more: if the rider, at a run, throws the ball with his arm in the direction opposite to his course, then once it reaches the ground it will sometimes follow the horse's course all the same, despite having been hurled the other way; and sometimes it will lie still on the ground; and it will move against the direction of the run only when the motion received from the arm exceeds the speed of the gallop. And it's nonsense, what some people say — that a horseman can launch a javelin through the air in the direction of his run, then follow it with the horse, catch up to it, and finally take hold of it again. Nonsense, I say, because to have the projectile come back into your hand you must throw it straight up, exactly as if you were standing still; for however fast the run may be, provided it's uniform and the projectile isn't something extremely light, it will always fall back into the thrower's hand, no matter how high it's thrown.

Sagredo: From this doctrine I come to see some quite curious problems in the matter of these projectiles — the first of which is bound to strike Simplicio as very strange. The problem is this: I say it's possible that a ball simply dropped by someone running fast, in whatever manner, once it has reached the ground, will not only follow his course but get well out ahead of him. And that problem is connected to this one: that a body launched by a thrower over the horizontal plane can acquire new speed, considerably greater than what the thrower gave it. I've often watched this effect with astonishment while standing to see people playing at ruzzole — hurling those wooden discs. Once out of the hand, you see the discs going through the air at a certain speed, which then increases considerably when they reach the ground; and if in rolling they strike some obstacle that bounces them up into the air, you see them move through the air quite slowly, and on falling back to earth they move again with greater speed. But what's stranger still, I've also observed not only that they always go faster along the ground than through the air, but that of two stretches both covered along the ground, the motion in the second stretch is sometimes faster than in the first. Now what would Simplicio say to that?

Simplicio: I'd say, first of all, that I've never made any such observation; second, I'd say I don't believe it; and then I'd say, in the third place, that if you convinced me of it and taught it to me demonstratively, you'd be a great demon.

Sagredo: One of Socrates' demons, though — not one of Hell's. But there you go again with this "teaching." I tell you that when a man doesn't know a truth on his own, it's impossible for anyone else to make him know it. I can certainly teach you things that are neither true nor false; but the true ones — that is, the necessary ones, the ones that cannot possibly be otherwise — every mind of ordinary discourse either knows on its own, or can never know at all. And I know Salviati believes the same. And so I tell you that the reasons behind these present problems are known to you — you just may not have noticed them.

Simplicio: Let's set this dispute aside for now, and just let me admit that I don't understand, and don't know, these matters you're discussing — so please, see if you can make me grasp the problems.

Sagredo: This first problem depends on another — namely, why it is that when you throw the ruzzola with a string, it goes much farther, and so with greater force, than when you throw it with your bare hand.

Simplicio: Aristotle too raises some problems or other about these projectiles.

Salviati: Yes, and very ingenious ones — in particular the problem of why round ruzzole travel better than square ones.

Sagredo: And this one, Simplicio — don't you feel up to working out the reason on your own, without anyone teaching you?

Simplicio: All right, all right — but enough of the teasing.

Sagredo: Then you already know the reason for this other one too. So tell me: you know that a thing in motion, when it's obstructed, stops?

Simplicio: I know it — provided the obstruction is enough to do it.

Sagredo: And you know that a moving body meets greater obstruction having to move along the ground than through the air, the ground being rough and hard, the air soft and yielding?

Simplicio: And because I know this, I know the ruzzola will go faster through the air than along the ground — so what I know is the exact opposite of what you were expecting.

Sagredo: Not so fast, Simplicio. Do you know that in the parts of a body spinning about its center, you find motions in every direction — so that some rise, some fall, some go forward, some backward?

Simplicio: I know it — Aristotle taught me that.

Sagredo: And by what demonstration? Do tell me, please.

Simplicio: By the one from the senses.

Sagredo: So Aristotle made you see what you wouldn't have seen without him? Did he ever lend you his eyes? You meant to say Aristotle had told you, pointed it out, reminded you — not taught you. So then: when a ruzzola, without changing place, spins on itself — not lying flat, but standing upright to the horizon — some of its parts rise and the opposite parts fall; the upper parts go one way, the lower the contrary way. Now picture a ruzzola that, without changing place, spins fast on itself while hanging suspended in the air; and while spinning that way, it's let drop straight down to the ground. Do you think that, once it reaches the ground, it will go on spinning in place, as before?

Simplicio: No, it won't.

Sagredo: But what will it do?

Simplicio: It'll run fast along the ground.

Sagredo: And in which direction?

Simplicio: Toward wherever its spin carries it.

Sagredo: But in its spin there are parts — the upper ones — that move contrary to the lower ones; so you have to say which it will obey. As for the rising and falling parts, neither will yield to the other, and the whole won't go down — the ground blocks it — nor up, since it's heavy.

Simplicio: The ruzzola will go rolling along the ground toward wherever its upper parts are heading.

Sagredo: And why not toward where the contrary parts head — the ones touching the ground?

Simplicio: Because the parts at the ground are held back by the roughness of the contact — by the very unevenness of the ground; but the upper parts, in the thin, yielding air, are held back very little or not at all, so the ruzzola will go their way.

Sagredo: So that gripping, so to speak, of the lower parts against the ground keeps them back, and only the upper parts drive forward.

Salviati: And so, if the ruzzola fell on ice or some other very smooth surface, it wouldn't slide forward so well, but might perhaps go on spinning in place without gaining any forward motion.

Sagredo: That could easily happen; at least it wouldn't go rolling along so briskly as when it falls on a somewhat rough surface. But tell me, Simplicio: when the ruzzola, spinning fast on itself, is let fall, why doesn't it move forward through the air too, the way it does once it's on the ground?

Simplicio: Because, with air above and below, neither those parts nor these have anything to grip; and having no reason to go forward rather than back, it falls straight down.

Sagredo: So the spin by itself, without any other impetus, can drive the ruzzola quite fast once it reaches the ground. Now let's get to the rest. That string the thrower ties to his arm, and with which — wound around the ruzzola — he throws it: what effect does it have on it?

Simplicio: It forces it to spin on itself, as it unwinds from the cord.

Sagredo: So when the ruzzola reaches the ground, it arrives already spinning on itself, thanks to the string. Doesn't it then have within itself a cause to move faster along the ground than it did while in the air?

Simplicio: Certainly yes: because in the air it had no impulse but that of the thrower's arm, and though it also had the spin, that — as we said — drives nothing in the air; but on reaching the ground, to the arm's motion is added the forward push of the spin, so the speed doubles. And now I understand perfectly that when the ruzzola bounces up, its speed will drop, because it loses the help of the spinning; and on falling back to the ground it recovers that help, and so goes back to moving faster than in the air. The only thing left for me to grasp is that in this second stretch along the ground it moves faster than in the first — because at that rate it would move forever, always speeding up.

Sagredo: I didn't say flatly that this second stretch is faster than the first, only that it can sometimes happen to be faster.

Simplicio: That's the very thing I don't understand and would like to.

Sagredo: This too you know for yourself. So tell me: if you let the ruzzola drop from your hand without its spinning at all, what would it do on striking the ground?

Simplicio: Nothing — it would just stay put there.

Sagredo: Couldn't it happen that, in striking the ground, it gained some motion? Think it over more carefully.

Simplicio: Unless we let it fall on some stone that had a slope — the way children do in their games — and by striking slantwise on the sloping stone it gained a spinning motion of its own, with which it then kept moving forward along the ground, I wouldn't know any other way it could do anything but stop where it struck.

Sagredo: There — so in some fashion it can gain fresh spin. So when the ruzzola, having bounced up, falls back down, why couldn't it happen to strike the slant of some stone set in the ground and sloped toward the direction of the motion, and — gaining fresh spin from that blow, on top of the first spin from the string — double its motion and make it faster than it was on its first strike against the ground?

Simplicio: Now I see that this could easily happen. And I'm realizing that if the ruzzola were made to spin the opposite way, then on reaching the ground it would produce the opposite effect — that is, the motion of the spin would slow the thrower's.

Sagredo: And it would slow it, and sometimes stop it entirely, when the spin was fast enough. And this is the source of the trick the more expert tennis players use to their advantage — deceiving their opponent by slicing the ball (that's their word for it), that is, returning it with the racquet held at a slant, so that the ball takes on a spin contrary to its projected motion. From which it follows that, on reaching the ground, the bounce — which, if the ball weren't spinning, would carry toward the opponent and give him his usual time to return it — is left as if dead: the ball squashes against the ground, or bounces far less than usual, and breaks the timing of the return. For the same reason you'll see players of wooden bowls, competing to land closest to a set mark, do this: when they play on a stony road full of bumps that would knock the ball off course a thousand ways and keep it from the mark, then, to dodge all those obstacles, they toss the ball not rolling along the ground but straight through the air, as if throwing a flat quoit. But since, in throwing, the ball leaves the hand with some spin imparted by the fingers — whenever the hand is held under the ball, as it usually is — the ball, on striking the ground near the mark, would run far off, carried by the thrower's motion and the spin together. So to make it stop, they cunningly grip the ball with the hand above and the ball below, so that as it escapes, the fingers give it the opposite spin, by which, on striking the ground near the mark, it stops there or runs only a little farther. But to return to the main problem, which was the cause of raising all these others: I say it's possible for someone moving very fast to let a ball leave his hand which, once on the ground, not only keeps up with his motion but even outruns it, moving faster than he does. And to see such an effect, let the moving thing be a cart, to whose outer side is fixed a sloping board, so that its lower end faces the horses and its upper end the rear wheels. Now, at the cart's fastest, if someone inside lets a ball drop down the slope of that board, in coming down it will roll and gain a spin of its own, which — added to the motion impressed by the cart — will carry the ball along the ground much faster than the cart. And if you fitted another sloping board the opposite way, you could tune the cart's motion so that the ball, after running down the board, on reaching the ground would stay still, or even sometimes run backward, against the cart. But we've strayed too long from our subject; and if Simplicio is satisfied with the solution of the first argument against the Earth's mobility — the one drawn from bodies falling straight down — we can move on to the others.

Salviati: The digressions we've made so far aren't so foreign to the subject we're treating that they can be called wholly separate from it. Besides, our reasonings depend on whatever things get stirred up in the imagination — and not one imagination, but three; and, on top of that, we're talking for our own pleasure, and aren't bound by the strictness of someone who ex professo — as a formal undertaking — sets out to treat a subject methodically, meaning also to publish it. I don't want our poem so tied to that unity that it leaves us no open field for episodes; to bring one in, the slightest connection should be enough — as if we'd gathered to swap tales, where I'm free to tell whatever story hearing yours happens to call to mind.

Sagredo: This pleases me greatly; and since we're allowing ourselves this latitude, let me — before we go any further — ask you, Salviati, whether it's ever crossed your mind what one might take to be the line traced by a heavy body falling naturally from the top of a tower to the bottom. If you've reflected on it, do tell me your thought.

Salviati: I've thought about it now and then; and I've no doubt that if one were sure of the nature of the motion by which a heavy body descends to reach the center of the terrestrial globe, and then combined it with the common circular motion of the daily rotation, one would find exactly what sort of line is traced by the body's center of gravity out of the composition of those two motions.

Sagredo: As for the simple motion toward the center, due to gravity, I think one may believe, quite without error, that it's along a straight line — exactly as it would be if the Earth were motionless.

Salviati: On that point we not only may believe it — experience makes us certain of it.

Sagredo: But how does experience assure us of it, if we never see any motion but the compound of the two, circular and downward?

Salviati: On the contrary, Sagredo — we see nothing but the simple downward motion, since the other, the circular one shared by the Earth, the tower, and us, stays imperceptible and as if it didn't exist; the only motion left noticeable to us is the stone's, which we don't share. And of that one, the senses show it to be along a straight line, since it comes down always parallel to the tower itself, which stands built straight up and perpendicular on the Earth's surface.

Sagredo: You're right — and I've shown myself far too dull, not to have hit on something so easy. But since this is perfectly well known, what else do you say we need in order to understand the nature of this downward motion?

Salviati: It's not enough to know that it's straight; we need to know whether it's uniform or non-uniform — that is, whether it keeps always the same speed, or goes on slowing down or speeding up.

Sagredo: It's already clear that it keeps continually speeding up.

Salviati: Nor is that enough; we'd need to know by what proportion this acceleration takes place — a problem that so far, I don't think, has been solved by any philosopher or mathematician, even though whole and enormous volumes have been written on motion by philosophers, and by the Peripatetics in particular.

Simplicio: Philosophers concern themselves chiefly with universals; they find the definitions and the most common properties, then leave certain subtleties and trifling details — which are really more curiosities — to the mathematicians. Aristotle was content to define excellently what motion is in general, and, for local motion, to show its principal attributes: that one kind is natural, another violent; one simple, another compound; one uniform, another accelerated. And for accelerated motion he was content to give the cause of the acceleration, leaving the investigation of the proportion of that acceleration, and of other more particular details, to the mechanic or some lower craftsman.

Sagredo: All very well, my dear Simplicio. But you, Salviati — stepping down now and then from the throne of Peripatetic majesty — have you ever toyed with the investigation of this proportion of the acceleration of falling heavy bodies?

Salviati: I've had no need to think about it, since the Academician, our mutual friend, once showed me a treatise of his on motion, where this was demonstrated, along with many other details. But it would be too great a digression if, for this, we wanted to interrupt the present discussion — which is itself already a digression — and make, as they say, a play within a play.

Sagredo: I'm content to let you off that account for now — on condition, though, that this be one of the propositions set aside to be examined, among the others, in another special session, since that knowledge is something I most keenly want. And meanwhile, let's return to the line traced by the heavy body falling from the top of the tower down to its base.

Salviati: If the straight motion toward the center of the Earth were uniform — the circular motion toward the east being uniform as well — the two together would compose a motion along a spiral line, of the kind Archimedes defined in his book on spirals: those you get when a point moves uniformly along a straight line while that line, also uniformly, turns about one of its endpoints, fixed as the center of its rotation. But because the straight motion of the falling body is continually accelerated, the line composed of the two motions must keep drawing away, in ever greater proportion, from the circumference of the circle that the stone's center of gravity would have traced had it stayed always atop the tower. And this drawing-away must, at the start, be small — indeed tiny, indeed the tiniest of all — since the falling body, setting out from rest (that is, from the absence of downward motion) and entering into the straight downward motion, must pass through all the degrees of slowness between rest and any given speed; and those degrees are infinite, as we discussed and concluded at length earlier.

Granting, then, that such is the course of the acceleration, and given too that the falling body is bound to end at the center of the Earth, the line of its compound motion must be such that it keeps drawing away in ever greater proportion from the top of the tower — or, better put, from the circumference of the circle traced by the top of the tower through the Earth's rotation — yet with those departures growing smaller and smaller without limit, the less and less the body has moved away from the first terminus where it rested. On top of this, this line of the compound motion must necessarily end at the center of the Earth. Now, having made these two assumptions, I went ahead and drew, about the center A with the radius AB, the circle BI, which represents the terrestrial globe to me. Extending the radius AB out to C, I laid off the height of the tower BC, which, carried by the Earth over the circumference BI, traces with its top the arc CD. Then, dividing the line CA in half at E, with center E and radius EC I draw the semicircle CIA — along which I now say that one may quite probably believe a stone, falling from the top of the tower at C, comes to move with the compound motion of the common circular motion and its own straight one. For if I mark on the circumference CD some equal parts CF, FG, GH, HL, and from the points F, G, H, L draw straight lines toward the center A, the segments of those lines caught between the two circumferences CD and BI will always represent that same tower CB, carried by the terrestrial globe toward DI. And on those lines, the points where they're cut by the arc of the semicircle CI are the places where, from moment to moment, the falling stone is found; and those points keep drawing away in ever greater proportion from the top of the tower — which is what makes the straight motion along the tower show itself more and more accelerated. One sees, too, how — thanks to the infinite sharpness of the angle of contact between the two circles DC and CI — the falling body's departure from the circumference CFD, that is from the top of the tower, is at the start the very smallest, which is as much as to say that the downward motion is the very slowest, and slower and slower without limit the nearer it is to the terminus C, that is to the state of rest. And finally one understands how, at the last, such motion would end at the center of the Earth, A.

Sagredo: I understand the whole thing perfectly, and I can't believe the falling body traces, with its center of gravity, any line but one like this.

Salviati: But gently, Sagredo — I still have three little musings to bring you, which perhaps won't displease you. The first is that, if we consider it rightly, the body really moves with nothing but a simple circular motion, just as, when it rested on the tower, it likewise moved with a simple circular motion. The second is even prettier: for it moves not a whit more or less than if it had stayed continuously on the tower, since the arcs CF, FG, GH, and so on, that it would have passed through by staying always on the tower are precisely equal to the arcs of the circumference CI that answer to them beneath those same CF, FG, GH, and so on. From which follows the third marvel: that the true and real motion of the stone is not accelerated at all, but is always steady and uniform, since all the equal arcs marked on the circumference CD, and their counterparts marked on the circumference CI, are passed through in equal times. So we're freed from hunting for new causes of acceleration, or of any other motions, since the body — both while resting on the tower and while descending from it — always moves in the same way, that is, circularly, with the same speed and the same uniformity. Now tell me what you make of this odd fancy of mine.

Sagredo: I tell you, I couldn't put into words how marvelous it seems to me; and as far as it now presents itself to my mind, I don't believe the business goes any other way. Would to God all the demonstrations of the philosophers had half the plausibility of this one. I'd very much like, for my complete satisfaction, to hear the proof that those arcs are equal.

Salviati: The demonstration is very easy. Imagine the line I E is drawn. Since the radius of the circle C D — that is, the line C A — is double the radius C E of the circle C I, the larger circumference will be double the smaller, and every arc of the larger circle will be double the corresponding arc of the smaller, so that half of any arc of the larger circle equals the matching arc of the smaller. Now the angle C E I, formed at the center E of the smaller circle and standing on the arc C I, is double the angle C A D, formed at the center A of the larger circle and subtended by the arc C D. Therefore the arc C D is half of the arc of the larger circle that corresponds to the arc C I, and so the two arcs C D and C I are equal. The same can be shown for all the parts. But that the business proceeds exactly this way as far as the fall of heavy bodies goes, I won't insist on for now. What I will say is that, if the line traced by a falling body isn't precisely this one, it comes very close to it.

Sagredo: But now, Salviati, I find myself dwelling on another marvelous thing — namely, that given these considerations, straight-line motion goes entirely out the window, and nature never makes use of it at all. Even the one job that was granted to it at the outset — to carry the parts of whole bodies back to their proper place when they'd been separated from the whole and were therefore in a bad arrangement — even that is now taken away from it and assigned instead to circular motion.

Salviati: This would follow necessarily if it had been established that the terrestrial globe moves in a circle — something I don't claim we've done. All we've done so far, and all we'll go on doing, is to weigh the force of the reasons the philosophers offer as proof of the Earth's immobility. Of these, the first, drawn from bodies falling straight down, runs into the difficulties you've heard. How much weight those difficulties carry in Simplicio's eyes I don't know, so before we move on to test the other arguments, it would be good if he brought out anything he has to say in reply.

Simplicio: As for this first argument, I confess I've heard several subtleties I hadn't thought of, and since they're new to me, I don't have my answers ready. But this argument from bodies falling straight down I don't count among the strongest proofs of the Earth's immobility, and I don't know how things will turn out with the artillery shots — especially those fired against the daily rotation.

Sagredo: I only wish the flight of birds troubled me as little as the artillery and all the other experiences brought up so far! But these birds, which fly back and forth at their pleasure and wheel about in a thousand ways, and — what matters more — hang suspended in the air for hours on end: these, I say, throw my imagination into confusion. I can't understand how, amid so many spinning turns, they don't lose track of the Earth's motion, or how they can keep pace with a speed that in the end outstrips their own flight many, many times over.

Salviati: Your doubt really isn't without reason, and perhaps Copernicus himself never found a solution to his full satisfaction — which may be why he passed over it in silence. Even in examining the other arguments against him he was quite terse, I think out of loftiness of mind, being grounded on greater and higher contemplations, the way lions stir little at the yapping of small dogs. So we'll save the objection about the birds for last, and meanwhile try to satisfy Simplicio on the others, showing him in the usual way that he himself holds the solutions in hand, though he doesn't realize it. Let's start with the point-blank shots, fired with the same piece, the same powder and ball, one toward the east and the other toward the west. Tell me: what makes you believe that the westward shot (supposing the daily rotation belonged to the terrestrial globe) ought to come out much longer than the eastward one?

Simplicio: I'm moved to believe it because, in the eastward shot, while the ball is outside the cannon it's followed by the cannon itself, which — carried by the Earth — is also racing swiftly in the same direction, so that the ball falls to earth not far from the piece. In the westward shot, on the contrary, before the ball strikes the ground the piece has drawn back a good way toward the east, so that the space between the ball and the piece — that is, the range of the shot — will look longer than the other by as much ground as the artillery, and hence the Earth, has covered during the time both balls were in the air.

Salviati: I wish we could find some way to make an experiment matching the motion of these projectiles, the way the shipboard experiment matched the motion of bodies falling from a height. I'm thinking about how.

Sagredo: I think a very suitable test would be to take an open carriage and fit it with a heavy crossbow for bolts, set at half elevation so the shot comes out the longest possible; then, while the horses are running, fire once toward the direction of travel and once toward the opposite direction, marking very carefully where the carriage is at the exact moment the bolt sticks in the ground, in each shot. That way you could see exactly how much longer one comes out than the other.

Simplicio: It seems to me a very suitable experiment, and I've no doubt the shot — that is, the space between the arrow and where the carriage is at the moment the arrow sticks in the ground — will be much smaller when you fire toward the carriage's motion than when you fire the opposite way. Say, for example, the shot in itself is three hundred arm-lengths, and the carriage's travel while the bolt is in the air is a hundred. Then, firing toward the direction of travel, out of the three hundred arm-lengths of the shot the carriage covers a hundred, so that when the bolt strikes the ground the space between it and the carriage will be only two hundred arm-lengths. But in the other shot, on the contrary, the carriage running opposite to the bolt, when the bolt has covered its three hundred arm-lengths and the carriage its hundred in the opposite direction, the distance between them will turn out to be four hundred arm-lengths.

Salviati: Would there be any way to make these two shots come out equal?

Simplicio: I know of no way except to hold the carriage still.

Salviati: That's obvious. But I'm asking with the carriage running at full speed.

Simplicio: You'd have to strengthen the bow when firing with the direction of travel, and then weaken it for firing against it.

Salviati: So there's yet another remedy after all. But by how much would you need to strengthen it, and by how much then weaken it?

Simplicio: In our example, where we supposed the bow shot three hundred arm-lengths, you'd need — for the shot with the direction of travel — to strengthen it so it shot four hundred, and for the other to weaken it so it shot no more than two hundred. That way each shot would come out at three hundred arm-lengths relative to the carriage, which with its own travel of a hundred arm-lengths — subtracting them from the four-hundred shot and adding them to the two-hundred — would bring both back to three hundred.

Salviati: But what effect does the greater or lesser strength of the bow have on the arrow?

Simplicio: A strong bow drives it with greater speed, a weaker one with less; and the same arrow flies farther one time than another in proportion to how much greater the speed with which it leaves the nock one time than the other.

Salviati: So, to make the arrow — fired equally one way and the other — move away from the running carriage by the same amount, if in the first shot of the proposed example it leaves with, say, four degrees of speed, in the other shot it must leave with only two. But if you use the same bow, it always gets three degrees from it.

Simplicio: Just so; and that's why, firing with the same bow while the carriage is running, the shots can't come out equal.

Salviati: I forgot to ask with what speed we're supposing, in this particular experiment, that the carriage runs.

Simplicio: We have to suppose the carriage's speed at one degree, compared with the bow's, which is three.

Salviati: Yes, yes, that makes the reckoning come out right. But tell me: when the carriage runs, don't all the things in the carriage move with the same speed too?

Simplicio: Without a doubt.

Salviati: So the bolt too, and the bow, and the string it's drawn on.

Simplicio: Just so.

Salviati: So, in discharging the bolt toward the carriage's direction of travel, the bow impresses its three degrees of speed on a bolt that already has one degree — thanks to the carriage, which carries it that way with just that speed — so that as it leaves the nock it finds itself with four degrees of speed. On the contrary, firing the other way, the same bow confers its same three degrees on a bolt that's moving the opposite way with one degree, so that as it parts from the string it's left with only two degrees of speed. But you yourself have already granted that to make the shots equal the bolt must leave one time with four degrees and the other with two. So, without changing the bow, it's the carriage's own motion that squares the accounts, and experience then seals the matter for those who wouldn't or couldn't grasp the reasoning. Now apply this discussion to the artillery, and you'll find that whether the Earth moves or stands still, shots fired with the same force must always come out equal, aimed in whatever direction you please.
