The error of Aristotle, of Ptolemy, of Tycho, of yourself, and of everyone else, is rooted in that fixed and inveterate impression that the Earth stands still — an impression you can't or don't know how to strip off, not even when you want to philosophize about what would happen supposing the Earth did move. It's the same in the other argument: not considering that while the stone is on the tower it does, as regards moving or not moving, exactly what the terrestrial globe does — because you have it fixed in your mind that the Earth stands still — you reason about the stone's fall always as though it set off from rest. Whereas you ought to say: if the Earth stands still, the stone sets off from rest and descends straight down; but if the Earth moves, the stone likewise moves with equal speed, and doesn't set off from rest, but from a motion equal to the Earth's, with which it blends the added downward motion and composes a slanting one.

Simplicio: But, good God, if it moves on a slant, how is it I see it move straight down and perpendicular? This is simply to deny plain sense; and if we're not to trust the senses, by what other door are we to enter into philosophizing?

Salviati: With respect to the Earth, the tower, and ourselves — all of which move together, with the daily rotation, along with the stone — the daily rotation is as if it weren't there: it stays imperceptible, undetectable, without any effect at all, and the only motion left observable to us is the one we don't share, which is the stone's coming down grazing the tower. You're not the first to feel great reluctance in grasping this — that motion has no effect among the things that share in it.

Sagredo: Now I recall a certain fancy of mine that passed through my imagination one day while I was sailing on the voyage to Aleppo, where I was going as consul of our nation. It might perhaps be of some help in explaining how motion shared in common has no effect and is as if it weren't there for all who share in it. And if it please Simplicio, I'd like to discuss with him what I was daydreaming then, all by myself.

Simplicio: The novelty of the things I'm hearing makes me not just tolerant but curious to listen. So do go on.

Sagredo: If the point of a writing pen that had been aboard ship throughout my whole voyage from Venice to Alexandretta had had the power to leave a visible trace of its entire journey, what mark, what track, what line would it have left?

Simplicio: It would have left a line stretched from Venice all the way there — not perfectly straight, or rather, not stretched in a perfect arc of a circle, but here more and there less wavering, according as the vessel had pitched now more, now less. But this bending, in some places by an arm-length or two, right or left, up or down, over a length of many hundreds of miles, would have made only a small alteration in the whole stretch of the line — so slight it would scarcely have been noticeable, and without any error worth mentioning you could have called it a part of a perfect arc.

Sagredo: So the true, true, very truest motion of that pen-point would also have been a perfect arc of a circle, provided the vessel's motion, setting aside the tossing of the waves, had been calm and smooth. And if I had held that same pen continually in my hand, and only now and then moved it a finger's-width or two this way or that, what alteration would I have made to that main and very long stretch?

Simplicio: Less than the deviation, here and there, from absolute straightness by the breadth of a flea's eye would make to a straight line a thousand arm-lengths long.

Sagredo: So if a painter, on setting out from port, had begun to draw on a sheet of paper with that pen, and continued the drawing all the way to Alexandretta, he could have derived from the pen's motion a whole story of many figures perfectly outlined and cross-hatched in a thousand thousand directions, with landscapes, buildings, animals, and other things — even though the whole true, real, and essential movement traced by the point of that pen would have been nothing but one very long but utterly simple line. And as for the painter's own proper work, he would have drawn exactly the same thing, to a hair, had the ship stood still. That no trace of the pen's very long motion remains except those strokes marked on the paper — the reason is that the great motion from Venice to Alexandretta was shared in common by the paper, the pen, and everything else aboard the ship. But the little motions back and forth, right and left, imparted by the painter's fingers to the pen and not to the sheet, being proper to the pen alone, could leave a trace of themselves on the paper, which stayed motionless with respect to those movements. In the same way it's true that, the Earth moving, the stone's motion in coming down was really a long stretch of many hundreds and even many thousands of arm-lengths; and if it could have marked in still air, or on some other surface, the track of its course, it would have left a very long slanting line. But that part of this whole motion which is shared by the stone, the tower, and ourselves stays imperceptible to us, as if it weren't there, and the only part that stays observable is the one neither the tower nor we share — which in the end is what the stone, in falling, measures the tower by.

Salviati: A very subtle thought for explaining this point, which many find quite hard to grasp. Now, if Simplicio has nothing further to reply, we can move on to the other experiences, whose solution will draw no small ease from what's been laid out so far.

Simplicio: I have nothing more to say, and I was half lost in that image, and in thinking how those strokes drawn in so many directions — here, there, up, down, back and forth, and interwoven with a hundred thousand curlicues — are, in essence and in strictest reality, nothing but little pieces of a single line drawn all in one same direction, with no other alteration than a slight leaning, now and then, a little to right and left from the perfectly straight track, and the pen-point moving now faster, now slower, but with the least inequality. And I consider that a letter would be written the same way; and that those more elegant penmen who, to show off the freedom of their hand, without lifting the pen from the paper trace in a single stroke, with a thousand thousand windings, a lovely interlacing — if they were in a boat running swiftly, they would convert the whole motion of the pen (which in essence is a single line drawn all toward the same side, and very little bent or leaning from perfect straightness) into a mere flourish. And I'm delighted that Sagredo has awakened this thought in me. So let's press on, for the hope of hearing more of the same will keep me more attentive.

Sagredo: If you're curious to hear subtleties of this kind, which don't occur to just anyone, we've no shortage of them — especially in this matter of navigation. Won't you find it a fine thought, this one that occurred to me on that same voyage, when I noticed that the ship's mast, without breaking or bending, had made a longer journey with its top — that is, with the crow's-nest — than with its foot? Because the top, being farther from the center of the Earth than the foot is, came to describe the arc of a larger circle than the circle the foot passed through.

Simplicio: And so, when a man walks, he makes a longer journey with his head than with his feet?

Sagredo: You've penetrated it beautifully, by yourself and by your own wit. But let's not interrupt Salviati.

Salviati: I'm glad to see Simplicio getting some training — provided, that is, the thought is his own, and he didn't learn it from a certain little book of conclusions, where there are several others no less pretty and clever. Let's go on to speak of the artillery aimed straight up, perpendicular to the horizon — that is, of the shot toward our zenith — and finally of the ball's return down the same line onto the same piece, even though during the long while it stays separated from the piece the Earth has carried it many miles toward the east; so it would seem that over so much ground the ball ought to fall far from the piece, toward the west. This doesn't happen; therefore the artillery, without having moved, has waited for it. The solution is the same as that of the stone falling from the tower, and the whole fallacy and equivocation consists in always supposing as true the very thing in question — because our opponent has it firmly in his head that the ball sets off from rest when it's driven out of the piece by the fire, and to set off from a state of rest can only be assumed on the supposition that the terrestrial globe is at rest, which is then the very conclusion under dispute. So I reply once more that those who make the Earth mobile answer that the artillery and the ball inside it share the same motion the Earth has — indeed, that they have this motion, along with her, by nature — and that the ball therefore doesn't set off from rest at all, but joined to its motion around the center, which the upward projection neither takes away nor hinders. In this way, following the universal motion of the Earth toward the east, it stays continually over the same piece, both in rising and in returning. And you'll see the same thing happen by making the experiment aboard ship with a ball shot straight up with a crossbow, which returns to the same place whether the ship moves or stands still.

Sagredo: This satisfies everything perfectly. But since I've seen that Simplicio takes pleasure in certain clever tricks for catching (as they say) his companion, I want to ask him whether — supposing for now that the Earth stands still, and on it the artillery raised perpendicularly and aimed at our zenith — he has any difficulty in understanding that this is the true perpendicular shot, and that the ball, in setting off and in returning, will travel along the same straight line, always understanding all external and accidental impediments removed.

Simplicio: I follow you — that's exactly how the thing must turn out.

Sagredo: But suppose the cannon were set up not straight up, but tilted off to one side — what would the ball's motion be then? Would it perhaps travel, as in the other shot, along the vertical line, and then come back down along that same line?

Simplicio: No, it wouldn't do that. Once it left the gun it would keep moving along the straight line that continues the line of the barrel — except insofar as its own weight would pull it down, away from that line, toward the ground.

Sagredo: So the line of the barrel is what governs the ball's motion; the ball doesn't move — and wouldn't move — outside that line, unless its own weight pulled it downward. And that's why, when the barrel is set straight up and the ball is driven upward, it comes back down along that same straight line: because the motion the ball gets from its heaviness is downward along that very vertical. So the ball's journey outside the gun continues the line of that little stretch of journey it made inside the gun — isn't that so?

Simplicio: So it seems to me.

Sagredo: Now picture the barrel standing straight up, and the Earth turning on itself with the daily rotation, carrying the cannon along with it. Tell me: once the powder is fired, what will the ball's motion be inside the barrel?

Simplicio: It will be a straight, vertical motion, since the barrel is aimed straight up.

Sagredo: Look carefully, because I don't think it will be vertical at all. It would indeed be vertical if the Earth stood still, because then the ball would have no motion except what the firing gives it. But if the Earth turns, the ball inside the gun already shares the daily rotation too; so when the push of the powder comes on top of that, it travels from the breech of the gun to the muzzle with two motions, and out of the compound of the two it turns out that the path traced by the ball's center of gravity is a slanted line. To make it clearer: let the cannon AC stand upright, with the ball B inside it. Plainly, if the gun stays still and is fired, the ball will come out at the muzzle A, and its center, traveling up the barrel, will have traced the vertical line BA, and it will go on following that same line outside the gun, moving up toward the point overhead. But if the Earth were turning, and so carried the cannon along with it, then during the time the ball driven by the powder moves up the barrel, the cannon carried by the Earth would pass to the position DE, and the ball B, as it emerged, would be at the mouth D; and the motion of the ball's center would have followed the line BD — no longer vertical, but slanted toward the east. And since (as we've already concluded) the ball must go on moving through the air along the direction of the motion it made inside the gun, its flight will follow the slant of the line BD: and so it won't be vertical after all, but tilted toward the east — toward where the gun itself is also traveling — so that the ball can keep up with the motion of the Earth and of the gun. And there, Simplicio, I've shown you how the shot that seemed bound to go straight up doesn't, after all.

Simplicio: I'm not quite satisfied about this business. What about you, Salviati?

Salviati: I'm partly satisfied; but I have some scruple or other — God grant I can put it into words. It seems to me that, by what's just been said, when the gun points straight up and the Earth moves, the ball ought not only to fail to fall back far from the gun toward the west, as Aristotle and Tycho want, but not even back onto the gun, as you'd have it — rather, quite far off toward the east. Because, on your explanation, it would have two motions, both driving it in that direction together: the shared motion of the Earth, which carries the cannon and the ball from CA toward ED, and the firing, which drives it along the slanted line BD — both motions eastward, and so greater than the Earth's motion alone.

Sagredo: No, sir. The motion that carries the ball eastward comes entirely from the Earth; the firing has no part in it. And the motion that drives the ball upward comes entirely from the firing, with which the Earth has nothing to do. To see that this is true: don't fire, and the ball will never leave the gun, nor rise so much as a hair; and likewise, stop the Earth and fire — the ball, without leaning aside at all, will go straight up the vertical. So the ball has two motions, one upward and one circular, which together make up the slanting line BD: the upward push is all from the firing, the circular is all from the Earth and equal to the Earth's; and because it's equal, the ball stays always straight above the mouth of the gun, and in the end falls back into it. And since it stays always over the line of the barrel, it also keeps appearing continuously over the head of anyone standing near the gun — and so it looks to us as if it rises exactly straight up toward the point overhead.

Simplicio: I have another difficulty left. Since the ball's motion inside the gun is extremely fast, it doesn't seem possible that in that instant of time the shift of the cannon from CA to ED could give the slanting line CD enough tilt for the ball to be able, afterward, through the air, to keep pace with the Earth's course.

Sagredo: You're mistaken on several counts. First, the tilt of the diagonal CD is, I believe, much greater than you imagine, because I hold it beyond doubt that the speed of the Earth's motion — not only at the equator, but even at our own latitude — is greater than that of the ball while it moves inside the gun; so that the interval CE would be absolutely greater than the whole length of the gun, and the tilt of the diagonal, consequently, greater than half a right angle. But whether the Earth's speed is small or large compared with the firing's, that doesn't matter in the least: for if the Earth's speed is small, and so the tilt of the diagonal is small, then only a small tilt is needed to make the ball keep to its flight over the gun. In short, if you consider it attentively, you'll see that the motion of the Earth, by carrying the gun along with it from CA to ED, gives the diagonal CD exactly as much tilt — less or more — as is required to adjust the shot to its need. But you're mistaken in a second way, when you'd credit the ball's ability to keep up with the Earth's motion to the impetus of the firing; there you fall back into the very error Salviati seemed to slip into a moment ago. For keeping up with the Earth is that most ancient and perpetual motion, shared indelibly and inseparably by the ball itself, as by a thing that is earthly and by its own nature possesses that motion and always will.

Salviati: Let's set our minds at rest, Simplicio, because the business really does work just like this. And now, from this discussion, I've come to understand the reason behind a hunting puzzle of those fowlers who bring down birds on the wing with the harquebus — a shoulder gun. I'd imagined that to bag the bird they aimed the sight some distance ahead of it, leading it by a certain interval — more or less according to the speed of the flight and the bird's distance — so that, firing and sending the ball straight along the line of sight, the ball with its motion and the bird with its flight would arrive at the same point at the same moment, and so meet. But when I asked one of them whether that was their practice, he told me no: the trick was far easier and surer, and they do exactly the same thing as when they shoot at a bird sitting still — that is, they fix the sight on the flying bird and follow it by swinging the gun, keeping the sight always on it until they fire, and so they hit the flying birds just like the still ones. So that motion, slow though it is, which the gun makes as it turns to follow the bird's flight with the sight, must be communicated to the ball as well, and must join in the ball with the other motion from the firing — so that the ball gets from the firing its straight motion upward, and from the barrel its leaning to follow the bird's flight, just as was said just now about the artillery shot; where the ball gets from the firing its going up toward the point overhead, and from the Earth's motion its bending toward the east, and out of both makes a compound that follows the Earth's course and that, to anyone watching, seems only to go straight up, coming back down afterward along the same line. So keeping the sight steadily fixed on the target is what makes the shot strike true; and to keep the sight on the mark: if the target stands still, the barrel must be held still too; and if the target moves, the barrel is kept on the mark by moving with it. And on this depends the proper answer to that other argument about firing artillery at a target set toward the south or toward the north — where they urged that if the Earth moved, the shots would all veer off toward the west, because in the time the ball, once out of the gun, travels through the air to the mark, the mark, carried eastward, leaves the ball behind to the west. So I answer by asking: once the cannon has been adjusted to the mark and left just so, does it go on aiming always at the same mark, whether the Earth moves or stands still? The answer must be that the aim doesn't change at all: for if the mark stands still, the cannon likewise stands still, and if the mark, carried by the Earth, moves, the cannon moves right along with it in the same measure; and since the aim is kept, the shot always comes out true, as is clear from what's been said above.

Sagredo: Hold on a moment, please, Salviati, while I put forward a thought that's stirred in me about these shooters of birds on the wing. The way they work is, I believe, just as you say, and I believe the result — hitting the bird — follows just as you describe; but it doesn't seem to me that this operation is entirely of a piece with that of the artillery shots, which must strike both when the gun and the target are in motion and when both are at common rest. And the differences seem to me these. In the artillery shot, the gun and the target move with equal speed, both being carried by the motion of the terrestrial globe; and even if sometimes the gun's being planted more toward the pole than the target — and so its motion somewhat slower, as made in a smaller circle — such a difference is imperceptible, given how little distance there is from gun to mark. But in the fowler's shot, the motion of the gun, with which he follows the bird, is extremely slow compared with the bird's flight; from which it seems to me to follow that the small motion the turning of the barrel gives to the ball inside it cannot, once the ball is out, multiply itself through the air up to the speed of the bird's flight, so that the ball would stay always aimed at it — rather, it seems the ball must fall behind and be left at the bird's tail. Add to this that in this case the air through which the ball has to pass isn't supposed to share the bird's motion; whereas in the artillery's case the gun and the target and the air between them all share equally in the universal daily motion. So for the fowler's hitting, I'd believe the causes were — besides following the flight with the motion of the barrel — leading it somewhat, by keeping the sight ahead; and besides that, firing (as I believe) not with a single ball but with a good number of little pellets, which, spreading through the air, take up a very large space; and besides this, the extreme speed with which, coming out of the barrel, they're carried to the bird.

Salviati: And there's how far the flight of Sagredo's wit outruns and forestalls the slowness of mine — which would perhaps have noticed these disparities, but not without a long application of thought. Now, coming back to our subject, we still have to consider the point-blank shots toward the east and toward the west. The first of these, if the Earth moved, ought always to come out high above the target, and the second low; seeing that, by the daily rotation, the eastern parts of the Earth are continually sinking below the tangent line parallel to the horizon — which is why the eastern stars appear to us to rise — while, on the contrary, the western parts are rising, so that the western stars appear to sink. And so the shots aimed along that tangent at an eastern target, which sinks while the ball travels along the tangent, ought to come out high; and the western ones low, because the target rises while the ball runs along the tangent. The answer is like the others: just as the eastern target, by the Earth's motion, keeps sinking below a tangent that stayed fixed, so likewise the gun, for the same reason, keeps tilting and goes on aiming always at the same target, so that the shots come out true. But here it seems a fitting occasion to point out a certain generosity — shown, perhaps, with too much liberality — by the followers of Copernicus toward the opposing side: I mean, granting them as sure and certain some experiments the opponents have in truth never made — such as, for example, that of the bodies dropping from the mast of the ship while it's in motion, and many others; among which I hold it for certain that one is this — testing whether the eastern artillery shots come out high and the western low. And because I believe they've never made it, I'd like them to tell me what difference they think ought to be seen between these same shots, whether the Earth is set at rest or set in motion; and let Simplicio answer for them now.

Simplicio: I won't presume to answer as soundly as someone more knowledgeable than me perhaps could, but I'll say what I think, off the top of my head, they would answer — which is, in effect, what's already been brought up: namely, that if the Earth moved, the eastern shots would always come out high, and so on, since the ball, as seems likely, must move along the tangent.

Salviati: But if I said that this is in fact what happens, how would you go about refuting what I said?

Simplicio: One would have to come to experiment to clear it up.

Salviati: But do you believe you'd find a gunner so skilled that he'd undertake to hit the target with every single shot at a distance of, say, five hundred braccia?

Simplicio: No, sir; and I don't think there'd be anyone, however expert, who'd promise not to miss, on average, by more than a braccio.

Salviati: How, then, could we settle what we're in doubt about with shots as unreliable as that?

Simplicio: We could settle it in two ways: one, by firing many shots; the other, because, given the great speed of the Earth's motion, the deviation from the target would, in my opinion, be enormous.

Salviati: Enormous — that is, much more than a braccio; seeing that a variation of that much, and even more, is admitted to happen ordinarily even when the terrestrial globe is at rest.

Simplicio: I firmly believe the variation would be much greater.

Salviati: Now, for our own satisfaction, let's do a bit of rough calculation, if you like — one that will also serve us (if the reckoning comes out as I hope) as a warning not to let ourselves be so easily carried away, as the saying goes, by the shouting, giving our assent to everything that first presents itself to our fancy. And, to give every advantage to the Peripatetics and the Tychonians, let's imagine ourselves at the equator, firing point-blank westward with a culverin at a target five hundred braccia away. First let's find, roughly (as I said), how much time the ball takes, once out of the gun, to reach the mark — which we know to be very brief, and certainly no more than the time in which a man on foot takes two steps; and this is still less than one second of an hour. For, granting the walker goes three miles an hour, which is nine thousand braccia, and since an hour holds three thousand six hundred seconds, that comes to two and a half steps in a second: so a second is more than the time of the ball's motion. And since the daily revolution is twenty-four hours, the western horizon rises fifteen degrees an hour — that is, fifteen first minutes of a degree for one first minute of an hour, that is, fifteen seconds of a degree for one second of an hour; and since one second is the time of the shot, in this time the western horizon rises fifteen seconds of a degree, and so does the target: fifteen seconds, that is, of that circle whose radius is five hundred braccia (for that's what we set the target's distance from the culverin to be). Now let's look in the table of arcs and chords — and here's Copernicus's book, right at hand — for what part the chord of fifteen seconds is of a radius of five hundred braccia. Here we see that the chord of one first minute is less than thirty of the parts of which the radius holds a hundred thousand; so, of those same parts, the chord of one second minute will be less than half — that is, less than one of the parts of which the radius is two hundred thousand — and therefore the chord of fifteen seconds will be less than fifteen of those same two-hundred-thousandth parts. Now fifteen of those two-hundred-thousandth parts, taken of a radius of five hundred braccia, come to less than four hundredths of a braccio; and so the rise of the target during the ball's flight is less than four hundredths — that is, a twenty-fifth — of a braccio, and thus about a finger's width. And a single finger's width, consequently, will be the deviation of each westward shot, if the daily rotation belonged to the Earth. Now, if I tell you that this deviation actually happens in every shot — I mean that the shots strike a finger's width lower than they would if the Earth didn't move — how, Simplicio, would you go about convincing me, showing me by experiment that it doesn't happen? Don't you see it's impossible to shake me off, unless you first find a way of aiming so exact that you never miss by a hair? Because, so long as the shots come out varying by whole braccia, as they in fact do, I'll always say that contained in each of those variations is that one finger's width, caused by the motion of the Earth.

Sagredo: Forgive me, Salviati, you're too generous; for I'd say to the Peripatetics that even if every shot struck the very center of the target, that wouldn't contradict the motion of the Earth in the least: because the gunners have always trained themselves to fit the aim to the target, and have made a practice of setting the gun on the mark in such a way that they hit it, given the motion of the Earth; and I say that if the Earth stopped, the shots would not come out true, but the western ones would come out high and the eastern low. Now let Simplicio convince me.

Salviati: A subtlety worthy of Sagredo. But whether this variation is to be seen in the Earth's motion or in its rest — since it can only be extremely tiny — it can only stay submerged among the very large ones that continually happen through many accidents. And let all this be said and granted, in full measure, to Simplicio, and only as a warning of how cautious one must be in granting as true so many experiments to people who have never made them, but boldly put them forward just as they'd need them to be, to serve their cause. I say this is thrown in as a bonus to Simplicio, because the plain truth is that, as regards the effects of these shots, exactly the same thing must happen whether the terrestrial globe is in motion or at rest — just as it will with all the other experiments brought forward, and that can be brought forward, which have at first sight some semblance of truth only insofar as the old, ingrained notion of the Earth's immobility keeps us among the ambiguities.

Sagredo: For my part, I'm fully satisfied up to this point. I understand perfectly well that anyone who fixes in his imagination this general sharing of the daily rotation among all earthly things — a rotation that naturally belongs to them all, in just the way the old conception supposed that rest around the center belonged to them — will have no trouble at all making out the fallacy and the equivocation that made the arguments brought against us seem conclusive. There's only one scruple I have left, the one I hinted at earlier, about the flight of birds. Birds are living creatures, so they can move themselves at will through a hundred thousand different motions, and can keep themselves aloft, cut off from the Earth, hanging in the air for a long time, and go wandering about with the most disorderly twists and turns. So I don't quite grasp how, in such a great jumble of motions, they don't get confused and lose that first shared motion — and how, once they've been stripped of it, they can make it up and match it again by flying, and keep pace with the towers and the trees that go rushing eastward at such a headlong speed. And I mean truly headlong: on the greatest circle of the globe it comes to a little under a thousand miles an hour, of which I don't believe a swallow's flight makes even fifty.

Salviati: If the birds had to keep pace with the racing trees by the help of their wings, they'd be in a sorry state; and if they were stripped of the universal rotation, they'd be left so far behind, and their course toward the west would look so furious — to anyone who could see them — that it would far outstrip an arrow's. But I don't think we could make them out at all, any more than we can see cannonballs while they streak through the air, driven by the fury of the gunpowder. The truth is that the birds' own motion — I mean their flying — has nothing to do with the universal motion, to which it neither adds help nor gives hindrance. What keeps that motion unaltered in the birds is the very air they go wandering through, which, following the Earth's spin naturally, carries the clouds along with it and likewise carries the birds and everything else hanging in it. So as for keeping up with the Earth, the birds needn't give it a thought — for that service, they could sleep forever.

Sagredo: That the air can carry the clouds along with it — those being materials very easily moved because of their lightness, and stripped of any contrary inclination, or rather materials that themselves share in the conditions and properties of the Earth — that I grasp without any difficulty. But that the birds, which, being living creatures, can move even with a motion contrary to the daily one, should have it restored to them by the air once they've broken it off — that seems to me a bit hard to swallow. Especially since they're solid, heavy bodies; and we, as was said above, see that stones and other heavy bodies stay stubborn against the push of the air, and even when they do let themselves be overcome, they never pick up as much speed as the wind that carries them.

Salviati: Let's not credit moving air with so little force, Sagredo. When it moves quickly it's powerful enough to shift and drive fully loaded ships, to uproot forests and topple towers — and yet in these very violent operations you can't say its motion is anywhere near as fast as that of the daily revolution.

Simplicio: So there it is — moving air can indeed keep up the motion of projectiles, in line with Aristotle's doctrine. And I did think it strange that he should have gone wrong on this particular point.

Salviati: It could, no doubt, if it were able to keep the motion up in itself. But just as, once the wind dies, the ships stop traveling and the trees stop being uprooted, so, since the motion isn't kept up in the air after the stone has left the hand and the arm has stopped, it follows that something other than the air is what makes the projectile move.

Simplicio: And how is it that when the wind stops, the ship's motion stops? On the contrary, you can see that even when the wind has died and the sails have been furled, the vessel keeps running on for whole miles.

Salviati: But that's against you, Simplicio, since once the air is stilled — the air that, striking the sails, was driving the ship — the vessel nevertheless keeps up its course without the help of the medium.

Simplicio: One might say the water was the medium that carried the ship along and kept up its motion.

Salviati: One might indeed say so — to say the exact opposite of the truth. The truth is that the water, with its great resistance to being parted by the body of the vessel, fights it with a great roar, and won't let it take on anything like the speed the wind would give it if the water's obstacle weren't there. You, Simplicio, can never have paid attention to the fury with which the water goes scraping around the boat while it's driven swiftly along by oars or by wind, running through still water. If you'd noticed such an effect, it wouldn't now occur to you to come out with such nonsense. And I'm beginning to suspect that up to now you've been one of the flock of those who, to learn how such matters proceed and to acquire knowledge of nature's effects, don't go out on boats or around crossbows and cannon, but shut themselves up in the study to leaf through the indexes and reference-lists, hunting to see whether Aristotle has said anything about it — and once they've made sure of the true sense of the text, they desire nothing further and reckon there's nothing else worth knowing.

Sagredo: A great happiness, and much to be envied. For if knowledge is naturally desired by all, and if to be is as good as to fancy oneself to be, then they enjoy an enormous good: they can persuade themselves that they understand and know all things — snapping their fingers at those who, knowing that they don't know what they don't know, and consequently seeing that they don't know even the tiniest little particle of what can be known, wear themselves out with late nights and contemplations, and grind themselves down over experiments and observations. But please, let's get back to our birds. On their account you'd said that air moving with tremendous speed could restore to them that part of the daily motion which, amid the play of their flights, they might have lost. To which I object that moving air doesn't seem able to give a solid, heavy body as much speed as it has itself; and since the air's speed is only equal to the Earth's, it didn't seem that the air would be enough to make good the loss in the birds' flight.

Salviati: Your argument has a good deal of the plausible about it in appearance, and to raise a doubt at just the right point is no mark of an ordinary mind. All the same, once you strip away the appearance, I think that in reality it hasn't a hair more force than the others already considered and dissolved.

Sagredo: There's no doubt whatever that when an argument isn't necessarily conclusive, its force can only be absolutely nothing; because when the conclusion holds necessarily in one way only, no reasoning of any weight can be produced for the other side.

Salviati: Your finding more difficulty in this case than in the others seems to me to hinge on the birds' being living creatures, and therefore able to use force at their own pleasure against the primary motion inborn in earthly things — in just the way we see, while they're alive, that they can fly upward too, a motion impossible for them as heavy bodies, whereas when dead they can only fall. And so you reckon that the reasons which hold for all the sorts of projectiles mentioned above can't hold for the birds. That's perfectly true; and because it's true, you don't see those projectiles doing what the birds do, Sagredo. For if from the top of the tower you let a dead bird and a live one drop, the dead one will do just what a stone does — it will first follow the general daily motion, and then the downward motion, as a heavy body. But if the bird let go is alive, what's to stop it — the daily motion always remaining in it — from throwing itself, by beating its wings, toward whatever quarter of the horizon it pleases? And this new motion, being its own particular one and not shared by us, must become perceptible to us. And once it has moved by its flight toward the west, what's to stop it from returning to the tower with an equal beating of feathers? For, in the end, setting off in flight toward the west was nothing but subtracting one single degree from the daily motion, which has, say, ten degrees of speed, leaving it nine while it was flying; and once it settled on the ground, the ten shared degrees came back to it, to which, by flying eastward, it could add one, and with the eleven return to the top of the tower. In sum, if we consider carefully and contemplate more intimately the effects of the birds' flying, they differ in nothing from projectiles moving toward every part of the world, except that these are moved by an external thrower and those by an internal principle.

And here, as a final seal on the nullity of all the experiences brought forward, I think it's the time and place to show the way to test them all with the greatest ease. Shut yourself up with a friend in the main cabin below decks on some large ship, and have with you there some flies, butterflies, and other small flying creatures. Have a big bowl of water with some fish in it; hang up a little bucket that drips water, drop by drop, into another vessel with a narrow neck placed underneath. Then, with the ship standing still, watch carefully how those little flying creatures go with equal speed toward every part of the cabin; the fish will be seen swimming indifferently in all directions; the falling drops will all go into the vessel placed below; and if you throw something to your friend, you won't need to throw it any harder toward one side than the other, when the distances are equal; and if you jump, as they say, with both feet together, you'll cover equal spaces in every direction. Once you've carefully observed all these things — though there's no doubt they must happen this way while the ship stands still — have the ship move with as much speed as you please. So long as the motion is uniform and not rocking this way and that, you won't recognize the slightest change in any of the effects just named, nor from any of them will you be able to tell whether the ship is moving or standing still. In jumping, you'll cover the same distances on the deck as before, and you won't make bigger jumps toward the stern than toward the bow just because the ship is moving very fast — even though, during the time you're in the air, the deck beneath you runs the opposite way to your jump. If you throw something to your companion, you won't need any more force to reach him whether he's toward the bow and you toward the stern or the other way around. The drops will fall as before into the lower vessel, without a single one falling toward the stern, even though, while the drop is in the air, the ship runs on many handbreadths. The fish in their water will swim toward the front of the bowl with no more effort than toward the back, and will come with equal ease to food set on any spot along the bowl's rim. And finally the butterflies and the flies will go on with their flights indifferently toward every part, and it will never happen that they gather toward the wall facing the stern, as though tired out from keeping up with the ship's swift course — from which they've been separated a long while by staying up in the air. And if you burn a little grain of incense so that a bit of smoke rises, you'll see it climb upward and, like a little cloud, hang there, moving no more toward one side than the other. The cause of all this correspondence of effects is that the ship's motion is shared by everything contained in it, and by the air too — which is why I said you should be below decks. If you were up above, in the open air that doesn't follow the ship's course, you'd see more or less noticeable differences in some of the effects named: there's no doubt the smoke would be left behind, along with the air itself; the flies too and the butterflies, hindered by the air, wouldn't be able to follow the ship's motion once they'd separated from it by any considerable distance. But keeping close by, they'd follow the ship without any hindrance or effort, because the ship — being of a rambling, hollowed-out shape — carries along with it part of the air nearest to it; and for a like reason we sometimes see, in riding post, the pestering flies and horseflies following the horses, flying now to this part of their body and now to that. But with the falling drops the difference would be very slight, and in the jumps and in the heavy projectiles altogether imperceptible.

Sagredo: Even though it never occurred to me, while sailing, to make these observations on purpose, still I'm more than sure they'd turn out in the way you've described. In confirmation of which, I remember that a hundred times, being in my cabin, I've wondered whether the ship was moving or standing still; and sometimes, lost in a daydream, I've believed it was going one way when the motion was the opposite. So up to this point I'm satisfied, and entirely convinced of the nullity of all the experiences brought forward to prove the negative rather than the affirmative side of the Earth's rotation. There now remains the objection founded on seeing by experience how a swift whirling has the power to extrude and scatter the materials clinging to the machine that goes spinning around. Because of this it seemed to many — and to Ptolemy too — that if the Earth turned upon itself with such speed, the stones and the animals would have to be flung off toward the stars, and that the buildings couldn't be fastened to their foundations with mortar tough enough to keep them from suffering the same ruin.

Salviati: Before coming to the solution of this objection, I can't keep silent about something I've noticed a thousand times, not without a laugh, coming into the minds of almost all men at the first word they hear of this moving of the Earth. They've believed it so utterly fixed and motionless that not only have they never doubted that rest, but they've firmly believed that all other men, along with themselves, have reckoned it was created immovable and has stayed that way through all the centuries gone by. Settled in this notion, they're then astonished to hear that someone grants it motion — as if, after he had held it motionless, he foolishly supposed that only then, and not before, did it set itself in motion, at the moment when Pythagoras (or whoever it was) first said it moved. Now, that such a foolish thought — I mean the belief that those who admit the Earth's motion first held it fixed from its creation down to Pythagoras's time, and only made it mobile afterward, once Pythagoras reckoned it so — should find a place in the minds of common men of light judgment, doesn't surprise me. But that the Aristotles and the Ptolemies should have fallen into this childishness too seems to me a much stranger and more inexcusable simplicity.

Sagredo: So then, Salviati, you believe that Ptolemy thought he had to maintain, in his disputation, the stability of the Earth against men who, granting that it had been motionless down to Pythagoras's time, only then asserted that it had become mobile, when Pythagoras attributed motion to it?

Salviati: One can't believe otherwise, if we consider carefully the manner he uses in refuting their claim. His refutation consists in the demolition of buildings and the flinging of stones, of animals, and of men themselves toward the sky. And since such a ruin and hurling-off can't be done to buildings and animals that aren't first on the Earth, and men can't be set on the Earth nor buildings raised except while it stands still, it's clear from this that Ptolemy is proceeding against those who, having for some time granted rest to the Earth — that is, while the animals, the stones, and the builders could dwell on it and raise the palaces and the cities — then suddenly make it mobile, to the ruin and destruction of the buildings and the animals, and so on. For if he had undertaken to dispute against someone who attributed such a whirling to the Earth from its first creation, he'd have refuted him by saying that if the Earth had always moved, there could never have been established on it either beasts or men or stones, much less buildings raised or cities founded, and so on.

Simplicio: I don't quite grasp this Aristotelian and Ptolemaic inconsistency.

Salviati: Ptolemy either argues against those who have reckoned the Earth always mobile, or against those who have reckoned that it stood still for some time and then set itself in motion. If against the first, he ought to have said: "The Earth hasn't always moved, because there would never have been men or animals or buildings on the Earth, the terrestrial whirling not allowing them to dwell there." But since, in arguing, he says instead: "The Earth doesn't move, because the beasts, the men, and the buildings already set on the Earth would fall headlong" — he's supposing that the Earth was once found in such a state that it allowed the beasts and the men to dwell there and to build; which carries as a consequence that it was for some time at rest, that is, fit for the dwelling of animals and the raising of buildings. Are you now clear on what I've been trying to say?

Simplicio: I am and I'm not. But this matters little to the merit of the case, nor can a little slip of Ptolemy's, committed through inadvertence, be enough to move the Earth when it's motionless. But leaving the jokes aside, let's come at last to the nerve of the argument, which seems to me insoluble.

Salviati: And I, Simplicio, want to knot it up and tighten it still further, by showing even more palpably how it's true that heavy bodies, whirled with speed around a stable center, acquire an impetus to move away from that center, even when they have a natural propensity to go toward it. Tie a little bucket to the end of a cord, with water in it, and holding the other end firmly in your hand, making the cord and your arm the radius and the joint of your shoulder the center, swing the vessel quickly around so that it describes the circumference of a circle. Whether that circle is parallel to the horizon, or upright to it, or inclined in any way you please, in every case it will follow that the water doesn't spill out of the vessel; in fact, the one swinging it will always feel the cord pull and strain to get farther from the shoulder. And if you make a hole in the bottom of the bucket, you'll see the water spurt out no less toward the sky than sideways and toward the ground. And if instead of water you put in little pebbles, swinging them the same way, you'll feel them make the same force against the cord. And finally, we see children throw stones to a great distance by swinging around a piece of cane with the stone lodged in its tip — all arguments for the truth of the conclusion, namely that whirling confers on the moving body an impetus toward the circumference when the motion is fast; and therefore, if the Earth turned upon itself, the motion of its surface — especially toward the greatest circle, being incomparably faster than the ones just named — would have to extrude everything toward the sky.

Simplicio: The objection seems to me very well set up and tightly knotted, and it'll take something considerable, I believe, to shift it and untie it.

Salviati: Untying it depends on certain facts that you know and believe no less than I do; but because they don't come to mind, you don't see the solution. So without my teaching them to you — since you already know them — simply by reminding you of them I'll get you to resolve the objection yourself.

Simplicio: I've paid attention more than once to your way of reasoning, and it's stirred up a thought in me — that you lean toward that opinion of Plato's, that nostrum scire sit quoddam reminisci — that our knowing is a kind of recollection. So please, clear up this doubt for me by telling me what you really think.

Salviati: What I think of Plato's opinion I can convey to you both in words and in deeds. More than once already, in the discussions we've had so far, I've shown my hand by deed; I'll keep to the same style in the particular case we have in hand, which can then serve you as an example for grasping more easily my notion about how knowledge is acquired — that is, if we have time left over on another day, and if it won't bore Sagredo to have us make this digression.

Sagredo: On the contrary, it'll be most welcome, because I remember that when I studied logic I could never get my head around that so-vaunted demonstratio potissima — the "most powerful proof" — of Aristotle's.

Salviati: Let's go on, then. Simplicio, tell me: what is the motion of that little stone gripped in the notch of the staff while the boy swings it to throw it far?

Simplicio: The stone's motion, as long as it's in the notch, is circular — that is, it travels along an arc of a circle, whose fixed center is the joint of the shoulder, and whose radius is the staff together with the arm.

Salviati: And when the stone escapes from the staff, what is its motion? Does it go on continuing its earlier circular motion, or does it travel along some other line?

Simplicio: It doesn't go on moving in a circle any longer, because if it did it wouldn't get away from the thrower's shoulder — whereas we see it fly off very far indeed.

Salviati: By what motion, then, does it move?

Simplicio: Let me think about it a little, because I've never turned my mind to it before.

Salviati: Sagredo, a word in your ear: there's the quoddam reminisci out in the open field, plain as day. — You're thinking hard, Simplicio!

Simplicio: As I see it, the motion taken on as the stone leaves the notch can only be along a straight line — indeed it must necessarily be along a straight line, if we mean the pure acquired impetus alone. It bothered me a bit to see it trace an arc; but since that arc always bends downward, and not to any other side, I realize that the bending comes from the stone's own weight, which naturally pulls it down. The impressed impetus, I say without hesitation, is along a straight line.

Salviati: But along which straight line? For infinitely many, running off in every direction, can be drawn from the notch of the staff and from the point where the stone separates from it.

Simplicio: It moves along the one that lies in line with the motion the stone made together with the staff.

Salviati: The stone's motion, while it was in the notch, you've already said was circular; now, "circular" and "in a straight line" are at odds, since there's no straight part anywhere in a circular line.

Simplicio: I don't mean the projected motion is in line with the whole of the circular motion, but with that last point where the circular motion ended. I understand it inside myself, but I can't quite put it into words.

Salviati: And I too can see that you understand the thing, but you don't have the proper terms to express it. Well, those I can certainly teach you — teach you the words, that is, not the truths, which are things. And to let you feel it with your own hands, that you know the thing and only lack the terms to express it, tell me: when you fire a ball from a harquebus, in what direction does it acquire its impetus to travel?

Simplicio: It acquires the impetus to travel along that straight line which follows the line of the barrel — that is, one that veers neither right nor left, neither up nor down.

Salviati: Which in short amounts to saying that it makes no angle at all with the line of the straight motion made along the barrel.

Simplicio: That's what I meant to say.

Salviati: If, then, the line of the projectile's motion is to continue without making an angle upon the circular line it described while it was with the thrower, and if it must pass from this circular motion to straight motion, what will this straight line have to be?

Simplicio: It can only be the one that touches the circle at the point of separation, because all the others, it seems to me, would — if extended — cut the circumference, and so would contain some angle with it.

Salviati: You've reasoned very well, and shown yourself half a geometer. Keep it in memory, then, that your real idea is spelled out in these words: the projectile acquires the impetus to move along the tangent to the arc described by the thrower's motion, at the point where the projectile separates from the thrower.

Simplicio: I understand perfectly, and that's just what I wanted to say.

Salviati: Of a straight line that touches a circle, which of its points is the closest of all to the center of that circle?

Simplicio: The point of contact, without a doubt; because that one is on the circle's circumference, and the others outside it, and the points of the circumference are all equally distant from the center.

Salviati: Therefore a moving body, setting out from the point of contact and moving along the straight tangent, goes continually farther from the point of contact, and also from the center of the circle.

Simplicio: That's certainly so.

Salviati: Now, if you've kept in mind the propositions you've told me, put them back together and tell me what follows from them.

Simplicio: I don't think I'm so forgetful that I can't recall it. From what's been said it follows that the projectile, swung rapidly around by the thrower, on separating from it keeps the impetus to continue its motion along the straight line that touches the circle described by the thrower's motion, at the point of separation; and by this motion the projectile goes ever farther from the center of the circle described by the thrower's motion.

Salviati: So by now you know the reason why heavy bodies clinging to the surface of a rapidly turning wheel are extruded — flung off, I mean, beyond the circumference, ever farther from the center.

Simplicio: Of this I feel I've got a pretty good grasp; but this new understanding rather increases than lessens my disbelief that the Earth could turn with such speed without flinging stones, animals, and the rest up toward the sky.

Salviati: In the same way you've known things up to now, you'll know — indeed you already know — the rest too; and by thinking it over you'd recall it on your own again. But to save time, I'll help you remember it. So far you've known by yourself that the thrower's circular motion impresses on the projectile the impetus to move (whenever they happen to separate) along the straight tangent to the circle of the motion, at the point of separation, and that, continuing its motion along that tangent, it goes ever farther from the thrower. And you've said that the projectile would keep moving along that straight line if its own weight didn't add to it a downward inclination, from which comes the curving of the line of motion. It also seems to me you've known by yourself that this bending always tends toward the center of the Earth, because that's where all heavy bodies tend. Now I go a little further, and ask you whether the moving body, after separation, in continuing its straight motion, goes ever equally farther from the center — or, if you like, from the circumference — of that circle of which the earlier motion was a part; which is the same as asking whether a moving body that sets out from the point of the tangent, and moves along that tangent, gets equally farther from the point of contact and from the circumference of the circle.

Simplicio: No, sir; because near the point of contact the tangent departs very little from the circumference, with which it contains a very tight angle, but as it goes farther and farther, the separation always grows in greater proportion. So that in a circle with, say, a diameter of ten braccia, a point on the tangent two palms away from the contact would be found three or four times farther from the circumference than a point one palm away from the touch; and the point half a palm away, likewise, I believe, would barely be a quarter as far as the second; so that within a finger or two of the contact, one can hardly make out that the tangent is separated from the circumference at all.

Salviati: So that the projectile's departure from the circumference of the earlier circular motion is, at the very start, extremely small?

Simplicio: Almost imperceptible.

Salviati: Now tell me: the projectile that from the thrower's motion receives the impetus to move along the straight tangent — and would still go on along it if its own weight didn't pull it down — how long does it take, after separation, to begin to bend downward?

Simplicio: I believe it begins at once, because having nothing to hold it up, it can't be that its own weight doesn't act.

Salviati: So that if that stone flung from that wheel turning at great speed had a natural tendency to move toward the center of that same wheel, just as it has one to move toward the center of the Earth, it would be an easy thing for it to return to the wheel — or rather, for it never to leave it; because, at the start of separation, the departure being so very minimal, thanks to the infinite sharpness of the angle of contact, every least bit of inclination drawing it back toward the wheel's center would be enough to keep it on the circumference.

Simplicio: I have no doubt at all that, granting what is not and cannot be — namely, that the inclination of those heavy bodies were to go to the center of that wheel — they wouldn't be extruded or flung off.

Salviati: Nor do I suppose, or need to suppose, what is not, because I don't want to deny that stones do get flung off; I'm just saying it by way of supposition, so that you'll tell me the rest. Now picture the Earth as the great wheel that, turning with such speed, is to fling off the stones. You've already been able to tell me very well that the projected motion will have to be along that straight line which touches the Earth at the point of separation. And how far, notably, does this tangent get from the surface of the terrestrial globe?

Simplicio: I believe that in a thousand braccia it doesn't get a finger's width away.

Salviati: And don't you say that the projectile, pulled by its own weight, bends away from the tangent toward the center of the Earth?

Simplicio: I've said it — and I'll say the rest too, and I understand perfectly that the stone won't separate from the Earth, since its departure at the start would be so very minimal that the stone's inclination to move toward the center of the Earth is fully a thousand times greater; and in this case that center is also the center of the wheel. And really one is forced to grant that stones, animals, and other heavy bodies can't be extruded. But now the very light things give me a new difficulty — the ones that have a very feeble inclination to sink toward the center; so that, since they lack the power to draw themselves back to the surface, I don't see why they wouldn't be flung off. And you know that ad destruendum sufficit unum — one instance is enough to destroy the claim.

Salviati: We'll satisfy this point too. But first tell me what you mean by "light things" — whether you mean stuff so truly light that it goes upward, or not absolutely light, but so slightly heavy that it does come down, only slowly; because if you mean the absolutely light ones, I'll let them be flung off even more than you'd like.

Simplicio: I mean the second kind — things like feathers, wool, cotton wadding, and the like, which the least force is enough to lift; yet we see them resting quite peacefully on the ground.

Salviati: As long as this feather has some natural tendency to descend toward the surface of the Earth, however slight it may be, I tell you it's enough to keep it from being lifted; and this isn't unknown to you either. So tell me: if the feather were flung off by the Earth's whirling, along what line would it move?

Simplicio: Along the tangent at the point of separation.

Salviati: And if it had to come back and rejoin, along what line would it move?

Simplicio: Along the one that goes from it to the center of the Earth.

Salviati: So that here two motions come into consideration: one of projection, which begins at the point of contact and follows along the tangent; and the other of the downward inclination, which begins at the projectile and goes along the secant toward the center. And for the projection to take place, the impetus along the tangent must prevail over the inclination along the secant — isn't that so?

Simplicio: So it seems to me.

Salviati: But what do you think must be present in the projecting motion, so that it prevails over that of the inclination, and from that follows the feather's detachment and departure from the Earth?

Simplicio: I don't know.

Salviati: How do you not know? Here the moving body is the same — the same feather; now how can one and the same moving body outdo itself in motion and prevail over itself?

Simplicio: I don't see how it could prevail over or yield to itself in motion, except by moving now faster and now slower.

Salviati: There, you see, you did know it after all. So then, if the feather's projection is to take place, and its motion along the tangent is to prevail over the motion along the secant, what must their speeds be?

Simplicio: The motion along the tangent must be greater than the other along the secant. Oh, poor me! But isn't it even a hundred thousand times greater — and not only than the downward motion of the feather, but even than that of the stone? And I, like a real simpleton, had let myself be persuaded that stones couldn't be flung off by the Earth's whirling! So I take it all back, and say that if the Earth moved, the stones, the elephants, the towers, and the cities would necessarily fly up toward the sky; and since that doesn't happen, I say the Earth does not move.

Salviati: Oh, Simplicio, you fly up so quickly that I'll start fearing more for you than for the feather. Calm down a little, and listen. If, to keep the stone or the feather attached to the surface of the Earth, it were necessary that its descent downward be more than — or as much as — the motion made along the tangent, you'd be right to say it would have to move as fast or faster along the secant downward as along the tangent toward the east. But didn't you tell me just now that a thousand braccia of distance along the tangent from the contact scarcely move it a finger's width from the circumference? It's not enough, then, for the motion along the tangent — which is the motion of the daily whirl — simply to be faster than the motion along the secant, which is the feather's downward motion; it has to be so much faster that the time enough to carry the feather, say, a thousand braccia along the tangent is too little for it to move a single finger's width downward along the secant. And this, I tell you, will never be — make that motion as fast, and this one as slow, as you please.

Simplicio: And why couldn't the one along the tangent be so fast that it gave the feather no time to reach the surface of the Earth?

Salviati: Try putting the case in definite terms, and I'll answer you. So tell me how much faster you think that motion would have to be than this one.

Simplicio: I'll say, for example, that if that one were a million times faster than this one, the feather — and the stone too — would be flung off.

Salviati: You say so, and you say what's false, only for a lack — not of logic, or of physics, or of metaphysics, but of geometry. Because if you understood only the first elements, you'd know that from the center of the circle one can draw a straight line out to the tangent that cuts it in such a way that the part of the tangent between the contact and the secant is one, two, three million times greater than that part of the secant left between the tangent and the circumference; and as the secant comes closer and closer to the contact, this ratio grows greater without limit. So there's no fear that, however fast the whirl and slow the downward motion, the feather — or anything else lighter — could begin to rise; because the downward inclination always overcomes the speed of the projection.

Sagredo: I'm not entirely satisfied about this business.

Salviati: I'll give you a thoroughly universal demonstration of it, and quite an easy one too. Let the given ratio be that of BA to C, and let BA be greater than C by as much as you like; and let there be the circle whose center is D, from which we must draw a secant such that the tangent bears to that secant the ratio of BA to C. Take AI, the third proportional of the two lines BA and C; and as BI is to IA, so make the diameter FE to EG; and from the point G draw the tangent GH. I say that what was required has been done, and that as BA is to C, so HG is to GE. For, since as BI is to IA so FE is to EG, then — componendo, combining the terms — as BA is to AI so FG is to GE; and because C is the mean proportional between BA and AI, and GH is the mean between FG and GE, therefore as BA is to C, so will FG be to GH, that is, HG to GE — which is what needed to be done.

Sagredo: I follow this demonstration; yet it doesn't entirely take away every scruple — rather, I feel a certain confusion turning about in my mind which, like a thick, dark fog, doesn't let me make out, with the clarity that's usually proper to mathematical reasoning, the plainness and necessity of the conclusion. And what confuses me is this. It's true that the spaces between the tangent and the circumference go on diminishing without limit toward the contact; but it's also true, on the other hand, that the moving body's tendency to descend keeps growing ever smaller in it the closer it finds itself to the first terminus of its descent — that is, to the state of rest — as is clear from what you declared to us, showing that a descending heavy body, setting out from rest, must pass through all the intermediate degrees of slowness between that rest and any given degree of speed, degrees that are smaller and smaller without limit. Add to this that this speed and tendency to motion goes on diminishing, for another reason, likewise without limit, and this comes about because the heaviness of the moving body itself can be diminished without limit. So that the causes that diminish the tendency to descend — and consequently favor the projection — are two, namely the lightness of the moving body and its nearness to the terminus of rest, both of them increasable without limit; and against them, on the other hand, stands the opposition of a single cause producing the projection, which, though it too is increasable without limit, I can't grasp how, all by itself, it wouldn't end up overcome by the union and coupling of the other two, likewise increasable without limit.

Salviati: A doubt worthy of Sagredo; and to clear it up, so that we may grasp it more distinctly — since you say yourself that you hold it only confusedly — let us sort it out by reducing it to a figure, which may perhaps also make it easier to resolve. So let us draw a line perpendicular toward the center, and let this be AC, and at right angles to it the horizontal AB, along which the motion of the projection would take place, and along which the projectile would keep traveling with uniform motion, if gravity did not draw it downward. Now imagine a straight line drawn from the point A, making any angle you please with AB, and let this be AE; and let us mark off on AB some equal spaces AF, FH, HK, and from them draw the perpendiculars FG, HI, KL up to AE. And because, as we've said before, a heavy body falling from rest keeps acquiring an ever greater degree of speed from one moment to the next, as the time itself increases, we can picture the spaces AF, FH, HK as representing equal times, and the perpendiculars FG, HI, KL as the degrees of speed acquired in those times — so that the degree of speed acquired in the whole time AK is as the line KL, compared with the degree HI acquired in the time AH, and the degree FG in the time AF; and these degrees KL, HI, FG have (as is plain) the same proportion as the times KA, HA, FA. And if other perpendiculars are drawn from points marked at will on the line FA, we shall always find smaller and smaller degrees, on to infinity, as we proceed toward the point A, which represents the first instant of time and the first state of rest. And this drawing-back toward A represents to us the first propensity toward downward motion, diminished to infinity by the body's approach to that first state of rest — an approach that can itself be increased to infinity.

Now let us find the other diminution of speed, which can likewise be carried on to infinity through the diminution of the body's heaviness; and this will be represented by drawing other lines from the point A, making angles smaller than the angle BAE — as this line AD would be, which, cutting the parallels KL, HI, FG at the points M, N, O, pictures for us the degrees FO, HN, KM acquired in the times AF, AH, AK, smaller than the other degrees FG, HI, KL acquired in the same times — but these last as if by a heavier body, and those first by a lighter one. And it's plain that by drawing the line EA toward AB, narrowing the angle EAB (which can be done to infinity, just as heaviness can be diminished to infinity), the speed of the falling body is likewise diminished to infinity, and with it the cause that hindered the projection. And so it seems that, from the union of these two grounds against the projection — both diminished to infinity — the projection cannot possibly be hindered.

And reducing the whole argument to a few words, we'll say: by narrowing the angle EAB the degrees of speed LK, IH, GF are diminished; and moreover, by drawing the parallels KL, HI, FG back toward the angle A, the same degrees are likewise diminished; and each of the two diminutions extends to infinity. Therefore the speed of the downward motion can be diminished so far and so far (being diminishable doubly to infinity) that it will not suffice to restore the body onto the circumference of the wheel, and consequently to make the projection be hindered and eliminated. On the other hand, to keep the projection from happening, the spaces through which the projectile must descend to rejoin the wheel would have to be made so short and narrow that, however slow — indeed, however infinitely diminished — the body's descent may be, it still suffices to carry it back; and so there would have to be found a diminution of those spaces made not only to infinity, but of such an infinity as would exceed the double infinity produced in the diminution of the falling body's speed. But how shall one magnitude be diminished more than another that is being diminished doubly to infinity? Now let Simplicio note how well one may philosophize about nature without geometry! The degrees of speed, diminished to infinity — both by the diminution of the body's heaviness and by the approach to the first terminus of motion, that is, to the state of rest — are always determinate, and correspond proportionally to the parallels contained between two straight lines meeting at an angle, whether the angle BAE or BAD, or another infinitely more acute, but always rectilinear. But the diminution of the spaces through which the body has to be carried back onto the circumference of the wheel is proportional to another sort of diminution altogether — one contained within lines that make an angle infinitely narrower and more acute than any acute rectilinear angle whatsoever. And here it is. Take any point C on the perpendicular AC, and making it the center, describe with the radius CA an arc AMP, which will cut the parallels that determine the degrees of speed, however small they may be and however contained within a most narrow rectilinear angle. Of these parallels, the parts that remain between the arc and the tangent AB are the amounts of the spaces and of the returns onto the wheel — always smaller, and smaller in ever greater proportion the closer they come to the point of contact; smaller, I say, than those parallels of which they are parts.

The parallels contained between the straight lines, as they draw back toward the angle, always diminish in the same proportion: as, for example, AH being divided in half at the point F, the parallel HI will be double FG, and subdividing FA in half, the parallel produced from the point of division will be half of FG, and continuing the subdivision to infinity, the successive parallels will always be half of the ones just before. But it does not go this way with the lines intercepted between the tangent and the circumference of the circle; for, making the same subdivision on FA and supposing, say, that the parallel coming from the point H were double the one coming from F, this one will then be more than double the following one; and continually, as we come toward the point of contact A, we shall find the preceding lines containing the next following ones three, four, ten, a hundred, a thousand, a hundred thousand, a hundred million times over, and more, to infinity. The shortness, then, of such lines is reduced to such a degree that it far exceeds what is needed to make the projectile — however light it may be — return, indeed even keep itself, upon the circumference.

Sagredo: I follow the whole argument perfectly well, and the force with which it presses home; still, it seems to me that anyone who wanted to give it more trouble could raise a difficulty for us, by saying that of the two causes which make the body's descent slower and slower to infinity, it's plain that the one depending on nearness to the first terminus of the descent always grows in the same proportion — just as the parallels always keep the same proportion among themselves, and so on. But that the diminution of that same speed depending on the diminution of the body's heaviness (which was the second cause) is also made in the same proportion — that doesn't seem so plain. And who assures us that it isn't made according to the proportion of the lines intercepted between the tangent and the circumference, or even in a greater proportion still?

Salviati: I had taken it as true that the speeds of naturally descending bodies follow the proportion of their heavinesses — as a favor to Simplicio and to Aristotle, who in several places asserts it as an obvious proposition. You, as a favor to our adversary, put this in doubt, and assert that the speed may increase in a greater proportion — even infinitely greater — than that of the heaviness, so that the whole preceding argument falls to the ground. It remains for me, in order to uphold it, to say that the proportion of the speeds is much smaller than that of the heavinesses, and thus not only to sustain but to fortify what has been said. And as proof of this I bring in experience, which will show us that a body even thirty or forty times heavier than another — as, for example, a ball of lead and one of cork — will not move even by a long way twice as fast. Now, if the projection would not occur even when the falling body's speed were diminished according to the proportion of the heaviness, much less will it occur whenever the speed is lessened only a little, however much weight is taken away. But even granting that the speed were diminished in a proportion far greater than that by which the heaviness is lessened — even if it were the very proportion by which those parallels between the tangent and the circumference diminish — I still perceive no necessity whatsoever that would persuade me the projection must occur with materials however light you please; indeed, I affirm that it will not occur — understanding, however, materials that are not properly weightless, that is, devoid of all heaviness and by their nature rising upward, but materials that descend most slowly and have the very least heaviness. And what moves me to believe so is this: the diminution of heaviness, made according to the proportion of the parallels between the tangent and the circumference, has for its last and utmost terminus the nullity of weight, just as those parallels have for the last terminus of their diminution the contact itself, which is an indivisible point. Now heaviness is never diminished all the way to that last terminus, for then the body would not be heavy at all; but the space of the projectile's return to the circumference is indeed reduced to the utmost smallness — which is when the body rests upon the circumference at the very point of contact, so that to return there it needs no space at all. And therefore, however slight the propensity toward downward motion may be, it is always more than enough to bring the body back onto the circumference, from which it is distant by the smallest space — that is, by nothing.

Sagredo: Truly the argument is very subtle, but just as conclusive; and one is forced to confess that to try to treat questions of nature without geometry is to attempt what is impossible to be done.

Salviati: But Simplicio won't say so — though I don't believe he's one of those Peripatetics who dissuade their disciples from the study of mathematics, on the grounds that it corrupts the reasoning and makes it less fit for contemplation.

Simplicio: I wouldn't do Plato that wrong; but I would say, with Aristotle, that he immersed himself too deeply and grew too enamored of that geometry of his. For in the end these mathematical subtleties, Salviati, are true in the abstract, but applied to sensible, physical matter they don't answer.
