The mathematicians will prove very well, with their principles, that (for example) sphaera tangit planum in puncto — a sphere touches a plane at a point — a proposition much like the present one; but when it comes to matter, things go another way. And I'd say the same of these angles of contact and these proportions: they all come to nothing when one comes to material, sensible things.

Salviati: So you don't believe, then, that the tangent touches the surface of the terrestrial globe at a point?

Simplicio: Not at a single point; I believe that a straight line goes on touching the surface even of water — let alone of the Earth — for many, many dozens, and perhaps hundreds, of braccia before it separates from it.

Salviati: But if I grant you this, don't you see that it's so much the worse for your case? For if, even granting that the tangent were separated from the Earth's surface everywhere but a single point, it has nonetheless been demonstrated that, because of the great narrowness of the angle of contact (if it should even be called an angle), the projectile would not separate — how much less cause will it have to separate if that angle is closed up altogether, and the surface and the tangent proceed together as one? Don't you see that in this way the projection would take place upon the Earth's very surface, which is as much as to say that it wouldn't take place at all? You see, then, the force of truth: while you try to knock it down, your own assaults raise it up and strengthen it. But now that I've drawn you out of this error, I wouldn't want to leave you in this other one — your supposing that a material sphere does not touch a plane at a single point. I'd wish, rather, that this conversation, of just a few hours though it be, held with people who have some knowledge of geometry, might make you appear a little more intelligent among those who know nothing of it. Now, to show you how great is the error of those who say that a sphere — say, of bronze — does not touch a plane — say, of steel — at a point, tell me: what would you make of someone who said, and constantly maintained, that the sphere were not truly a sphere?

Simplicio: I'd judge him utterly devoid of reason.

Salviati: In just this state is the man who says that a material sphere doesn't touch a plane — a material one too — at a point; for to say this is the same as saying that the sphere is not a sphere. And to prove that this is so, tell me: in what do you place the essence of the sphere? That is, what is it that makes the sphere differ from all other solid bodies?

Simplicio: I believe that being a sphere consists in having all the straight lines drawn from its center to the circumference equal.

Salviati: So that when such lines were not equal, that solid would no longer be a sphere.

Simplicio: No, sir.

Salviati: Tell me next whether you believe that, of the many lines that can be drawn between two points, there can be more than a single straight one.

Simplicio: No, sir.

Salviati: But you understand, too, that this single straight line will then of necessity be the shortest of all the others.

Simplicio: I do, and I even have a clear demonstration of it, brought forward by a great Peripatetic philosopher; and it seems to me, if I remember rightly, that he offers it while reproving Archimedes, who assumes it as known when it could be demonstrated.

Salviati: This must have been a great mathematician, to have been able to demonstrate what Archimedes neither knew how nor was able to demonstrate; and if the demonstration comes back to you, I'd gladly hear it, for I remember perfectly well that Archimedes, in his books On the Sphere and the Cylinder, puts this proposition among the postulates, and I hold it for certain that he took it to be indemonstrable.

Simplicio: I think it will come back to me, for it's quite easy and short.

Salviati: So much the greater, then, will be Archimedes' shame, and the glory of this philosopher.

Simplicio: I'll draw his figure. Between the points A and B draw the straight line AB and the curve ACB, of which he wants to prove the straight one to be the shorter; and the proof runs like this. On the curve take a point, which would be C, and draw two other straight lines AC, CB, which two are longer than AB alone — as Euclid demonstrates. But the curve ACB is greater than the two straight lines AC, CB; therefore, a fortiori — all the more so — the curve ACB will be much greater than the straight line AB, which is what was to be demonstrated.

Salviati: I don't believe that if you searched out all the paralogisms in the world you could find one more fitting than this to give an example of the most solemn fallacy among all fallacies — namely, the one that proves ignotum per ignotius, the unknown by the more unknown.

Simplicio: How so?

Salviati: How so? The unknown conclusion you want to prove — isn't it that the curve ACB is longer than the straight line AB? And the middle term, taken as known — isn't it that the curve ACB is greater than the two lines AC, CB, which are known to be greater than AB? And if it's unknown to you that the curve is greater than the straight line AB alone, how will it not be much more unknown that it's greater than the two lines AC, CB, which are known to be greater than AB alone? And yet you take that as known?

Simplicio: I still don't quite understand where the fallacy lies.

Salviati: Since the two straight lines are greater than AB (as is known from Euclid), then whenever the curve is greater than the two lines AC, CB, will it not be much greater than the straight line AB alone?

Simplicio: Yes, sir.

Salviati: That the curve ACB is greater than the straight line AB is the conclusion — better known than the middle term, which is that the same curve is greater than the two lines AC, CB. Now, when the middle is less known than the conclusion, that's called proving ignotum per ignotius. Now let's get back to our subject: it's enough that you grant the straight line to be the shortest of all the lines that can be drawn between two points. And as for the main conclusion — you say the material sphere doesn't touch the plane at a single point: what, then, is its contact?

Simplicio: It will be a part of its surface.

Salviati: And the contact, likewise, of another sphere equal to the first — will that too be a similar little piece of its surface?

Simplicio: There's no reason it shouldn't be so.

Salviati: Then the two spheres too, in touching, will touch with those same two little pieces of surface; because, since each of them fits the same plane, they must necessarily fit each other as well. Now imagine the two spheres, whose centers are A and B, touching, and let their centers be joined by the straight line AB, which will pass through the point of contact. Let it pass through the point C, and taking another point D in the contact, let the two lines AD, BD be joined, so that the triangle ADB is formed, of which the two sides AD, DB will be equal to the single other line ACB — since both those and this contain two radii, which by the definition of the sphere are all equal. And thus the straight line AB, drawn between the two centers A and B, will not be the shortest of all, there being the two AD, DB equal to it — which, by your own concessions, is absurd.

Simplicio: This demonstration holds for spheres in the abstract, not for material ones.

Salviati: Then point out to me where the fallacy of my argument lies, since it doesn't hold for material spheres but does hold for immaterial, abstract ones.

Simplicio: Material spheres are subject to many accidents that immaterial ones aren't. And why couldn't it be that, when a metal sphere rests on a plane, its own weight presses in such a way that the plane gives a little, or else that the sphere itself gets dented at the contact? Besides, that plane can hardly be perfect — if for no other reason, at least because matter is porous; and perhaps it will be no less hard to find a sphere so perfect that it has all the lines from center to surface exactly and precisely equal.

Salviati: Oh, all these things I readily grant you, but they're quite beside the point; for while you want to show me that a material sphere doesn't touch a material plane at a point, you make use of a sphere that isn't a sphere and a plane that isn't a plane — since, by your own account, either these things aren't to be found in the world, or, if they are found, they get spoiled in being applied to produce the effect. It would have been less bad, then, for you to grant the conclusion, but conditionally — that is, that if there were given in matter a sphere and a plane that were, and stayed, perfect, they would touch at a single point — and then to deny that this can ever be had.

Simplicio: I believe the philosophers' proposition should be understood in that sense; for there's no doubt that the imperfection of matter makes it so that things taken in the concrete don't answer to those considered in the abstract.

Salviati: Don't answer? On the contrary — what you yourself have just said proves that they answer exactly.

Simplicio: How so?

Salviati: Don't you say that, because of the imperfection of matter, that body which ought to be a perfect sphere, and that plane which ought to be a perfect plane, don't turn out in the concrete to be such as one pictures them in the abstract?

Simplicio: Just so.

Salviati: So then, whenever you actually bring a material sphere up against a material plane, you're bringing an imperfect sphere against an imperfect plane; and these, you say, don't touch at a point. But I tell you that in the abstract too, an immaterial sphere that isn't a perfect sphere can touch an immaterial plane that isn't a perfect plane — not at a point, but along part of its surface. So that so far, what happens in the concrete happens in exactly the same way in the abstract. And it would be a strange new thing indeed if the sums and reckonings done in abstract numbers didn't then match up with the gold and silver coins and the goods in the concrete world. But do you know what really happens, Simplicio? Just as, to make the accounts come out right on sugar, silk, and wool, the accountant has to make his deductions for the weight of crates, wrappings, and other packaging, so too, when the geometer-philosopher wants to recognize in the concrete the effects he's proved in the abstract, he has to subtract the hindrances of matter. And if he knows how to do that, I assure you the results will tally every bit as exactly as arithmetical calculations. So the errors lie neither in the abstract nor in the concrete, neither in geometry nor in physics, but in the reckoner who doesn't know how to do his sums correctly. So if you had a perfect sphere and a perfect plane, material though they were, have no doubt they would touch at a point; and if that was and is impossible to obtain, then it was quite beside the point to say that sphaera aenea non tangit in puncto — a bronze sphere does not touch at a point. But let me add one thing more, Simplicio: granting you that no perfect spherical shape and no perfect plane can be had in matter, do you believe two material bodies can be had whose surfaces are curved in some part and in some way — even as irregularly as you please?

Simplicio: Of those, I don't think we're short.

Salviati: Well, if there are such, these too will touch at a point — for touching at a single point is by no means the special privilege of the perfect sphere and the perfect plane. In fact, anyone who looked into this matter more closely would find it far harder to find two bodies that touch along part of their surfaces than at a single point. Because for two surfaces to fit snugly together, either both must be exactly flat, or, if one bulges out, the other must be hollowed — and hollowed with a curve that answers exactly to the bulge of the other; and these conditions, by their too narrow specification, are far harder to meet than the others, which in their loose randomness are infinite in number.

Simplicio: So you believe that two stones, or two pieces of iron, taken at random and set together, most of the time touch at a single point?

Salviati: In random encounters, I think not — partly because there'll usually be a bit of yielding grime on them, partly because no care is taken to bring them together without some knock, and the slightest one is enough to make one surface give a little to the other, so that they mutually shape themselves, at least in some tiny patch, each to the imprint of the other. But if their surfaces were well polished, and both were laid on a table — so that neither pressed down on the other — and pushed gently toward each other, I have no doubt they could be brought to simple contact at a single point.

Sagredo: With your leave, I really must raise a certain difficulty of mine, which came to me on hearing Simplicio put forward the impossibility of finding a solid material body with a perfectly spherical shape, and on seeing Salviati in a way grant him his assent by not contradicting him. So I'd like to know whether the same difficulty arises in shaping a solid into some other figure — that is, to put it more clearly, whether there's any greater difficulty in reducing a block of marble to the shape of a perfect sphere than to that of a perfect pyramid, or a perfect horse, or a perfect locust.

Salviati: I'll take this first answer myself. And first let me excuse the assent you think I gave Simplicio — it was only for the moment, because I too had it in mind, before moving on to another subject, to say something that may well be the same as your thought, or very close to it. And in answer to your first question, I say that if any shape can be given to a solid, the spherical is the easiest of them all — as it's also the simplest, and holds among solid figures the place the circle holds among plane ones. The drawing of that circle, being easier than any other, has alone been judged by mathematicians worthy of a place among the postulates governing the drawing of every other figure. And forming a sphere is so easy that if you cut a circular hollow in a flat plate of hard metal, and inside it you roll about at random any solid roughly rounded off, it will by itself, with no further contrivance, be reduced to a spherical shape as perfect as can be — provided that solid isn't smaller than the sphere that would pass through that circle. And what's still more worth noting is that within that same hollow, spheres of different sizes will be formed. As for what it takes to form a horse — or, as you say, a locust — I leave that for you to judge, knowing as you do that very few sculptors in the world are up to it. And I believe Simplicio won't disagree with me on this particular point.

Simplicio: I don't know that I disagree with you in the least. My opinion is that none of the shapes named can be perfectly obtained; but as for coming as close as possible to the most perfect degree, I think it's incomparably easier to reduce the solid to a spherical shape than to the form of a horse or a locust.

Sagredo: And this greater difficulty — what do you think it depends on?

Simplicio: Just as the great ease in forming the sphere derives from its absolute simplicity and uniformity, so the utmost irregularity makes it extremely difficult to introduce the other figures.

Sagredo: So then, since irregularity is the cause of difficulty, the shape of a stone broken at random with a hammer will also be among the hard ones to bring about — being itself irregular, perhaps more so than the horse's?

Simplicio: So it must be.

Sagredo: But tell me: that shape, whatever it may be, that the stone has — does it have it most perfectly, or not?

Simplicio: The one it has, it has so perfectly that no other fits it so exactly.

Sagredo: So then, if among irregular shapes — hard, therefore, to achieve — we nonetheless find infinitely many obtained most perfectly, by what reasoning can it be said that the simplest, and therefore the easiest of all, is impossible to find?

Salviati: Gentlemen, by your leave, it seems to me we've wandered into a dispute not much weightier than the old squabble over goat's wool; and whereas our discussions ought to go on being about serious and weighty matters, we're spending our time in frivolous, trifling wrangles. Let's remember, please, that the search for the constitution of the world is one of the greatest and noblest problems there is in nature — and all the greater for being aimed at the solution of that other one, I mean the cause of the ebb and flow of the sea, sought by every great man there's ever been and perhaps found by none. So, when we've nothing left to bring forward for the complete resolution of the objection drawn from the whirling of the Earth — which was the last one raised as an argument for its immobility about its own center — we can move on to examining the things for and against the annual motion.

Sagredo: I wouldn't have you, Salviati, measure the rest of our minds by the measure of your own. You, always used to busying yourself with the loftiest contemplations, judge as frivolous and low some of the very things that seem to us worthy food for our intellects. So now and then, for our satisfaction, don't scorn to stoop and grant something to our curiosity. As for the resolution of that last objection, drawn from the flinging-off of the daily whirl, far less than what's been offered would have satisfied me. Still, the things that have been said over and above the need struck me as so intriguing that, far from tiring my imagination, they've kept it entertained by their novelty, with a delight I couldn't wish greater. So if you have some further speculation to add, do bring it out — for my part I'll very gladly hear it.

Salviati: In the things I've discovered myself I've always felt the greatest delight; and after that, which is the greatest of all, I take great pleasure in sharing them with some friend who understands them and shows a taste for them. Now, since you're one of these, I'll loosen the rein a little on my ambition — which quietly enjoys itself when I show myself sharper than some other reputed to be keen-sighted — and produce, to cap off and round out our past discussion, another fallacy of the followers of Ptolemy and Aristotle, drawn from the very argument already put forward.

Sagredo: There — I'm eagerly bracing myself to hear it.

Salviati: So far we've passed over and granted Ptolemy, as an undoubted fact, that since the flinging-off of the stone proceeds from the speed of the wheel turned about its center, the cause of that flinging grows exactly as the speed of the whirl increases. From which it was inferred that, since the speed of the Earth's whirl is vastly greater than that of any machine we can artificially set spinning, the extrusion of stones and animals and so on would consequently have to be violent in the extreme. Now I note that in this reasoning there's a very great fallacy, in that we compare the speeds with one another indiscriminately and absolutely. It's true that if I compare the speeds of the same wheel, or of two wheels equal to each other, the one turned faster will fling the stones with greater impetus, and as the speed grows, the cause of the projection grows in the same proportion. But when the speed is made greater not by increasing the speed in the same wheel — which would be by making it turn more times in equal stretches of time — but by increasing the diameter and making the wheel bigger, so that the time of one turn stays the same in the small wheel as in the great one, and only in the great one is the speed greater because its circumference is greater: let no one believe that the cause of the flinging in the great wheel grows according to the proportion of the speed of its circumference to the speed of the circumference of the smaller wheel — for this is utterly false, as a very quick experiment can show us roughly for now. A stone that we could fling with a rod one braccio long, we couldn't fling with one six braccia long — even though the motion of the end of the long rod, that is, of the stone set into it, were twice as fast as the motion of the tip of the shorter rod, which would be the case if the speeds were such that in the time of one full turn of the longer rod, the shorter made three.

Sagredo: What you tell me, Salviati, I already grasp must necessarily come out so; but the reason doesn't readily come to me — why equal speeds shouldn't act equally in extruding projectiles, but the smaller wheel's far more than the larger's. So please explain to me how the matter runs.

Simplicio: You, Sagredo, have this time shown yourself unlike your usual self — you who ordinarily see into everything in a moment, and now you've passed over a fallacy lodged in the experiment of the rods, one that I've managed to see into. And it's the different way of operating when you make the throw now with the short rod, now with the long one. Because to make the stone fly out of the notch, you mustn't keep its motion uniform; rather, at the moment it's fastest, you have to hold back your arm and check the rod's speed, whereupon the stone, already in very swift motion, flies off and moves with impetus. But such a check can't be made with the longer rod, which, by its length and flexibility, doesn't wholly obey the arm's braking; instead, going on to accompany the stone for some distance, by gently reining it in it keeps it attached, and doesn't — as if it had struck a hard obstacle — let it fly free of itself. For if both rods were to strike against a stop that halted them, I believe the stone would fly off equally from the one and the other, even though their motions were equally fast.

Sagredo: With Salviati's leave, I'll make some reply to Simplicio, since he's turned to me. And I say that in his account there's good and bad: good, because nearly all of it is true; bad, because it doesn't quite bear on our point. Very true it is that when the thing carrying the stones at speed strikes an immovable stop, they run on with impetus — the same effect one sees happen all the time in a boat that, running swiftly, runs aground or strikes some obstacle: everyone aboard, caught off guard, suddenly pitches and falls toward the way the vessel was going. And if the terrestrial globe were to meet an obstacle such that it wholly resisted its whirl and stopped it, then indeed I believe that not only the beasts, the buildings, and the cities, but the mountains, the lakes, and the seas would be overturned — and it would be a wonder if the globe itself didn't fall to pieces. But none of this bears on our point, for we're speaking of what might follow from the motion of the Earth turning uniformly and placidly upon itself, even if at great speed. Likewise what you say about the rods is in part true, but Salviati didn't bring it forward as something that fits our subject exactly — only as an example that might roughly rouse our minds to consider more carefully whether, when the speed is increased in whatever way, the cause of the projection increases in the same proportion. So that, for example, if a wheel ten braccia in diameter, moving so that a point on its circumference passed over a hundred braccia in a minute of an hour, thereby had the impetus to fling a stone — whether that impetus grows a hundred thousand times over in a wheel a million braccia in diameter. This Salviati denies, and I'm inclined to believe the same; but not knowing the reason, I've asked him for it, and I'm eagerly awaiting it.

Salviati: Here I am to give you what satisfaction lies within my powers; and though in my first words it may seem I'm going after things foreign to our point, I believe that as the reasoning proceeds we'll find they aren't so after all. So let Sagredo tell me in what things he's observed the resistance of a movable body to being moved to consist.

Sagredo: For now I don't see any internal resistance in the body to being moved except its natural inclination and propensity toward the contrary motion — as in heavy bodies, which have a propensity to motion downward, the resistance is to motion upward. And I said internal resistance because I believe that's the one you mean, not the external ones, which are accidental and many.

Salviati: That's what I meant, and your sharpness has outrun my care in asking. But if I've been sparing in my questioning, I suspect that Sagredo hasn't, with his answer, fully matched the question — and that in the body, besides the natural inclination toward the contrary terminus, there's another quality, likewise intrinsic and natural, that makes it resistant to motion. So tell me again: don't you believe that the inclination, say, of heavy bodies to move downward is equal to their resistance to being pushed upward?

Sagredo: I believe it's exactly so; and that's why I see two equal weights in a balance stay still in equilibrium, the heaviness of the one resisting being lifted against the heaviness with which the other, pressing down, would raise it.

Salviati: Very good; so that to have the one lift the other, you'd have to add weight to the one pressing down, or take some from the other. But if the resistance to upward motion consists in heaviness alone, how does it come about that in a balance with unequal arms — that is, in the steelyard — a weight of a hundred pounds, by its pressing down, sometimes isn't enough to lift one of four pounds that opposes it, while this four-pound one, by lowering, can lift the hundred-pound one? For such is the effect of the sliding weight against the heavy load we want to weigh. If the resistance to being moved resides in heaviness alone, how can the sliding weight, with its mere four pounds, resist the weight of a bale of wool or silk that runs to eight hundred or a thousand — indeed, how can it overcome the bale with its moment and lift it? We really must say, Sagredo, that here we're working with a resistance and a force other than that of simple heaviness.

Sagredo: It must be so; but tell me, what is this second power?

Salviati: It's what wasn't there in the equal-armed balance. Consider what's new in the steelyard — and in that novelty necessarily lies the cause of the new effect.

Sagredo: I think your prompting has jogged something in me. In both instruments we work with weight and with motion: in the balance the motions are equal, and so one weight has to exceed the other in heaviness to move it; in the steelyard the smaller weight won't move the larger unless the larger moves little — hanging at the smaller distance — while the smaller moves much, hanging at the greater distance. So we must say that the smaller weight overcomes the larger's resistance by moving much, while the other moves little.

Salviati: Which is as much as to say that the speed of the less heavy body makes up for the heaviness of the heavier, slower one.

Sagredo: But do you believe that speed makes up for heaviness exactly? That is, that the moment and force of a body of, say, four pounds is as great as that of one of a hundred, whenever the first has a hundred degrees of speed and the second only four?

Salviati: Certainly, as I could show you with many experiments; but for now let the confirmation of this one alone, the steelyard, suffice — where you'll see the light sliding weight able to hold up and balance the very heavy bale just when its distance from the center, on which the steelyard rests and turns, is as much greater than the bale's smaller distance as the bale's absolute weight is greater than the sliding weight's. And that the great bale can't with its weight lift the sliding weight, so much lighter — no cause for this can be seen but the disparity of the motions each would have to make, since the bale, by lowering a single finger's width, would raise the sliding weight a hundred fingers' width (granting that the bale weighed as much as a hundred sliding weights, and that the sliding weight's distance from the steelyard's center was a hundred times the distance between that same center and the point where the bale hangs). And the sliding weight's moving a hundred fingers' width in the time the bale moves a single one is the same as saying the speed of the sliding weight's motion is a hundred times greater than the speed of the bale's. Now fix it firmly in your imagination, as a true and well-known principle, that the resistance coming from the speed of motion makes up for what depends on the heaviness of another body. So that, consequently, a body of one pound moving with a hundred degrees of speed resists being checked exactly as much as another body of a hundred pounds whose speed is a single degree. And two equal bodies will resist being moved equally, if they're to be made to move with equal speed; but if one is to be moved faster than the other, it will offer greater resistance, in proportion to the greater speed you want to give it. These things settled, let's come to the working-out of our problem; and for easier understanding let's draw ourselves a little figure. Let there be two unequal wheels about this center A: let the circumference of the smaller be BG, and of the larger CEH, and let the semidiameter ABC stand upright to the horizon; through the points B and C let's mark the straight tangent lines BF and CD, and on the arcs BG, CE let two equal parts BG, CE be taken. And let it be understood that the two wheels are turned about their centers with equal speeds, so that two bodies — which would be, say, two stones — placed at the points B and C are carried along the circumferences BG, CE with equal speeds, such that in the same time the stone B would run through the arc BG, the stone C would pass through the arc CE. I now say that the whirl of the smaller wheel is far more powerful to make the projection of stone B than the whirl of the larger wheel is for stone C. For since, as already explained, the projection must be made along the tangent, when the stones B, C were to separate from their wheels and begin the motion of projection from the points B, C, they'd be flung along the tangents BF, CD by the impetus conceived from the whirl. Along the tangents BF, CD, then, the two stones have equal impetuses to run, and would run along them if they weren't deflected by some other force. Isn't that so, Sagredo?

Sagredo: That's how the matter seems to stand.

Salviati: But what force do you suppose could be the one that deflects the stones from moving along the tangents, where the impetus of the whirl truly flings them?

Sagredo: It's either their own gravity, or some glue that keeps them settled and stuck onto the wheels.

Salviati: But to deflect a moving body from the path in which it has its impetus, doesn't it take a greater or a smaller force depending on whether the deflection has to be greater or smaller — that is, depending on whether, in the deflection, the body must in the same time cross a greater or a smaller space?

Sagredo: Yes, because we already concluded above that to make a body move, the greater the speed you have to give it, the greater the moving power must be.

Salviati: Now consider this. To deflect the stone on the smaller wheel from the projectile motion it would make along the tangent BF, and to keep it fastened to the wheel, its own gravity has to draw it back by the length of the secant FG — that is, by the perpendicular dropped from point G onto the line BF. On the larger wheel, though, the drawing-back need be no more than the secant DE — the perpendicular dropped from point E onto the tangent DC — which is far shorter than FG, and shorter and shorter still as the wheel is made larger. And since these drawings-back must be accomplished in equal times — that is, in the while that the two equal arcs BG and CE are traversed — the one belonging to stone B, namely the pull-back FG, will have to be faster than the other, DE. So much greater force is needed to keep stone B joined to its little wheel than to keep stone C joined to its big one; which is the same as saying that some trifling thing will block the flinging-off on the big wheel that would not prevent it on the little one. Plainly, then, the larger the wheel grows, the weaker the cause of projection becomes.

Sagredo: From what I now understand, thanks to your patient breaking-down of it, I think I can satisfy my mind with a fairly brief account. Since the equal speed of the two wheels impresses an equal impetus along the tangents into both stones, one sees that the great circumference, parting only slightly from its tangent, goes along humoring the stone, in a way — with a gentle bit softly curbing in it the appetite, so to speak, to leave the circumference — so that any small restraint, whether of the stone's own downward inclination or of some glue, is enough to keep it joined. But that same restraint turns out too weak to do the job on the little wheel, which, humoring the tangent's direction so little, tries with too greedy a hunger to hold the stone to itself; and since the curb and the glue are no stronger than what kept the other stone united with the larger wheel, the halter snaps and off it runs along the tangent. So I not only see that everyone was mistaken who believed the cause of projection grows as the speed of the whirl grows; I go further, and consider that, since the projection diminishes as the wheel grows — provided the same speed is kept in the wheels — it might perhaps be true that, to make the big wheel fling like the little one, you'd have to increase its speed by as much as you increase its diameter. That would be the case when the whole revolutions are completed in equal times; and so one might reckon that the whirl of the Earth is no more able to fling off stones than any other little wheel turning so slowly that in twenty-four hours it makes a single turn.

Salviati: For now I don't want us to press the inquiry that far. It's enough that we've shown — abundantly enough, if I'm not mistaken — the emptiness of an argument that at first glance looked utterly conclusive, and was reckoned so by very great men. And I'll count the time and the words well spent if I've won even a little belief in Simplicio's mind — not, I'll say, in the mobility of the Earth, but at least in this: that the opinion of those who believe it is not so ridiculous and foolish as the ranks of the common philosophers hold it to be.

Simplicio: The solutions offered so far to the objections raised against this daily revolution of the Earth — the ones drawn from heavy bodies falling from the top of a tower, and from projectiles shot straight up, or at any sideways slant, toward east, west, south, or north, and so on — have in some measure lessened the old disbelief I'd conceived against this opinion. But other, larger difficulties are now turning in my imagination, ones I absolutely would not know how to work myself free of — nor perhaps do I think you yourselves could untangle them. It may even be that they haven't reached your ears, since they're quite recent. These are the objections of two authors who write ex professo against Copernicus. The first are read in a little book of natural conclusions; the others are by a great philosopher and mathematician both, set into a treatise he wrote in favor of Aristotle and his opinion about the unalterability of the heavens, where he proves that not only the comets but also the new stars — I mean the one of '72 in Cassiopeia and the one of 1604 in Sagittarius — were not above the spheres of the planets at all, but absolutely below the concave of the Moon, in the elemental sphere. And this he demonstrates against Tycho, Kepler, and many other observing astronomers, beating them with their own weapons, that is, by way of the parallaxes. If it please you, I'll lay out the reasons of the one and the other, for I've read them more than once with attention; and you can weigh their force and give your opinion.

Salviati: Since our chief aim is to bring out and weigh everything that has been urged for and against the two systems, the Ptolemaic and the Copernican, it's not right to pass over anything written on the matter.

Simplicio: Then I'll begin with the objections in the little book of conclusions, and come to the others afterward. First, then, the author very shrewdly goes calculating how many miles per hour a point on the Earth's surface set under the equinoctial line travels, and how many are traveled by other points set on other parallels; and not content to investigate these motions by the hour, he finds them also for a single minute, and not content with the minute, he tracks it down to a single second. And more: he goes on to show, as plainly as could be, how many miles an artillery ball would travel in such spans of time if it were placed in the concave of the lunar sphere — supposing that concave even as large as Copernicus himself imagines it, so as to strip the adversary of every loophole. And having made this most ingenious and exquisite reckoning, he demonstrates that a heavy body falling from up there would take a good deal more than six days to reach the center of the Earth, toward which all heavy things naturally tend. Now, if by God's absolute power, or by some angel, a very large artillery ball were miraculously carried up there and set at the point directly above us, our vertical point, and from there let go free, it is — in his view and mine — a most incredible thing that in coming down it would keep itself always in our vertical line, continuing to turn with the Earth about its center for so many days, tracing under the equinoctial a spiral line in the plane of that greatest circle, and under other parallels spiral lines around cones, and under the poles falling by a simple straight line. He then establishes and confirms this great improbability by putting forward, in the form of questions, many difficulties impossible for the followers of Copernicus to remove; and they are, if I remember rightly —

Salviati: Gently, a moment — please, Simplicio, don't tangle me up with so many novelties at one stroke. I have a poor memory, and so I need to go step by step. And since it comes back to me that I once wanted to calculate how long a similar heavy body, falling from the concave of the Moon, would take to arrive at the center of the Earth — and I seem to recall that the time wouldn't be so long — it'll be well for you to tell us by what rule this author made his computation.

Simplicio: He made it so as to prove his point a fortiori, granting a great advantage to the opposing side: he supposes that the speed of the falling body down the vertical line toward the center of the Earth was equal to the speed of its circular motion made on the greatest circle of the concave of the lunar sphere — at which rate it would come to travel twelve thousand six hundred German miles in an hour, a thing that truly has the impossible about it. Yet, to abound in caution and give every advantage to the other side, he supposes it true, and concludes that the time of the fall must in any case be more than six days.

Salviati: And that's the whole of his procedure? And with this demonstration he proves the time of such a fall must be more than six days?

Sagredo: It seems to me he's conducted himself all too modestly. Since it lay within his own choosing to give the falling body whatever speed he liked — and consequently to make it reach the Earth in six months, or even six years — he contented himself with six days. But please, Salviati, restore my appetite a little by telling me how your own computation went, since you say you once did it. For I'm quite sure that if the problem hadn't called for some clever operation, you'd never have set your mind to it.

Salviati: It isn't enough, Sagredo, that the conclusion be noble and grand; the point is to treat it nobly. Who doesn't know that in cutting up the limbs of an animal one can uncover infinite marvels of provident and most wise nature? Yet for every one that the anatomist cuts, the butcher hacks up a thousand. And I, in trying now to satisfy your request, don't know in which of the two costumes I'm to appear on stage. Still, taking heart from the entrance of Simplicio's author, I won't refuse to recite for you — if it comes back to me — the method I used. But before I set my hand to anything else, I can't help saying that I strongly doubt Simplicio has faithfully reported the way this author of his finds that the artillery ball, in coming from the concave of the Moon to the center of the Earth, would take more than six days. For if he had supposed its speed in descending to be equal to that of the concave — as Simplicio says he supposes — he'd have shown himself utterly stripped even of the first and simplest notions of geometry. Indeed, I marvel that Simplicio himself, in accepting the supposition he describes, doesn't see the immense absurdity it contains.

Simplicio: That I may have blundered in reporting it is possible; but that you should uncover a fallacy inside it — that, surely, is not.

Salviati: Perhaps I haven't rightly grasped what you reported. Don't you say this author makes the speed of the ball's motion in descending equal to the speed it had while going around, standing in the concave of the Moon — and that, dropping with that speed, it would take itself to the center in six days?

Simplicio: So he seems to write.

Salviati: And you don't see so great an absurdity? But surely you're dissembling — for it can't be that you don't know that the semidiameter of a circle is less than a sixth part of the circumference; and that, in consequence, the time in which a moving body will pass over the semidiameter will be less than a sixth part of the time in which, moved with the same speed, it would pass over the circumference; and therefore the ball, descending with the speed at which it was moving in the concave, will arrive at the center in less than four hours — granting that in the concave it completed one revolution in twenty-four hours, as he must suppose in order to keep it always in the same vertical line.

Simplicio: Now I understand the error perfectly; but I wouldn't want to lay it on him undeservedly, and it must be that I erred in reciting his argument. To avoid saddling him with still more, I'd like to have his book; and if there were someone to go and fetch it, I'd be much obliged.

Sagredo: There'll be no shortage of a footman to go flying for it; and it can be done without any loss of time, for meanwhile Salviati will favor us with his computation.

Simplicio: He can go — he'll find it lying open on my desk, together with the other man's, who also argues against Copernicus.

Sagredo: Let's have that one brought as well, for extra safety; and meanwhile Salviati will make his calculation. I've dispatched a servant.

Salviati: Before anything else, we have to consider that the motion of descending heavy bodies is not uniform: starting from rest, they go on continually accelerating — an effect known and observed by everyone except the aforesaid modern author, who, saying nothing of acceleration, makes it steady. But this general knowledge is of no use unless one knows according to what proportion this increase of speed is made — a conclusion that, down to our own times, was unknown to all philosophers, and was first discovered and demonstrated by the Academician, our mutual friend. In certain of his writings, not yet published but shown in confidence to me and to some others of his friends, he demonstrates how the acceleration of the straight-line motion of heavy bodies follows the odd numbers ab unitate — starting from one. That is: mark off however many equal intervals of time you please; if in the first interval the moving body, setting out from rest, has passed over a certain space — say, one canna, a rod's length — then in the second interval it will pass three canne, in the third five, in the fourth seven, and so on in order according to the succeeding odd numbers. Which in sum is the same as saying that the spaces passed by the moving body, starting from rest, stand to one another in the doubled ratio of the times in which those spaces are measured — or, if you like, that the spaces passed are to one another as the squares of the times.

Sagredo: A marvelous thing I'm hearing. And you say there's mathematical demonstration of this?

Salviati: Purest mathematics — and not of this alone, but of many other beautiful properties belonging to natural motions, and to projectiles too, all discovered and demonstrated by our friend. I've seen and studied them all, with the greatest delight and wonder, watching a whole new field of knowledge spring up around a subject on which hundreds of volumes have been written — and not a single one of the infinite admirable conclusions it contains had been observed and understood by anyone before our friend.

Sagredo: You're making me lose my appetite to hear more of the discussions we've begun, and want only to hear some of the demonstrations you're hinting at. So either tell them to me now, or at least give me your firm word to hold a special session on them with me — and with Simplicio present too, if he'd care to hear the properties and incidents of nature's primary effect.

Simplicio: I'll certainly have the will for it, though as far as the natural philosopher is concerned, I don't believe that descending to certain minute particulars is necessary — a general knowledge of the definition of motion, and of the distinction between natural and violent, equable and accelerated, and the like, being enough. For if that had not been enough, I don't believe Aristotle would have failed to teach us all that was lacking.

Salviati: Maybe so. But let's lose no more time on this, for I promise to spend half a day on it separately, for your satisfaction — indeed, it comes back to me now that I once before promised you this very satisfaction. And returning to our begun calculation of the time in which the falling heavy body would come from the concave of the Moon to the center of the Earth: to proceed not arbitrarily and by chance, but by a thoroughly conclusive method, we'll first try to make sure, by experience repeated many times, in how much time a ball — of iron, say — comes to the ground from a height of a hundred braccia.

Sagredo: Taking, though, a ball of some determinate weight — the very one on which we mean to make the computation of the time of the descent from the Moon.

Salviati: That matters not at all, because balls of one, of ten, of a hundred, of a thousand pounds will all measure out the same hundred braccia in the same time.

Simplicio: Oh, that I don't believe — nor does Aristotle believe it, who writes that the speeds of descending heavy bodies stand to one another in the same proportion as their weights.

Salviati: If you, Simplicio, want to admit that as true, then you must also believe that, when two balls of the same material are let fall at the same instant — one of a hundred pounds, the other of one — from a height of a hundred braccia, the big one arrives at the ground before the smaller has descended a single braccio. Now fit your brain, if you can, to imagining that you see the big ball landed on the ground while the little one is still within less than a braccio of the top of the tower.

Sagredo: That this proposition is utterly false, I haven't a doubt in the world; but that yours is entirely true, I'm not quite able to grasp — still, I believe it, since you affirm it so resolutely, which I'm sure you wouldn't do unless you had sure experience or firm demonstration of it.

Salviati: I have both; and when we treat the matter of motions separately, I'll share it with you. Meanwhile, so as not to have occasion to interrupt the thread further, let's set ourselves to make the computation on an iron ball of a hundred pounds, which by repeated experiments descends from a height of a hundred braccia in five seconds. And since, as I told you, the spaces measured by the falling body increase in the doubled proportion — that is, as the squares of the times — then, the time of one minute being twelve times the time of five seconds, if we multiply the hundred braccia by the square of 12, that is, by 144, we'll have 14,400, which will be the number of braccia the same body will pass in one minute. Following the same rule, since an hour is 60 minutes, by multiplying 14,400 — the number of braccia passed in a minute — by the square of 60, that is, by 3,600, we get 51,840,000, the number of braccia to be passed in an hour, which is 17,280 miles. And wanting to know the space that would be passed in 4 hours, we'll multiply 17,280 by 16 (which is the square of 4), and we'll get 276,480 miles — a number far greater than the distance from the concave of the Moon to the center of the Earth, which is 196,000 miles, making the distance of the concave 56 Earth semidiameters, as the modern author does, and the Earth's semidiameter 3,500 miles of 3,000 braccia each, which are our Italian miles. So, Simplicio, that stretch from the concave of the Moon to the center of the Earth, which your reckoner said could not be traversed except in a good deal more than six days — see how, making the computation on experience and not on one's fingers, it would be traversed in a good deal less than 4 hours; and, making the exact computation, it's traversed in 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds.

Sagredo: Please, my dear sir, don't cheat me of this exact calculation, for it must be a most beautiful thing.

Salviati: So it truly is. Having, then (as I said), observed by careful experiment how such a body, falling, passes the height of 100 braccia in 5 seconds, we'll say: if 100 braccia are passed in 5 seconds, in how many seconds will 588,000,000 braccia be passed (for that is what 56 semidiameters of the Earth come to)? The rule for this operation is that the third number be multiplied by the square of the second; that gives 14,700,000,000, which is to be divided by the first, that is, by 100; and the square root of the quotient, which is 12,124, is the number sought — that is, 12,124 seconds, which are 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds.

Sagredo: I've watched the operation, but I understand nothing of the reason for operating so — nor does it seem the moment now to ask it.

Salviati: On the contrary, I want to tell you, even though you don't ask, because it's quite easy. Let's mark these three numbers with the letters A first, B second, C third: A and C are the numbers of the spaces, B is the number of the time; the fourth number is sought, likewise of the time. And since we know that whatever proportion space A has to space C, the same must the square of time B have to the square of the time sought, therefore, by the golden rule — the rule of three — the number C will be multiplied by the square of the number B, and the product divided by the number A, and the quotient will be the square of the number sought, and its square root will be the very number sought. Now see how easy it is to understand.

Sagredo: All true things are like that, once they've been found — the whole difficulty is in knowing how to find them. I'm entirely satisfied, and I thank you. And if any other curiosity remains for you on this subject, please, tell it — because, if I may speak freely, I'll say, with Simplicio's leave, that from your discourses I always learn some fine new thing, but from those of his philosophers I can't say I've yet learned anything of much weight.

Salviati: There's unfortunately a great deal more we could say about these matters of local motion — motion from place to place — but, as we agreed, we'll save that for a session of its own. For now I'll say something bearing on the author Simplicio has put forward, who thinks he's granted a great advantage to our side in conceding that the cannonball, in falling from the hollow of the Moon's sphere, could arrive with a speed equal to the speed at which it would have moved in its circle had it stayed up there and turned with the daily rotation. Now I tell him that that ball, falling from the hollow all the way to the center, will pick up a degree of speed well more than double the speed of the daily motion of the Moon's hollow; and I'll show this with assumptions that are perfectly true, and not arbitrary.

You must know, then, that a heavy body, as it falls and keeps gaining fresh speed by the proportion already stated, has in itself, at whatever point of its line of motion it happens to be, such a degree of speed that, if it went on moving with that speed uniformly, without increasing it any further, then in a time equal to the time of its descent it would pass twice the space it had passed in the line of its earlier downward motion. So, for example, if that ball, in coming from the hollow of the Moon to its center, has used up 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds, I say that on reaching the center it finds itself established in such a degree of speed that, if with that speed — without increasing it any further — it went on moving uniformly, it would pass in another 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds twice the space, that is, as much as the whole diameter of the lunar orb. And since from the hollow of the Moon to the center is 196,000 miles, which the ball passes in 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds, then (given what has been said) if the ball went on moving with the speed it has on arriving at the center, it would pass, in another 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds, twice that space, that is, 392,000 miles: but the same ball, staying in the hollow of the Moon — whose circuit is 1,232,000 miles — and moving with it at the daily motion, would cover in the same time, that is, in 3 hours, 22 minutes, and 4 seconds, 172,880 miles, which is far less than half of the 392,000 miles. There, then, you see how the motion in the hollow is not what the modern author says — of a speed impossible for the falling ball to share, and so on.

Sagredo: The argument would run beautifully and set my mind at rest, if only that one item were settled for me — the moving body's passing twice the space it fell through, in another time equal to the time of its descent, supposing it went on moving uniformly with the greatest degree of speed it had acquired in falling. That's a proposition you've assumed as true once before too, but never proved.

Salviati: That's one of the things our friend has demonstrated, and you'll see it in due time; but meanwhile, with a few conjectures, I want — not to teach you anything new — but to shake you loose from a certain contrary opinion, by showing you that perhaps it can be so. Hang a lead ball by a long, thin thread fastened to the ceiling; if we pull it aside from the vertical and then let it go free, haven't you noticed that as it swings down it passes, of its own accord, beyond the vertical by very nearly as much again?

Sagredo: I've observed it very well, and seen — especially if the ball is quite heavy — that it rises so little short of its drop that I've sometimes believed the upward arc equal to the downward, and so wondered whether its swings might go on forever; and I'd believe they would, if one could remove the hindrance of the air, which, resisting being parted, slows and impedes the pendulum's motion a little. But the hindrance is slight indeed — witness the great number of swings that happen before the moving body comes fully to rest.

Salviati: The motion wouldn't go on forever, Sagredo, even if the air's hindrance were removed entirely, because there's another one, far more hidden.

Sagredo: And what's that? Nothing else comes to my mind.

Salviati: You'll enjoy hearing it, but I'll tell you later; for now let's go on. I put the observation of this pendulum to you so that you'd grasp that the impetus acquired in the downward arc, where the motion is natural, is of itself powerful enough to drive that same ball, by violent motion, through just as much space in the matching upward arc; of itself, I say, once all external hindrances are removed. And I think it's understood without any doubt that, just as in the downward arc the speed keeps growing right down to the lowest point of the vertical, so from that point, up the other arc, it keeps diminishing to the topmost extreme point — diminishing by the very same proportions by which it first went on increasing, so that the degrees of speed at points equally distant from the lowest point are equal to one another. From this it seems to me — reasoning by a certain fittingness — that I may believe that, if the terrestrial globe were bored through the center, a cannonball dropping down such a well would pick up, by the time it reached the center, such an impetus of speed that, having passed the center, it would drive it up on the far side through just as much space as the fall had been, its speed always diminishing beyond the center by decrements matching the increments gained in descending; and the time spent in this second, upward motion would, I think, be equal to the time of the descent.

Now, if the moving body — by successively diminishing, down to total extinction, the highest degree of speed it had at the center — carries itself in a given time through a given space, the same space it had traversed in an equal time while gaining speed from the total lack of it up to that highest degree, then it seems quite reasonable that, if it moved always with the highest degree of speed, it would pass in an equal time both those spaces together. For if we go through in our mind dividing those speeds into growing and shrinking degrees — like, say, these numbers, so that the first up to 10 are the growing ones and the rest down to 1 the shrinking ones, the former belonging to the time of the descent and the latter to the time of the rise — you'll see that, all joined together, they add up to as much as if one of the two parts had been made entirely of the greatest degrees. And therefore the whole space passed with all the degrees of speed, growing and shrinking (which is the whole entire diameter), must equal the space passed by the greatest speeds, which are half as many in number as the aggregate of the growing and the shrinking together. I know I've explained myself rather clumsily, and God grant I make myself understood.

Sagredo: I think I've understood it very well, and can even show in a few words that I've understood. You meant to say that, when the motion begins from rest and the speed goes on growing successively by equal increments — like those of the counting numbers, starting from one, or rather from zero, which represents the state of rest — laying them out like this, and going on for as many as you please, so that the least degree is zero and the greatest is, say, 5: all these degrees of speed, with which the moving body has moved, make the sum of 15. But if the moving body were to move with just as many degrees in number as these, and each of them equal to the greatest, which is 5, the aggregate of all these speeds would be double the others, that is, 30. And so, moving for an equal time, but with a steady speed equal to that of the highest degree, 5, it will have to pass twice the space it passed in the accelerated time, which began from the state of rest.

Salviati: You, true to your very quick and very subtle grasp, have explained the whole thing far more clearly than I did, and have even put me in mind to add something more. For since in accelerated motion the increase is continuous, one cannot parcel out the degrees of speed — which is forever growing — into any fixed number, because, changing from moment to moment, they are always infinite. So we can better illustrate our meaning by picturing a triangle, which would be this one, ABC. Take on the side AC as many equal parts as you like — AD, DE, EF, FG — and through the points D, E, F, G draw straight lines parallel to the base BC. Here I want us to imagine that the parts marked off on the line AC are equal times, and that the parallels drawn through the points D, E, F, G represent for us the degrees of speed, accelerated and increasing equally in equal times; and that the point A is the state of rest, from which the moving body, setting out, has, say, in the time AD acquired the degree of speed DH; in the next stretch of time it has increased the speed above the degree DH up to the degree EI; and so it has made it greater in the times that follow, according to the growth of the lines FK, GL, and so on. But because the acceleration happens continuously, from moment to moment, and not in discrete jumps from one whole chunk of time to another — the terminus A being posited as the least moment of speed, that is, as the state of rest and as the first instant of the ensuing time AD — it's clear that before the degree of speed DH is acquired in the time AD, the body has passed through infinitely many other degrees, smaller and smaller, gained in the infinitely many instants that are in the time DA, corresponding to the infinitely many points in the line DA. So, to represent the infinity of degrees of speed that come before the degree DH, we must imagine infinitely many lines, always smaller and smaller, drawn from the infinitely many points of the line DA, parallel to DH; and this infinity of lines represents for us, in the end, the surface of the triangle AHD. And in this way we'll understand that any space passed by the moving body with a motion that, beginning from rest, goes uniformly accelerating, has used up and made use of infinitely many degrees of growing speed, answering to the infinitely many lines that, beginning from the point A, are imagined drawn parallel to the line HD and to IE, KF, LG, BC, the motion continuing as far as you please. Now let's complete the whole parallelogram AMBC, and prolong up to its side BM not only the parallels marked in the triangle, but the infinity of those imagined produced from all the points of the side AC. And just as BC was the greatest of the triangle's infinite lines, representing for us the greatest degree of speed the moving body acquired in the accelerated motion, and the whole surface of that triangle was the mass and the sum of all the speed with which, in the time AC, it passed such a space, so the parallelogram comes to be a mass and aggregate of just as many degrees of speed, but each one equal to the greatest, BC — and this mass of speed turns out to be double the mass of the growing speeds of the triangle, just as the parallelogram is double the triangle. And therefore, if the moving body that fell made use of the degrees of accelerated speed answering to the triangle ABC, and passed a certain space in a certain time, it's quite reasonable and probable that, making use of the uniform speeds answering to the parallelogram, it will pass, with steady motion in the same time, twice the space passed by the accelerated motion.

Sagredo: I'm entirely satisfied. And if you call this a merely probable argument, what will the necessary demonstrations be like? Would to God that in all of common philosophy even one could be found as conclusive as this!

Simplicio: In natural science one mustn't go looking for exact mathematical proof.

Sagredo: But isn't this matter of motion a natural question? And yet I find that Aristotle doesn't demonstrate for me even the least little property of it. But let's not sidetrack our discussion any further; and you, Salviati, please don't fail to tell me what you hinted was the cause of the pendulum's stopping, over and above the medium's resistance to being parted.

Salviati: Tell me: of two things hanging from unequal lengths, doesn't the one fastened to the longer cord make its swings less frequent?

Sagredo: Yes — provided they moved through equal distances from the vertical.

Salviati: That moving out more or less matters not at all, because the same pendulum always makes its swings in equal times, whether they're very long or very short — that is, whether the pendulum is drawn very far or very little from the vertical; and even if they aren't perfectly equal, they differ imperceptibly, as experience can show you. But even were they very unequal, that wouldn't hurt our case — it would help it. So let's mark the vertical AB, and let a weight C hang from the point A on the cord AC, and another, higher up on the same cord, be E. Draw the cord AC aside from the vertical, and then let it go free: the weights C and E will move through the arcs CBD and EGF. And the weight E, hanging from a shorter distance — and also, by your own account, drawn aside less — wants to come back sooner and make its swings more frequent than the weight C, and so it will keep C from running as far out toward the terminus D as it would if it were free; and thus, bringing it a continual hindrance at every swing, it will finally reduce it to rest. Now the cord itself (take away the weights) is a compound of many heavy pendulums — that is, each of its parts is such a pendulum, attached nearer and nearer to the point A, and so disposed to make its swings ever more and more frequent; and consequently it's able to bring a continual hindrance to the weight C. A sign of this is that, if we observe the cord AC, we'll see it stretched not in a straight line but in an arc; and if, instead of a cord, we take a chain, we'll see this effect far more plainly, especially by drawing the heavy weight C well aside from the vertical AB — for, the chain being composed of many jointed links, each quite heavy, the arcs AEC and AFD will be seen noticeably curved. For this reason, then — that the parts of the chain, the nearer they are to the point A, want to make their swings more frequent — they don't let the lower parts run on as far as they naturally would; and by continually subtracting from the swings of the weight C, they finally stop it, even if the air's hindrance could be taken away.

Sagredo: The books have just arrived, right on time. Take them, Simplicio, and find the passage in question.

Simplicio: Here it is, where he begins to argue against the Earth's daily rotation, having first refuted the annual one: Motus Terrae annuus asserere Copernicanos cogit conversionem eiusdem quotidianam; alias idem Terrae hemispherium continenter ad Solem esset conversum, obumbrato semper averso — and so half the Earth would never see the Sun.

Salviati: It seems to me, from this very first step, that this man hasn't rightly pictured Copernicus's position; for if he'd noticed how Copernicus keeps the axis of the terrestrial globe perpetually parallel to itself, he wouldn't have said that half the Earth would never see the Sun, but that the year would have been a single natural day — that is, that over all parts of the Earth there'd have been six months of day and six months of night, as happens now to those who live under the pole. But let that be forgiven him, and let's come to the rest.

Simplicio: It goes on: Hanc autem gyrationem Terrae impossibilem esse, sic demonstramus — this whirling of the Earth we prove to be impossible, as follows. What comes next is the explanation of the following figure, where you can see drawn a lot of heavy bodies descending, and light ones ascending, and birds that hold themselves up in the air, and so on.

Sagredo: Show me, please. Oh, what lovely pictures — what birds, what balls, and what other pretty things are these?

Simplicio: These are balls coming from the hollow of the Moon.

Sagredo: And this one, what is it?

Simplicio: It's a snail — the kind we call buovoli here in Venice — and it too comes from the hollow of the Moon.

Sagredo: Yes, yes — that's because the Moon has such great power over these shelled creatures, which we call pesci armai.

Simplicio: This next is that calculation I mentioned, of the journey that a point of the Earth placed under the equator — and also on the parallel of 48 degrees — would make in a natural day, in an hour, in a minute, and in a second. And then follows this, where I fear I may have gone wrong in reporting it; so let's read it: His positis, necesse est, Terra circulariter mota, omnia ex aëre eidem etc. Quod si hasce pilas aequales ponemus pondere, magnitudine, gravitate, et in concavo spherae lunaris positas libero descensui permittamus, si motum deorsum aequemus celeritate motui circum (quod tamen secus est, cum pila A etc.), elabentur minimum (ut multum cedamus adversariis) dies sex: quo tempore sexies circa Terram etc.

Salviati: You reported this man's objection all too faithfully. From this you can see, Simplicio, with how much caution people ought to proceed who want to talk others into believing things that perhaps they don't believe themselves: because it seems impossible to me that this author shouldn't have noticed he was imagining a circle whose diameter — which, for mathematicians, is less than a third of the circumference — was more than 12 times greater than the circumference itself; an error that makes something less than one out to be more than 36.

Sagredo: Perhaps these mathematical proportions, which are true in the abstract, don't answer quite so exactly once they're applied concretely to physical, material circles — though it does seem to me that coopers, to find the radius of the head they need to make for a barrel, use the mathematicians' abstract rule, even though such barrel-heads are very material, concrete things. So let Simplicio give this author's excuse, and tell us whether he thinks physics can differ so much from mathematics.

Simplicio: The retreat doesn't seem enough to me, because the discrepancy is too great; and in this case I wouldn't know what else to say, except that quandoque bonus etc. — even the best of them nods now and then. But granting that Salviati's calculation is the sounder one, and that the time of the ball's fall was no more than three hours, it still seems to me that, coming from the hollow of the Moon — so vast an interval away — it would be a marvelous thing for it to have an instinct from nature to keep itself always over the same point of the Earth that it stood above when it set out, rather than falling behind by an enormous interval.

Salviati: The effect may be marvelous, or not marvelous but natural and ordinary, according to what the preceding conditions are. For if the ball (in keeping with the author's own assumptions), while it lingered in the hollow of the Moon, had the circular motion of the twenty-four hours together with the Earth and with the rest of what's contained inside that hollow, then the very same power that made it go around before it descended will go on making it go around during the descent too; and so far is it from failing to follow the Earth's motion — from having to fall behind — that it ought rather to run ahead of it, since, as it draws nearer to the Earth, the circular motion has to be made through continually smaller circles: so that, the ball keeping the same speed it had in the hollow, it ought, as I said, to outrun the Earth's whirl. But if the ball, up in the hollow, had no such circling, then in descending it's under no obligation to keep itself perpendicularly over that point of the Earth which lay beneath it when the fall began; nor will Copernicus, nor any of his followers, say so.

Simplicio: But the author will press the point, as you see, asking from what principle this circular motion of heavy and light bodies derives — that is, whether from an internal principle or an external one.

Salviati: Keeping to the problem we're dealing with, I say that the principle which made the ball go around while it was in the Moon's hollow is the same one that keeps up its circling during the descent too; I'll then leave the author free to make it internal or external as he likes.

Simplicio: The author will prove that it can be neither internal nor external.

Salviati: And I'll answer that the ball up in the concave wasn't moving at all — which frees me from having to explain how, in coming down, it stays forever vertical over the same point, seeing that it won't stay there in the first place.

Simplicio: Fine; but since heavy and light bodies can have no principle, internal or external, for moving in a circle, neither will the terrestrial globe move with circular motion — and so we'll have exactly what we set out to prove.

Salviati: I never said the Earth has no principle of circular motion, internal or external; I said I don't know which of the two it has — and my not knowing has no power to strip it of one. But if this author knows from what principle the other bodies of the world are moved around in their circles — bodies that certainly do move — then I say that whatever makes the Earth move is something like whatever moves Mars and Jupiter, and like whatever he believes moves the starry sphere as well. And if he'll assure me who the mover is of any one of these moving bodies, I bind myself to tell him who makes the Earth move. What's more, I'll do the very same thing if he can teach me who moves the parts of the Earth downward.

Simplicio: The cause of that effect is perfectly well known — everyone knows it's gravity.

Salviati: You're mistaken, Simplicio; you should have said that everyone knows it's called gravity. But I'm not asking its name — I'm asking the essence of the thing; and of that essence you know not one bit more than you know of the essence of whatever moves the stars around. The only difference is the name: to this one we've given a name and made it familiar and homely through the frequent experience of seeing it happen a thousand times a day. But we don't really understand any better what principle or power moves a stone downward than we understand what moves it upward once it's parted from the thrower, or what moves the Moon around — except, as I said, the name: to this one we've assigned the more particular and proper name of gravity, whereas to that one, in a more general term, we assign impressed force; to another we give an intelligence, or an assisting spirit, or an informing one; and to countless other motions we give nature as their cause.

Simplicio: It seems to me this author asks far less than what you're refusing to answer; for he doesn't ask you what, particularly and by name, the principle is that moves heavy and light bodies in a circle — whatever it may be, he only asks whether you judge it intrinsic or extrinsic. So that, for example, even though I don't know what gravity is — the gravity by which the Earth descends — I do know that it's an internal principle, since, unimpeded, it moves spontaneously; and, on the other hand, I know that the principle moving it upward is external, even though I don't know what the force impressed on it by the thrower actually is.

Salviati: Into how many questions we'd have to wander off, if we wanted to settle every difficulty that keeps hooking onto the last one in a chain! You call external — and preternatural and violent too — the principle that moves a heavy projectile upward. But perhaps it's no less internal and natural than the one that moves it down. It might, if you like, be called external and violent while the moving body is still joined to the thrower; but once they're separated, what external thing is left to move the arrow or the ball? So we're forced to admit that the power carrying it upward is no less internal than the one that moves it down; and I hold the upward motion of heavy bodies, from the impetus they've taken on, to be every bit as natural as their downward motion, which depends on gravity.

Simplicio: This I'll never grant; because the one has an internal principle, natural and perpetual, while the other has an external one, violent and finite.

Salviati: If you back away from granting me that the principles of the downward and upward motions of heavy bodies are equally internal and natural, what would you do if I told you they might even be one and the same in number?

Simplicio: I'll leave that for you to judge.

Salviati: No, I want you yourself for judge. So tell me: do you believe that in one and the same natural body there can reside internal principles that are contrary to each other?

Simplicio: Absolutely not, I believe.

Salviati: Of earth, of lead, of gold — in short, of the heaviest kinds of matter — what do you take their natural, intrinsic inclination to be? That is, toward what motion do you believe their internal principle draws them?

Simplicio: Toward motion to the center of heavy things — that is, to the center of the universe and of the Earth, where, unimpeded, they would take themselves.

Salviati: So that if the terrestrial globe were bored through by a well passing through its center, a cannonball dropped down it, moved by a natural and intrinsic principle, would carry itself to the center; and all this motion it would perform spontaneously, by an intrinsic principle — isn't that so?

Simplicio: That I hold for certain.

Salviati: But once it reached the center, do you believe it would pass on beyond, or that right there it would immediately cease from motion?

Simplicio: I believe it would go on moving for a very long stretch.

Salviati: But this motion past the center — wouldn't it be upward, and, by your own account, preternatural and violent? And on what other principle will you make it depend, except that very same one that carried the ball to the center — the one you called intrinsic and natural? Find me an external thrower that comes along again to drive it up. And what we say about the motion through the center can be seen up here among us too: for the internal impetus of a heavy body falling down a sloping surface, if that same surface curves up at the bottom and bends back upward, will carry the body up as well, without interrupting its motion in the least. A lead ball hanging from a string, drawn aside from the vertical, descends spontaneously, pulled by its internal inclination, and — without any pause of rest in between — passes through the lowest point and, with no fresh mover arriving, moves upward. I know you won't deny that the principle moving heavy bodies down is every bit as natural and internal to them as the one moving light bodies up. So I put before you a ball of wood, which, coming down through the air from a great height — and therefore moving by an internal principle — reaches the surface of a deep pool of water and continues its descent, sinking for a long stretch with no other external mover; and yet its downward motion through the water is preternatural to it, and for all that it depends on a principle that is internal, not external, to the ball. There, then, you have it demonstrated: one and the same internal principle can move a moving body in contrary motions.

Simplicio: I believe there are answers to all these objections, though for the moment they don't come to me. But however that may be, the author goes on to ask on what principle this circular motion of heavy and light bodies depends — that is, whether on an internal or an external one — and proceeds to prove that it can be neither, saying: Si ab externo, Deusne illum excitat per continuum miraculum? an vero angelus? an aër? Et hunc quidem multi assignant. Sed contra... — "If from something external, is it God who stirs it up by a continual miracle? Or is it an angel? Or the air? And indeed many assign it to the air. But against this..."

Salviati: Don't wear yourself out reading the objections, because I'm not one of those who attribute such a principle to the surrounding air.
