As for the miracle or the angel, I'd lean rather that way; for something that begins from a divine miracle or an angelic operation — such as the carrying of a cannonball up to the concave of the Moon — makes it not at all improbable that, by virtue of the same principle, it should do the rest as well. But as for the air, it's enough for me that it doesn't hinder the circular motion of the bodies that are said to move through it; and to do that, it's enough — nothing more is required — that the air move with the same motion and complete its circuits at the same speed as the terrestrial globe.

Simplicio: And he'll rise up against this too, asking who carries the air around — nature, or violence? And he refutes nature, saying that this goes against truth, against experience, against Copernicus himself.

Salviati: Against Copernicus it's nothing of the kind — Copernicus writes no such thing, and this author credits it to him with far too much courtesy. On the contrary, Copernicus says — and to my mind says it well — that the part of the air near the Earth, being rather a terrestrial exhalation, may share the Earth's nature and naturally follow its motion; or else, being right next to it, follow it in the way the Peripatetics say the upper region and the element of fire follow the motion of the Moon's concave. So it's for them to explain whether that motion is natural or violent.

Simplicio: The author will reply that if Copernicus makes only a lower part of the air move, the upper part lacking such motion, he won't be able to account for how that still air could carry the heavy bodies along with it and make them follow the Earth's motion.

Salviati: Copernicus will say that this natural propensity of the elemental bodies to follow the Earth's motion has a limited sphere, beyond which that natural inclination would cease; and besides, as I've said, it isn't the air that carries the moving bodies along — bodies that, once separated from the Earth, still follow its motion. So all the objections this author brings to prove that the air might not cause such effects collapse.

Simplicio: Since, then, that's not how it is, we'll have to say that such effects depend on an internal principle; against which position oboriuntur difficillimae, immo inextricabiles, quaestiones secundae — "there arise most difficult, indeed inextricable, second-order questions" — which are these: Principium illud internum vel est accidens, vel substantia: si primum, quale nam illud? nam qualitas loco motiva circum hactenus nulla videtur esse agnita. — "That internal principle is either an accident or a substance: if the first, what is it, then? For no quality producing motion in a circle has, up to now, been recognized to exist."

Salviati: How is it that none is known? Aren't these here the very ones — the qualities that move all this elemental matter around, together with the Earth? See how this author assumes as true the very thing in question.

Simplicio: He says that this isn't seen, and on this point it seems to me he's right.

Salviati: It isn't seen by us, because we're going around together with them.

Simplicio: Listen to the next objection:

Salviati: Granting for now that water and fire are contraries — and likewise air and earth (though there'd be plenty to say about that) — the most that could follow from it is that motions which are contrary to each other can't be common to them: so that, for example, the upward motion which naturally belongs to fire couldn't belong to water; rather, since water is by nature contrary to fire, the motion that suits it is the one contrary to fire's motion, which will be the motion deorsum — downward. But circular motion, which is contrary neither to sursum nor to deorsum — neither to up nor to down — and can in fact be mixed with both, as Aristotle himself affirms, why shouldn't it belong equally to heavy and to light bodies? Then again, the motions that can't be common to the living and the non-living are those that depend on the soul; but the motions that belong to the body, insofar as it is elemental and consequently shares in the qualities of the elements — why shouldn't those be common to the corpse and to the living thing? And so, if circular motion is proper to the elements, it will have to be common to the compounds as well.

Sagredo: This author must believe that when a dead cat falls from a window, it can't be that a live one could fall too — since it wouldn't be fitting for a corpse to share the qualities that belong to a living thing.

Salviati: So this author's argument doesn't hold against anyone who'd say that the principle of the circular motion of heavy and light bodies is an internal accident. How well he'll manage to prove it can't be a substance, I don't know.

Simplicio: He rises up against this with many objections, the first of which is this: Si secundum (nempe si dicas, tale principium esse substantiam), illud est aut materia, aut forma, aut compositum; sed repugnant iterum tot diversae rerum naturae, quales sunt aves, limaces, saxa, sagittae, nives, fumi, grandines, pisces, etc., quae tamen omnia, specie et genere differentia, moverentur a natura sua circulariter, ipsa naturis diversissima, etc. — "If the second — namely, if you say that such a principle is a substance — then it is either matter, or form, or a composite; but again there stand in the way so many different natures of things, such as birds, snails, stones, arrows, snow, smoke, hail, fish, and so on — all of which, differing in species and in genus, would nonetheless be moved by their own nature in a circle, they themselves being utterly different in nature, and so forth."

Salviati: If these things he lists are of different natures, and things of different natures can't have a common motion, then, if we're to satisfy them all, we'll have to think of something more than just two motions, up and down. And if we must find one motion for arrows, one for snails, another for stones, one for fish, then we'll also have to think about earthworms, and topazes, and agaric — which are no less different in nature from one another than hail and snow.

Simplicio: It seems you're making fun of these arguments.

Salviati: Not at all, Simplicio; but it's already been answered above — namely, that if a downward or an upward motion can belong to the things he lists, a circular one can belong to them no less. And staying within Peripatetic doctrine: wouldn't you put a greater difference between an elemental comet and a celestial star than between a fish and a bird? And yet those two both move in a circle. Now go on to the second argument.

Simplicio: Si Terra staret per voluntatem Dei, rotarentne caetera annon? si hoc, falsum est a natura gyrari; si illud, redeunt priores quaestiones; et sane mirum esset, quod gavia pisciculo, alauda nidulo suo et corvus limaci petraeque, etiam volens, imminere non posset. — "If the Earth stood still by the will of God, would the rest go on turning or not? If yes, then it's false that they spin by nature; if no, the earlier questions come back. And it would surely be a marvel that a gull could not hover — even if it wanted to — over its little fish, nor a lark over its nest, nor a crow over a snail or a stone."

Salviati: For my part, I'd give a general answer: that, granting the Earth were to stop its daily whirl by the will of God, those birds would do whatever that same will of God pleased. But if this author wants a more particular answer, I'd tell him they'd do exactly the opposite of what they would do if, while they were up in the air separated from the Earth, the terrestrial globe were by divine will suddenly thrown into a headlong motion. It's now for this author to assure us what would happen in that case.

Sagredo: Please, Salviati, grant this author my request — that, the Earth having stopped by the will of God, the other things separated from it went on wheeling around in their natural motion — and let's hear what impossibilities or absurdities would follow. For my own part, I can't see any greater disorders than the ones the author himself produces: namely, that the larks, however much they wanted to, couldn't keep themselves over their nests, nor the crows over the snails or the stones; from which it would follow that the crows would have to go without their craving for snails, and the little larks would die of hunger and cold, since they could be neither fed nor brooded by their mothers. That's the whole ruin I can draw out of it, going by what the author says. See for yourself, Simplicio, whether any greater troubles ought to follow.

Simplicio: I can't make out any greater ones myself, but it's quite believable that the author sees, beyond these, other disorders in nature which perhaps, for reasons of his own worthy of respect, he chose not to bring forward. So I'll go on to the third objection: Insuper, quî fit ut istae res tam variae tantum moveantur ab occasu in ortum parallelae ad aequatorem? ut semper moveantur, numquam quiescant? — "Besides, how does it come about that things so various all move only from west to east, parallel to the equator? That they always move and never rest?"

Salviati: They move from west to east, parallel to the equinoctial, without stopping — in exactly the way you believe the fixed stars move from east to west, parallel to the equinoctial, without stopping.

Simplicio: Quare quo sunt altiores celerius, quo humiliores tardius? — "Why do the higher ones move faster, the lower ones slower?"

Salviati: Because in a sphere or a circle turning about its center, the parts farther out trace larger circles, and the nearer ones trace smaller circles in the same time.

Simplicio: Quare quae aequinoctiali propiores in maiori, quae remotiores in minori, circulo feruntur? — "Why are the ones nearer the equinoctial carried in a larger circle, and those farther off in a smaller one?"

Salviati: To imitate the starry sphere, in which the ones nearer the equinoctial move in larger circles than the ones farther away.

Simplicio: Quare pila eadem sub aequinoctiali tota circa centrum Terrae ambitu maximo, celeritate incredibili, sub polo vero circa centrum proprium gyro nullo, tarditate suprema, volveretur? — "Why would one and the same ball, under the equinoctial, revolve wholly around the center of the Earth in the greatest circuit, with incredible speed, but under the pole revolve around its own center with no circuit at all, at the utmost slowness?"

Salviati: To imitate the stars of the firmament, which would do the same if the daily rotation were theirs.

Simplicio: Quare eadem res, pila v. g. plumbea, si semel Terram circuivit descripto circulo maximo, eamdem ubique non circummigret secundum circulum maximum, sed translata extra aequinoctialem in circulis minoribus agetur? — "Why does the same thing — a lead ball, say — if it has once gone around the Earth describing a great circle, not travel everywhere along a great circle, but instead, carried away from the equinoctial, get driven in smaller circles?"

Salviati: Because that's just what they'd do — indeed, on Ptolemy's doctrine, what they have done: certain fixed stars that were once very close to the equinoctial and traced enormous circles, and now, being far from it, trace smaller ones.

Sagredo: Oh, if only I could keep all these fine things in my head, I'd feel I'd made a real fortune! Simplicio, you simply must lend me this little book — there's bound to be a whole sea of rare and exquisite things inside it.

Simplicio: I'll make you a present of it.

Sagredo: Oh no, that I'd never do — I wouldn't deprive you of it for anything. But are the questions finished yet?

Simplicio: No, sir; just listen: Si latio circularis gravibus et levibus est naturalis, qualis est ea quae fit secundum lineam rectam? nam si naturalis, quomodo et is motus qui circum est, naturalis est, cum specie differat a recto? si violentus, quî fit ut missile ignitum, sursum evolans, scintillosum caput sursum a Terra, non autem circum, volvatur, etc.? — "If circular motion is natural to heavy and light bodies, what sort of motion is the one that goes in a straight line? For if it's natural, how is the motion that goes around also natural, since it differs in species from the straight? And if it's violent, how does it come about that a fiery missile, flying upward, turns its sparkling head upward, away from the Earth, and not around it, and so on?"

Salviati: It's been said a thousand times already that circular motion is natural to the whole and to the parts, so long as they're in their best arrangement; the straight is there to bring disordered parts back into order — though it's better to say that they never, whether ordered or disordered, move with straight motion, but with a mixed motion, which might even be pure circular. Only, of this mixed motion just one part stays visible and observable to us, namely the straight part, while the other part, the circular, remains imperceptible to us, because we share in it too. And this answers the case of the rockets, which move upward and around, but we can't make out the circular part, because we're moving along with it. But I don't think this author has ever grasped this mixture, since one can see how flatly he says that rockets go straight up and don't go around at all.

Simplicio: Quare centrum spherae delapsae sub aequatore spiram describit in eius plano, sub aliis parallelis spiram describit in cono? sub polo descendit in axe, lineam gyralem decurrens in superficie cylindrica consignatam? — "Why does the center of a falling sphere, under the equator, describe a spiral in the plane of the equator; under the other parallels, describe a spiral on a cone; while under the pole it descends along the axis, running out a spiral line traced on the surface of a cylinder?"

Salviati: Because of the lines drawn from the center to the circumference of the sphere — the lines along which heavy bodies descend — the one ending at the equinoctial marks out a circle, and those ending at the other parallels trace conical surfaces, while the axis traces nothing at all, but stays as it is. And if I'm to tell you my opinion freely, I'll say that I can't get out of all these questions any conclusion that weighs against the motion of the Earth; because if I asked this author — granting him that the Earth doesn't move — what would happen with all these particulars supposing it did move as Copernicus wants, I'm quite sure he'd say that all these very effects would follow: the ones he now urges as absurdities to rule out the Earth's mobility. So that in this man's mind, necessary consequences are reckoned as absurdities. But please, if there's anything more, let's get ourselves free of this tedious business.

Simplicio: In what comes next, there's an attack on Copernicus and his followers, who hold that the motion of the parts, once separated from their whole, is only for the sake of reuniting with that whole, but that what's absolutely natural is to move in a circle with the daily whirl. Against them he presses, saying that, according to these men's opinion, si tota Terra, una cum aqua, in nihilum redigeretur, nulla grando aut pluvia e nube decideret, sed naturaliter tantum circumferretur; neque ignis ullus aut igneum ascenderet, cum, illorum non improbabili sententia, ignis nullus sit supra. — "if the whole Earth, together with the water, were reduced to nothing, no hail or rain would fall from a cloud, but would only be carried around naturally; nor would any fire or fiery thing rise, since, by their not improbable opinion, there is no fire up above."

Salviati: This philosopher's foresight is marvelous, and worthy of great praise, seeing that he isn't content to think about the things that might happen while nature runs its course, but wants to be provided in advance in case those things should come about that we know absolutely will never come about. So then — just to hear some fine subtlety — I'll grant him that if the Earth and the water went to nothing, neither hail nor rain would fall anymore, nor would fiery matter rise any higher but would keep going around: what then? And what will the philosopher have to raise against me?

Simplicio: The objection is in the words that come right after; here they are: Quibus tamen experientia et ratio adversatur — yet experience and reason stand against these.

Salviati: Now I have to give in, since he has so great an advantage over me — namely experience, which I lack; for up to now I've never once had the luck to see the terrestrial globe, together with the element of water, reduced to nothing, so that I might have observed what the hail and the water got up to in this little end-of-the-world. But does he at least tell us, for our instruction, what they were doing?

Simplicio: He doesn't say.

Salviati: I'd pay anything to be able to have a word with this person, to ask him whether, when this globe vanished, it carried off the common center of gravity as well — as I believe it did; in which case I think the hail and the water would have been left dazed and stupefied up among the clouds, with no idea what to do with themselves. It could also be that, drawn by that great empty space left behind by the departure of the terrestrial globe, all the surroundings would rarefy — the air in particular, being supremely stretchable — and would rush in at top speed to fill it; and perhaps the more solid and material bodies, like the birds, of which by rights there must have been many up in the air, would have drawn in more toward the center of the great empty sphere (it seems quite reasonable that substances which pack a lot of matter into a smaller bulk should be assigned the tighter quarters, leaving the roomier ones to the rarer substances); and there, having finally died of hunger and dissolved into earth, they'd have formed a new little globe, with the bit of water that happened to be among the clouds at the time. It could also be that these same materials, seeing no light, wouldn't have noticed the Earth's departure, and would have gone on falling as usual, blindly, expecting to meet it, and little by little would have made their way to the center — where they'd go even now, if the globe itself weren't in the way. And finally, to give this philosopher a less wishy-washy answer, I tell him that I know as much about what would follow the annihilation of the terrestrial globe as he would have known about what was going to happen to it and around it before it was created; and since I'm sure he'd say that he couldn't even have imagined any of the things that in fact followed — of which experience alone has made him the expert — he'll have to grant me pardon and excuse me if I don't know what he knows about the things that would follow the annihilation of that globe, seeing that I lack this experience of his. Now tell me if there's anything else.

Simplicio: There's this figure, which represents the terrestrial globe with a great cavity around its center, filled with air; and to show that heavy bodies don't move downward to join the terrestrial globe, as Copernicus says, he sets this stone at the center and asks what it would do if set free; and he places another in the hollow of this great cavern and puts the same question, saying, as to the first: Lapis in centro constitutus aut ascendet ad Terram in punctum aliquod, aut non: si secundum, falsum est partes ob solam seiunctionem a toto ad illud moveri; si primum, omnis ratio et experientia renititur, neque gravia in suae gravitatis centro conquiescent. Item, si suspensus lapis liberatus decidat in centrum, separabit se a toto, contra Copernicum; si pendeat, refragatur omnis experientia, cum videamus integros fornices corruere — A stone placed at the center will either rise toward the Earth to some point, or it will not: if it will not, it is false that parts move toward the whole merely because they have been separated from it; if it does rise, all reason and experience fight against it, and heavy bodies will not rest at the center of their own gravity. Likewise, if a suspended stone, once released, drops to the center, it separates itself from the whole, against Copernicus; and if it stays hanging, all experience is contradicted, since we see whole vaults collapse.

Salviati: I'll answer, though at great disadvantage to myself, since I'm up against someone who has seen by experience what these stones do in this great cavern — something I've never seen. And I'll say that I believe the heavy bodies come before the common center of gravity; so that it is not a center — which is nothing but an indivisible point, and therefore of no power — that draws the heavy materials to itself, but rather that these materials, conspiring naturally toward union, form for themselves a common center, the one around which parts of equal moment come to rest. So I reckon that, if the great aggregate of heavy bodies were transported to any place whatever, the particles separated from the whole would follow it, and, unimpeded, would penetrate it until they found parts less heavy than themselves; but once they reached the point where they met heavier matter, they would sink no farther. And so I reckon that in the cavern filled with air, the whole vault would press down, and would hold itself up over that air only by force — that is, only when its hardness could not be overcome and broken by its weight; but detached stones, I believe, would descend to the center and would not float on top of the air. Nor could one say for that reason that they weren't moving toward their whole, since they'd be moving to just the place where all the parts of the whole would move, if they weren't impeded.

Simplicio: What's left is a certain error he points out in a follower of Copernicus who, in making the Earth move with the annual and the daily motion in the way that a cart wheel moves along the ground and on its own axle, ended up making either the terrestrial globe too big or the great orb — the annual orbit — too small; since 365 revolutions of the equinoctial are far less than the circumference of the great orb.

Salviati: Watch out — you're mixing it up, and saying the opposite of what must be written in the pamphlet: for it has to say that this author ended up making the terrestrial globe too small or the great orb too big, not the globe too big and the annual orbit too small.

Simplicio: The mix-up isn't mine at all: here are the pamphlet's own words: Non videt quod vel circulum annuum aequo minorem, vel orbem terreum iusto multo fabricet maiorem — He does not see that he makes either the annual circle too small, or the terrestrial globe much too large.

Salviati: Whether the first author erred, I can't know, since the pamphlet's author doesn't name him; but the pamphlet's own error is plain and inexcusable, whether or not that first follower of Copernicus erred, since the pamphleteer passes right over so gross a mistake without noticing it, and neither flags it nor corrects it. But let that be forgiven him, as an error of carelessness rather than anything else. Besides — were I not by now tired and sick of spending any more time, to very little profit, on these very slight squabbles — I could show how it's not impossible for a circle, no bigger even than a cart wheel, by making not just 365 but even fewer than 20 revolutions, to trace out or measure the circumference not only of the great orb but of one a thousand times bigger. I say this to show that there's no shortage of subtleties far greater than the one this author uses to catch Copernicus in error. But please, let's catch our breath a moment, and then come to this other philosopher, another opponent of the same Copernicus.

Sagredo: I really need it too, even though I've only worn out my ears; and if I thought I wasn't going to hear anything more ingenious from this other author, I don't know but I might just decide to go take the cool air out in a gondola.

Simplicio: I think you'll hear things with more weight to them, because this is a most accomplished philosopher, and a great mathematician besides, and he has refuted Tycho on the matter of the comets and the new stars.

Salviati: Is he perhaps the very author of the Anti-Tycho?

Simplicio: He's the very same one: but the refutation against the new stars isn't in the Anti-Tycho, except insofar as he shows there that they were no threat to the inalterability and ungenerability of the heavens, as I already told you. But after the Anti-Tycho, having found a way — by means of parallax — to demonstrate that they too are elemental things, contained within the sphere of the Moon, he wrote this other book, De tribus novis stellis, and so on, and inserted into it his arguments against Copernicus as well. Last time I brought you what he'd written about these new stars in the Anti-Tycho, where he didn't deny that they were up in the heavens, but showed that their appearance didn't upset the heavens' inalterability — and he did this by pure philosophical reasoning, in the way I described. It slipped my mind to tell you how he later found a way to remove them from the heavens, because, since he proceeds in this refutation by way of calculations and parallaxes — matters I understand little or not at all — I hadn't read it, and had only made a study of these objections against the Earth's motion, which are, after all, matters of natural philosophy.

Salviati: I understand perfectly, and it will be fitting — once we've heard the objections to Copernicus — for us to hear, or at least see, the way in which, by means of parallax, he demonstrates that those new stars were elemental — the very stars that so many astronomers of great renown all placed extremely high up, among the stars of the firmament. And if this author brings so great an enterprise to completion — pulling the new stars down out of the heavens and into the elemental sphere — he'll be well worthy of being exalted on high himself and carried up among the stars, or at least of having his name immortalized in fame among them. But let's dispatch as quickly as we can this part that opposes Copernicus's view, and start bringing forward his objections.

Simplicio: There'll be no need to read these ad verbum — word for word — since they're very long-winded; but, as you see, in reading them carefully several times over, I've marked in the margin the words where the whole nerve of the demonstration lies, and it'll be enough to read those. The first argument begins here: Et primo, si opinio Copernici recipiatur, criterium naturalis philosophiae, ni prorsus tollatur, vehementer saltem labefactari videtur — First, if Copernicus's opinion is accepted, the criterion of natural philosophy, if it isn't abolished altogether, seems at the very least severely shaken. This criterion holds — according to the view of every school of philosophers — that sense and experience are our guides in philosophizing; but on Copernicus's assumption the senses come to be greatly deceived, since they plainly see, close up and in the purest media, the heaviest bodies descend straight down along the perpendicular, never deviating so much as a hair from the straight line; and yet, for Copernicus, sight is deceived in a thing so clear, and that motion is not straight at all, but mixed of straight and circular.

Salviati: This is the first argument that Aristotle and Ptolemy and all their followers put forward — to which a full answer has already been given, the paralogism exposed, and it's been made quite plain how the motion shared by us and the other moving bodies is as if it did not exist. But since true conclusions have a thousand favorable confirmations backing them up, I'd like, for this philosopher's sake, to add something more; and you, Simplicio, playing his part, answer my questions. First, tell me: what effect does that stone have on you — the one which, falling from the top of the tower, is the cause of your noticing its motion at all? For if its falling worked nothing more, nothing new, in you than its resting on top of the tower did, you surely wouldn't notice its descent, nor tell its moving apart from its standing still.

Simplicio: I grasp its descent in relation to the tower, because now I see it beside one mark on the tower, then beside a lower one, and so on in succession, until I make it out reaching the ground.

Salviati: So then, if that stone had fallen from the talons of a flying eagle and were coming down through the plain, invisible air, and you had no other visible, fixed object to set it against, you wouldn't be able to grasp its motion?

Simplicio: On the contrary, I would notice it, since, to see it while it's way up high, I'd have to raise my head, and as it came down I'd have to lower it — in short, keep continually moving either my head or my eyes, following along with its motion.

Salviati: Now you've given the true answer. So you recognize that the stone is at rest while, without moving your eye at all, you keep seeing it there in front of you; and you recognize that it's moving when, to keep from losing sight of it, you have to move the organ of sight — that is, the eye. Therefore, whenever you saw an object continuously in the same aspect without ever moving your eye, you would always judge it to be motionless.

Simplicio: I think that would necessarily have to be so.

Salviati: Now imagine you're aboard a ship, and you've fixed your eye on the tip of the yardarm: do you think that, just because the ship was moving — even very fast — you'd need to move your eye to keep your gaze always on the tip of the yardarm and follow its motion?

Simplicio: I'm sure I wouldn't need to make any change at all — and not just my gaze: even if I'd trained the aim of a harquebus on it, I'd never, for any motion of the ship whatever, need to shift it so much as a hair to keep it lined up on the mark.

Salviati: And this happens because the motion the ship imparts to the yardarm, it imparts to you and to your eye as well, so that you don't need to move it at all to keep looking at the top of the yardarm; and consequently it appears motionless to you. Now carry this reasoning over to the whirling of the Earth and to the stone set on top of the tower, in which you can't make out the motion, because the motion needed to follow it you share in common with the stone, from the Earth, and you don't need to move your eye. But when the downward motion is then added — which is the stone's own, and not yours, and which mixes with the circular — the circular part, shared by the stone and your eye, goes on being imperceptible, and only the straight part becomes perceptible, because to follow it you have to move your eye, lowering it. To cure this philosopher of his error, I'd like to be able to tell him: some time when he's out in a boat, let him set up a fairly deep vessel full of water, and fit into it a ball of wax or some other material that sinks to the bottom very slowly — so slowly that in a minute it barely drops a single braccio, an arm-length — and then, driving the boat as fast as it will go, so that in a minute it covers more than a hundred braccia, let him gently drop the ball into the water and let it sink freely, and carefully observe its motion. First of all, he'd see it heading straight toward the point at the bottom of the vessel that it would tend to if the boat were standing still, and to his eye, in relation to the vessel, this motion would look perfectly perpendicular and perfectly straight; and yet one can't say it wasn't compounded of the straight-down and the circular around the element of water. And if these things happen in motions that aren't natural, and in materials on which we can run the experiment both in their state of rest and, oppositely, in motion — and yet, as far as appearances go, no difference is to be seen, and the senses seem to be fooled — what can we hope to make out concerning the Earth, which has forever been in the same condition as regards motion or rest? And at what time do we hope to test on it whether any difference is to be seen between these effects of local motion in its different states of motion and rest, if it eternally keeps to just one of these two?

Sagredo: These discussions have settled my stomach somewhat — those fish and those snails had rather turned it; and the first of them reminded me of the correction of an error so plausible that I'm not sure one in a thousand wouldn't accept it as beyond doubt. It was this: sailing off Syria, and having with me a very good telescope, given me by our mutual friend, who had invented it not many days before, I proposed to the sailors that it would be a great help in navigation to use it up in the maintop to spot ships from far off and identify them. The benefit was granted, but they raised the difficulty of using it, because of the ship's continual pitching and rolling — especially at the top of the mast, where the tossing is so much greater — and said it would be better to use it down at the foot of the mast, where that motion is less than anywhere else on the vessel. I (I won't hide my own mistake) fell in with the same opinion, and for the moment said nothing more; but afterward — I couldn't tell you what prompted me — I went back to chewing the matter over on my own, and finally saw my own simplicity (excusable, though) in accepting as true something utterly false: false, I say, that the maintop's greater tossing, compared with the small motion at the foot of the mast, should make it harder to use the telescope to catch an object.

Salviati: I'd have sided with the sailors — and with you too — at first.

Simplicio: And I likewise would have been, and still am; nor do I think that pondering it for a hundred years I'd understand it any other way.

Sagredo: So this time I'll get to play the schoolmaster over the pair of you (as they say); and since proceeding by questions seems to me to clear things up a great deal — quite apart from the pleasure of tripping up your companion, drawing out of his mouth what he didn't know he knew — I'll make use of that device. First, I'll assume that the ships, galliots, or other craft we're trying to spot and identify are a good way off — say 4, 6, 10, or 20 miles — since for recognizing the ones nearby there's no need of a glass; and consequently the telescope can, at such a distance of 4 or 6 miles, comfortably pick out the whole vessel, and an even bigger hull besides. Now I ask: of what kinds, and how many in number, are the motions made at the maintop, arising from the ship's tossing?

Salviati: Let's suppose the ship is heading east. First, on a perfectly calm sea, there'd be no motion but this forward one; but once the tossing of the waves is added, there'll be one that, raising and lowering the stern and the bow by turns, makes the maintop lean forward and back; other waves, making the vessel heel over, tip the mast right and left; others can turn the ship somewhat and make it swing — by the mizzen, let's say — off the due-east point, now toward the northeast, now toward the southeast; others, heaving the keel up from below, could make the ship, without swinging off course, simply rise and fall. In sum, it seems to me that in kind these motions are two: one that changes the telescope's direction by an angle, and another that changes it — let's say — along a line, without changing the angle: that is, always keeping the tube of the instrument parallel to itself.

Sagredo: Tell me next: if we, having first trained the telescope over there on that tower of Burano, six miles off from here, were to tilt it by an angle right or left, or up or down, by only a nail's-breadth, what effect would it have on our catching that tower?

Salviati: It would make it vanish from sight at once, because such a deflection, however tiny here, can amount over there to hundreds and thousands of braccia.

Sagredo: But if, without changing the angle, always keeping the tube parallel to itself, we shifted it 10 or 12 braccia farther over — right or left, up or down — what effect would that have as regards the tower?

Salviati: Utterly imperceptible; because, since the spaces here and there are contained between parallel rays, the shifts made here and there must be equal; and since the space the instrument takes in over there could hold many of those towers, we wouldn't lose it from sight at all.

Sagredo: Coming back now to the ship, we can state beyond doubt that moving the telescope right or left, up or down, and even forward or back, by 20 or 25 braccia — while always keeping it parallel to itself — cannot throw the line of sight off the observed point on the object by more than those same 25 braccia; and since, at a distance of 8 or 10 miles, the instrument's field takes in a space much wider than the galliot or other vessel being viewed, such a small shift doesn't make me lose it from sight. The hindrance, then, and the cause of losing the object, can come only from the change made by an angle — since, for the ship's tossing, shifting the telescope up or down, right or left, cannot amount to any great number of braccia. Now suppose you have two telescopes fixed in place, one at the lower part of the ship's mast, the other at the top not just of the mast but of the very tallest yardarm — when it's peaked up steeply — and both are trained on a vessel 10 miles off: tell me whether you think that, for any tossing of the ship and leaning of the mast whatever, a greater change of angle is made in the topmost tube than in the lowest. When a wave lifts the bow, it will indeed drive the tip of the yardarm back 30 or 40 braccia more than the foot of the mast, and so will pull the upper tube back by that much space, and the lower by only a single palm; but the angle changes just as much in the one instrument as in the other. Likewise, a wave coming on the beam carries the high tube right and left a hundred times more than the low one; but the angles either don't change or change equally. And whereas the change right or left, forward or back, up or down, brings no perceptible hindrance to the sight of distant objects, the change of angle brings a very great one. So we must necessarily confess that using the telescope at the top of the mast is no harder than at the foot, seeing that the angular changes are equal in both places.

Salviati: How careful one has to be before affirming or denying any proposition! I'll say it again: hearing it stated so confidently that, because the top of the mast moves through a greater distance than its foot, everyone will be persuaded that using a telescope up high must be far harder than down low. And in the same way I'm even willing to excuse those philosophers who fall into despair and throw up their hands at people who won't grant them that a cannonball — which they plainly see coming straight down along a straight, perpendicular line — really does move that way; those people insist instead that its motion runs along an arc, and a very, very slanting and sideways one at that. But let's leave them in that fix, and listen to the other objections the author we have in hand raises against Copernicus.

Simplicio: The author goes on to show how, on Copernicus's teaching, one has to deny the senses — and the plainest sensations at that. Take this: we, who feel the stirring of the lightest little breeze, would then have to feel nothing of the force of a perpetual wind striking us at a speed of more than 2,529 miles an hour — for that is how much ground the center of the Earth covers in a single hour, with its annual motion, along the circumference of the great orb (the Earth's yearly path), as he carefully calculates. And because — as he says, still following Copernicus's own view — cum Terra movetur circumpositus aër; motus tamen eius, velocior licet ac rapidior celerrimo quocumque vento, a nobis non sentiretur, sed summa tum tranquillitas reputaretur, nisi alius motus accederet. Quid est vero decipi sensum, nisi haec esset deceptio? — that is: when the Earth moves, the air surrounding it moves too; and yet its motion, faster and swifter though it be than any wind however swift, would not be felt by us, but would rather be reckoned the deepest calm, unless some other motion were added to it. And what is it to be deceived in one's senses, if this would not be a deception?

Salviati: This philosopher must believe that the Earth Copernicus sends wheeling around — together with the surrounding air — along the circumference of the great orb is not the one we live on, but some other, separate Earth; because ours carries us along with it too, at the very same speed as itself and the air around it. And what buffeting could we possibly feel, while we flee at exactly the same pace as the thing that means to strike us? This gentleman has forgotten that we, no less than the Earth and the air, are being carried around in the turn — and that in consequence we are always in contact with the same portion of air, which therefore never strikes us at all.

Simplicio: On the contrary — here are the very words that follow immediately: Præterea nos quoque rotamur ex circumductione Terræ, etc. — that is: besides, we too are spun around by the Earth's carrying-round, and so on.

Salviati: Now I can neither help him nor excuse him any longer; you excuse him and help him, Simplicio.

Simplicio: For the moment, caught off guard like this, no defense comes to me that satisfies even myself.

Salviati: Well then, sleep on it tonight and defend him tomorrow; meanwhile, let's hear the other objections.

Simplicio: He keeps pressing the same objection, showing that on Copernicus's road we have to deny our own sensations. For this principle by which we go around with the Earth is either internal to us, or external — that is, a snatching-along by the Earth itself. And if it's the second, then, since we feel no such snatching-along, we'd have to say that the sense of touch does not feel its own proper object when that object is joined to it, nor feel the impression it makes on the sensorium. But if the principle is internal, then we won't feel a local motion arising from ourselves, and we'll never notice an inclination perpetually bound up with us.

Salviati: So this philosopher's objection comes down to this: whether the principle by which we move with the Earth is external or internal, we ought in either case to feel it; and since we don't feel it, it's neither the one nor the other; and therefore we don't move, and consequently neither does the Earth. And I say it can be either way without our feeling it. As for its possibly being external, the experiment with the boat clears away every difficulty and more — and I say "and more" because, since we can set the boat moving whenever we like, and just as well bring it to rest, and watch with great care to see whether, from some difference the sense of touch can pick up, we might learn to tell whether it's moving or not — seeing that no such knowledge has ever yet been gained, why be surprised if the same fact stays hidden from us on the Earth, which may have been carrying us along forever, without our ever being able to test it at rest? Now, Simplicio, I believe you've gone by boat to Padua a thousand times; and if you'll confess the truth, you never once felt in yourself any share in that motion — except when the boat ran aground or struck some obstacle and stopped, and you, with the other passengers, caught off guard, were nearly pitched over headlong and in danger. The terrestrial globe would have to run into some obstacle that brought it up short; and I promise you that then you would indeed become aware of the impetus lodged in you, when it flung you off toward the stars. It's true you can become aware of the boat's motion by another sense — but only with the help of reasoning — namely by sight, when you look at the trees and buildings out in the countryside, which, being separate from the boat, seem to move the opposite way. But if you'd like to rest content about the Earth's motion on the strength of such an experience, I'd tell you to look at the stars, which for that very reason appear to move the opposite way. As for being surprised at not feeling this principle, supposing it were internal to us — that's an even less reasonable thought; because if we don't feel one that comes from outside us and frequently leaves us, why should we feel it when it resides in us unchangingly and without a break? Now, is there anything more in this first argument?

Simplicio: There's this little outburst: Ex hac itaque opinione necesse est diffidere nostris sensibus, ut penitus fallacibus vel stupidis in sensibilibus, etiam coniunctissimis, diiudicandis; quam ergo veritatem sperare possumus, a facultate adeo fallaci ortum trahentem? — that is: from this opinion, then, we are bound to distrust our senses, as utterly deceptive or dull in judging sensible things, even the ones most closely joined to us. What truth, then, can we hope for, when it takes its origin from so deceptive a faculty?

Salviati: Oh, I'd rather draw more useful and more reliable lessons from it — learning to be more circumspect and less trusting about whatever the senses present to us at first glance, since they can easily deceive us. And I wish this author hadn't worn himself out so trying to make us grasp, through the senses, that the motion of falling heavy bodies is simply straight and of no other kind; nor taken offense and cried out because something so clear, plain, and obvious is called into question. For by doing so he betrays a belief that those who say such motion is not straight after all, but rather circular, seem to actually see that stone travel in an arc — since he appeals more to their senses than to their reasoning to settle the matter. And that's not so, Simplicio; because just as I, who am neutral between these opinions and only put on Copernicus's mask, actor-fashion, in these performances of ours, have never seen — nor ever thought I saw — that stone fall any way but plumb straight down, so I believe the very same thing appears to everyone else's eyes. Better, then, that we set aside the appearance, on which we all agree, and bring reasoning to bear — either to confirm that the appearance is real, or to expose it as a fallacy.

Sagredo: If I could just once meet this philosopher — who does seem to me to rise well above many other followers of the same doctrines — I'd like, as a token of affection, to remind him of something he has certainly seen a thousand times, from which, much in keeping with what we're discussing, one can grasp how easily a person can be fooled by the bare appearance — or let's say, the presentation — of the senses. And the thing is this: to people walking along a road at night, the Moon seems to follow them, keeping step with them, as they watch her come skimming the eaves of the rooftops above which she appears — exactly as a cat would do that, really walking along the roof tiles, kept pace behind them. An appearance that, if reasoning didn't step in, would only too plainly deceive the eye.

Simplicio: True enough, there's no shortage of experiences that assure us of the fallacies of the bare senses. But setting such sensations aside for now, let's hear the arguments that follow, which are drawn, as he says, ex rerum natura — from the nature of things. The first is that the Earth cannot by its own nature move with three greatly different motions, unless we're willing to reject many plain axioms — self-evident truths. The first of these is that every effect depends on some cause. The second, that nothing produces itself — from which it follows that it's impossible for the mover and the thing moved to be entirely one and the same. And this is plain not only in things moved by an external mover; from the principles laid down, the same is gathered to hold in natural motion that depends on an internal principle. Otherwise, since the mover, as mover, is cause, and the moved, as moved, is effect, the very same thing would be entirely both cause and effect. So a body does not move the whole of itself — that is, such that the whole moves and the whole is moved; rather, in the thing moved we must somehow distinguish the efficient principle of the motion from that which is moved by it. The third axiom is that, among things subject to the senses, one thing, insofar as it is one, produces a single thing only. Thus the soul in an animal does indeed produce different operations — sight, hearing, smell, generation — but with different instruments; and in short, one sees that in sensible things the different operations derive from some diversity in the cause. Now, if we put these axioms together, it will be perfectly clear that a simple body, such as the Earth, cannot by its own nature move with three greatly different motions at once. For, by the assumptions made, the whole does not move the whole of itself; we must therefore distinguish in it three principles for three motions, otherwise one and the same principle would produce several motions. But since it would then contain within itself three principles of natural motion, besides the part that is moved, it would not be a simple body, but one compounded of three moving principles and of the part moved. If, then, the Earth is a simple body, it will not move with three motions. Indeed, it won't move with any of those Copernicus assigns it, since it must move with one only — it being plain, by Aristotle's reasoning, that it moves toward its own center, as its parts show, which descend at right angles to the spherical surface of the Earth.

Salviati: Many things could be said and weighed about the fabric of this argument; but since we can settle it in a few words, I don't care to spread myself out needlessly right now — all the more since the answer is handed to me by the author himself, when he says that in an animal different operations are produced by a single principle. So for now I answer him that, in the same way, different motions derive in the Earth from a single principle.

Simplicio: The author of the objection won't be quieted in the least by this answer; on the contrary, your answer is completely knocked down by what he adds immediately afterward to shore up the attack he's made — as you're about to hear. He reinforces the argument, I say, with another axiom, which is this: that nature is neither deficient nor excessive in things necessary. This is plain to observers of natural things, and above all of animals, in which, because they had to move with many motions, nature has given them many joints, and there has aptly bound the parts together for movement — as at the knees and the hips, for the walking of animals and for lying down at their pleasure; and further, in man, she has built many bends and articulations at the elbow and the hand, so as to be able to carry out many motions. From these things the argument against the Earth's triple motion is drawn: either a single, continuous body, without being articulated by any joint, can carry out different motions, or it cannot without having joints. If it can without them, then nature made the joints in animals in vain, which is against the axiom; but if it cannot without them, then the Earth, a single continuous body devoid of joints and articulations, cannot by its own nature move with several motions. Now see how shrewdly he goes to meet your answer — almost as if he'd foreseen it.

Salviati: Are you speaking in earnest, or ironically?

Simplicio: I'm speaking with the best sense I've got.

Salviati: Then you must feel you have such a good hand that you can even keep up this philosopher's defense against some further reply that might be made against him. So do me the kindness, please, of answering me on his behalf, since we can't have him here in person. First, you grant it as true that nature made the joints, bends, and articulations in animals so that they could move with many and various motions; and I deny you this proposition. I say the joints were made so that the animal can move one or more of its parts while the rest stays still; and I say that as for the kinds and differences of motion, they are all of one sort only — that is, all circular. And this is why you see that all the heads of the movable bones are either rounded or hollowed: of these, some are spherical, namely those that have to move every which way — as the standard-bearer's arm does at the shoulder joint when he handles the flag, and the falconer's when he calls back the falcon with the lure; and such is the bend of the elbow, on which the hand turns when boring with an auger. Others are circular in one direction only, and nearly cylindrical, serving the limbs that bend in a single way — like the sections of the fingers, one upon another, and so on. But without going through more particular cases, a single general argument can make this truth known: namely, that when a solid body moves while one of its ends stays in place without shifting, the motion can only be circular; and since, when the animal moves one of its limbs, it does not separate it from the neighboring limb, therefore that motion is of necessity circular.

Simplicio: I don't understand it that way; on the contrary, I see the animal move with a hundred non-circular motions, all utterly different from one another — running and jumping and climbing and descending and swimming and many more.

Salviati: Fair enough — but those are secondary motions, depending on the primary ones, which belong to the joints and bends. From the bending of the legs at the knees and the thighs at the hips, which are circular motions of the parts, there follows the jump or the run, which are motions of the whole body, and these can be non-circular. Now, since in the terrestrial globe there's no need to move one part over another that stays still — the motion being of the whole body — there's no need for joints.

Simplicio: This (the other side will say) could hold when the motion is just one; but their being three, and utterly different from one another — it's impossible that they be accommodated in an unjointed body.

Salviati: That, I truly believe, would be the philosopher's reply; and against it I rise from another quarter, and ask you whether you think the terrestrial globe could be fitted, by way of joints and bends, to share in three different circular motions. You don't answer? Since you keep silent, I'll answer for the philosopher, who would absolutely say yes — because otherwise it would have been pointless and beside the case to bring up that nature makes joints so that a movable body can move with different motions, and that therefore, the terrestrial globe having no joints, it cannot have the three motions assigned to it. For if he had judged that not even by way of joints could it be made fit for such motions, he'd have freely declared that the globe cannot move with three motions at all. Now, this being so, I beg you — and through you, if it were possible, the philosopher who authored the argument — to be so kind as to teach me in what manner the joints would have to be arranged so that the three motions could be comfortably carried out; and I'll allow you four, or even six, months for your answer. Meanwhile it seems to me that a single principle can cause several motions in the terrestrial globe, in exactly the way I answered before — that a single principle, by means of various instruments, produces multiple and different motions in an animal. And as for jointing, there's no need of it, since the motions must be of the whole and not of some parts; and because they have to be circular, the simple spherical shape is the finest jointing one could ask for.

Simplicio: The most that could be granted you would be that this might happen with a single motion; but with three different ones, in my opinion and the author's, it's not possible — as he goes on, still reinforcing the objection, writing: "Let us imagine, with Copernicus, that the Earth moves, by its own faculty and from an internal principle, from west to east in the plane of the ecliptic; and beyond that, that it turns, also from an internal principle, about its own center from east to west; and, for the third motion, that by its own inclination it tilts from north to south and back again. Since it is a continuous body, not linked by bends and joints, can our estimation and our judgment ever grasp that one and the same natural, undivided principle — that is, one and the same propensity — should split itself at once into different and almost contrary motions? I cannot believe that anyone would say such a thing, except one who had set himself, rightly or wrongly, to uphold this position."

Salviati: Hold on a moment, and find me this passage in the book; show me. Fingamus modo cum Copernico, Terram aliqua sua vi et ab indito principio impelli ab occasu ad ortum in eclipticæ plano, tum rursus revolvi ab indito etiam principio circa suimet centrum ab ortu in occasum, tertio deflecti rursus suopte nutu a septentrione in austrum et vicissim — that is: let us suppose, with Copernicus, that the Earth is driven by some force of its own, and from an inborn principle, from west to east in the plane of the ecliptic; then again turned, also by an inborn principle, about its own center from east to west; and, third, deflected once more, by its own nod, from north to south and back again. I had my doubts, Simplicio, that you might have made a slip in reporting the author's words to us; but I see that he himself, and all too gravely, is mistaken — and to my regret I realize he has set out to attack a position he hasn't properly understood; for these are not the motions Copernicus assigns to the Earth. And where does he get the idea that Copernicus makes the annual motion along the ecliptic run contrary to the motion about its own center? He can't have read Copernicus's book, which in a hundred places — and even in the opening chapters — writes that both these motions go toward the same quarter, that is, from west to east. But even without hearing it from anyone else, shouldn't he have grasped on his own that, when you assign to the Earth the motions taken away one from the Sun and the other from the primum mobile, they'd necessarily have to be made in the same direction?

Simplicio: Just take care that you don't go wrong yourself — and Copernicus along with you. Isn't the daily motion of the primum mobile from east to west? And isn't the Sun's annual motion along the ecliptic, on the contrary, from west to east? How, then, do you expect these same motions, transferred to the Earth, to turn from contrary into concordant?

Sagredo: Simplicio has certainly uncovered for us the source of this philosopher's error: he too must have reasoned in just this way.

Salviati: Well, while we can, let's at least clear Simplicio of his error. Seeing the stars rise above the eastern horizon at their rising, he'll have no trouble understanding that, if that motion did not belong to the stars, one would necessarily have to say that the horizon sank with a contrary motion — and consequently that the Earth turned upon itself the opposite way from how the stars seem to move to us, that is, from west to east, which is the order of the signs of the zodiac. As for the other motion: since the Sun is fixed at the center of the zodiac and the Earth moves along its circumference, then for the Sun to appear to us to move through that zodiac in the order of the signs, the Earth must travel in that same order — seeing that the Sun always appears to us to occupy the degree of the zodiac opposite the degree in which the Earth is found. And so, while the Earth runs through, say, Aries, the Sun will appear to run through Libra; and as the Earth passes through the sign of Taurus, the Sun will run through Scorpio; the Earth through Gemini, the Sun through Sagittarius. But this is for both to move in the same direction — that is, according to the order of the signs — just as the Earth's revolution about its own center was too.

Simplicio: I understand perfectly, and I couldn't produce a single thing to excuse so great a blunder.

Salviati: But gently, Simplicio — there's another mistake bigger than this one. He has the Earth turn with its daily rotation about its own center from east to west, and he doesn't grasp that if that were so, the twenty-four-hour motion of the universe would appear to us to run from west to east — exactly the opposite of what we actually see.

Simplicio: Oh, I — who've barely glimpsed the first elements of the sphere — I'm certain I wouldn't have gone so badly wrong.

Salviati: Judge for yourself, then, how much study you can credit this critic with having put into Copernicus's books, if he takes backwards the one principal, supreme hypothesis on which rests the whole sum of the matters where Copernicus parts company with Aristotle and Ptolemy. As for this third motion that the author — again claiming to speak Copernicus's own mind — assigns to the terrestrial globe, I don't know which one he thinks he means. It's certainly not the one Copernicus gives it alongside the other two, the annual motion and the daily rotation, and which has nothing to do with any tilting toward north or south, but serves only to keep the axis of the daily revolution perpetually parallel to itself. So we have to say either that the critic didn't understand this, or that he pretended not to. And though this one grave failing would be enough to release us from any duty to keep occupying ourselves with his objections, still I want to hold them in esteem — for they truly deserve to be valued far more than a thousand others from other empty critics. Coming back, then, to his challenge: I say that the two motions, the annual and the daily, are not in the least contrary; on the contrary, they run the same way, and so can spring from one and the same principle. And the third follows so naturally from the annual, of itself and spontaneously, that there's no need to summon any principle, inner or outer (as I'll show in its proper place), to produce it as a cause.

Sagredo: I'd like to add a word of my own to this critic, guided by plain common sense. He wants to condemn Copernicus if I can't resolve, point by point, every doubt and answer every objection he raises — as though the falsity of Copernicus's doctrine followed necessarily from my ignorance. But if this way of condemning writers strikes him as sound legal procedure, then he shouldn't find it unreasonable that I withhold my approval of Aristotle and Ptolemy, so long as he can't resolve, any better than I can, the very same difficulties I raise against their doctrine. He asks me what principles move the terrestrial globe through its annual motion in the zodiac, and its daily rotation on itself along the equinoctial. I tell him they're much like the principles by which Saturn moves through the zodiac in thirty years, and turns on itself in a far shorter time along its own equinoctial — as the appearing and vanishing of its flanking globes shows us. And much like the principle by which he himself would concede, without a qualm, that the Sun runs along the ecliptic in a year and turns on its own axis, parallel to the equinoctial, in less than a month — as its spots plainly show. And much like the principle by which the Medicean Stars — Jupiter's four little moons — run through the zodiac in twelve years, and meanwhile wheel in tiny circles and very short times around Jupiter itself.

Simplicio: This author will deny you all these things — as illusions of eyesight, produced by the crystals of the telescope.

Sagredo: Oh, that would be claiming far too much for himself. He insists the naked eye can't be fooled when it judges the straight downward motion of falling weights, and yet he wants that same eye fooled when it takes in these other motions — even with its power perfected and multiplied thirtyfold. So let's tell him instead that the Earth shares in a plurality of motions in a way much like — perhaps exactly like — the way the lodestone moves downward as a heavy body and also has two circular motions, one horizontal and the other vertical, beneath the meridian. But what more? Tell me, Simplicio: which do you think this author would judge more different from each other — straight motion and circular, or motion and rest?

Simplicio: Motion and rest, surely. And that's obvious, because for Aristotle circular motion is not contrary to straight motion — he even grants that the two can be mixed — whereas that's impossible for motion and rest.

Sagredo: Then it's the less improbable proposition to lodge in one natural body two inner principles, one for straight and the other for circular motion, than to lodge two — also inner — one for motion and the other for rest. Now, as for the natural inclination that resides in the parts of the Earth, to return to their whole when they're forcibly torn away from it, both positions agree; they part company only over what the whole does. The one wants the whole to stand still by an inner principle; the other credits it with circular motion. But by your own admission and this philosopher's, two principles — one for motion, the other for rest — are mutually incompatible, since their effects are incompatible. Whereas that doesn't happen with the two motions, straight and circular, which have no quarrel between them at all.

Salviati: Add to this that, very probably, the motion made by a piece of the Earth as it works its way back to its whole may itself be circular too, as we've already stated. So that on every count, as far as the present case goes, mobility seems more acceptable than rest. Now go on, Simplicio, with whatever's left.

Simplicio: The author reinforces his challenge by pointing out another absurdity — namely, that the very same motions belong to natures utterly different from one another. But observation teaches us that the operations and motions of different natures are themselves different; and reason confirms it, for otherwise we'd have no way in to knowing and distinguishing natures, if they didn't have their own motions and operations to guide us to a knowledge of the substances.

Sagredo: Two or three times now I've noticed, in this author's arguments, that to prove a thing stands in such-and-such a way, he falls back on saying that in that way it fits our intelligence, or that otherwise we'd have no access to the knowledge of this or that particular, or that the whole standard of philosophy would be ruined — as if nature first made the brains in men, and only afterward arranged things to suit the capacity of their intellects. But I'd sooner reckon that nature first made things her own way, and only afterward fashioned human reasoning capable of grasping (though with great effort) some little part of her secrets.

Salviati: I'm of the same opinion. But tell me, Simplicio: which are these different natures to which Copernicus, against observation and reason, assigns the same motions and operations?

Simplicio: Here they are: water and air (which are, after all, natures different from earth), and everything found in those elements, will each have those three motions that Copernicus invents for the terrestrial globe. And he goes on to demonstrate geometrically how, on Copernicus's scheme, a cloud that hangs in the air and stays a long while over our heads without changing place must necessarily have all three of the motions the terrestrial globe has. Here's the demonstration — you can read it for yourself, for I couldn't repeat it from memory.

Salviati: I won't trouble to read it. In fact I think it was pointless to include it, because I'm sure not one of those who hold the Earth to move will deny it to him. So, granting him the demonstration, let's talk about the challenge itself — which doesn't seem to me to have much force to conclude anything against Copernicus's position, since it takes nothing away from those motions and operations by which we come to know natures, and so on. Answer me, if you'd be so kind, Simplicio: those properties in which certain things agree most exactly — can they serve to make us recognize the different natures of those things?

Simplicio: No, sir — quite the opposite, because from an identity of operations and properties you can argue nothing except an identity of natures.

Salviati: So the different natures of water, of earth, of air, and of the other things scattered through these elements — you don't infer them from the operations in which all these elements and their contents agree, but from other operations. Is that right?

Simplicio: That's how it stands, in fact.

Salviati: So that anyone who left the elements all those motions, operations, and other properties by which their natures are distinguished would not deprive us of the power to come to know them — even if he then removed the one operation in which they all agree together, and which therefore does nothing to distinguish such natures.

Simplicio: I believe the argument runs very well.

Salviati: But that earth, water, and air are by nature equally constituted to be motionless around the center — isn't that your opinion, and the author's, and Aristotle's, and Ptolemy's, and all their followers'?

Simplicio: It's accepted as an irrefutable truth.

Salviati: Then from this common natural condition — resting around the center — you can draw no argument about the different natures of these elements and elemental things; such knowledge has to be gathered from other, non-shared qualities. And so anyone who stripped from the elements only this shared rest, and left them all their other operations, would in no way block the road that leads us to knowledge of their essences. But Copernicus strips from them nothing except this shared rest, and transmutes it into a most thoroughly shared motion — leaving them their heaviness, their lightness, their motions up and down, slower and faster, their rarity, their density, the qualities of hot, cold, dry, wet, and in short everything else. So the absurdity this author imagines simply isn't there in the Copernican position; nor does agreeing in an identity of motion count for any more or less than agreeing in an identity of rest, as far as diversifying natures — or not — is concerned. Now tell me if there's any other argument on the other side.

Simplicio: A fourth challenge follows, again drawn from natural observation: that bodies of the same kind have motions that agree in kind, or else agree in rest. But on Copernicus's position, bodies that agree in kind, and are extremely alike, would as to their motion be in supreme disagreement — indeed in flat, diametrical opposition. For stars so alike among themselves would nonetheless be so unlike in motion, since six planets would revolve forever, while the Sun and all the fixed stars would forever stay still.

Salviati: The form of the argument seems conclusive to me; but I strongly suspect the application, or the subject matter, is defective — and provided the author insists on his assumption, the conclusion will come out flatly against him. The argument runs like this. Among the bodies of the world there are six that move perpetually, and these are the six planets. Of the rest — that is, the Earth, the Sun, and the fixed stars — it's in doubt which of them moves and which stands still; for necessarily, if the Earth stands still, the Sun and the fixed stars move, and it could equally be that the Sun and the fixed stars stand motionless while the Earth moves. So the question, with the fact in doubt, is to whom motion can more fittingly be assigned, and to whom rest. Natural reasoning dictates that the motion should be reckoned to belong to whatever agrees most, in kind and in essence, with the bodies that indisputably move, and rest to whatever differs from them most. And since eternal rest and perpetual motion are properties as unlike as can be, it's plain that the nature of the ever-moving body must be utterly unlike the nature of the ever-still one. Let's search, then — while we're still undecided between motion and rest — to see whether, by way of some other telling condition, we might discover which agrees more with the surely moving bodies: the Earth, or the Sun and fixed stars. And behold, nature, kind to our need and desire, furnishes us two striking conditions, no less different than motion and rest: light and darkness — that is, being by nature most brilliant, and being dark and stripped of all light. Utterly unlike in essence, then, are the bodies adorned with an inner and eternal splendor and the bodies deprived of all light. The Earth is deprived of light; the Sun is most brilliant of itself, and no less the fixed stars; the six moving planets wholly lack light, like the Earth. Therefore their essence agrees with the Earth and differs from the Sun and fixed stars. So the Earth is a moving body; the Sun and the starry sphere are motionless.

Simplicio: But the author won't concede that the six planets are dark, and he'll dig in on that denial — or else he'll argue the great likeness of nature between the six planets and the Sun and fixed stars, and their unlikeness to the Earth, from conditions other than darkness and light. In fact, now that it strikes me, in the fifth challenge that comes next he sets out the supreme disparity between the Earth and the heavenly bodies. There he writes that great confusion and disorder would be introduced into the system of the universe and among its parts on Copernicus's hypothesis. For among heavenly bodies immutable and incorruptible — according to Aristotle and Tycho and others — among bodies, I say, of such nobility (as everyone confesses, and Copernicus himself, who declares them ordered and arranged in the finest constitution and clears them of every inconstancy of power) — among bodies, I say, so pure, that is, between Venus and Mars, to plant the sink of all corruptible matter: the Earth, the water, the air, and all the mixed bodies! Whereas how much finer a distribution, and more befitting nature — indeed befitting God himself as architect — to sequester the pure from the impure, the mortal from the immortal, as the other schools teach; schools that show us how these impure and perishable materials are confined within the narrow concave of the lunar orb, above which the heavenly things then rise in an unbroken series!

Salviati: It's true that the Copernican system throws Aristotle's universe into turmoil; but we're dealing with our universe, the true and real one. Now, when this author wants to infer the disparity of essence between the Earth and the heavenly bodies from the incorruptibility of the latter and the corruptibility of the former — in Aristotle's manner — and from that disparity to conclude that motion must belong to the Sun and fixed stars and immobility to the Earth, he goes wandering into the paralogism of assuming the very thing in question. For Aristotle infers the incorruptibility of the heavenly bodies from motion — and it's precisely that motion whose ownership is in dispute, whether it's theirs or the Earth's. As for the emptiness of these rhetorical inferences, enough has been said. And what could be more foolish than to say the Earth and the elements are banished and cut off from the heavenly spheres, penned up inside the lunar orb? But isn't the lunar orb one of the heavenly spheres — and, by their own agreement, set in the very middle of all the rest? A fine new way to separate the pure from the impure and the plague-stricken from the healthy: to lodge the infected in the heart of the city! I'd have thought the pest-house ought to be pushed as far off as possible. Copernicus admires the arrangement of the parts of the universe precisely because God set the great lamp, which was to pour its supreme splendor over all his temple, at the center of it, and not off to one side. As for the terrestrial globe standing between Venus and Mars, we'll deal with that shortly — and you yourself, for this author's sake, will try your hand at moving it out from there. But please, let's not weave these rhetorical posies together with the solidity of demonstrations; let's leave them to the orators, or rather to the poets, who've known how, with their charms, to lift up with praise the vilest things — and sometimes even harmful ones. And if anything else is left, let's dispatch it as quickly as we can.

Simplicio: There's the sixth and last argument, in which he sets it down as most unlikely that a corruptible, dissipable body could move with a perpetual, regular motion; and he backs this with the example of animals, which, though they move with a motion natural to them, still grow tired, and need rest to restore their strength. But what has such movement to do with that of the Earth, which is immense by comparison with theirs? And, worse, to make it move with three motions running and pulling in different directions? Who could ever assert such things, except men who'd sworn themselves its defenders? Nor does it help in this case what Copernicus brings forward — that because this motion is natural to the Earth, and not violent, it produces effects contrary to those of violent motions; and that the things to which force is applied do indeed dissolve, and can't last long, whereas the things made by nature are preserved in their best condition. This answer, I say, is no help, for it's overthrown by our own. Because the animal is, after all, a natural body, not built by art, and its movement is natural, deriving from the soul — that is, from an inner principle; while violent motion is the kind whose principle lies outside, and to which the thing moved contributes nothing. And yet, if the animal keeps up its motion a long time, it grows tired, and even dies, if it stubbornly tries to force itself. See, then, how in nature one meets, on every side, traces that run counter to Copernicus's position, and never a single one in its favor. And so as not to have to take up this critic's part yet again, hear what he brings forward against Kepler (with whom he's arguing), concerning the point Kepler pressed against those who find it awkward — indeed impossible — to enlarge the starry sphere immensely, as Copernicus's position requires. Kepler presses them, then, saying: Difficilius est accidens praeter modulum subiecti intendere, quam subiectum sine accidente augere — it is harder to stretch a property beyond the scale of its subject than to enlarge the subject without the property. Copernicus igitur verisimilius facit, qui auget orbem stellarum fixarum absque motu, quam Ptolemaeus, qui auget motum fixarum immensa velocitate — Copernicus, then, does the more plausible thing, who enlarges the sphere of the fixed stars without motion, than Ptolemy, who enlarges the motion of the fixed stars to an immense velocity. This challenge the author dissolves, marveling at how badly Kepler deceives himself in saying that on Ptolemy's hypothesis the motion is stretched beyond the scale of its subject. For to him it seems the motion is stretched only in keeping with the scale, and that as the scale grows the velocity of the motion grows with it. He proves this by imagining a millstone that makes one revolution in twenty-four hours — a motion we'd call extremely slow. Now suppose its radius stretched out to the distance of the Sun: its far end would match the velocity of the Sun. Stretched to the starry sphere, it would match the velocity of the fixed stars — even though at the rim of the millstone the motion is extremely slow. Now apply this consideration of the millstone to the starry sphere: take a point on its radius, as near the center as the millstone's radius is short.
