That same motion, which out at the starry sphere is exceedingly swift, will at that point be exceedingly slow. It's the size of the body that turns the exceedingly slow into the exceedingly swift, even while the motion stays one and the same. And so the velocity grows not beyond the scale of the subject, but in keeping with it and with its size — quite differently from what Kepler supposes.

Salviati: I don't believe this author has formed so thin and low an opinion of Kepler that he could persuade himself Kepler failed to understand that the topmost end of a line drawn from the center out to the starry orb moves faster than a point on that same line only a couple of arm-lengths from the center. So he's bound to grasp and understand that Kepler's meaning and intention was to say this: it is the smaller drawback to enlarge a motionless body to an enormous size than to attribute an enormous velocity to a body already vast — bearing in mind the scale, that is, the norm and the example, of the other natural bodies, in which we see that as the distance from the center increases, the velocity decreases; that is, that the periods of their revolutions require longer times. Whereas in rest — which can't be made greater or smaller — the largeness or smallness of the body makes no difference at all. So that, if the author's reply is really to meet Kepler's argument, the author must be reckoning that it's all the same to the moving principle whether it moves, in the same span of time, a tiny body or an immense one — since on his view the increase of velocity follows, without more ado, from the increase of the bulk. But that runs against the architectural rules of nature, which, in the pattern of the smaller spheres — as we see in the planets, and most plainly in the Medicean Stars — makes the smaller orbs revolve in shorter times; so that Saturn's period of revolution is longer than all the periods of the other, smaller spheres, being thirty years. Now to jump from this to a vastly greater sphere and make it turn in twenty-four hours may well, and reasonably, be called going outside the rules of the pattern. So that, if we consider carefully, the author's reply goes not against the concept and sense of the argument, but only against its wording and manner of expression — where, moreover, the author is in the wrong, and can't deny having deliberately feigned not to understand the words, so as to saddle Kepler with a too-gross ignorance. But the fraud has been so crude that, for all so large a discount, it couldn't knock down the opinion of his doctrine that Kepler has stamped on the minds of learned men. Then, as for the challenge against the perpetual motion of the Earth, drawn from its being impossible for the Earth to keep it up without tiring — seeing that the very animals, which do move naturally and from an inner principle, grow tired and need rest to relax their limbs...

Sagredo: I seem to hear Kepler answering him that, after all, there are animals that refresh themselves from weariness by rolling about on the ground, and that we needn't fear the terrestrial globe wearing itself out — on the contrary, one might reasonably say it enjoys a perpetual and utterly tranquil rest, keeping itself in an eternal rolling-about.

Salviati: Sagredo, you're too clever by half, and too fond of satire. But let's set the jokes aside while we're dealing with serious matters.

Sagredo: Forgive me, Salviati, but what I'm saying isn't so far beside the point as you perhaps make it out to be. A motion that serves as rest and removes the weariness from a body worn out by travel can far more easily serve to keep that weariness from ever setting in — since preventive remedies are easier than curative ones. And I hold it for certain that if the motion of animals proceeded like this one attributed to the Earth, they wouldn't tire at all; for a body's growing tired derives, to my mind, from employing a single part to move both itself and all the rest of the body. To walk, for instance, we use only the thighs and legs, which have to carry themselves and everything else besides; whereas you'll notice the motion of the heart is, as it were, tireless, because it moves itself alone. What's more, I don't know how true it is that an animal's motion is natural rather than violent — indeed I think one can truly say that the soul moves the animal's limbs naturally by a preternatural motion, a motion beyond their own nature. For if upward motion is preternatural to heavy bodies, then raising the legs and thighs, which are heavy bodies, in order to walk, can't be done without violence, and therefore not without effort on the mover's part. Climbing a staircase carries the heavy body upward, against its natural inclination, and from that follows weariness, by way of gravity's natural resistance to such motion. But to move a movable body with a motion to which it has no resistance whatever — what tiring, what draining of power and force, is there to fear in the mover? And why should force be spent where none is exercised at all?

Simplicio: It's the contrary motions — the ones the terrestrial globe is imagined to move by — that the author builds his objection on.

Sagredo: We've already said they aren't contrary at all, and that on this point the author badly deceived himself, so that the whole force of the objection turns back against the objector himself — once he wants the primum mobile to sweep all the lower spheres along, against the very motion those spheres are exercising at the same time and continuously. So it's the primum mobile that ought to get tired, since, on top of moving itself, it has to drag along so many other spheres — spheres that, what's more, fight back against it with a contrary motion. And so that final conclusion the author drew — that when you run through the effects of nature you always meet things favorable to the opinion of Aristotle and Ptolemy, and never anything that doesn't tell against Copernicus — needs a great deal of rethinking. Better to say that, since one of these two positions is true and the other necessarily false, it's impossible for any reason, experience, or sound argument ever to be found in favor of the false one, just as none of these things can be repugnant to the true one. So there's bound to be a great difference between the arguments and reasons produced on the one side and the other, for and against these two opinions — and I'll leave you yourself to judge their force, Simplicio.

Salviati: Sagredo, carried away by the quickness of your wit, you cut off my train of thought a moment ago, just as I was about to say something in reply to this last argument of the author's. And even though you've answered him more than sufficiently, I'd still like to add a little something I had in mind at the time. He sets it down as highly improbable that a dissipable, corruptible body like the Earth could move perpetually with a regular motion — especially since we see that animals, in the end, tire and need rest; and he heightens the improbability by insisting that such a motion would have to be of incomparable and immense speed, compared with an animal's. Now, I can't see why the Earth's speed should trouble him at present, when the speed of the starry sphere — so very much greater — gives him no more concern than that of a millstone making a single revolution in 24 hours. If, because the speed of the Earth's turning is on the model of the millstone's, nothing of greater consequence follows than that, then let the author stop fearing that the Earth will tire — for not even the feeblest, laziest animal you please, I mean not even a chameleon, would wear itself out by moving no more than five or six arm-lengths in 24 hours. But if he wants to consider the speed not on the millstone's model, but in absolute terms — inasmuch as in 24 hours the moving body has to pass through an enormous space — then he ought to be far more reluctant to grant that speed to the starry sphere, which, at a speed incomparably greater than the Earth's, has to carry along with it thousands of bodies, each one vastly larger than the terrestrial globe.

It would remain now for us to look at the proofs by which the author concludes that the new stars of 1572 and 1604 were sublunar, and not celestial, as the astronomers of those times generally persuaded themselves — a truly great undertaking. But since this piece of writing is new to me, and long, with all its calculations, I've thought it would be more practical for me to look over as much of it as I can between this evening and tomorrow morning, and then tomorrow, returning to our usual discussions, to report what I've gathered. And if we have time left over, we'll come to discuss the annual motion attributed to the Earth. Meanwhile, if you have anything to say — and Simplicio in particular — about matters bearing on the daily rotation, which I've now examined at such length, we still have a little time left to talk it over.

Simplicio: I've nothing left to say, except that the discussions we've had today have struck me as full of very sharp and ingenious thoughts, brought forward on Copernicus's side to confirm the Earth's motion — but I don't feel persuaded to believe it. Because, in the end, what's been said comes to nothing more than that the reasons for the Earth's stability aren't conclusive; and yet no demonstration whatever has been produced on the other side that necessarily proves and clinches the Earth's mobility.

Salviati: I never undertook, Simplicio, to move you from your opinion, and I'd hardly dare to hand down a final verdict on so great a dispute. It has only been my intention — and it will be in the discussions to come as well — to make plain to you that those who have believed this extremely swift 24-hour motion belongs to the Earth alone, and not to the whole universe with the Earth alone left out, didn't persuade themselves it could and should be so blindly, as the saying goes. Rather, they had thoroughly seen, heard, and weighed the reasons for the contrary opinion, and answered them, too, not lightly. With this same intention — whenever it suits you and Sagredo — we can move on to consider that other motion, attributed to the same terrestrial globe first by Aristarchus of Samos and then by Nicholas Copernicus: a motion which, as I believe you've already heard, is made beneath the zodiac, within the space of a year, around the Sun, set motionless at the center of that zodiac.

Simplicio: The question is so great and so noble that I'll listen to it discussed with the keenest curiosity, expecting to hear everything that can be said on the subject. Afterward I'll go on, at my own leisure, reflecting more deeply on the things I've heard and have yet to hear; and even if I gain nothing else, it'll be no small thing to be able to discuss the matter on firmer ground.

Sagredo: Then, so as not to tire Salviati any further, let's put a full stop to today's discussions, and tomorrow we'll take up the conversation again as usual, in the hope of hearing some great new things.

Simplicio: I'll leave the book on the new stars behind, but I'll carry this one of conclusions back with me, to look over what's written in it against the annual motion — which is to be the subject of tomorrow's discussions.

The Third Day

Sagredo: The great eagerness with which I've been awaiting your arrival — to hear the fresh thinking about the annual motion, our globe's yearly circuit around the Sun — made the hours of the night seem endless to me, and these morning ones too. Though I didn't waste them: I spent a good part of the night awake, going back over yesterday's discussions in my mind, weighing the arguments each side brought forward for the two opposing positions — that of Aristotle and Ptolemy on the one hand, and that of Aristarchus and Copernicus on the other. And truly it seems to me that whichever of these has gone wrong deserves to be forgiven; so plausible, on the face of it, are the reasons that could have won them over — at least so long as we stick to the ones the great original authors themselves put forward. But since the Peripatetic view, by its sheer antiquity, has had many followers and cultivators, while the other has had very few — first because of its obscurity, then because of its novelty — I seem to notice that among all those many, and the moderns in particular, there are some who, to prop up the opinion they take for true, have brought in other arguments so childish I hardly dare call them anything but ridiculous.

Salviati: The same thing has happened to me — and even more so than to you, since I've heard such things produced that I'd be ashamed to repeat them. Not, I'll admit, to spare the reputations of their authors, whose names can always be kept quiet, but so as not to drag down the honor of the human race itself. In the end, from watching them, I've become convinced that among men there are some who reason backwards: they first plant the conclusion in their heads, and — whether because it's their own or belongs to someone they hold in high esteem — they fix it there so firmly that it becomes utterly impossible to root it out. To any argument that occurs to them, or that they hear others produce in support of their settled notion — however simple and insipid it may be — they instantly give their assent and applause; while to any brought against it, however ingenious and conclusive, they react not merely with distaste but with disdain and the bitterest anger. And some of them, driven by fury, would not even hold back from attempting any contrivance to suppress their opponent and shut him up. I've seen a little of that firsthand.

Sagredo: So these men don't draw their conclusion from their premises, nor establish it by reasons — instead they fit, or rather force and twist, the premises and reasons to fit conclusions they've already settled and nailed down. It's just as well, then, not to test ourselves against such people; all the less so given that their company is not only unpleasant but downright dangerous. So we'll carry on with our own Simplicio, whom I've known for ages as a man of the greatest candor, wholly stripped of any malice. Besides, he's so well versed in Peripatetic doctrine that I can be sure whatever won't occur to him in support of Aristotle's opinion won't easily occur to anyone else. But here he is now, out of breath — the fellow's kept us waiting a good long while today. We were just speaking ill of you.

Simplicio: Don't blame me — blame Neptune for my long delay. This morning's ebb tide pulled the waters back so far that the gondola carrying me, having turned into a certain canal not far from here where there are no foundations along the bank, ran aground on the mud, and I had to sit there more than a solid hour waiting for the sea to come back. And while I was stuck there, unable to step out of the boat, which grounded almost all at once, I found myself watching a detail that struck me as quite marvelous. As the waters fell, you could see them fleeing away very fast through various little channels, the mud already showing in several places; and while I'm busy taking this in, all of a sudden I see the motion stop — and, without a single instant's pause, the very same water begins to run back the other way, the ebb turning straight into flow, without staying still for even a moment. It's an effect I've never happened to see once in all my time in Venice.

Sagredo: You must not have run aground very often among such tiny channels. Since they have so little slope, a fall or rise of even a paper's thickness in the surface of the open sea is enough to send the water racing back and forth through them for quite long stretches — just as, on certain seacoasts, a rise of only four or six braccia — arm-lengths — spreads the water across those flats for many hundreds and thousands of pertiche, the old land-rods.

Simplicio: That much I understand perfectly. But I'd have thought that between the last point of the fall and the first beginning of the rise there had to be some noticeable interval of rest.

Sagredo: That will look true to you when you fix your eyes on the walls or the pilings, where these changes happen straight up and down; but there's no genuine rest in it at all.

Simplicio: It seemed to me that since these are two contrary motions, there had to be some rest in between them — in keeping with Aristotle's doctrine, too, which proves that in puncto regressus mediat quies, that at the turning point a moment of rest steps in.

Sagredo: I remember that passage very well. But I also remember that when I was studying philosophy I wasn't persuaded by Aristotle's demonstration — indeed, I had a good many experiences pointing the other way, which I could lay out for you. But I don't want us wading off into other deep waters, since we've gathered here to discuss our own subject, if it's at all possible, without breaking it off the way we did these past days.

Simplicio: And yet we'll have to — if not break it off, then at least stretch it out considerably. Because when I got home last night I set about rereading the little book of theses, where I find some very conclusive demonstrations against this annual motion attributed to the Earth; and since I didn't trust myself to report them so precisely, I decided to bring the book along with me.

Sagredo: You did well. But if we want to take up our discussion according to yesterday's arrangement, we'll need to hear first what Salviati has to report about the book on the new stars, and then, with no further interruptions, we'll come to the annual motion. Now — what does Salviati have to say about these stars? Were they really carried down from the heavens into these lower regions by the power of the calculations of that author Simplicio brought us?

Salviati: Last night I set about reading through his line of argument, and this morning I gave it another once-over, just to be sure that what I thought I'd read in the evening was actually written there — or whether it had been phantoms and fantastic night-imaginings of mine. And in the end I find, to my great sorrow, that what I'd never have wished to see, for this philosopher's own reputation, is really written and printed there. That he doesn't recognize the emptiness of his whole undertaking seems impossible to me — both because it's far too plain to miss, and because I remember hearing him named with praise by our friend the Academician. It also seems far too unlikely that, to please someone else, he could have brought himself to hold his own reputation so cheap as to publish a work from which he could expect nothing but blame from anyone who knows the subject.

Sagredo: Add that those will amount to fewer than one in a hundred, compared with the crowd who'll celebrate and exalt him above every greatest mind that is or ever was. A man who's known how to defend the Peripatetic unchangeableness of the heavens against a whole army of astronomers — and, to their greater shame, floored them with their own weapons! What can four or six per province, who see through his frivolities, do against the countless multitude who, being unable to detect or grasp them, are swept along by the shouting and applaud him all the louder the less they understand? Add, too, that even those few who do understand will decline to answer writings so shabby and so empty of any real conclusion — and rightly so, for the knowledgeable have no need of it, and for those who don't understand, it's effort thrown away.

Salviati: The punishment best fitted to what they deserve really would be silence — if there weren't other reasons that make speaking up almost necessary. One of them is that we Italians get ourselves passed off, one and all, as ignorant, and give the foreigners north of the Alps something to laugh at — especially those cut off from our religion. I could point you to some quite famous ones who mock our Academician, and every mathematician in Italy, for having let the nonsense of a certain Lorenzini against the astronomers come out into the open and stand there unchallenged. But even that could be let go, compared with a still greater occasion for laughter we might hand them — one that comes precisely from the knowledgeable holding their tongues about the frivolities of opponents like these, who attack doctrines they don't understand.

Sagredo: I want no better example of the sheer insolence of these people — and of the misfortune of a man like Copernicus, exposed to being attacked by someone who doesn't even understand the basic position he's making war on.

Salviati: You'll be no less astonished at the way he sets about refuting the astronomers who maintain that the new stars were higher than the orbs of the planets — and perhaps in the firmament itself.

Sagredo: But how could you, in such a short time, have gone through that whole book? It's a big volume, and the demonstrations must be very many.

Salviati: I stopped at these first refutations of his. In them, with twelve demonstrations founded on the observations of twelve astronomers — all of whom judged that the new star of '72, the one that appeared in Cassiopeia, was in the firmament — he proves the opposite: that it was sublunar, below the Moon. He does it by comparing, two at a time, the meridian altitudes taken by different observers in places of different latitude, proceeding in the manner you'll understand shortly. And since it seems to me, examining this first stretch of his, that I've found this author a very long way from being able to conclude anything against the astronomers in favor of the Peripatetic philosophers — and that, on the contrary, the astronomers' opinion comes out far, far more firmly confirmed — I didn't bother applying the same patience to his other methods, but gave them only a superficial glance, confident that whatever ineffectiveness is in these first attacks runs through the rest as well. And as you'll see, in fact, very few words are enough to demolish this whole work, for all that it's built up with so very many laborious calculations, as you can see. So listen to how I proceed. To transfix his opponents, as I said, with their own weapons, this author takes a great many observations made by the astronomers themselves — there are twelve or thirteen of them in all — and on some part of these he makes his calculations, and concludes that these stars were below the Moon. Now, since I'm quite fond of proceeding by questions, and the author himself isn't here, let Simplicio answer the questions I put with whatever he believes the author would answer. Supposing we're dealing with that star of '72 I mentioned, the one that appeared in Cassiopeia — tell me, Simplicio: do you believe it could have been in several places at once? That is, among the elements, and also among the orbs of the planets, and also above those and among the fixed stars, and also infinitely higher still?

Simplicio: There's no doubt one has to say it was in a single place, at one single, determinate distance from the Earth.

Salviati: Then, if the observations made by the astronomers were correct, and the calculations made by this author weren't in error, it would necessarily follow that from all of the former and all of the latter you'd always gather one and the same distance, exactly. Isn't that so?

Simplicio: My reasoning gets this far — that it would have to be so of necessity; nor do I believe the author would contradict it.

Salviati: But if, out of the many, many computations made, not even two came out agreeing with each other, what would you make of that?

Simplicio: I'd judge that all of them were faulty, either through the fault of the one doing the computing or the failing of the observers. At most I could say that one — one only, and no more — was correct, but I wouldn't be able to pick which one.

Salviati: So would you want to deduce and establish a doubtful conclusion as true, from false foundations? Certainly not. Now, this author's calculations are such that no one of them agrees with another — so you can see how much faith they deserve.

Simplicio: Truly, if the matter stands like that, it's a notable failing.

Sagredo: Let me lend a hand to Simplicio and the author, and say to Salviati that his point would follow of strict necessity only if the author had set out to determine precisely how far the star was from the Earth. But I don't think that was his aim — only to show that from those observations one drew the result that the star was sublunar. So that if, from those observations and from all the computations made on them, the star's altitude always comes out less than the Moon's, that's enough for the author to convict of the crassest ignorance all those astronomers who, through some failing of geometry or arithmetic, hadn't known how to draw true conclusions from their own observations.

Salviati: Then it'll be fitting for me to turn to you, Sagredo, since you're defending this author's doctrine so shrewdly. And to try to make even Simplicio — inexperienced though he is in calculations and demonstrations — at least able to see how inconclusive this author's demonstrations are, let me first point out that both he and all the astronomers he's quarreling with agree on this: the new star had no motion of its own, and only went around with the daily motion of the primum mobile. Where they disagree is over its location. The astronomers put it in the celestial region — that is, above the Moon, and perhaps among the fixed stars — while he judges it near the Earth, that is, below the concave of the lunar orb. Now, since the new star in question stood toward the north and not far from the pole, so that for us northerners it never set, it was an easy matter to take its meridian altitudes with astronomical instruments — both the lowest, below the pole, and the highest, above it. From the comparison of these altitudes, taken from different places on Earth set at various distances from the north — that is, differing from one another in their polar altitudes — one could work out the star's distance. For if the star had been up in the firmament among the other fixed stars, its meridian altitudes, taken at different pole-elevations, would have to differ from one another by the very same differences found among those pole-elevations. For example: if the star's height above the horizon was 30 degrees when taken in the place where the pole's altitude was, say, 45 degrees, then the same star's height would have to have grown by 4 or 5 degrees in those more northerly countries where the pole stood 4 or 5 degrees higher. But if the star's distance from the Earth was quite small compared with that of the firmament, then its meridian altitudes must, as we move north, grow noticeably more than the polar altitudes do. And from that extra growth — that is, from the excess of the increase in the star's height over the increase in the pole's altitude (this is what's called the parallax difference) — one calculates promptly, by a clear and reliable method, the star's distance from the center of the Earth. Now, this author takes the observations made by 13 astronomers at different pole-elevations, and, comparing a small selection of them of his own choosing, he calculates, through twelve pairings, that the new star's altitude was always below the Moon. But he pulls this off only by counting on such crass ignorance in everyone into whose hands his book might fall that it honestly turned my stomach. And I sit here wondering how the other astronomers — and Kepler in particular, against whom this author chiefly rails — can keep so silent, when the tongue doesn't usually die in his mouth. Unless, that is, he judged such an undertaking too low to bother with. Now, to put you in the picture, I've copied out onto this sheet the conclusions he draws from his twelve investigations. Of these, the first is from the two observations —

These, then, are twelve investigations the author made at his own choosing, out of the very many that, as he says, could be made from the combinations of the observations of these thirteen observers. And it's fair to assume these twelve are the ones most favorable to proving his case.

Sagredo: But I'd like to know whether, among all the other investigations the author passed over, there are any that told against him — that is, from which, calculating, one would gather that the new star was above the Moon. It seems to me, at first glance, that I can reasonably suspect as much, when I see the ones he did produce differing so wildly among themselves: some give me the new star's distance from the Earth as 4, 6, 10, 100, even a thousand and fifteen hundred times greater than others. So I can well suspect that among the ones he left uncalculated there was some in favor of the opposing side — and I'm all the more inclined to believe it since I don't imagine those observing astronomers lacked the skill and practice for these computations, which I don't think depend on the most abstruse things in the world. In fact it'll strike me as more than miraculous if, when even these twelve investigations alone include some that put the star just a few miles from the Earth, and others that put it below the Moon by a tiny margin, not a single one turns out that, in favor of the other side, puts it even 20 braccia above the lunar orb — and, what would be stranger still, that all those astronomers were so blind they failed to spot a fallacy so glaring.

Salviati: Start bracing your ears now, to hear with boundless amazement the excesses of confidence — in one's own authority and in everyone else's stupidity — to which a man can be carried by the itch to contradict and to show himself cleverer than others. Among the investigations the author left out, there are some that put the new star not merely above the Moon, but above the fixed stars as well; and these aren't a few — they're the majority, as you'll see on this other sheet, where I've written them down.

Sagredo: But what does the author say about these? Perhaps he hasn't taken them into account?

Salviati: He's taken them into account all too well. But he says that the observations on which the calculations put the star infinitely far off are erroneous, and can't be combined with one another.

Simplicio: Oh, that strikes me as a feeble retreat — because the other side could say, with just as much reason, that the erroneous ones are the very observations he's drawing on to place the star down in the elemental region.

Salviati: Oh, Simplicio — if I manage to make you see through the trick of this author, unremarkable as the trick really is, I mean to stir up wonder in you, and even indignation. For then you'd see how he, cloaking his cunning behind the veil of your good faith and that of the other honest philosophers, tries to worm his way into your favor by tickling your ears and puffing up your ambition — parading how he's supposedly convinced and struck dumb these little astronomers who dared to assault the impregnable unchangeableness of the Peripatetic heaven, and, what's more, silenced and beaten them with their own weapons. I mean to give it my best effort. And in the meantime, let Sagredo forgive Simplicio and me for wearying him perhaps a bit too much, while, with a longer circuit of words than his own very quick grasp really needs, I go about laying plain something it's well he shouldn't be left in the dark about.

Sagredo: Not only without boredom — I'll listen to your discussions with real pleasure. And I only wish all the Peripatetic philosophers could sit in on them too, so they might grasp how much they owe to this protector of theirs.

Salviati: Tell me, Simplicio, whether you've thoroughly taken in this: since the new star is located on the meridian circle up toward the north, then for someone traveling from the south toward the north, that same new star would climb above the horizon by exactly as much as the pole does — provided it were truly located among the fixed stars; but that if it were notably lower, that is, nearer the Earth, it would appear to rise more than the pole itself, and ever more so the greater its nearness.

Simplicio: I think I've grasped it perfectly, and to show it I'll try my hand at a mathematical figure. In this large circle I'll mark the pole P, and in these two lower circles I'll mark two stars seen from a point on Earth — call it A — and let the two stars be these, B and C, seen along the one line ABC against a fixed star D. Then, traveling on Earth as far as the point E, the two stars will appear to me separated from the fixed star D and drawn nearer the pole P — the lower one, B, more so, appearing at G, and C less so, appearing at F; but the fixed star D will have kept the same distance from the pole.

Salviati: I see you understand it very well. I believe you also see how, because star B is lower than C, the angle formed by the sightlines that set out from the two places A and E and meet at C — that is, this angle ACE — is narrower, or we might say more acute, than the angle formed at B by the lines AB, EB.

Simplicio: That's plain to the eye.

Salviati: And likewise, since the Earth is exceedingly small and all but imperceptible next to the firmament, and since in consequence the distance AE — as far as one can travel on Earth — is very short compared with the immense length of the lines EG, EF stretching from Earth up to the firmament, you come to understand that star C could be raised and carried so very far from the Earth that the angle formed at it by the rays setting out from those same points A and E would become extremely acute, and virtually null and imperceptible altogether.

Simplicio: This too I understand perfectly.

Salviati: Now know, Simplicio, that astronomers and mathematicians have found infallible rules, by way of geometry and arithmetic, whereby — from the size of these angles B and C and their differences, joined with knowledge of the distance between the two places A and E — they can find the distance of these lofty things to within a hand's breadth, provided always that the distance and the angles are taken correctly.

Simplicio: So then, if the rules that depend on geometry and arithmetic are sound, all the fallacies and errors one might meet in trying to investigate such heights — of new stars, or comets, or anything else — must come down to the distance AE and the angles B, C not being well measured. And so all the discrepancies one sees in these twelve investigations depend not on any defect in the rules of calculation, but on errors committed in investigating those angles and those distances by means of instrumental observations.

Salviati: Just so, and no difficulty arises there. Now you must carefully note how, as the star is carried farther from B toward C — so that the angle grows ever more acute — the ray EBG keeps drawing away from the ray ABD on the lower side of the angle, as the line ECF shows, whose lower part EC is more remote from the part AC than EB is. But it can never happen, however immense the removal, that the lines AD, EF part company altogether, since in the end they must meet at the star; one could only say they separate and become parallel if the removal were infinite, and that case cannot occur. But because — note this well — the firmament's distance, set against the smallness of the Earth, is reckoned (as already said) as infinite, the angle enclosed by the rays that, drawn from points A and E, would end at a fixed star is judged to be null, and those rays are taken as two parallel lines. And so one concludes that only then may it be affirmed that the new star was in the firmament, when a collation of observations made in different places yields, by calculation, that this angle is imperceptible and the lines as good as parallel. But when the angle is of notable size, the new star must necessarily be lower than the fixed stars, and lower even than the Moon — that is, whenever the angle ABE were greater than the one that would be formed at the center of the Moon.

Simplicio: So then the Moon's distance is not so great that a similar angle at it stays imperceptible?

Salviati: No, sir. On the contrary, it is perceptible not only at the Moon, but at the Sun as well.

Simplicio: But if that's so, it could also be that such an angle is observable in the new star without its being lower than the Sun — let alone lower than the Moon.

Salviati: That can be, and it is indeed so in the present cases, as you'll see in due place — that is, once I've smoothed the road enough that you too, though no expert in astronomical calculations, can grasp and touch with your own hand how far this author has aimed at writing to please the Peripatetics, by cloaking and disguising various things, rather than at establishing the truth by bringing them forward with naked honesty. But let's press on. From what's been laid out so far, I believe you're fully able to see how the new star's distance can never be made so immense that the angle we've named so often entirely vanishes, and the two observers' rays from points A and E become parallel lines. And in consequence you come to understand perfectly that, if the calculation drew from the observations that this angle is totally null, or the lines truly parallel, we would be certain the observations were — by at least some tiny amount — in error; but if the calculation gave us those lines separated not merely to equidistance, that is, to being parallel, but past that point, and spread wider at the top than at the bottom, then we must firmly conclude that the observations were made with too little accuracy — in short, that they are erroneous, as leading us to a manifest impossibility. Then you must believe me, and take it for absolutely certain, that two straight lines setting out from two points marked on another straight line are wider at the top than at the bottom whenever the angles they enclose upon that straight line are greater than two right angles; and when those angles equal two right angles, the lines are parallel; but if they are less than two right angles, the lines converge, and extended far enough would beyond all doubt close up into a triangle.

Simplicio: Without taking it on your word, I have the science of it, and I'm not so bare of geometry that I don't know a proposition I've had occasion to read a thousand times in Aristotle — namely, that the three angles of every triangle equal two right angles. So that if I take, in my figure, the triangle ABE — granting the line EA to be straight — I understand very well how its three angles A, E, B equal two right angles, and that in consequence the two alone, E and A, fall short of two right angles by exactly the amount of the angle B; whence, by opening the lines AB, EB (while keeping them fixed at points A and E) until the angle they contain toward the B side vanishes, the two below will come to equal two right angles, and those lines will be reduced to being parallel; and if one went on opening them further, the angles at points E and A would become greater than two right angles.

Salviati: You're an Archimedes, and you've spared me spending more words to explain to you how, whenever the calculations turn up the two angles A and E as greater than two right angles, the observations are without question in error. This is exactly what I wanted you to understand perfectly, and what I feared I might not manage to explain in a way that a pure Peripatetic philosopher could take in with any certainty. Now let's carry on with what's left. And picking up again what you granted me a moment ago — namely, that since the new star cannot be in several places, but in one only, then whenever the calculations made upon these astronomers' observations don't give it back to us in the same place, there must be error in the observations: either in taking the polar altitudes, or in taking the star's elevations, or in both operations at once — now, since among the many investigations, made by combining the observations two by two, very few come out placing the star in the same spot, then those very few alone could be the ones not in error, while all the rest are absolutely erroneous.

Sagredo: Then we'll have to trust these very few alone rather than all the others together. And since you say the ones that agree are very few, and I see among these twelve two that both make the star's distance from the Earth's center 4 semidiameters — four Earth-radii — this fifth and the sixth, then it's more probable that the new star was elemental than celestial.

Salviati: It's not so; for if you look carefully, it isn't written that the distance was exactly 4 semidiameters, but about 4 semidiameters — and yet you'll see that those two distances differed from each other by many hundreds of miles. Here they are: you see that this fifth, which is 13,389 miles, exceeds the sixth, which is 13,100 miles, by nearly 300 miles.

Sagredo: Which, then, are these few that agree in placing the star in the same spot?

Salviati: They are, to this author's misfortune, five investigations, all of which lodge it in the firmament — as you'll see in this other note, where I record many further combinations. But I'll grant the author more than he'd perhaps ask of me: namely, that in each combination of observations there is some error. Which I believe is absolutely unavoidable; for since the observations that serve for a single investigation are 4 in number — that is, two different polar altitudes and two different star elevations, made by different observers, in different places and with different instruments — anyone with any knowledge of the practice will say it can't be that among all 4 no error slipped in, especially when we see that in taking a single polar altitude — with the same instrument, in the same place, and by the same observer, who could have done it a thousand times — one still wavers by some minutes, and often by many, as you can see in various places in this very book. These things granted, I ask you, Simplicio: do you believe this author holds his 13 observers to be shrewd, intelligent, and skilled in handling such instruments, or rather crude and inexpert men?

Simplicio: It can't be that he reckons them anything but very careful and intelligent. For if he judged them unfit for the work, he might as well throw out his own book as proving nothing, being founded on suppositions full of errors; and he'd be writing us off as too simple, if he thought he could use their inexperience to persuade us that a false proposition of his was true.

Salviati: Then, since these observers are such, and yet for all that have erred — so that their errors must be amended in order to draw from their observations what further knowledge is possible — the fitting thing is to apply to them the smallest and closest amendments and corrections we can, so long as these suffice to draw the observations out of impossibility and into possibility. So that, for instance, if a manifest error and a patent impossibility in one of their observations can be eased by adding or subtracting 2 or 3 minutes, and by that amendment brought within the possible, one shouldn't insist on adjusting it by adding or subtracting 15 or 20 or 50.

Simplicio: I don't believe the author would contradict this; for, granted that they're judicious and expert men, one should sooner believe they erred by a little than by a lot.

Salviati: Now note what follows. Of the places where the new star might be lodged, some are manifestly impossible, others possible. Absolutely impossible is that it lay an infinite interval beyond the fixed stars, for no such site exists in the world — and if it did, the star set there would have been invisible to us. It's also impossible that it went creeping over the Earth's surface, and still more that it lay inside the terrestrial globe itself. The possible places are these ones under dispute, since it doesn't offend our intellect that a visible object, with the look of a star, could be above the Moon no less than below it. Now, while we try to draw out — by way of observations and calculations made with all the certainty human diligence can reach — where its place truly was, we find that most of these calculations set it more than an infinite interval above the firmament, others set it close to the Earth's surface, and some even below that surface; and of the rest, which lodge it in not-impossible places, none agree with one another. So that we're forced to say all the observations are necessarily fallacious; and thus, if we still mean to draw some fruit from so much labor, we must resort to corrections, amending all the observations.

Simplicio: But the author will say that of the observations which set the star in impossible places, no account should be taken, being infinitely erroneous and fallacious; and one should accept only those that place it in not-impossible places, and among these alone go searching, by way of the more probable and more numerous agreements, for — if not the particular and exact site, that is, its true distance from the Earth's center — at least the knowledge of whether it was among the elements or among the celestial bodies.

Salviati: The reasoning you now make is the very one the author has made on behalf of his own cause — but with too unreasonable a disadvantage to the other side. And this is the chief point that has made me marvel beyond measure at the excessive confidence he's taken — no less in his own authority than in the blindness and heedlessness of the astronomers. I'll speak for them, and you'll answer for the author. And first I ask you: when the astronomers observe with their instruments, seeking, say, how great a star's elevation above the horizon is, can they stray from the truth as much toward the more as toward the less — that is, err by making it sometimes higher than the truth and sometimes lower — or can the error only ever be of one kind: that in erring they always sin toward excess and never toward the less, or always toward the less and never toward excess?

Simplicio: I've no doubt it's equally easy to err in the one way as in the other.

Salviati: I believe the author would answer the same. Now, of these two kinds of error — which are contrary, and into which the observers of the new star may equally have fallen — when applied to the calculation, one kind will make the star higher than the truth, and the other lower. And since we already agree that all the observations are erroneous, on what grounds does this author want us to accept as more in keeping with the truth those that show the star to have been near, rather than the others that show it excessively far?

Simplicio: From what I seem to have gathered from what's been said so far, I don't see that the author refuses those observations and investigations that would set the star farther than the Moon, or even farther than the Sun — but only those that make it remote (as you yourself have said) by more than an infinite interval. And since you too refuse that distance as impossible, he passes over such observations as infinitely convicted of falsity and impossibility. It seems to me, then, that if you want to convince the author, you ought to produce investigations more exact, or more numerous, or by more diligent observers, which set the star at such-and-such a distance above the Moon or above the Sun — in a place, in short, where it could possibly be — just as he produces these twelve, all of which set the star below the Moon, in places that exist in the world and where it could have been.

Salviati: Now — now, Simplicio, here is where the misunderstanding lies, yours and the author's: yours in one respect, and the author's in another. I gather from your talk that you've formed the notion that the wildly exaggerated figures one arrives at in fixing the star's distance grow in proportion to the errors made on the instrument in taking the observations; and that, conversely, from the size of those wild figures one can argue the size of the errors; and that therefore, on hearing it said that a given observation yields the star's distance as infinite, the error in observing must necessarily have been infinite too, and hence beyond amending and so to be refused. But the business, my dear Simplicio, doesn't run that way. And for not having grasped how this matter stands, I excuse you, as inexperienced in such dealings — but I cannot cloak the author's error under a like mantle. For he, disguising his understanding of it (having persuaded himself that we truly weren't going to understand), has hoped to make use of our ignorance in order to lend his doctrine more credit among the crowd of the little-understanding. So, as a warning to those who are more credulous than discerning, and to draw you out of your error, know this: it may be — and most of the time it will happen — that an observation which gives you the star, for example, at the distance of Saturn, will, by adding or subtracting a single minute from the elevation taken with the instrument, make it come out at an infinite distance, and so turn it from possible to impossible; and conversely, those calculations built on such observations that render the star infinitely far, many a time may, by adding or shaving off a single minute, pull it back to a possible site. And what I say of one minute can happen with a correction of a half, or a sixth, or less. Now fix it well in your mind that at the very highest distances — such as, for example, the altitude of Saturn or that of the fixed stars — the very tiniest errors made by the observer on the instrument turn the site from determinate and possible into infinite and impossible. It doesn't work that way with the sublunar distances near the Earth, where it can happen that an observation from which the star was gathered to be, say, 4 terrestrial semidiameters away, can be increased or diminished not merely by a minute, but by ten, and a hundred, and much more, without the calculation rendering it even higher than the Moon, let alone infinitely remote. Understand from this that the size of the errors — the instrumental errors, so to speak — is not to be judged from the outcome of the calculation, but from the very quantity of the degrees and minutes counted off on the instrument. And those observations are to be called more correct, or less erroneous, which, with the addition or subtraction of fewer minutes, restore the star to a possible place; and among the possible places, the true site must be believed to be the one around which the greater number of the distances concur, as calculated upon the more correct observations.

Simplicio: I can't quite get hold of what you're saying, nor make out on my own how it can be that at the very greatest distances a wilder exaggeration should spring from an error of a single minute than at small distances from an error of 10 or of 100; and so I'd be glad to have it explained.

Salviati: You'll see it — if not by theory, at least by practice — from this short summary I've drawn up of all the combinations, and of some of the investigations the author left out, which I've calculated and noted down on this very sheet.

Sagredo: So it must be that from yesterday to now — and it's not been more than 18 hours — you've done nothing but calculate, taking neither food nor sleep.

Salviati: On the contrary, I've had both those refreshments: but I run through such calculations very quickly; and, to tell the truth, I've been more than a little amazed that this author should go on at such length, larding it with so many computations that have nothing to do with the question being asked. And so that this may be fully understood, and also so that one may quickly see how much more probably it follows, from the observations of the astronomers the author himself relies on, that the new star could have been above the Moon — and even above all the planets, and higher still, among the fixed stars — I have copied onto this sheet all the observations recorded by that same author, made by 13 astronomers, where the polar elevations and the star's altitudes on the meridian are noted, both the lowest under the pole and the highest above: and here they are.

Now, to see my whole procedure, we can begin with these calculations — 5 of them the author passed over, perhaps because they tell against him, seeing that they set the star above the Moon by many terrestrial semidiameters. The first of them is this one, worked from the observations of the Landgrave of Hesse and of Tycho, who are, even by the author's own admission, among the most exact observers: and in this first one I'll spell out the order I keep in the investigation, and that knowledge will serve you for all the rest, since they go by the same rule, differing only in the quantity of the data — that is, in the number of degrees of the polar altitudes and of the elevations of the new star above the horizon, whose distance from the Earth's center we seek in proportion to the semidiameter of the terrestrial globe. And in this case it doesn't matter in the least to know how many miles that semidiameter is, so that working out that figure, and the distance between the places where the observations were made — as this author does — is labor and time thrown away, nor do I know why he did it, especially since in the end he turns the miles he found back into semidiameters of the terrestrial globe.

Simplicio: Perhaps he does this to find, by such smaller measures and their fractions, the star's distance pinned down to the last 4 fingerbreadths; because we others, who don't understand your rules of arithmetic, are struck dumb on hearing the conclusions, when we read, for instance: "Therefore the comet, or the new star, was distant from the Earth's center three hundred seventy-three thousand eight hundred and seven miles, plus two hundred eleven four-thousand-ninety-sevenths, 373807 211/4097" — and over such precise exactitudes, where these tiny fractions are set down, we form the notion that it's impossible that you, who in your calculations take account of a single finger, could in the end deceive us by 100 miles.

Salviati: This reasoning of yours, and this excuse, would be acceptable if, over a distance of thousands of miles, an arm's length more or less were of great weight, and if the suppositions we take for true were so certain that they assured us we'd draw out an indubitable truth in the end: but here you see, in the author's 12 investigations, that the star's distances gathered from them differ one from another — and therefore stray from the truth — by many hundreds and thousands of miles. Now, since I'm more than sure that what I'm looking for must necessarily differ from the right figure by hundreds of miles, why torment myself over the calculation out of a jealous fear of slipping by a single finger? But let's come at last to the operation, which I work out in this way.

Tycho, as you see in the note, observed the star at the polar altitude of 55° 58′; and the Landgrave's polar altitude was 51° 18′: the star's altitude on the meridian, taken by Tycho, was 27° 45′; the Landgrave found it at 23° 3′: which altitudes are the ones noted here below, as you see:

...the quantity for the star, which here is 62° 15′, gives us the size of angle BDC, 154° 45′; which — together with its sine, taken from the table, which is 42657 — I note down; and beneath it I note the angle of the parallax, BCD, 0° 2′, with its sine, 58. And since in triangle BCD side DB is to side BC as the sine of the opposite angle BCD is to the sine of the opposite angle BDC, therefore when line BD is 58, BC would be 42657; and since the chord DB is 8142, of which the radius BA is 100000, and we want to know how many of those same parts BC amounts to, we'll say, by the rule of three: if when BD is 58, BC is 42657, then when that same DB is 8142, how much would BC be? So I multiply the second term by the third; I get 347313294, which must be divided by the first — that is, by 58 — and the quotient would be the number of parts of line BC of which the radius AB is 100000: and to know how many radii BA that same line BC contains, we'd have again to divide that quotient by 100000, and we'd have the number of semidiameters comprised in BC. Now, the number 347313294 divided by 58 gives 5988160¼, as you see here:

and this, divided by 100000, gives us 59 and 88160/100000.

But we can greatly shorten the operation by dividing the first product we found — that is, 347313294 — by the product of multiplying the two numbers 58 and 100000, which is 5,800,000;

and it likewise comes out 59 and 5113294/5800000.

And that many semidiameters are contained in line BC, to which, adding one for line AB, we'll have a little less than 61 semidiameters for the two lines ABC together, and so the straight distance from the center A to the star C will be more than 60 semidiameters; therefore it comes out above the Moon — by Ptolemy's reckoning more than 27 semidiameters, and by Copernicus's more than 8, granting that the Moon's distance from the Earth's center, by Copernicus's account, is, as the author says, 52 semidiameters.

By this same kind of investigation I find, from the observations of Camerarius and Muñoz, that the star comes out set at a like distance — this one too more than 60 semidiameters: and these are the observations, and here following is the calculation.

The next investigation is made upon two observations of Tycho and Muñoz, from which one calculates that the star was distant from the Earth's center 478 semidiameters and more.

From this other investigation the star is found to be distant from the center more than 716 semidiameters.

These, as you see, are five investigations that render the star well above the Moon; and here I want you to weigh that particular point I mentioned to you a little while ago — namely, that at the great distances the changing, or say the correcting, of very few minutes shifts the star through enormous stretches. As, for example, in the first of these investigations, where the calculation made the star 60 semidiameters remote from the center with a parallax of 2 minutes, anyone who wanted to maintain that it was in the firmament has only to correct, in the observations, 2 minutes and even less — for then the parallax ceases, or grows so small that it puts the star at an immense distance, such as everyone grants the firmament's to be. In the second investigation an amendment of less than 4′ does the same. In the third and the fourth, again just as in the first, a mere 2 minutes lift the star even above the fixed stars. In the previous one a quarter of a minute — that is, 15 seconds — gives us the same. But it won't go this way at the sublunar heights: for imagine whatever distance you please, and try to correct the investigations the author made and adjust them so that they all answer to that same fixed distance; you'll see how much larger the amendments you'll need to make.

Sagredo: It can only do us good, for our full understanding, to see some example of what you say.

Salviati: Fix, at your own pleasure, whatever determined sublunar distance you like, where the star is to be set; for with little trouble we'll be able to satisfy ourselves whether corrections like these — which we've seen suffice to bring it among the fixed stars — will bring it to the place you've fixed.

Sagredo: To take the most favorable distance for the author, let's set it at the greatest of all those he investigated in his 12 investigations; because, while the astronomers and he are in controversy — they saying the star was above the Moon, and he that it was below — every little space by which he proves it to have been below gives him the victory.

Salviati: Let's take, then, the seventh investigation, made upon the observations of Tycho and Thaddeus Hagecius, from which the author finds the star to have been distant from the center 32 semidiameters — the site most favorable to his side; and to give him every advantage, I want us, on top of this, to set it at the least favorable distance for the astronomers, which is to place it even above the firmament. That granted, then, let's go looking for what corrections would be needed to apply to his other 11 investigations, so as to raise the star up to the distance of 32 semidiameters; and let's begin with the first, calculated upon the observations of Hainzel and Maurolyco, in which the author finds the distance from the center to be about 3 semidiameters, with a parallax of 4° 42′ 30″: let's see now whether, by pulling it back to just 20′, it rises to 32 semidiameters. Here is the operation, very brief and exact: I multiply the sine of angle BDC by the chord BD, and divide the result — dropping the last 5 figures — by the sine of the parallax; out comes 28½ semidiameters: so that not even with a correction of 4° 22′ 30″, taken away from 4° 42′ 30″, does the star rise to the height of 32 semidiameters; which correction, for Simplicio's understanding, amounts to 262½ minutes.

In the second operation, made upon the observations of Hainzel and Schuler, with a parallax of 0° 8′ 30″, the star is found at a height of about 25 semidiameters, as you see in the following operation.

And pulling the parallax of 0° 8′ 30″ back to 7′, whose sine is 204, the star rises to about 30 semidiameters: so a correction of 1′ 30″ isn't enough.

Now let's see what correction is needed for the third investigation, made upon the observations of Hainzel and Tycho, which renders the star at a height of about 19 semidiameters, with a parallax of 10′. The usual angles, and their sines and chord, found by the author, are these; and they render (as also in the author's own operation) the star distant about 19 semidiameters; so, to raise it, we must shrink the parallax, following the rule he himself observes in the ninth investigation: let's put the parallax, then, at 6′, whose sine is 175; and, the division done, one finds still less than 31 semidiameters for the star's distance. So a correction of 4′ is too little for the author's need.

Let's come to the fourth investigation, and to the rest, by the same rule, and with the chords and sines the author himself found. In this one the parallax is 14′, and the height found less than 10 semidiameters; and diminishing the parallax from 14′ to 4′, you see all the same how the star doesn't rise even to 31 semidiameters: so a correction of 10′ out of 14′ isn't enough.

In the author's fifth operation we have the sines and the chord as you see:

and the parallax is 0° 42′ 30″, which renders the star's height about 4 semidiameters; and correcting the parallax by reducing it from 42′ 30″ to just 5′ isn't enough to raise it even to 28 semidiameters: so an amendment of 37′ 30″ is too little.

In the sixth operation the chord, the sines, and the parallax are these,

and the star is found to be at a height of about 4 semidiameters: let's see where it's brought by lowering the parallax from 8′ to a single 1′. Here is the operation, and the star is raised no higher than to about 27 semidiameters: so a correction of 7′ out of 8′ isn't enough. In the eighth operation the chord, the sines, and the parallax, as you see, are these:

and from these the author calculates the star's height at 1½ semidiameters, with a parallax of 43′; which, reduced to 1′, still leaves the star distant less than 24 semidiameters: so a correction of 42′ isn't enough. Now let's see the ninth. Here is the chord, the sines, and the parallax, which is 15′: from which the author calculates the star's distance from the Earth's surface to be less than a forty-seventh of a semidiameter. But this is a calculation error; for it truly comes out, as we shall see right now, more than a fifth: here it comes to about 90/436, which is more than a fifth.

What the author then adds by way of amending the observations — namely, that it isn't enough to draw the parallax difference back even to a single minute, nor even to an eighth of 1′ — is true. But I say that not even a tenth of 1′ will reduce the star's height to 32 semidiameters: for the sine of a tenth of 1′, that is, of 6 seconds, is 3; by which, if in our rule we divide 90 — or rather, if we divide 9058672 by 300000 — it will come to 30 and 58672/100000, that is, a little more than 30½ semidiameters. The tenth gives the star's height at a fifth of a semidiameter, with this angle, sines, and parallax, which is 4° 30′: and I see that, reduced from 4° 30′ to 2′, it still doesn't advance the star to 29 semidiameters.

The eleventh renders the star, for the author, remote about 13 semidiameters, with a parallax of 55′: let's see, reducing it to 20′, how high it will lift the star. Here is the calculation: it raises it to a little less than 33 semidiameters: so the correction is of a little less than 35, out of 55′.

The twelfth, with a parallax of 1° 36′, renders the star at a height of less than 6 semidiameters: pulling the parallax back to 20′ brings the star to less than 30 semidiameters of distance: so a correction of 1° 16′ isn't enough.

From this it is seen (as I have noted here on the back) that if the author decided to accept, as the true site of the new star, the distance of 32 semidiameters, then correcting his other 10 investigations (and I say 10, because the second, being quite high already, is brought to the height of 32 semidiameters with a 2′ correction) so as to make them all restore the said star to that distance would require such a drawing-back of the parallaxes that all the subtractions together would amount to more than 756′: whereas in the 5 I calculated, which render the star above the Moon, to correct them so that they set it in the firmament, a correction of only 10¼ minutes suffices. Now add to these 5 others that render the star precisely in the firmament with no need of any correction at all, and we'll have 10 investigations agreeing to set it in the firmament, with only 5 of them corrected (as we've seen) by 10¼ minutes: whereas to correct the author's other 10, so as to bring the star to a height of 32 semidiameters, you'll need amendments of 756′ out of 836′; that is, from the sum of 836 one must subtract 756 if the star is to rise to the height of 32 semidiameters — and even that correction isn't enough.

The investigations that, at once and without any further correction, render the star with no parallax, and therefore in the firmament, and even in its most remote parts — in short, as high as the pole itself — are these 5 noted here:

As for the rest of the pairings that can be made from the observations of all these astronomers, those that render the star sublime by an infinite interval are far more numerous — about 30 more — than the others that, by calculation, place the star below the Moon; and since (as we've agreed) it's to be believed the observers erred by a little sooner than by a lot, it is plain that the corrections to be applied to the observations that place the star infinitely high, in drawing it back down, will bring it to the firmament sooner, and with a smaller amendment, than below the Moon: so that all of these applaud the opinion of those who set it among the fixed stars. Add to this that the corrections required for such amendments are far smaller than those by which the star can be brought from its improbable nearness up to the height most favorable to this author, as we've seen in the past examples: among which impossible nearnesses there are 3 that seem to move the star from the Earth's center by less than a semidiameter's distance, making it in a certain way go wheeling under the Earth; and these are the combinations in which, the polar altitude of one of the observers being greater than the polar altitude of the other, the star's elevation taken by the former is less than the star's elevation by the latter. And such are the combinations noted here below. This first is the Landgrave's with Gemma: where the Landgrave's polar altitude, 51° 18′, is greater than Gemma's polar altitude, which is 50° 50′; but the Landgrave's star altitude, 79° 30′, is less than that of Gemma's star, 79° 45′.

The other two are these below:

From what I've shown you up to now, you can grasp how unfavorable to his own cause is this first method of investigating the star's distance and proving it sublunar, introduced by the author — and how much more probably and clearly one gathers that its distance was among the most remote fixed stars.

Simplicio: Up to this point it seems to me that the small force of the author's demonstrations has been quite plainly uncovered; but I see that all this is contained in not many pages of the book, and it could be that other arguments of his are more conclusive than these first ones.

Salviati: On the contrary, they can only be less valid, if we let the past ones be our sample for the rest; for (as is manifest) the uncertainty and inconclusiveness of those plainly derives from the errors committed in the instrumental observations, from which the polar altitudes and the star's altitudes were believed to have been correctly taken, when in fact they're all easily in error; and yet for finding the altitudes of the pole the astronomers have had centuries of time to spend on it at their leisure, and the star's meridian altitudes are the easiest to observe, being very sharply defined and allowing the observer some room to keep at them, since they don't change appreciably in a very short time, as do those far from the meridian: and if this is so — as it most truly is — what faith shall we place in calculations founded on observations more numerous, harder to make, more momentary in their variation, with the added burden of more awkward and more fallacious instruments? From a single glance I've given at the demonstrations that follow, the computations are made upon altitudes of the star taken in various vertical circles, which they call by the Arabic word azimuths: in which observations movable instruments are used, not only on the vertical circles but on the horizon as well, at the same time; so that at the very moment one takes the altitude, one must also have observed on the horizon the distance of the vertical circle in which the star lies from the meridian; besides which, after a notable interval of time, one must repeat the operation and keep close account of the elapsed time, trusting either to clocks or to other observations of stars: such a tangle of observations is then compared with another like it, made by a different observer, in a different country, with a different instrument and at a different time; and from this the author tries to draw out what the star's altitudes and horizontal latitudes would have been at the time and hour of those other, first observations, and upon such an adjustment he finally builds his calculation. I leave it now to you to judge how much faith is to be placed in what is drawn from such investigations. Besides, I've no doubt at all that if anyone chose to martyr himself over such enormously long computations, he'd find, as in the past ones, more that favored the opposing side than the author: but it doesn't seem to me worth the trouble of taking on such a labor for a thing that isn't among the primary matters we have in view.

Sagredo: I'm with you on this point; but since this whole business is hemmed in by so much confusion, uncertainty, and error, on what confidence did so many astronomers so positively declare that the new star was very high up?

Salviati: On two kinds of observation, both of them utterly simple, easy, and reliable — and either one alone is more than enough to assure us that the star was set in the firmament, or at least a very long way above the Moon. The first is drawn from the equality, or the very slight inequality, of its distances from the pole, whether it stood in the lowest part of the meridian or in the highest. The second is that it kept, permanently, the very same distances from certain fixed stars near it — in particular from the eleventh star of Cassiopeia, from which it was never more than a degree and a half away. From these two facts one gathers, beyond any doubt, either that there was no parallax at all, or else a parallax so small that a very quick calculation assures us of the star's great distance from the Earth.

Sagredo: But didn't this author take these things into account? And if he did see them, how does he defend himself?

Salviati: We have a saying: that when a man, finding no dodge that will hold up against his own blunders, comes out with the feeblest of excuses, he's trying to catch hold of the ropes of the sky. Well, this author reaches not for the ropes but for the threads of the sky's cobwebs — as you'll plainly see once we go through the two points I just mentioned. First, what the polar distances of each observer show us I've set down in these short calculations. To follow them fully, I must first point out this: whenever the new star, or any other phenomenon, is near the Earth, then as it wheels with the daily rotation around the pole, it will appear farther from the pole when it lies in the lower part of the meridian than when it is in the upper part — as this figure shows. In it, the point T marks the center of the Earth, O the place of the observer, the firmament the arc VPC, and P the pole. The phenomenon, moving along the circle FS, is seen now below the pole, along the ray OFC, and now above it, along the ray OSD, so that the places seen in the firmament are D and C; but the true places, reckoned from the center T, are B and A, equally distant from the pole. Now it is already clear that the apparent place of the phenomenon at S — that is, the point D — is nearer the pole than the other apparent place, C, seen along the ray OFC. That's the first thing to note.

In the second place, you should note that the amount by which the apparent lower distance from the pole exceeds the apparent upper distance from the pole is greater than the lower parallax of the phenomenon. What I mean is that the excess of arc CP (the apparent lower distance) over arc PD (the apparent upper distance) is greater than arc CA (which is the lower parallax). This is easy to gather: for CP exceeds PD by more than it exceeds PB, since PB is greater than PD; but PB equals PA, and the excess of CP over PA is the arc CA; therefore the excess of arc CP over arc PD is greater than arc CA, which is the parallax of the phenomenon placed at F. That's what we needed to know. And to give the author every advantage, let's suppose that the parallax of the star at F is the whole excess of arc CP (the lower distance from the pole) over arc PD (the upper distance).

Now I come to examine what the observations of all the astronomers cited by the author give us — and among them there isn't a single one that doesn't work against him and run contrary to his aim. Let's begin with Busch's. He found the star's distance from the pole, when it was above the pole, to be 28° 10′, and the lower to be 28° 30′, so the excess is 0° 20′, which I'll take (in the author's favor) as if it were all the parallax of the star at F — that is, the angle TFO. The distance from the point overhead, that is arc CV, is 67° 20′. Once these two things are found, extend the line CO, and onto it drop the perpendicular TI, and consider the triangle TOI, in which the angle I is a right angle and the angle IOT is known (being at the tip of the angle VOC, the star's distance from the point overhead). Moreover, in the triangle TIF, also right-angled, the angle F is known, taken as the parallax. So set aside the two angles IOT and IFT, and take their sines, as you see noted. And since in the triangle IOT, of the parts of which the whole sine TO is 100000, the sine TI is 92276, and since in the triangle IFT, of the parts of which the whole sine TF is 100000, the sine TI is 582 — then to find out how many parts TF is, of those of which TO is 100000, we'll say, by the rule of three: when TI is 582, TF is 100000; but when TI is 92276, how much would TF be? Multiply 92276 by 100000; that gives 9227600000; and this must be divided by 582, which yields, as you see, 15854982 — and TF will contain that many parts of those of which TO holds 100000. So, to learn how many lengths TO there are in TF, we'll divide 15854982 by 100000; that gives roughly 158½; and that many semidiameters is the distance of the star F from the center T. And to shorten the operation: seeing that the product of 92276 multiplied by 100000 must first be divided by 582 and then the quotient by 100000, we can reach the same result at once, without multiplying 92276 by 100000, by a single division of the sine 92276 by the sine 582, as you see below — where 92276 divided by 582 gives us that same 158½ or so. So let's keep in mind that simply dividing the sine TI, as the sine of the angle TOI, by the sine TI, as the sine of the angle IFT, gives us the distance we're after, TF, in that many semidiameters TO.

Now see what Peucer's observations give us: his lower distance from the pole is 28° 21′, the upper 28° 2′, the difference 0° 19′, and the distance from the point overhead 66° 27′; from all of which the star's distance from the center comes out at nearly 166 semidiameters.

Here is what Tycho's observation shows us, taking the one most favorable to our opponent: the lower distance from the pole, 28° 13′, and the upper, 28° 2′, leaving the difference, which is 0° 11′, as if it were all parallax; the distance from the point overhead, 62° 15′. Here below is the operation, with the star's distance from the center found to be 276 9/16 semidiameters.

Reinhold's observation, which follows, gives us the star's distance from the center as 793 semidiameters.

From the Landgrave's next observation, the star's distance from the center comes out at 1057 semidiameters.

Taking two of Camerarius's observations most favorable to the author, the star's distance from the center is found to be 3143 semidiameters.

Muñoz's observation gives no parallax, and so puts the new star among the fixed stars, very high up. Hainzel's makes it infinitely far off, but with a correction of half a minute it sets it among the fixed stars; and the same follows from Ursinus, with a correction of 12′. As for the other astronomers, we don't have their distances above and below the pole, so nothing can be drawn from them. Now see how all the observations, of every one of them, agree — against the author — in placing the star in the high celestial regions.

Sagredo: But what defense does he find against such open contradictions?

Salviati: One of those flimsiest of threads. He says the parallaxes are reduced by refraction, which, working in the opposite direction, lifts the phenomenon up, whereas the parallaxes push it down. Now, judge how much this wretched refuge is worth from this: even if the effect of refraction were as powerful as some astronomers have lately claimed, the most it could do to raise a phenomenon higher than its true place above the horizon — when the object is already 23 or 24 degrees up — would be to shave off about 3 minutes of parallax; and that allowance is far too meager to drag the star down below the Moon. In some cases it's even smaller than the advantage we've already granted, in admitting that the excess of the lower distance from the pole over the upper is entirely parallax — an advantage that is far clearer and more palpable than the effect of refraction, whose size I doubt, and not without reason. But there's more. I ask this author whether he believes that the astronomers whose observations he uses knew about these effects of refraction and took them into account, or not.
