If they knew and reckoned with them, it's reasonable to think they allowed for them in fixing the star's true elevations — making, from the degrees of altitude their instruments showed, the corrections that were fitting on account of the alterations of refraction, so that the distances they announced were then the corrected and true ones, not the apparent and false ones. But if he believes those authors gave no thought to refraction, then he must confess that they likewise erred in determining all the things that can't be perfectly adjusted without correcting for refraction — among which is the precise finding of the polar altitudes, which are commonly taken from the two meridian altitudes of some fixed star that is always visible. Those altitudes will be altered by refraction in exactly the same way as the new star's, so that the polar altitude deduced from them will come out defective, sharing the very same flaw the author ascribes to the altitudes assigned to the new star — that is, both the one and the others are set, with equal error, higher than the truth. But such an error, as far as our present business goes, does no harm at all; for since we need to know nothing beyond the difference between the two distances of the new star from the pole, when it was below and then above the pole, it's clear that those distances will be the same once the alteration of refraction is applied in common to both the star and the pole, being commonly corrected for the one and for the other. The author's argument would carry some weight, however feeble, if he had assured us that the height of the pole had been fixed precisely, corrected for the error due to refraction, while the same astronomers had then failed to guard against that error in fixing the new star's altitudes for us. But of this he has given us no assurance — nor perhaps could he — and perhaps (and this is the likelier case) the observers left out that very precaution.

Sagredo: This objection seems to me more than thoroughly demolished; but tell me now, in what fashion does he then free himself from the star's having always kept the same distance from the fixed stars near it?

Salviati: By clinging, in the same way, to two threads even weaker than the last. One of them is again tied to refraction, but this time even less firmly. He says that refraction, working on the new star and lifting it above its true place, makes the distances we see differ from the true ones when the star is compared with the fixed stars near it. And I can't marvel enough at how he pretends not to notice that the very same refraction will work the same way on the new star as on the old one beside it, lifting them both equally — so that from such an accident the interval between them stays unchanged. The other refuge is even more hapless, and has a good deal of the ridiculous about it. It rests on the error that can arise in the very handling of the instrument, when the observer — unable to set the center of the pupil of his eye at the center of the sextant (the instrument used for observing the intervals between two stars), but holding it raised above that center by the distance of the pupil from some cheekbone or other, against which the head of the instrument is propped — comes to form in his eye an angle sharper than the one made by the sides of the sextant; and this angle of the rays also differs from itself, depending on whether one looks at stars low over the horizon or the same stars set at a great height. Such an angle, he says, comes out different as one raises the instrument while keeping the head still; but if, in lifting the sextant, one bent the neck back and raised the head along with the instrument, the angle would then stay the same. The author's answer therefore supposes that the observers, in using the instrument, did not raise their heads as they needed to — which has no likelihood about it. But even granting it happened so, I'll leave it to you to judge what difference there can be between the two sharp angles of two isosceles triangles, the sides of one being four arm-lengths each, and those of the other four arm-lengths less the diameter of a lens. For the difference between the lengths of the two lines of sight can be no greater than that: when the line is drawn perpendicularly from the center of the pupil onto the plane of the sextant's bar (a line no longer than the thickness of a thumb), and the length of those same rays when, the sextant being raised without lifting the head with it, that line no longer falls perpendicular onto the plane but slants, making the angle toward the circumference somewhat sharp. But to free this author, once and for all, from these most miserable beggaries, let him know (since it's plain he hasn't much practice in the use of astronomical instruments) that on the sides of the sextant or quadrant two sights are fitted, one at the center and the other at the opposite end, raised a finger or more above the plane of the bar; and the ray from the eye is made to pass over the tops of these sights, the eye itself being held a hand's breadth or two, or even more, away from the instrument — so that neither pupil, nor cheekbone, nor any part of the person touches or leans against the instrument. Nor is the instrument even held up or raised in the arms — least of all if it's one of the big ones, as is customary, which, weighing tens and hundreds and even thousands of pounds, are supported on the firmest of bases. So the whole objection vanishes. These are this author's subterfuges, which, even were they made all of steel, couldn't lift him a hundredth of a minute; and with these he flatters himself that he can make us believe he has made up for that difference of more than a hundred minutes — I mean the fact that no notable variation was observed in the distances between a fixed star and the new star throughout their whole circuit, which, had the star been close to the Moon, would have had to grow greatly conspicuous even to the naked eye, without any instrument at all — especially when set against the eleventh star of Cassiopeia, its neighbor at a degree and a half, from which it would have had to vary by more than two diameters of the Moon, as the more intelligent astronomers of those days rightly noted.

Sagredo: I seem to be looking at that unlucky farmer who, after the hail has beaten down and destroyed his whole expected harvest, goes about with a drooping, downcast face, scraping together leavings so scanty they won't be enough to feed even a single chick for a single day.

Salviati: Truly this author has taken the field against the champions of the heavens' unchangeableness with too poor a stock of weapons, and has tried, with chains too fragile, to haul the new star of Cassiopeia down out of the highest regions into these low and elemental ones of ours. And since it seems to me we've shown quite clearly the great difference between the reasoning of those astronomers and that of their opponent here, it will be well to leave this part and return to our main matter. Next in it comes the consideration of the annual motion, commonly attributed to the Sun, but then — first by Aristarchus of Samos, and afterward by Copernicus — taken away from the Sun and transferred to the Earth. Against that position I hear Simplicio come stoutly armed, and in particular with the rapier and the shield of the little book of mathematical conclusions, or disquisitions — whose attacks it will be well to begin laying out.

Simplicio: If it's all the same to you, I'd like to hold those back for the end, since they're the latest to have been thought up.

Salviati: Then it will be necessary that you, following the method we've kept to so far, set out in orderly fashion the arguments to the contrary, both Aristotle's and those of other ancients — which I too intend to do, so that nothing is left behind without being carefully weighed and examined. And likewise Signor Sagredo, with the liveliness of his wit, will offer up his thoughts as they stir in him.

Sagredo: I'll do it with my usual freedom; and since you command it, you'll also be obliged to excuse it.

Salviati: The favor will oblige me to thank you, not to excuse you. But let Simplicio begin now to bring forward the difficulties that hold him back from being able to believe that the Earth, like the other planets, can move in a circle around a fixed center.

Simplicio: The first and greatest difficulty is the repugnance and incompatibility between being at the center and being far from it. For if the terrestrial globe is to move for a year along the circumference of a circle — that is, under the zodiac — it's impossible that it should at the same time be at the center of the zodiac; but that the Earth is at that center is proved in many ways by Aristotle, by Ptolemy, and by others.

Salviati: You reason very well; and there's no doubt that whoever wants to make the Earth move along the circumference of a circle must first prove that it isn't at the center of that circle. So it now follows that we should see whether the Earth is, or is not, at that center around which I say it turns and where you say it's placed. And before that, we must also make clear whether you and I have the same idea of this center or not. So tell me: what and where is this center you have in mind?

Simplicio: By center I mean the center of the universe, of the world, of the starry sphere, of the heavens.

Salviati: Though with very good reason I might set up a dispute with you over whether there is any such center in nature — since neither you nor anyone else has ever proved whether the world is finite and shaped, or else infinite and boundless — nevertheless, granting you for now that it's finite and bounded in a spherical figure, and that it therefore has its center, it will be worth seeing how believable it is that the Earth, rather than some other body, is to be found at that center.

Simplicio: That the world is finite, bounded, and spherical, Aristotle proves with a hundred demonstrations.

Salviati: Which then all reduce to a single one, and that single one to nothing; for if I deny him his premise — namely, that the universe is in motion — all his demonstrations collapse, since he proves nothing to be finite and bounded except the part of the universe that is in motion. But so as not to multiply disputes, let's grant for now that the world is finite, spherical, and has its center; and since that figure and center were argued from its mobility, it will be nothing but reasonable if, from those same circular motions of the world's bodies, we go on to the particular investigation of the proper site of that center. Indeed, Aristotle himself reasoned and settled the matter in just this way, making the center of the universe that very point around which all the celestial spheres turn, and in which he believed the terrestrial globe to be placed. Now tell me, Simplicio: if Aristotle found himself forced by the most obvious experiences to alter this arrangement and order of the universe in part, and to confess he had been mistaken in one of these two propositions — either in putting the Earth at the center, or in saying that the celestial spheres move around that center — which of the two confessions do you think he would choose?

Simplicio: I believe that if the case arose, the Peripatetics—

Salviati: I'm not asking about the Peripatetics; I'm asking about Aristotle himself. As for them, I know perfectly well what they'd say. They, as Aristotle's most reverent and most humble bondsmen, would deny every experiment and every observation in the world—they'd even refuse to look, so as not to have to admit them—and they'd insist the world stands as Aristotle wrote it, not as nature will have it. For once you strip away the prop of that authority, with what would you have them take the field? So tell me, rather, what you think Aristotle himself would have done.

Simplicio: Honestly, I couldn't decide which of the two drawbacks he'd have judged the lesser.

Salviati: Please don't use that word "drawback" for something that might necessarily have to be the case. It was a drawback to want to put the Earth at the center of the heavenly revolutions. But since you don't know which way he'd have leaned—and since I take him for a man of great intelligence—let's examine which of the two choices is the more reasonable, and reckon that one to be the position Aristotle would have adopted. So, picking up our reasoning from the beginning, and granting, for Aristotle's sake, that the world (of whose size we have no sense-based knowledge beyond the fixed stars) is spherical in shape and moves in a circle, and therefore necessarily has a center, both with respect to its shape and with respect to its motion; and being further sure that inside the starry sphere there are many orbs, one within another, each with its stars, that likewise move in circles—we ask what it is more reasonable to believe and to say: that these contained orbs move around the very center of the world, or else around some other point far removed from it. Now tell me, Simplicio, your opinion on this particular point.

Simplicio: If we could rest on this single assumption alone, and were sure we'd meet nothing else to trouble us, I'd say it's far more reasonable to hold that the container and the parts it contains all move around one common center, rather than around different ones.

Salviati: Now, if it's true that the center of the world is the same as the point around which the orbs of the world's bodies—that is, of the planets—move, then it's absolutely certain that not the Earth but rather the Sun is the thing situated at the center of the world; so that, as far as this first simple and general grasp of things goes, the middle place belongs to the Sun, and the Earth is as far from the center as it is from the Sun itself.

Simplicio: But from what do you argue that it's the Sun, not the Earth, that lies at the center of the planets' revolutions?

Salviati: It's concluded from the plainest observations—observations that therefore compel the conclusion. The most palpable of them, for excluding the Earth from that center and putting the Sun there, is that all the planets are found now nearer and now farther from the Earth, and by differences so great that, for example, Venus at her farthest is six times more remote from us than when she's nearest, and Mars rises to almost eight times as far in one state as in another. Judge, meanwhile, whether Aristotle was off by a little in believing they were always equally distant from us.

Simplicio: And what are the signs that their motions are around the Sun?

Salviati: For the three outer planets—Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn—the argument comes from their always being nearest the Earth when they're at opposition to the Sun, and farthest when they're toward conjunction; and this approaching and receding matters so much that Mars, when near, looks fully 60 times bigger than when it's at its farthest. As for Venus and Mercury, we're certain they revolve around the Sun from the fact that they never stray far from it, and that they're seen now above it and now below—as the change of phases in Venus necessarily proves. As for the Moon, it's true that she can't in any way be separated from the Earth, for reasons that will be brought out more distinctly as we go on.

Sagredo: I expect I'm about to hear things even more marvelous, following from this annual motion of the Earth, than the ones that followed from the daily rotation.

Salviati: You're not deceived in the least. As for what the daily rotation does to the heavenly bodies, it was nothing—and could be nothing—but to make the universe appear to go rushing past us the other way; but this annual motion, mingling with the particular motions of all the planets, produces a great many strange effects, which have so far made all the greatest men in the world lose their fencing-guard. But coming back to those first general notions, I repeat that the center of the heavenly revolutions of the five planets—Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury—is the Sun; and it will be the center of the Earth's motion too, if we manage to set her in the heavens. As for the Moon, she has a circular motion around the Earth, from which (as I've already said) she can in no way be separated; but that doesn't stop her from going around the Sun along with the Earth, by the annual motion.

Simplicio: I still don't quite have a grip on this arrangement; perhaps making a little drawing of it would help me understand it better, and we could discuss it more easily.

Salviati: So be it—indeed, for your greater satisfaction and wonder both, I want you to draw it yourself, and to see how, while thinking you don't understand it, you understand it perfectly; just by answering my questions you'll lay it out exactly. So take a sheet of paper and a compass: let this blank page be the immense expanse of the universe, in which you're to distribute and arrange its parts as reason dictates to you. And first, since without any teaching from me you hold it firm that the Earth is placed in this universe, mark a point wherever you like, around which you take her to be positioned, and label it with some letter.

Simplicio: Let this point, marked A, be the place of the terrestrial globe.

Salviati: Good. Second, I know you know perfectly well that the Earth is not inside the body of the Sun, nor even next to it, but a certain distance away; so assign the Sun whatever other place you please, as far from the Earth as you like, and label that too.

Simplicio: There, done: let the place of the Sun's body be this one, marked O.

Salviati: With these two settled, I want us to think about fitting in the body of Venus in such a way that her position and motion can satisfy what the observed appearances show us about them; so call to mind what, whether from our earlier discussions or from your own observations, you've gathered happens with that star; and then assign her the position you judge suits her.

Simplicio: Granting that the appearances you've described are true—the ones I've also read in the little book of conclusions—namely that this star never departs from the Sun beyond a certain fixed interval of forty-odd degrees, so that she never reaches opposition to the Sun, nor even quadrature, nor so much as the sextile aspect; and further, that at one time she appears nearly forty times bigger than at another—that is, largest when, moving in retrograde, she goes toward her evening conjunction with the Sun, and smallest when, in direct motion, she goes toward her morning conjunction; and moreover, it being true that when she appears largest she shows herself crescent-shaped, and when she appears smallest she's seen perfectly round—granting, I say, that these appearances are true, I don't see how one can avoid asserting that this star circles around the Sun. For that circle can in no way be said to embrace the Earth and hold it inside itself, nor to lie below the Sun—that is, between it and the Earth—nor above the Sun either. The circle can't embrace the Earth, because then Venus would sometimes come to opposition with the Sun; it can't lie below, because around both of her conjunctions with the Sun Venus would show herself sickle-shaped; and it can't lie above, because she'd always show herself round and never crescent. And so, for her lodging, I'll mark the circle CH around the Sun, without its embracing the Earth.

Salviati: Venus settled, you'd do well to think about Mercury, which, as you know, always keeps around the Sun and strays much less far from it than Venus does; so consider what place it's fitting to assign him.

Simplicio: No doubt that, since it imitates Venus, the most fitting quarters for it will be a smaller circle inside this one of Venus, and likewise around the Sun—the liveliness of its brilliance over that of Venus and the other planets being a quite conclusive argument and sign, above all, of its nearness to the Sun. So on that basis we can mark its circle, labeling it with the letters B G.

Salviati: And where shall we put Mars?

Simplicio: Mars, because it comes to opposition with the Sun, must with its circle embrace the Earth; but I see that it necessarily must embrace the Sun as well. For, coming to conjunction with the Sun, if it didn't pass above it but were below it, it would appear crescent, as Venus and the Moon do; yet it always shows itself round. So it must enclose within its circle the Sun no less than the Earth. And since I recall you saying that when it's at opposition to the Sun it shows itself 60 times bigger than when it's toward conjunction, it seems to me that these appearances will be very well satisfied by a circle around the center of the Sun that embraces the Earth—which I now draw and mark D I: where Mars at point D is nearest the Earth and is opposite the Sun; but when it's at point I, it's in conjunction with the Sun and farthest from the Earth. And since the same appearances are observed in Jupiter and Saturn—though with far less variation in Jupiter than in Mars, and less still in Saturn than in Jupiter—it seems to me we'll very neatly satisfy these two planets too with two circles, likewise around the Sun: this first one for Jupiter, marking it E L, and another outer one for Saturn, labeled F M.

Salviati: You've acquitted yourself splendidly so far. And since (as you see) the approaching and receding of the three outer planets is measured by twice the distance between the Earth and the Sun, this makes a greater variation in Mars than in Jupiter, because Mars's circle D I is smaller than Jupiter's circle E L; and likewise, because this E L is smaller than Saturn's circle F M, the same variation is smaller still in Saturn than in Jupiter—and that answers exactly to the appearances. It remains now for you to think about assigning the Moon her place.

Simplicio: Following the same method, which strikes me as thoroughly conclusive: since we see that the Moon comes to conjunction and opposition with the Sun, we must say her circle embraces the Earth; but it need not embrace the Sun, because if she were toward conjunction she wouldn't show herself sickle-shaped, but always round and full of light—besides which she could never give us, as she often does, an eclipse of the Sun by interposing herself between it and us. So we must assign her a circle around the Earth, which would be this one, N P, such that when she's set at P she appears to us from the Earth, A, joined with the Sun—so that she can sometimes eclipse it—and when placed at N she's seen opposite the Sun, and in that state can fall into the Earth's shadow and be darkened.

Salviati: Now what shall we do, Simplicio, with the fixed stars? Do we want to set them scattered through the immense depths of the universe, at various distances from any given point, or else placed on a surface spread out spherically around a center of its own, so that each of them is equally distant from that same center?

Simplicio: I'd rather take a middle road, and assign them an orb drawn around a fixed center and enclosed between two spherical surfaces—one very high and concave, the other lower and convex—between which I'd set the innumerable multitude of the stars, but at various heights; and this could be called the sphere of the universe, containing within itself the orbs of the planets we've already drawn.

Salviati: So then, Simplicio, we've now arranged the bodies of the world exactly according to Copernicus's distribution—and it's been done by your own hand. What's more, you've assigned proper motions to all of them except the Sun, the Earth, and the starry sphere; you've given Mercury and Venus a circular motion around the Sun without embracing the Earth; around the same Sun you make the three outer planets—Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn—move, taking the Earth inside their circles; and the Moon can't move any other way than around the Earth, without embracing the Sun. And in all these motions you agree, once again, with Copernicus himself. What remains to be decided, among the Sun, the Earth, and the starry sphere, are three things: namely rest, which appears to belong to the Earth; the annual motion under the zodiac, which appears to belong to the Sun; and the daily rotation, which appears to belong to the starry sphere, sharing it out to all the rest of the universe except the Earth. And since it's true that all the orbs of the planets—I mean Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn—move around the Sun as their center, it seems all the more reasonable that rest belongs to the Sun than to the Earth, inasmuch as, for moving spheres, it's more reasonable that the center should stand still than any other place remote from that center. To the Earth, then, which is left set in the midst of moving parts—I mean between Venus and Mars, one of which makes its revolution in nine months and the other in two years—we can very neatly attribute the motion of a year, leaving rest to the Sun. And once that's so, it follows by necessary consequence that the daily rotation belongs to the Earth as well: for if, with the Sun standing still, the Earth did not turn on itself but only had the annual motion around the Sun, our year would be nothing but a single day and a single night—that is, six months of day and six months of night, as was said before. See, then, how neatly the headlong motion of 24 hours is lifted away from the universe, and how the fixed stars, which are so many Suns, enjoy—like our own Sun—a perpetual rest. See, moreover, how much ease there is in this first sketch for giving the reasons behind such great appearances in the heavenly bodies.

Sagredo: I see it very well. But just as you gather from this simplicity a great probability for the truth of such a system, someone else might, on the contrary, draw the opposite conclusions—doubting, not without reason, how it is that a construction so ancient, held by the Pythagoreans and fitted so well to the appearances, should nonetheless in the course of thousands of years have had so few followers, and should have been rejected by Aristotle himself, and even after Copernicus should go on meeting the same fate.

Salviati: If you, Sagredo, had ever happened—as I have, many and many a time—to hear what sort of nonsense is enough to make the common crowd stubborn and unpersuadable, unwilling even to lend an ear, let alone their assent, to these new ideas, I think your wonder at finding so few followers of this opinion would shrink considerably. But little esteem, in my view, should be given to minds for whom, to confirm them and hold them fixed in the immobility of the Earth, the most conclusive demonstration is to observe that they won't be dining in Constantinople this morning nor supping in Japan this evening, and that they're certain the Earth, being so very heavy, can't climb up above the Sun and then come tumbling headlong back down. Of such people, whose number is infinite, there's no need to take account, nor to record their silly notions, nor to try to win over men whose definition includes only the genus and lacks the difference, just to have them as companions in opinions of the utmost subtlety and delicacy. Besides, what gain would you ever expect to make, with all the demonstrations in the world, upon minds so dull that they can't by themselves recognize their own most extreme follies? But my wonder, Sagredo, is very different from yours: you wonder that so few are followers of the Pythagoreans' opinion; I, for my part, am astonished that anyone has ever, up to now, been found who embraced and followed it. Nor can I sufficiently admire the towering genius of those who received it and held it true, and who, by the sheer liveliness of their intellect, did such violence to their own senses that they were able to prefer what reason dictated to them over what sense experience showed them, plainly and to the contrary. That the arguments against the Earth's daily whirling, which you've already examined, have a great show of truth, we've seen already; and that the Ptolemaics, the Aristotelians, and all their followers took them as thoroughly conclusive is itself a very strong sign of their force. But those experiences that openly contradict the annual motion carry so much greater an apparent repugnance that—I say it again—I can find no limit to my admiration at how reason could, in Aristarchus and in Copernicus, do such violence to sense as to make herself, against sense, the mistress of their belief.

Sagredo: So we're going to have other vigorous objections against this annual motion as well?

Salviati: We are; and ones so evident and so sensory that, if a higher and more excellent sense than the common and natural ones hadn't kept company with reason, I greatly suspect that I too would have been far more reluctant to accept the Copernican system than I've been ever since a brighter lamp than the usual one gave me light.

Sagredo: Well then, Salviati, let's come, as they say, to grips—because every word spent on anything else seems to me thrown away.

Salviati: Here I am, at your service. I've already drawn you the form of the Copernican system. Against its truth, the first and fiercest assault is launched by Mars itself. For if it were true that Mars varied its distances from the Earth so much that between its least and greatest remove there were a difference equal to twice the distance from the Earth to the Sun, then it would necessarily follow that when it's nearest to us its disk should show itself more than 60 times bigger than it shows when it's farthest away. Yet no such difference in apparent size is to be seen; on the contrary, at opposition to the Sun, when it's near the Earth, it doesn't show itself even 4 or 5 times bigger than when, toward conjunction, it's hidden away beneath the Sun's rays. Venus gives us another and greater difficulty. For if, circling around the Sun as Copernicus asserts, it were now above the Sun and now below—receding from and approaching us by as much as the diameter of the circle it describes—then when it was below the Sun and nearest to us, its disk ought to show itself a little less than 40 times bigger than when it's above the Sun, near its other conjunction; yet the difference is almost imperceptible. Add another difficulty: since the body of Venus is dark in itself, and shines only, like the Moon, by the Sun's illumination—as seems reasonable—then when she's below the Sun she ought to show herself sickle-shaped, as the Moon does when she too is near the Sun; an occurrence that doesn't appear in her. On account of which, Copernicus declared that either she was luminous by herself, or her matter was such that it could soak up the Sun's light and transmit it through her whole depth, so that she could always show herself shining; and in this way Copernicus excused Venus's not changing shape. But of her little-varied size he said nothing at all, and of Mars far less than the case required—I believe because he couldn't, to his own satisfaction, save an appearance so repugnant to his position; and yet, persuaded by so many other correspondences, he held to it and took it for true. Beyond these things, making all the planets, together with the Earth, move around the Sun as the center of their revolutions, while the Moon alone disturbs that order and has its own proper motion around the Earth, and while it, the Earth, and the whole elemental sphere all together move in a year around the Sun—this seems to alter the order in such a way as to render it unlikely and false. These are the difficulties that make me marvel that Aristarchus and Copernicus—who can't possibly have failed to notice them, and who couldn't then resolve them—nevertheless, on the strength of other marvelous correspondences, trusted so much in what reason dictated to them that they confidently affirmed the structure of the universe could have no other form than the one they'd drawn. There are, besides, other very grave and very beautiful difficulties, not so easily resolved by middling minds, but penetrated and set forth by Copernicus, which we'll put off until later, after we've answered other objections raised by others against this position. Now, coming to the explanations and replies to the three grave objections brought forward, I say that the first two not only don't contradict the Copernican system but strongly and absolutely favor it; for both Mars and Venus do show themselves unequal to themselves, according to the proportions assigned, and Venus below the Sun shows herself sickle-shaped, and goes on changing her phases in exactly the same way the Moon does.

Sagredo: But how did this stay hidden from Copernicus, and yet be plain to you?

Salviati: These things can't be grasped except through the sense of sight, which nature hasn't granted to human beings perfect enough to make out such differences; indeed, the very instrument of seeing gets in its own way. But after it pleased God, in our own age, to grant human ingenuity so marvelous an invention — the power to perfect our sight by magnifying it 4, 6, 10, 20, 30, and 40 times — countless objects that were invisible to us, whether because of their distance or their extreme smallness, have, by means of the telescope, been made perfectly visible.

Sagredo: But Venus and Mars aren't objects invisible for their distance or smallness — we take them in perfectly well with plain, natural eyesight. So why can't we make out the differences in their sizes and shapes?

Salviati: A big part of this comes from the interference of our own eye, as I just hinted: because of it, bright, distant objects aren't presented to us plain and clean, but handed to us wreathed in stray, foreign rays, so long and thick that the naked little body shows up magnified 10, 20, 100, even a thousand times more than it would look if you stripped away that radiant head of hair that isn't its own.

Sagredo: Now I recall having read something or other on this subject — I'm not sure whether in the Letters on Sunspots or in The Assayer, by our mutual friend. But it would do no harm — both to refresh my own memory and for Simplicio's benefit, since he perhaps hasn't seen those writings — to set out for us more distinctly how this business stands. Understanding it, I think, is quite necessary to keep proper hold of what we're now discussing.

Simplicio: Honestly, everything Salviati is now bringing forward is new to me; for, to tell the truth, I've had no curiosity to read those books, nor have I so far put much faith in the newly introduced spyglass. In fact, following in the footsteps of my fellow Peripatetic philosophers, I've believed that what others have admired as stupendous feats were really just the tricks and deceptions of the lenses. So if I've been in error up to now, I'll be glad to be drawn out of it; and, tempted by the other novelties I've heard from you, I'll listen more attentively to the rest.

Salviati: The confidence such men have in their own discernment is no less unreasonable than the low regard they hold for other people's judgment; and it's a remarkable thing that they should think themselves fit to judge such an instrument better, without ever having tried it, than those who have made — and make every day — thousands upon thousands of trials with it. But let's leave aside, if you please, this breed of stubborn men, who can't even be reproached without honoring them more than they deserve. Returning to our subject, I say that bright objects — whether because their light is refracted in the moisture on the surface of the pupils, or reflected off the edges of the eyelids, scattering its reflected rays across those same pupils, or for some other reason — show themselves to our eye ringed with extra rays, and hence far larger than their bodies would look stripped of that irradiation. And this enlargement grows in ever greater proportion the smaller and smaller those luminous objects are. Just so — suppose the growth of the radiant hair were, say, four fingers: added around a circle four fingers in diameter, it would increase its apparent size ninefold, but—

Simplicio: I suspect you meant to say three times; because adding four fingers on one side and four on the other to the diameter of a circle that's itself four fingers, you triple its size — you don't increase it ninefold.

Salviati: A little geometry, Simplicio. It's true the diameter grows three times, but the surface — which is what we're talking about — grows nine times. Because, Simplicio, the surfaces of circles are to one another as the squares of their diameters, and a circle four fingers in diameter has to one twelve fingers across the same proportion the square of four has to the square of twelve — that is, 16 to 144 — and so it will be nine times greater, not three. Let that be a caution to you, Simplicio. And to go on: if we add that same head of hair, four fingers, to a circle only two fingers in diameter, the diameter of the wreath is now ten fingers, and the area of the whole disk to the area of the naked little body is as 100 to 4, since those are the squares of 10 and 2; so the enlargement would be 25 times over. And finally, the 4 fingers of hair added to a little circle one finger in diameter would enlarge it 81 times. And so, continually, the growths happen in ever greater proportion, the smaller and smaller the real objects being enlarged.

Sagredo: The difficulty that troubled Simplicio hasn't troubled me; but there are indeed some other things I'd like to understand more clearly — in particular, I'd like to know on what grounds you claim that this growth is always the same in all visible objects.

Salviati: I've already partly explained myself, when I said that only luminous objects grow, and not dark ones. Now let me add the rest: among bright objects, those with a livelier light produce a bigger, stronger reflection on our pupil, and so appear to enlarge much more than the less bright ones. And, so as not to dwell on this point any longer, let's turn to what the true schoolmistress — experience — teaches us. This evening, once the air has grown good and dark, let's look at the star of Jupiter: we'll see it very radiant and very big. Then let's pass our gaze through a little tube — or even through a small chink left between the palm and the fingers when we clench a fist and hold it up to the eye, or through a hole made with a fine needle in a sheet of paper. We'll see the disk of that same Jupiter stripped of its rays, but so small that we'll judge it less than a sixtieth part of what its great torch appears when seen with the free eye. Then we can look at the Dog Star, that most beautiful star, biggest of all the fixed stars, which to the naked eye shows up not much smaller than Jupiter; but once its hair is stripped away in the manner described, its disk will be seen so small that it won't be judged even a twentieth part of Jupiter's — indeed, anyone without perfect eyesight will barely make it out at all. From which one may reasonably conclude that this star, having a far livelier light than Jupiter's, makes its irradiation greater than Jupiter makes his. As for the irradiation of the Sun and the Moon, it's as good as nothing, on account of their size, which by itself takes up so much room in our eye that it leaves no space for stray rays; so their disks are seen sheared and sharply bounded.

We can assure ourselves of the same truth by another experiment I've made many times — assure ourselves, I mean, that bodies shining with a livelier light irradiate far more than those of a fainter light. I've often seen Jupiter and Venus together, 25 or 30 degrees from the Sun, and with the air well darkened, Venus looked a good 8 or even 10 times bigger than Jupiter — so long, that is, as they were viewed with the free eye. But looked at then through the telescope, Jupiter's disk was seen to be truly four times and more the size of Venus's; yet the liveliness of Venus's splendor was incomparably greater than Jupiter's very faint light. And this came from nothing but the fact that Jupiter is very far from the Sun and from us, while Venus is near to us and to the Sun.

With these things explained, it won't be hard to understand how it can be that Mars, when it's at opposition to the Sun — and therefore seven times and more nearer to the Earth than when it's toward conjunction — barely shows itself 4 or 5 times bigger in the one state than in the other, when we ought to see it more than 50 times as big. And the sole cause of this is irradiation: for if we strip it of the stray rays, we'll find it enlarged precisely in the proper proportion. And to remove its mane, the telescope is the one and best means: by magnifying its disk 900 or a thousand times, it lets us see it naked and sharply bounded like the Moon's, and differing from itself in the two positions according to the proper proportion, exactly to a hair.

As for Venus: in its evening conjunction, when it's below the Sun, it ought to show itself almost 40 times bigger than in the other, morning conjunction — and yet it isn't even seen doubled. What happens here, beyond the effect of irradiation, is that it's crescent-shaped, and its horns, besides being thin, receive the Sun's light obliquely, and so quite faintly; so that, being little and weak, its irradiation is less broad and lively than when it shows us its whole lit hemisphere. But the telescope plainly shows us its horns as sharply bounded and distinct as the Moon's, and they're seen as belonging to a very large circle — proportionally almost 40 times bigger than its own disk when it's above the Sun in its last morning appearance.

Sagredo: Oh, Nicolaus Copernicus, what delight would have been yours, to see this part of your system confirmed by such clear experiments!

Salviati: Yes — but how much less would be the fame of the sublimity of his genius among those who understand! For, as I said a moment ago, we see that he steadily went on affirming, led by reason, the very thing whose opposite the evidence of the senses displayed. I can't get over my astonishment that he should so steadfastly have insisted on saying that Venus revolves around the Sun and is at one time more than six times farther from us than at another, and yet always shows itself the same size, when it ought to appear forty times bigger.

Sagredo: In Jupiter, Saturn, and Mercury too, I believe the differences in their apparent sizes must be seen to answer punctually to their varying distances.

Salviati: In the two upper ones I've observed them precisely, almost every year, for twenty-two years now. With Mercury no observation of any weight can be made, since it lets itself be seen only at its greatest elongations from the Sun — its widest swings out from it — where its distances from the Earth are imperceptibly unequal, so that such differences are unobservable, as are the changes of shape, which absolutely must occur, just as in Venus. When we do see it, it ought to show itself in the shape of a half-circle, as Venus too does at its greatest elongations; but its disk is so small and its splendor so lively, being so close to the Sun, that the telescope's power isn't enough to shave off its hair and make it appear fully sheared. That leaves us to clear away what seemed a great impropriety in the motion of the Earth: namely, that while all the planets revolve around the Sun, the Earth alone should go around the Sun in a year not solitary like the others, but in the company of the Moon, together with the whole sphere of the elements — and that at the same time the Moon itself should move every month around the Earth. Here we're forced to exclaim once more, and to exalt the admirable sharp-sightedness of Copernicus, and at the same time to grieve his misfortune, since he doesn't live in our time — when, to take away the apparent absurdity of the Earth and Moon traveling in convoy, we see Jupiter, almost another Earth, going around the Sun in 12 years not in convoy with one Moon, but accompanied by four Moons, along with everything that may be contained within the orbits of the four Medicean stars.

Sagredo: For what reason do you call the four Jovian planets Moons?

Salviati: That's how they'd appear to anyone looking at them from Jupiter. For in themselves they're dark, and they receive their light from the Sun — which is plain from their becoming eclipsed when they enter the cone of Jupiter's shadow; and since only the hemisphere facing the Sun is lit, to us — who are outside their orbits and nearer the Sun — they always show up fully bright. But to someone on Jupiter they'd show fully bright when they were in the upper parts of their circles, while in the lower parts — that is, between Jupiter and the Sun — they'd be seen from Jupiter as crescents. In short, they'd go through, for the Jovians, the very same changes of shape that the Moon goes through for us Earthlings. Now you see how marvelously these first three strings harmonize with the Copernican system, that at the start seemed so dissonant. From this, meanwhile, Simplicio can see with what probability one may conclude that not the Earth, but the Sun, is at the center of the planets' revolutions. And since the Earth is placed among the worldly bodies that undoubtedly move around the Sun — that is, above Mercury and Venus, and below Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars — won't it likewise be highly probable, and perhaps necessary, to grant that it too goes around the Sun?

Simplicio: These occurrences are so great and conspicuous that it's not possible Ptolemy and his other followers had no knowledge of them; and having had it, they must surely also have found a way to give a sufficient account of such striking appearances — and one quite fitting and plausible, too, since for so long it's been accepted by so very many.

Salviati: You reason very well; but you must know that the chief aim of the pure astronomers is only to give an account of the appearances in the heavenly bodies, and to fit to them, and to the motions of the stars, such structures and arrangements of circles that the motions calculated from them answer to those same appearances — caring little about admitting some extravagance that, in fact, on other grounds, might be hard to swallow. And Copernicus himself writes that in his early studies he had restored the science of astronomy on those same suppositions of Ptolemy, and had corrected the motions of the planets so well that the computations answered very neatly to the appearances and the appearances to the calculations — so long, that is, as each planet was taken separately. But he adds that when he then tried to put together the whole structure out of the particular constructions, what resulted was a monster and a chimera made of limbs utterly out of proportion to one another and altogether incompatible; so that, however well the part of the pure calculating astronomer was satisfied, there was still no satisfaction and repose for the philosophical astronomer. And because he understood very well that if the celestial appearances could be saved by assumptions false in nature, it could be done far better from true suppositions, he set himself to search diligently whether any of the distinguished men of antiquity had attributed to the world a structure other than the commonly received one of Ptolemy. And finding that certain Pythagoreans had in particular attributed to the Earth the daily rotation, and others the annual motion as well, he began to check against these two new suppositions the appearances and the particulars of the planets' motions — all of which he had readily at hand — and seeing the whole correspond with its parts with marvelous ease, he embraced this new arrangement and found his repose in it.

Simplicio: But what extravagances are there in the Ptolemaic arrangement that aren't even greater in this Copernican one?

Salviati: In Ptolemy are the diseases, and in Copernicus their remedies. And first: won't all the sects of philosophers call it a great impropriety that a body naturally moving in a circle should move irregularly about its own center and regularly about some other point? And yet the fabric of Ptolemy is full of such misshapen motions; whereas in Copernicus every motion is uniform about its own center. In Ptolemy one must assign contrary motions to the heavenly bodies, and make them all move from east to west and, at the very same time, from west toward east; whereas in Copernicus all the celestial revolutions run one single way, from west to east. But what shall we say of the apparent motion of the planets, so irregular that not only do they now go fast and now slower, but sometimes stop altogether, and even afterward, for a good stretch, turn back? To save this appearance Ptolemy introduced enormous epicycles, fitting one after another to each planet, with certain rules of incongruent motions — all of which are done away with by a single, utterly simple motion of the Earth. And wouldn't you call it, Simplicio, a very great absurdity if, in Ptolemy's construction — where each planet is assigned its own orbits, one above another — it were often necessary to say that Mars, set above the sphere of the Sun, dropped so low that, breaking through the solar orb, it descended below it and came nearer the Earth than the body of the Sun, and a little later rose immeasurably above it again? And yet this extravagance, and others, are cured by the single, utterly simple annual motion of the Earth.

Sagredo: These stations, retrogressions, and direct motions, which have always struck me as great improbabilities, I'd like to understand better — how they come about in the Copernican system.

Salviati: You, Sagredo, will see them proceed in such a way that this single conjecture ought to be enough — for anyone not downright stubborn or past all teaching — to make him give his assent to everything else in this doctrine. So let me tell you: with nothing altered in Saturn's motion of thirty years, in Jupiter's of twelve, in Mars's of two, in Venus's of nine months, and in Mercury's of about eighty days, the Earth's annual motion alone — set between Mars and Venus — accounts for the apparent irregularities in the motions of all five of the planets I've named; and for an easy, full grasp of the whole thing I want to draw you its figure. So suppose the Sun is placed at the center O; around it we'll mark the orbit traced by the Earth with its annual motion, BGM; and let the circle traced, say, by Jupiter around the Sun in twelve years be this one, bgm; and in the starry sphere let us take the zodiac to be yus. On the Earth's yearly orbit we'll lay off some equal arcs, BC, CD, DE, EF, FG, GH, HI, IK, KL, LM; and on Jupiter's circle we'll mark other arcs, covered in the same stretches of time in which the Earth covers hers — namely bc, cd, de, ef, fg, gh, hi, ik, kl, lm — each of them proportionally smaller than those on the Earth's orbit, since Jupiter's motion under the zodiac is slower than the Earth's yearly one. Now suppose that when the Earth is at B, Jupiter is at b; drawing the straight line B‑b‑p, he'll appear to us in the zodiac at p. Now let the Earth move from B to C, and Jupiter from b to c in the same time: Jupiter will appear to have come to q in the zodiac, moved forward directly in the order of the signs, p, q. Then, as the Earth passes to D and Jupiter to d, he'll be seen in the zodiac at r; and from E, with Jupiter arrived at e, he'll appear in the zodiac at s — still moving forward directly. But once the Earth begins to slip more squarely between Jupiter and the Sun, having come to F while Jupiter is at f, he'll appear to us at t, already having begun apparently to go backward beneath the zodiac; and in the time the Earth has covered the arc EF, Jupiter will have held himself between the points s and t, showing us almost still and stationary. Then, when the Earth has come to G and Jupiter to g, at opposition to the Sun, he'll be seen in the zodiac at u, greatly gone backward through the whole arc of the zodiac t‑u — even though, still keeping his own steady course, he has really gone forward, not just in his own circle but in the zodiac too, with respect to that zodiac's center and to the Sun set there. Then, as both the Earth and Jupiter carry on their motions, once the Earth has come to H and Jupiter to h, he'll be seen greatly gone backward in the zodiac through the whole arc u‑x; and with the Earth at I and Jupiter at i, he'll have moved apparently through the small space x‑y, and there appear stationary. When, next, the Earth has come to K and Jupiter to k, he'll have passed the arc y‑n in the zodiac with direct motion; and continuing his course, the Earth from L will see Jupiter, at l, at the point z; and finally, with Jupiter at m, he'll be seen from the Earth at M passed on to a, again with direct motion. His whole apparent retrogradation in the zodiac will amount to the arc s‑y, made by Jupiter while in his own circle he passes the arc e‑i and the Earth in hers the arc EI. And what has been said of Jupiter, understand of Saturn and Mars as well: in Saturn these backward loops are somewhat more frequent than in Jupiter, since his motion is slower than Jupiter's, so that the Earth overtakes him in a shorter stretch of time; while in Mars they are rarer, because his motion is faster than Jupiter's, so the Earth spends more time catching up with him. As for Venus and Mercury, whose circles are enclosed within the Earth's, their stations and retrogradations likewise appear — caused not by any motions of theirs that are really such, but by the Earth's own annual motion, as Copernicus, following Apollonius of Perga, shrewdly demonstrates in Book Five of his Revolutions, chapter thirty‑five.

You see, gentlemen, with what ease and simplicity the annual motion — supposing it to be the Earth's — fits the case and explains the apparent oddities observed in the movements of the five planets, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury: it sweeps them all away and reduces them to even, regular motions. Nicolaus Copernicus was the first to make this marvelous effect's cause plain to us. But of another marvel — no less astonishing than this one, and one that binds the human mind, by a knot perhaps harder to loosen, to admit this yearly revolution and grant it to our terrestrial globe — the Sun itself brings us a new and unlooked‑for conjecture; the Sun, which shows that it would not, alone among all, dodge bearing witness to so weighty a conclusion, but rather, as a witness beyond every exception, insisted on taking its part. So hear this high, new wonder.

The first to discover and observe the sunspots — as with all the other novelties of the heavens — was our friend the Academician; he discovered them in the year 1610, while still holding the chair of Mathematics at the University of Padua, and he spoke of them there and in Venice to various people, some of whom are still living; and a year later he showed them at Rome to many gentlemen, as he states in the first of his Letters to Signor Mark Welser, Duumvir of Augsburg. He was the first, against the opinions of those too timid and too jealous of the heavens' unchangeableness, to affirm that these spots are matter produced and dissolved over short stretches of time; that, as to place, they are right up against the body of the Sun and turn around it — or rather, carried by the solar globe itself, which revolves upon its own center in the space of about a month, they finish their circuits with it. This motion he judged at first to be made by the Sun about an axis upright to the plane of the ecliptic, since the arcs the spots traced across the Sun's disk appeared to our eye as straight lines parallel to the plane of the ecliptic — lines that were, however, altered in part by certain accidental, wandering, irregular motions to which the spots are subject, and by which they keep shifting their positions among themselves, tumbling and without any order, now gathering many together, now separating, some splitting into several, and greatly changing their shapes, mostly into very outlandish ones. And though these fickle changes altered in part the primary periodic course of the spots, they didn't make our friend change his mind — as if he thought some fixed, essential cause lay behind such deviations; he went on believing that the whole apparent alteration came from those accidental changes, exactly as would happen to someone who, from far‑off regions, watched the motion of our clouds: he'd see them moving with a very swift, large, and constant motion, carried by the daily whirl of the Earth (if that motion were the Earth's) through twenty‑four hours along circles parallel to the equinoctial — yet altered in part by the accidental motions given them by the winds, which shove them here and there at random toward different quarters of the world. It happened at this time that Signor Welser sent him some letters written by a certain false Apelles on the matter of these spots, pressingly asking him to give his opinion freely on those letters, and further to make known what he himself thought about the essence of these spots. He satisfied him with three Letters, showing first how empty Apelles's notions were, and disclosing to him, second, his own opinions — and predicting, moreover, that Apelles, taking better counsel with time, would come round to his view, as afterward happened. And because it seemed to our Academician (as it seemed to others knowledgeable in the things of nature) that in those three Letters he had investigated and demonstrated, if not all that human curiosity could wish and seek, at least all that human reasoning could reach in such a matter, he set aside the continued observations for a time, busy with other studies, and only to oblige some friend would now and then make a scattered observation — until, running into me after some years, and the two of us being at my villa at Le Selve, one of the sunspots, solitary, quite large and dense, together with a most clear and unbroken calm of the sky, invited us; and at my request observations were made of that spot's whole transit, carefully marking on paper its places from day to day, at the hour when the Sun stood on the meridian. Noticing that its journey was not made along a straight line, but one somewhat curved, we took it into our heads to make further observations from time to time; and to that undertaking we were vigorously spurred by a thought that suddenly dropped into my guest's mind, which he shared with me in these words: "Filippo, it seems to me a road opens to a great consequence. For if the axis about which the Sun revolves is not set upright to the plane of the ecliptic, but tilted upon it — as this curved passage we've just observed hints to me — we'll have a conjecture about the standings of the Sun and the Earth as firm and as conclusive as any that has ever yet been supplied to us by any other line of evidence." Roused by so lofty a promise, I pressed him to lay his thought open to me plainly. And he: "If the annual motion belongs to the Earth, along the ecliptic around the Sun, and the Sun is set at the center of that ecliptic and turns upon itself there — not about the ecliptic's axis (which would be the axis of the Earth's annual motion), but about one that is tilted — then strange changes are bound to show themselves to us in the apparent motions of the sunspots, even granting that the Sun's axis stays forever and unchangeably at the same tilt and in the same direction, toward the same point of the universe. For, as the terrestrial globe walks around him in the annual motion, first of all it must happen that to us, carried by that globe, the passages of the spots will sometimes indeed appear made along straight lines — but this only twice a year, and at all other times they'll show as made along noticeably curved arcs. Second, the curvature of these arcs, for one half of the year, will appear tilted opposite to the way it shows in the other half; that is, for six months the convex side of the arcs will face the upper part of the solar disk, and for the other six months the lower. Third, since the spots begin to appear — to be born, so to speak — to our eye on the left side of the solar disk, and go to hide and set on the right, the eastern ends (that is, of the first appearances) will for six months be lower than the opposite ends of the settings, and for the other six the reverse will happen: the spots will be born from higher points and, descending from them in their courses, will come to hide themselves at lower points; and for only two days in the whole year will these ends, of the risings and settings, be level. After these balancings, the tilt of the spots' journeys will begin little by little, growing greater day by day, and in three months reach its greatest slant; then, beginning to lessen from there, in as much time again it will return to the other balance. It will happen, for the fourth wonder, that the day of the greatest slant will be the same as the day of the passage made along a straight line, and on the day of the balancing the arc of the journey will appear more curved than ever; while at other times, as the tilt lessens and heads toward the balance, the curving of the passages' arcs, on the contrary, will keep increasing."

Sagredo: My dear Salviati, I know that to break in on your discourse is bad manners; but I judge it no less bad to let you spread yourself at greater length in words while they are, as they say, tossed to the wind. For, to put it plainly, I can't form a single distinct notion of even one of the conclusions you've pronounced; yet because, taken so generally and confusedly, they strike me as things of admirable consequence, I'd very much like to be made able to grasp them somehow.

Salviati: The very thing that happens to you happened to me too, while they were brought to me by my guest in bare words; he then eased my understanding by picturing the matter for me on a material instrument — nothing but a plain sphere, using some of its circles, but for a purpose other than the one they're commonly meant for. Now, for want of the sphere, I'll make do by drawing figures on paper as needed. And to represent the first fact I put forward — that the passages of the spots could appear made along straight lines only twice a year — let's take this point O to be the center of the great orb, or say of the ecliptic, and likewise of the globe of the Sun itself, of which, thanks to the great distance between it and the Earth, we earth‑dwellers may suppose ourselves to see the half. So we'll draw this circle ABCD about that same center O, to stand for the extreme boundary that divides and separates the Sun's hemisphere that appears to us from the other, hidden one. And because our eye, no less than the center of the Earth, is understood to be in the plane of the ecliptic — where the center of the Sun also is — then, if we picture the solar body as cut by that plane, the section will appear to our eye as a straight line, say BOD; and setting the perpendicular AOC upon it, that will be the axis of the ecliptic and of the terrestrial globe's annual motion. Now understand the solar body (without changing its center) to turn upon itself, not about the axis AOC (the one upright to the plane of the ecliptic), but about one somewhat tilted, say this EOI — which axis, fixed and unchanging, keeps itself forever at the same tilt and direction toward the same points of the firmament and the universe. And because, in the revolutions of the solar globe, each point of its surface (except the poles) describes the circumference of a circle, larger or smaller according to how far it lies from those poles, take the point F equally distant from them and mark the diameter FOG, which will be perpendicular to the axis EI and will be the diameter of the greatest circle described about the poles E, I. Now suppose the Earth, and we with it, to be at such a place in the ecliptic that the Sun's hemisphere apparent to us is bounded by the circle ABCD, which, passing (as it always does) through the poles A, C, passes also through E, I: it's clear that the greatest circle whose diameter is FG will stand upright to the circle ABCD; and to that circle the ray falling from our eye upon the center O is perpendicular, so that this same ray falls in the plane of the circle whose diameter is FG, and therefore its circumference will appear to us as a straight line, the very same as FG. For this reason, whenever a spot was at the point F, then carried on by the solar rotation, it would trace on the Sun's surface the circumference of that circle which appears to us as a straight line. Straight, then, its passage will appear; and straight too will appear the motions of other spots which in the same revolution traced smaller circles, since all are parallel to the greatest and our eye is set at an immense distance from them. Now, if you'll consider how, after the Earth has run in six months through half of the great orb and stands over against the Sun's hemisphere now hidden from us — so that the boundary of the part then seen is the same circle ABCD, which will still pass through the poles E, I — you'll understand that the same will happen with the journeys of the spots: they'll all appear made along straight lines. But because this fact holds only when the boundary passes through the poles E, I, and that boundary keeps shifting from moment to moment through the Earth's annual motion, its passing through the fixed poles E, I is momentary, and consequently momentary is the time during which the spots' motions appear straight. From what has been said so far, one comes to grasp, too, that since the appearance and start of the spots' motion is from the F side, proceeding toward G, their passages run from the left, climbing toward the right; but with the Earth placed at the diametrically opposite part, the spots' appearance around G will indeed be at the observer's left, but the passage will be descending toward the right, F. Now let's picture the Earth situated a quarter away from its present standing, and mark in this other figure the boundary ABCD and the axis, as before, AC, through which the plane of our meridian would pass — a plane in which the axis of the Sun's revolution would also lie, with its poles, one toward us (that is, in the apparent hemisphere), which pole we'll represent with the point E, and the other falling in the hidden hemisphere, which I mark I. Tilting the axis EI, then, with its upper part E toward us, the greatest circle described by the Sun's turning will be this one, BFDG, whose half seen by us — that is, BFD — will no longer appear a straight line, since the poles E, I are not on the circumference ABCD, but will show itself curved, with its convex toward the lower part C; and clearly the same will appear of all the smaller circles parallel to the greatest, BFD. Understand too that when the Earth is diametrically opposite to this standing, so that it sees the Sun's other hemisphere, now hidden, it will see of that same greatest circle the part DGB curved with its convex toward the upper part A; and the courses of the spots in these positions will be first along the arc BFD and then along the other, DGB, and their first appearances and last settings, made around the points B, D, will be level, neither one more nor less raised than the other. But if we place the Earth at such a spot in the ecliptic that neither the bounding circle ABCD nor the meridian AC passes through the poles of the axis EI — as I now show you by drawing this third figure, where the apparent pole E falls between the arc of the boundary AB and the section of the meridian AC — the diameter of the greatest circle will be FOG, the apparent semicircle FNG, and the hidden one GSF: the first curved with its convex N toward the lower part, and the second bent with its crest S toward the upper part of the Sun. The entries and exits of the spots — that is, the ends F, G — won't be level like the earlier B, D, but F lower and G higher, though with less difference than in the first figure; the arc FNG will also be curved, but not as much as the earlier BFD. So in this position the passages of the spots will be ascending from the left part F toward the right G, and will be made along curved lines. And understanding the Earth to be placed in the diametrically opposite site, so that the Sun's now‑hidden hemisphere is the one seen, and bounded by the same circle ABCD, it's plainly made out that the course of the spots will be along the arc GSF, beginning from the high point G — which will likewise be at the observer's left — and going to end, descending toward the right, at the point F. Once what I've laid out so far is understood, I don't think any difficulty remains in grasping how, from the boundary of the solar hemispheres passing through the poles of the Sun's rotation, or near them, or far from them, arise all the differences in the apparent journeys of the spots: so that the farther those poles are from that boundary, the more the said journeys will be curved and the less oblique — whence at the greatest distance, which is when the poles are in the section of the meridian, the curvature is at its highest but the obliquity at its least, that is, at the balance, as the second figure shows; while on the other hand, when the poles are on the boundary, as the first figure shows, the tilt is greatest but the curvature least, reduced to straightness. And as the boundary parts from the poles, the curvature begins to become noticeable, always increasing, while the obliquity and tilt keep growing smaller.

These are the outlandish changes my guest told me would appear from time to time in the progress of the sunspots, provided it were true that the annual motion belonged to the Earth, and that the Sun, set at the center of the ecliptic, turned upon itself about an axis not upright, but tilted, to the plane of that ecliptic.

Sagredo: I'm quite satisfied that I follow these consequences, and I think I'll fix them in my mind better by checking them against a globe set at that tilt, and then looking at it from different sides. What's left now is for you to tell us what came of it all afterward — how the imagined consequences actually turned out.

Salviati: What came of it was this: as we went on for many, many months making the most careful observations, noting down with the utmost accuracy the crossings of various spots at different times of the year, the outcomes were found to answer the predictions exactly.

Sagredo: Simplicio, if what Salviati says is true — and it won't do to cast doubt on his word — then the Ptolemaics and the Aristotelians are going to need solid arguments, mighty conjectures, and rock-firm experiments to counterbalance a blow of such weight, and to keep their opinion from taking its final tumble.

Simplicio: Easy now, my good sir — perhaps you're not yet where you may fancy you've arrived. For although I haven't fully mastered the matter of Salviati's argument, my logic, when I attend to the form of it, does not teach me that this way of arguing forces me by any necessity to conclude in favor of the Copernican hypothesis — I mean the fixity of the Sun at the center of the zodiac and the mobility of the Earth beneath its circumference. For although it's true that, given such-and-such a rotation of the Sun and such-and-such a circling of the Earth, these and those oddities must necessarily be seen in the sunspots, it does not follow that, arguing the other way around, from seeing such oddities in the spots one must necessarily conclude that the Earth moves along the circumference and the Sun is placed at the center of the zodiac. For who assures me that such oddities couldn't also be seen in a Sun that moves along the ecliptic, by inhabitants of an Earth standing fixed at its center? Unless you first prove to me that no account of this appearance can be given when you make the Sun move and the Earth stand still, I won't budge from my opinion, or from my belief that the Sun moves and the Earth stays motionless.

Sagredo: Simplicio bears himself stoutly, and very shrewdly opposes and upholds the side of Aristotle and Ptolemy; and, if I'm to tell the truth, it seems to me that Salviati's company, brief as it's been, has trained him quite a bit in arguing to the point — an effect I gather it has had on others too. As for inquiring into and judging whether a competent account of the apparent extravagances in the sunspots' motions can be given while leaving the Earth motionless and keeping the Sun in motion, I'll wait for Salviati to lay out his thinking; for it's quite believable that he's reflected on it and drawn out whatever can be produced on the point.

Salviati: I've thought about it many times, and talked it over, too, with my friend and host. As for what the philosophers and astronomers will bring forward to prop up the old system: on the one side we're sure — sure, I say — that the true and pure Peripatetics, laughing at anyone who busies himself with such (to their taste) insipid trifles, will write off all these appearances as empty illusions in the glass, and in this way, with little effort, free themselves from any duty to think further. As for the philosopher-astronomers, after we'd speculated with some care on what might be brought to bear, we found no expedient enough to satisfy, at one and the same time, both the course of the spots and the reasoning of the mind. I'll set out for you what occurred to us, and you'll make of it whatever your judgment dictates.

Grant that the apparent motions of the sunspots are just as we've described above; and set the Earth motionless at the center of the ecliptic, on whose circumference the center of the Sun is placed. Then it necessarily follows that the causes of all the variations we see in those motions must reside in motions belonging to the body of the Sun itself. First, the Sun will have to rotate on its own axis, carrying the spots along with it — spots we've assumed, indeed proved, to be stuck fast to the solar surface. Second, we'll have to say that the axis of the Sun's rotation is not parallel to the axis of the ecliptic — which amounts to saying that it isn't set straight up, at right angles to the plane of the ecliptic. For if it were, the spots would appear to cross by straight lines parallel to the ecliptic; so the axis must be tilted, since the crossings mostly appear made along curved lines. Third, we'll need to say that the tilt of this axis is not fixed, forever pointing at the same point of the universe; rather, that from moment to moment it keeps changing its direction. Because if the lean pointed steadily toward the same point, the spots' crossings would never change their look, but — straight or curved, bent up or down, rising or falling — whatever they appeared once, so they would appear always. So we're forced to say that this axis shifts about: sometimes it lies in the plane of the outermost circle that bounds the visible hemisphere — I mean at those times when the spots' crossings appear made along straight lines, and steeper than ever, which happens twice a year; at other times it lies in the plane of the observer's meridian, in such a way that one of its poles falls in the Sun's visible hemisphere and the other in the hidden one, both of them far from the endpoints — the poles, if you like — of another axis of the Sun, one parallel to the axis of the ecliptic (a second axis we'll necessarily have to assign to the globe of the Sun); far from them, I mean, by exactly as much as the tilt of the axis of the spots' revolution comes to. And further, that the pole falling in the visible hemisphere is at one time in the upper part and at another in the lower; for the crossings give us necessary proof of this happening, at the times when they are balanced and at their greatest curvature — now bulging with their convex side toward the lower part of the solar disk, and now toward the upper. And because these states keep changing continuously — the tilts and the curvings growing now greater and now smaller, the tilts sometimes coming to perfect balance and the curves to perfect straightness — we're necessarily driven to lay it down that this very axis of the spots' monthly revolution has a turning of its own, by which its poles trace out two circles around the poles of yet another axis (the one we must, as I said, assign to the Sun), the radius of those circles matching the amount of that axis's tilt. And the period of this turning must be one year, since that is the time in which all the appearances and variations in the spots' crossings come back around. And that this axis makes its turn about the poles of that other axis — the one parallel to the ecliptic's — and not about any other points, is plainly shown by the greatest tilts and the greatest curvings, which are always of the same size. So that, in the end, to keep the Earth fixed at the center, we'll have to credit the Sun with two motions around its own center, on two different axes: one of them completing its turn in a year, the other in less than a month. And this supposition strikes my mind as very hard, next to impossible. And that comes on top of our having to credit the same solar body with two further motions around the Earth on different axes — with the one tracing out the ecliptic in a year, and with the other making spirals, or circles parallel to the equinoctial, one a day. So that for that third motion, the one we'd have to assign to the globe of the Sun in itself (I don't mean the roughly monthly one that carries the spots, but the other, which has to shift the axis and poles of that monthly one) — there is no reason to be seen why it should finish its period in a year, as though it depended on the annual motion along the ecliptic, rather than in twenty-four hours, as though it depended on the daily motion about the poles of the equinoctial. I know that what I'm saying is, for now, quite obscure; but it will become clear to you when we come to speak of the third motion — an annual one — that Copernicus assigns to the Earth. Now, when these four motions, so incompatible with one another (all of which would have to be credited, of necessity, to the one body of the Sun), can instead be reduced to a single, utterly simple motion, assigned to the Sun on an axis that never alters; and when, without introducing anything new into the motions already assigned to the terrestrial globe on so many other grounds, we can so easily save all these outlandish appearances in the motions of the sunspots — then it really does seem that the option is not to be refused.

This, Simplicio, is as much as has so far occurred to our friend and me that might be offered, in explaining this appearance, by the Copernicans and the Ptolemaics in support of their views. Make of it whatever your own judgment tells you.

Simplicio: I know myself unequal to entering into so weighty a decision; and as for my own opinion, I'll stay neutral — hoping, though, that a time will come when, enlightened by higher contemplations than these human discussions of ours, our minds will be unveiled, and the fog that now keeps them clouded will be lifted away.

Sagredo: Excellent and holy is the counsel Simplicio holds to, and worthy to be received and followed by everyone — for, deriving from the highest wisdom and the supreme authority, it alone can be embraced in safety.
