But as far as human reasoning is permitted to penetrate — keeping myself within the bounds of conjecture and probable argument — I'll say, a little more decidedly than Simplicio does, that among all the subtle arguments I've ever heard, I've never met anything that struck my mind with greater wonder, or bound my understanding more tightly (setting aside pure geometrical and arithmetical demonstrations), than these two conjectures: the one drawn from the stations and retrogradations of the five planets, the other from these strange behaviors in the motions of the sunspots. And because they seem to me to give, so easily and so lucidly, the true cause of appearances so bizarre — showing how a single simple motion, blended with many others that are likewise simple but different from one another, without raising any difficulty at all, and indeed sweeping away every difficulty that dogs the other position — I keep concluding to myself that those who stay obstinate against this doctrine must either not have heard, or not have understood, these so plainly conclusive arguments.

Salviati: I won't call them either conclusive or inconclusive; for, as I've said before, my aim has not been to settle anything on so lofty a question, but only to lay out the natural and astronomical arguments that I can bring on either side, leaving the decision to others — a decision that, in the end, ought not to be ambiguous. For since one of the two arrangements must necessarily be true and the other necessarily false, it's impossible that (staying, of course, within the bounds of human doctrine) the arguments offered for the true side should not show themselves just as conclusive as those on the other side are empty and ineffective.

Sagredo: Then it must be time we heard the objections in that little book of conclusions, or disquisitions, that Simplicio has brought along.

Simplicio: Here's the book; and here's the passage where the author first briefly describes the system of the world according to Copernicus's position, saying: Terram igitur una cum Luna totoque hoc elementari mundo Copernicus, etc. — "Copernicus, then, has the Earth, together with the Moon and this whole elemental world," and so on.

Salviati: Hold on a moment, Simplicio, for it seems to me that this author, at his very entrance, shows himself to understand very little of the position he's undertaking to refute — since he says Copernicus has the Earth, together with the Moon, trace out the great orb in a year while moving from east to west; a thing which, being false and impossible, was never once asserted by Copernicus. On the contrary, Copernicus has it go the other way — from west to east, I mean, that is, in the order of the signs — which is why the Sun's annual motion then appears as it does, the Sun set motionless at the center of the zodiac. See the overbold self-assurance of the man! — to set himself up to refute another man's doctrine, and be ignorant of its very first foundations, on which the greatest and most important part of the whole structure rests. This is a poor way to win credit with the reader. But let's go on.

Simplicio: Having set out the general system, he begins to raise his objections against this annual motion; and the first are these — which he delivers ironically, in mockery of Copernicus and his followers — writing that in this fantastical arrangement of the world one is bound to utter the most solemn absurdities: namely, that the Sun, Venus, and Mercury are below the Earth; that heavy matter naturally goes upward and light matter downward; that Christ, our Lord and Redeemer, ascended to hell and descended to heaven when he drew near the Sun; that when Joshua commanded the Sun to stand still, it was the Earth that stopped, or else the Sun moved contrary to the Earth; that when the Sun is in Cancer, the Earth runs through Capricorn; that the winter signs make summer and the summer signs make winter; that it is not the stars that rise and set to the Earth, but the Earth to the stars; that the east begins in the west and the west in the east; and, in short, that nearly the whole course of the world is turned upside down.

Salviati: It all pleases me well enough — except for his mixing passages of Holy Scripture, ever to be revered and feared, in among these childish and all-too-scurrilous jibes, and his wanting to wound with sacred things a man who, philosophizing in jest and in play, neither affirms nor denies, but, having laid down certain suppositions or hypotheses, reasons in a familiar way.

Simplicio: It scandalized me too, and not a little — above all with what he adds right after: that although the Copernicans do answer these and other such arguments (though in a very twisted way), still they'll never be able to satisfy and answer the things that follow.

Salviati: That, now, is worst of all — because it implies he has things more forceful and conclusive than the authority of Holy Writ. But please, let us revere Scripture, and pass on to natural and human arguments. Indeed, if among his natural reasons he produces nothing of better sense than what he's brought so far, we may set this whole enterprise aside — for I certainly don't mean to spend a word answering such silly trifles. And what he says, that the Copernicans reply to these objections, is utterly false; nor can anyone believe that a single man would set about wasting his time so uselessly.

Simplicio: I join in the same judgment; but let's hear the other objections, which he brings forward as much more powerful. And here, as you see, with the most exact calculations he concludes that if the great orb of the Earth — the one along which Copernicus has it run once a year around the Sun — were as good as imperceptible next to the immensity of the starry sphere (as Copernicus himself says one must suppose it to be), then one would necessarily have to say and grant that the fixed stars are at an unimaginable distance from us, and that the smallest of them are bigger than the whole great orb itself, and some others far larger than the entire sphere of Saturn — bulks truly all too vast, and incomprehensible, and incredible.

Salviati: I've seen something like this before, urged by Tycho against Copernicus; and it's not just now that I've uncovered the fallacy — or better, the fallacies — of this argument, built on utterly false assumptions and on a statement of Copernicus's own, seized by his opponents with the most pinpoint strictness, in the way those litigants do who, being in the wrong on the main merits of the case, fasten on a single little word the other side let drop in passing, and raise an endless clamor over it. And, for your clearer understanding: when Copernicus had set out those marvelous consequences that follow, in the other planets, from the Earth's annual motion — namely the forward and retrograde motions of the three upper planets in particular — he added that this apparent change (which showed up more in Mars than in Jupiter, Jupiter being farther off, and less still in Saturn, farther off than Jupiter) remained imperceptible in the fixed stars, on account of their immense distance from us compared with the distance of Jupiter or Saturn. Here the opponents of this view rise up; and taking that "imperceptibility" Copernicus spoke of as if he had laid it down for something really and absolutely equal to nothing, and adding that even one of the smaller fixed stars is nonetheless perceptible, since it falls under the sense of sight, they go on calculating — with the help of other false assumptions — and conclude that in Copernicus's doctrine one must admit that a fixed star is far bigger than the whole great orb. Now I, to expose the emptiness of this whole line of argument, will show that from supposing a fixed star of the sixth magnitude to be no bigger than the Sun, it follows by a true demonstration that the distance of those fixed stars from us comes out so great that it is enough to make the Earth's annual motion — which in the planets causes such large and observable variations — show no noticeable effect in them; and at the same time I'll show, point by point, the great fallacies in the assumptions of Copernicus's opponents.

And first, I suppose, with Copernicus himself and in agreement with the opponents, that the radius of the great orb — the half-diameter, that is, which is the distance from the Earth to the Sun — contains 1,208 radii of the Earth itself. Second, I lay down — with their assent, and with the truth — that the apparent diameter of the Sun, at its mean distance, is about half a degree, that is, 30 arcminutes, which are 1,800 seconds, that is, 108,000 thirds. And since the apparent diameter of a fixed star of the first magnitude is no more than 5 seconds, that is 300 thirds, and the diameter of a sixth-magnitude star is 50 thirds (and here lies the greatest error of Copernicus's opponents), it follows that the diameter of the Sun contains the diameter of a sixth-magnitude star 2,160 times. And so, if one supposed a sixth-magnitude star to be really equal to the Sun, and no bigger — which is the same as saying, if the Sun were moved away far enough that its diameter appeared one of the 2,160 parts of what it appears now — its distance would have to be 2,160 times greater than it actually is now; which is as much as to say that the distance of the sixth-magnitude fixed stars is 2,160 radii of the great orb. And because the distance of the Sun from the Earth contains, by common agreement, 1,208 radii of the Earth, while the distance of the fixed stars (as we've said) is 2,160 radii of the great orb, it follows that the radius of the Earth, compared with the great orb, is much larger — nearly double, in fact — than the radius of the great orb is in relation to the distance of the starry sphere. And for that reason, the difference of apparent position in the fixed stars, caused by the diameter of the great orb, can be scarcely more observable than the one we observe in the Sun that arises from the radius of the Earth.

Sagredo: That's quite a comedown for a first step.

Salviati: It truly is. A fixed star of the sixth magnitude, which on this author's reckoning had to be — if Copernicus's claim was to hold up — as big as the whole great orb: just by setting it merely equal to the Sun, and the Sun is far less than the ten-millionth part of that great orb, he makes the starry sphere so vast and so high that it's enough to dispose of the objection raised against Copernicus.

Sagredo: Do work that calculation for me, please.

Salviati: The calculation is easy and very quick. The Sun's diameter is eleven of the Earth's semidiameters, and the diameter of the great orb contains 2,416 of those same semidiameters, by the common account of these measures; so the diameter of the orb contains that of the Sun about 220 times over. And since spheres are to one another as the cubes of their diameters, let's take the cube of 220, which is 10,648,000, and we'll have the great orb bigger than the Sun ten million six hundred forty-eight thousand times over — and to this great orb this author said a star of the sixth magnitude would have to be equal.

Sagredo: So their error consists in going badly wrong when they take the apparent diameter of the fixed stars.

Salviati: That's the error, but it isn't the only one. And I'm truly amazed that so many astronomers — men of great name, too, like Alfraganus, Albategnius, and Thebit, and more recently men like Tycho and Clavius, in short all our Academician's predecessors — should have gone so badly wrong in determining the sizes of all the stars, both fixed and moving, apart from the two luminaries, and should have paid no heed to the stray radiance — that fringe of light around them — which deceptively shows them a hundred times and more larger than when they're seen without their rays. And this oversight of theirs can't be excused, because it was in their power to see the stars, whenever they liked, without that fringe; you need only look at them at their first appearance in the evening or their last vanishing at dawn. And if nothing else, Venus — which one often sees at midday so small that you really have to strain your eyes, and which then, the following night, shows up as an enormous torch — ought to have alerted them to their mistake. For I certainly won't believe they supposed the true disk to be the one that shows in deep darkness, and not the one seen in a lit setting; because our own lamps, which seen at night from far off look big but up close show their true flame small and sharply bounded, could well enough have put them on their guard. In fact, if I must speak my mind freely, I absolutely believe that none of them — not even Tycho himself, so careful in handling astronomical instruments, who built such large and exact ones sparing no expense — ever set out to take and measure the apparent diameter of a single star, apart from the Sun and the Moon; instead I think that arbitrarily, and as they say by eye, one of the older among them pronounced the thing to be so, and his followers then, without any further check, simply stuck with that first pronouncement. For had any one of them applied himself to putting it to some test, he would without doubt have caught the error.

Sagredo: But if they lacked the telescope — and you've already said our friend came to know the truth with that instrument — then the others ought to be excused, not charged with negligence.

Salviati: That would follow if the aim couldn't be achieved without the telescope. It's true that the instrument, by showing the star's disk bare and magnified a hundred and a thousand times over, makes the job much easier; but one can also, without the instrument, reach the same result — if not so exactly. And I've done it many times; the method I've used is this. I hung a fine cord toward some star — I used Lyra, which rises between north and northeast — and then, by moving closer to the cord and farther from it, with the cord placed between me and the star, I found the spot from which the thickness of the cord exactly hides the star. This done, I took the distance from my eye to the cord, which turns out to be one of the sides enclosing the angle formed at the eye and standing on the thickness of the cord — an angle similar to, indeed the very same as, the angle that at the starry sphere stands on the diameter of the star. And from the ratio of the cord's thickness to the distance from eye to cord, using the table of arcs and chords, I immediately found the size of the angle — using, of course, the usual precaution one observes in taking such sharp angles: not to place the meeting of the visual rays at the center of the eye, where they only reach after being bent, but beyond the eye, where in reality the size of the pupil sends them to converge.

Sagredo: I understand this precaution, though I have some slight doubt about it; but what troubles me more is that in this operation, when it's done in the dark of night, it seems to me one measures the diameter of the radiant disk, and not the true, bare diameter of the star.

Salviati: No, sir — because the cord, in covering the star's bare little body, strips away the hairs, which are not the star's but our own eye's, and of which it is robbed the moment its true disk is hidden. And when you make the observation, you'll see how unexpectedly that quite large torch — which seemed as though it couldn't be hidden except behind a much bigger obstacle — is covered by a thin little cord. Then, to measure most exactly and find out how many such cord-thicknesses fit into the distance to the eye, I take not a single diameter of the cord, but — laying many pieces of it side by side on a board so that they touch — I take with a compass the whole space occupied by fifteen or twenty of them, and with that measure I gauge the distance (already taken with another, finer thread) from the cord to the convergence of the visual rays. And by this quite exact operation I find that the apparent diameter of a fixed star of the first magnitude — commonly reckoned at 2 arcminutes, and even 3 by Tycho in his Astronomical Letters, page 167 — is no more than 5 arcseconds, which is a twenty-fourth or a thirty-sixth part of what they believed. Now see on what grave errors their doctrines are founded.

Sagredo: I see and understand it very well; but before we go any further, I'd like to raise the doubt that occurs to me about locating the convergence of the visual rays beyond the eye, when one looks at objects subtending very sharp angles. My difficulty comes from its seeming to me that this convergence may be now farther off and now nearer — and this not so much because of the greater or smaller size of the object looked at, as because, in looking at objects of the same size, it seems to me the convergence of the rays must, for some other reason, become more or less remote from the eye.

Salviati: I already see where Sagredo's sharp mind is heading — a most diligent observer of nature's workings. And I'd lay any bet you like that, among a thousand who have watched the pupil of a cat's eye contract and widen enormously, there aren't two — perhaps not one — who has noticed that a like effect happens in men's pupils when they look, according as the medium is much or little illuminated, and that in broad daylight the little circle of the pupil shrinks a great deal; so that in looking at the disk of the Sun it shrinks to something smaller than a grain of millet, whereas in gazing at non-shining objects, and within a dimmer medium, it widens to the size of a lentil or more. In short, this widening and narrowing varies by much more than a tenfold ratio. From which it's clear that when the pupil is much dilated, the angle where the rays converge must be more remote from the eye — which is what happens when we look at faintly lit objects. This is a lesson freshly supplied to me by Sagredo, by which, whenever a most exact observation of great consequence is to be made, we're warned that we must carry out the investigation of that convergence in the very act of the same, or a very similar, operation. But in this case, to expose the astronomers' error, so much accuracy isn't needed — because even if, in their favor, we supposed the convergence to fall right at the pupil itself, it would matter little, their error being so great. I don't know, Sagredo, whether this was the point you were driving at.

Sagredo: That's it exactly, and I'm glad it wasn't unreasonable — as I'm assured by finding I'd hit on the same thing as you. But now, while we're on it, I'd gladly hear in what way one can investigate the distance of the convergence of the visual rays.

Salviati: The method is quite easy, and it goes like this. I take two strips of paper, one black and one white, and I make the black one half as wide as the white. Then I fasten the white one to a wall, and at some distance from it I fix the other on a stick or some other support, fifteen or twenty braccia away. And moving off from this second strip, along the same straight line, by as much again, it's clear that at that distance the straight lines would converge — the lines that, setting out from the ends of the white strip's width, pass just grazing the width of the other strip set in the middle. Whence it follows that if the eye were placed at that meeting point, the black strip in the middle would hide the white one opposite exactly — provided sight took place at a single point. But if we find that the ends of the white strip appear uncovered, that will be a necessary proof that the visual rays don't issue from a single point. And to make the white strip get hidden by the black, we'll have to bring the eye closer: so, having moved it up until the middle strip covers the far one, and having noted how much closer we had to come, that amount of approach will be a sure measure of how far beyond the eye the true convergence of the visual rays falls in this operation. And we'll have besides the diameter of the pupil — or rather of that opening from which the visual rays issue. For the pupil's diameter will be the same fraction of the black paper's width as this ratio: the distance from the convergence of the lines (drawn through the ends of the papers) to the spot where the eye stood when it first saw the far paper hidden by the middle one, compared with the distance between the two papers. And so, if we wanted to measure with precision the apparent diameter of a star, once the observation had been made in the way described above, we'd need to compare the diameter of the cord with the diameter of the pupil. And having found, for instance, that the cord's diameter is four times the pupil's, and the distance from the eye to the cord is, say, 30 braccia, we'll say that the true convergence of the lines drawn from the ends of the star's diameter through the ends of the cord's diameter goes to meet at 40 braccia beyond the cord — for in this way the proportion will be duly preserved between the distance from the cord to the convergence of those lines and the distance from that convergence to the eye's position, which must be the same as the ratio between the cord's diameter and the pupil's diameter.

Sagredo: I've understood it perfectly. So let's hear what Simplicio brings forward in defense of Copernicus's opponents.

Simplicio: Even though that supreme and utterly incredible absurdity urged by these opponents of Copernicus has been much softened by Salviati's argument, it doesn't seem to me removed so completely that enough force doesn't still remain in it to bring down that opinion. For, if I've grasped the sum and final conclusion, even granting that stars of the sixth magnitude are as big as the Sun (which itself seems a lot to believe), it would still be true that the great orb ought to cause in the starry sphere a change and variation like the one the Earth's semidiameter produces in the Sun, which is observable. So, since neither such a variation nor even a smaller one is to be seen in the fixed stars, it seems to me that on this account the Earth's annual motion is left forlorn and demolished.

Salviati: You'd conclude rightly, Simplicio, if there were nothing else to produce on Copernicus's side; but many other things still remain to us. And as for your objection, nothing stands in the way of our supposing the distance of the fixed stars to be even much greater than has been assumed. And you yourself, and anyone else who won't set aside the propositions accepted by Ptolemy's followers, will have to admit it a most fitting thing to place the starry sphere still very much larger than what we've just said it must be reckoned. For, since all astronomers agree that the greater slowness of the planets' revolutions is caused by the greater size of their spheres — and that Saturn is therefore slower than Jupiter, and Jupiter than the Sun, because the one has to trace a larger circle than the other, and this one than that, and so on — considering that Saturn, for instance, the height of whose orb is nine times greater than the Sun's, and whose time for one revolution is therefore 30 times longer than that of one revolution of the Sun; seeing that in Ptolemy's doctrine one revolution of the starry sphere is completed in 36,000 years, whereas Saturn's is finished in 30 and the Sun's in one; arguing by a like proportion, and saying: if Saturn's orb, being 9 times larger than the Sun's, revolves in a time 30 times longer, then by inverse reasoning how large must that orb be which revolves 36,000 times more slowly? — it will be found that the distance of the starry sphere must be 10,800 semidiameters of the great orb, which would be exactly 5 times greater than what we calculated it to be a moment ago, on the supposition that a sixth-magnitude star was as big as the Sun. Now see how much smaller still, on this reckoning, the variation caused in the stars by the Earth's annual motion would have to be. And if by a similar relation we wanted to argue the distance of the starry sphere from Jupiter and from Mars, the one would give it to us as 15,000 and the other as 27,000 semidiameters of the great orb — that is, still larger, the one 7 and the other 12 times over, than the size of a fixed star supposed equal to the Sun gave it to us.

Simplicio: It seems to me one could answer this by saying that the motion of the starry sphere has, since Ptolemy, been observed not to be so slow as he reckoned it; indeed, I think I've heard that Copernicus himself was the observer.

Salviati: You put it very well, but you produce nothing that favors the Ptolemaics' cause in the least — for they never refused the 36,000-year motion of the starry sphere on the grounds that so great a slowness would make it too vast and immense. For if such immensity were not to be granted in nature, they ought — before now — to have denied a revolution so slow that it couldn't, by any reasonable proportion, be fitted to anything but a sphere of intolerable size.

Sagredo: Please, Salviati, let's waste no more time proceeding by such proportions with people who are content to admit the most disproportionate things — so that with them, by this road, it's absolutely impossible to gain anything. And what more disproportionate proportion can be imagined than the one these men swallow and admit? For, while writing that there's no more fitting way to order the celestial spheres than to regulate them by the differences in the lengths of their periods — placing, step by step, the slower above the faster — once they've set the starry sphere highest, as the slowest of all, they set another above it, and therefore larger, and make it move in twenty-four hours, while its lower neighbor moves in 36,000 years. But we spoke enough of these disproportions the other day.

Salviati: I'd like you, Simplicio, to suspend for a moment the affection you bear the followers of your opinion, and tell me sincerely whether you believe they grasp in their minds that magnitude which they then judge cannot, on account of its immensity, be attributed to the universe. Because I, for my part, believe not; and it seems to me that just as, in grasping numbers, once one begins to pass those thousands of millions the imagination gets confused and can no longer form a concept, so it happens too in grasping immense sizes and distances. So that the same effect befalls the reasoning as befalls the senses: when on a clear night I look toward the stars, my senses judge their distance to be a few miles, and the fixed stars no more remote than Jupiter or Saturn — indeed, no farther than the Moon. But, without more ado, consider the past controversies between the astronomers and the Peripatetic philosophers over the distance of the new stars in Cassiopeia and in Sagittarius — the former placing them among the fixed stars, the latter believing them lower than the Moon. So powerless is our sense to distinguish great distances from the very greatest, even though the latter are in fact many thousands of times greater than the former. And finally I ask you, oh foolish man: do you grasp with your imagination that magnitude of the universe which you then judge to be too vast? If you grasp it, will you claim that your apprehension reaches further than divine power — will you say you can imagine things greater than God can bring about? But if you don't grasp it, why do you presume to pass judgment on things you don't understand?

Simplicio: These arguments all run very well, and it's not denied that the heavens may exceed our imagination in size — nor that God could have created them a thousand times larger than they are. But we mustn't grant that anything was created in vain or is idle in the universe. Now, since we see this beautiful order of planets arranged around the Earth at distances proportioned to producing their effects upon it for our benefit — to what end interpose, then, between Saturn's highest orb and the starry sphere, a vast space empty of any star, superfluous and pointless? To what end? For whose comfort and use?

Salviati: We arrogate too much to ourselves, Simplicio, it seems to me, when we insist that the care of us alone is the whole adequate work — the limit beyond which divine wisdom and power does or arranges nothing else. But I wouldn't have us so shorten His hand; rather let us be content to be sure that God and nature are so occupied with the government of human affairs that they could not attend to them more closely even if they had no other care than mankind alone. And this I think I can make clear with a most apt and noble example, drawn from the working of the Sun's light: which, while it draws up those vapors or warms that plant, draws them up and warms it as though it had nothing else to do; indeed, in ripening that cluster of grapes — nay, that single grape — it applies itself so that it could not apply itself more effectively even if the goal of all its business were the ripening of that one grape alone. Now, if this grape receives from the Sun all that can be received, and nothing is filched from it by the Sun's producing, at the same time, a thousand and a thousand other effects, then that grape would be chargeable with envy or with folly if it believed or demanded that the action of the Sun's rays should be spent for its benefit alone. I'm certain that nothing is left undone by divine Providence of what pertains to the government of human affairs; but that there cannot be other things in the universe dependent on His infinite wisdom — that, for my own part, so far as my reason dictates to me, I could not bring myself to believe. Yet, even if the fact stood otherwise, there would be no reluctance in me to believe the reasons that a higher intelligence might bring forward to me. Meanwhile, when I'm told that an immense space set between the planets' orbs and the starry sphere would be useless and pointless, empty of stars and idle — and likewise that so great an immensity, as a receptacle for the fixed stars, surpassing all our comprehension, is superfluous — I say it's rashness to try to make our feeble reasoning the judge of God's works, and to call vain or superfluous everything in the universe that doesn't serve us.

Sagredo: Say rather — and I think you'll put it better — that we don't know what purpose they serve for us. And I count it one of the greatest arrogances, one of the greatest follies, that anyone could put forward, to say: "Because I don't know what use Jupiter or Saturn is to me, therefore they're superfluous — in fact they don't even exist in nature." Why, you utter fool, I don't even know what use my arteries are to me, or my cartilage, my spleen, my gall bladder; I wouldn't so much as know I had a gall bladder, a spleen, or kidneys, if they hadn't been shown to me cut out of many corpses; and even then I could only learn what my spleen does in me at the moment it was taken out of me. To understand what this or that heavenly body does in me — since you insist that every operation of theirs is aimed at us — I'd have to remove that body for a while, and then whatever effect I felt go missing in me, I could say depended on that star. What's more, who is going to claim that the space these people call too vast and useless, between Saturn and the fixed stars, is empty of any other worldly bodies? Perhaps because we don't see them? So then the four Medicean Stars and the companions of Saturn came into the sky when we first began to see them, and not before? And the other countless fixed stars weren't there before men saw them? And the nebulae were once just little whitish patches, but then with the telescope we made them turn into throngs of many bright and beautiful stars? Presumptuous — no, reckless — ignorance of men!

Salviati: There's no need, Sagredo, to go on any further with these fruitless outbursts. Let's stick to our plan, which is to weigh the force of the reasons brought forward on the one side and the other, without settling anything, and leave the judgment then to those who know more than we do. And coming back to our natural, human discussion, I say that these terms — great, small, immense, tiny, and the rest — are not absolute but relative, so that the very same thing, compared with different things, may now be called immense and now imperceptible, let alone small. That being so, I ask: in relation to what can Copernicus's starry sphere be called too vast? In my view, it can't be compared or called such except in relation to something else of the same kind. So let's take the smallest of the same kind, which will be the lunar orb; and if the starry orb is to be judged too vast compared with the Moon's, then every other magnitude that exceeds another of the same kind by a like or greater proportion will have to be called too vast — and by this reasoning we'll also have to deny that it exists in the world at all. And so elephants and whales will surely be chimeras and poetic fancies; because the former, being too vast compared with ants, which are land animals, and the latter compared with sprats, which are fish — and ants and sprats are certainly there in rerum natura, in the real world — would be too enormous, since, absolutely speaking, the elephant and the whale exceed the ant and the sprat by a far greater proportion than the starry sphere exceeds the Moon's, if we picture that sphere just as large as it needs to be to fit the Copernican system. What's more, how large is the sphere of Jupiter, how large that of Saturn, each assigned as the housing for a single star — and a very small one, compared with a fixed star? Surely, if each fixed star had to be given such a portion of the world's space for its lodging, the orb where that countless multitude of them resides would have to be made many, many thousands of times bigger than what suffices for Copernicus's needs. Besides, don't you call a fixed star very small — I mean even the most conspicuous of them, let alone those that escape our sight? And we call them so in comparison with the space surrounding them. Now, if the whole starry sphere were a single shining body, who doesn't grasp that within infinite space one could assign a distance so great that this shining sphere would look just as small — smaller, even — than a fixed star looks to us now from the Earth? From there, then, we would judge small the very same thing that from here we now call immeasurably great.

Sagredo: Enormous, it seems to me, is the folly of those who would have God make the universe more in proportion to the small capacity of their reasoning than to His immense — indeed, infinite — power.

Simplicio: Everything you say is fine; but the point the other side presses on is having to grant that a fixed star must be not merely equal to, but so much larger than the Sun — and both, after all, are particular bodies set inside the starry orb. And it seems to me very much to the purpose that this author asks, and demands: "To what end, and for whose benefit, are machines so vast? Produced, perhaps, for the Earth — that is, for a tiny little point? And why so remote, so that they appear so slight and can do absolutely nothing on Earth? To what purpose that monstrous, immense chasm between them and Saturn? Empty and pointless are all those things not supported by probable reasons."

Salviati: From the questions this man asks, it seems to me one can gather that if we leave the heavens, the stars, and the distances at the quantities and sizes he has believed so far — even though he's never securely pictured any comprehensible size for them — he grasps perfectly well, and is quite satisfied about, the benefits that come from them upon the Earth, which would then no longer be a tiny little thing, nor they so remote as to appear so small, but big enough to be able to act upon the Earth, and the distance between them and Saturn perfectly well proportioned; and that he has very probable reasons for all these things — some of which I'd gladly have heard. But to see that in these few words he tangles himself up and contradicts himself makes me think he's very poor and short of these probable reasons, and that what he calls reasons are rather fallacies — shadows, even, of empty fancies. For I now ask him whether these heavenly bodies really do act upon the Earth, and whether it's for such an effect that they were produced at such-and-such sizes and set at such-and-such distances — or whether, on the contrary, they have nothing to do with earthly things. If they have nothing to do with the Earth, it's great foolishness for us earthlings to want to be arbiters of their sizes and regulators of their local arrangements, when we're utterly ignorant of all their affairs and concerns. But if he says they do act, and that they're directed to this end, then he comes to affirm what, from another angle, he himself denies — and to praise what he's just now condemned, when he said that heavenly bodies placed at such a distance that they appear tiny from the Earth can't act upon it at all. But, my dear man, in the starry sphere — already fixed at the distance it's at, which you judge to be well proportioned for its influences on these earthly things — a great many stars appear extremely small, and there are a hundred times as many altogether invisible to us (which is to appear smaller still than tiny). So you must either — contradicting yourself — now deny that they act upon the Earth; or else — contradicting yourself again — grant that appearing tiny takes nothing away from their action; or else — and this will be the more honest and modest concession — grant and freely confess that our judgment about their sizes and distances is a vanity, not to say a presumption or a rashness.

Simplicio: Truly, I saw it myself at once, reading this passage — the plain contradiction, in saying that the stars of Copernicus, so to speak, appearing so tiny, couldn't act upon the Earth, and yet not noticing that he'd granted action upon the Earth to those of Ptolemy and his own, which appear not merely tiny, but for the most part are invisible.

Salviati: But I come to another point. On what basis does he say that the stars appear so small? Perhaps because that's how we see them? Doesn't he know that this comes from the instrument we use in looking at them — namely, our eye? And that this is true is shown by the fact that if we change the instrument we'll see them bigger and bigger, as much as we please; and who knows whether to the Earth, which gazes at them without eyes, they don't show themselves enormous, and just as they really are? But it's time we left these trifles and came to weightier matters. So, having already proved these two things — first, how far off the firmament must be placed so that in it the diameter of the great orb makes no greater difference than the terrestrial orb makes in the distance of the Sun; and then having likewise proved how, to make a star of the firmament appear to us the size we see it, there's no need to make it bigger than the Sun — I'd like to know whether Tycho, or any of his followers, has ever tried to investigate in any way whether in the starry sphere any appearance can be detected by which one could more decisively deny, or admit, the annual motion of the Earth.

Sagredo: I'd answer no on their behalf — nor did they even have any need to; since it's Copernicus himself who says there is no such difference, and they, arguing ad hominem — against the man's own admission — grant it to him, and on this assumption they show the improbability that follows: namely, that one would have to make the sphere so immense that a fixed star, in order to appear as large as it appears to us, would in reality have to be a bulk so immense that it exceeded the size of the whole great orb — which is then, as they say, altogether incredible.

Salviati: I'm of the same opinion, and I believe precisely that they argue against the man more to defend another man than out of any longing to come to knowledge of the truth. And not only do I not believe that any of them has applied himself to making such an observation, but I'm not even sure whether any of them knows what difference the annual motion of the Earth would produce in the fixed stars, if the starry sphere were not at so great a distance that such a difference vanished in them for its smallness. For to stop short of such an inquiry and fall back on Copernicus's mere say-so may well be enough to defeat a man in argument, but not to make the fact itself clear — since it may be that the difference is there, but not looked for, or, for its smallness or for lack of exact instruments, not detected by Copernicus. This wouldn't be the first thing that he, for lack of instruments or some other shortcoming, failed to know, and yet, grounding himself on other utterly sound conjectures, affirmed something that the things he didn't grasp seemed to contradict. For, as was said before, without the telescope neither could Mars be seen to grow 60 times, nor Venus 40 times, larger in the one position than in the other — indeed, their differences appear far smaller than the truth; and yet we've since come to be certain that these changes exist exactly as the Copernican system required. So it would be well done to search out, with the greatest precision one could manage, whether such a change — which ought to be discernible in the fixed stars, granting the Earth's annual motion — is actually observed: something I absolutely believe has not so far been done by anyone, and not only not done, but perhaps (as I said) not even well understood by many, what one ought to be looking for. Nor do I say this at random; for I once saw a certain handwritten paper by one of these anti-Copernicans, who said that, if that opinion were true, there would necessarily follow a continual raising and lowering of the pole every six months, as the Earth in that span withdraws, over as much space as the diameter of the great orb, now toward the north, now toward the south; and it seemed to him reasonable — indeed, necessary — that, we following the Earth, when we were toward the north we should have the pole higher than when we're toward the south. Into this same error fell a mathematician who was otherwise quite intelligent, and likewise a follower of Copernicus, according to what Tycho reports in his Progymnasmata, on page 684: he said he'd observed the polar altitude change, and be different in summer from winter. And because Tycho denies the merit of the cause but doesn't condemn the procedure — that is, he denies that any change is seen in the polar altitude, but doesn't condemn the inquiry itself as unsuited to obtaining what's sought — he comes to declare that he too reckons the polar altitude, whether varied or unvaried from six months to six months, to be a good test for excluding or admitting the annual motion of the Earth.

Simplicio: Really, Salviati, it seems to me too that the same thing ought to follow. For I don't believe you'll deny me that if we walk only 60 miles toward the north, the pole will rise a degree for us, and drawing likewise another 60 miles nearer the north, the pole will rise another degree for us, and so on. Now, if drawing 60 miles nearer or farther makes so notable a change in the polar altitudes, what must carrying the Earth — and us with it — not 60 miles, but 60 thousand, do?

Salviati: It will have to make (if we're to follow that proportion) the pole rise a thousand degrees for us. See, Simplicio, how much an inveterate impression can do! You, for having fixed it in your imagination for so many years that it's the heavens that turn round in twenty-four hours, and not the Earth, and that consequently the poles of that revolution are in the sky and not in the terrestrial globe, can't for even an hour strip yourself of this habit and dress up in the opposite — picturing that it's the Earth that moves, just for as long as it takes to conceive what would follow if this fib were true. If the Earth, Simplicio, is what turns on itself in twenty-four hours, then in it are the poles, in it is the axis, in it is the equinoctial — that is, the great circle described by the point equally distant from the poles — in it are the infinitely many parallels, greater and smaller, described by the points of its surface more and less distant from the poles: in it are all these things, and not in the starry sphere, which, being motionless, lacks them all, and only in imagination can they be pictured there — by prolonging the Earth's axis out to where, ending, it will mark two points overhead of our poles, and the plane of the equinoctial, extended, will trace out in the sky a circle answering to it. Now, if the true axis, the true poles, the true terrestrial equinoctial don't change on Earth so long as you too stay in the same place on Earth, then carry the Earth wherever you please — you'll never change your relation, either to the poles, or to the circles, or to anything else terrestrial; and this because such a carrying is common to you and to all earthly things, and motion, where it's common, is as though it weren't there at all. And just as you won't change your relation to the earthly poles — a relation, I mean, such that they rise or fall for you — so likewise you won't change it to the poles figured in the sky, so long as by celestial poles we understand (as was already defined) those two points marked out there by the Earth's axis prolonged that far. It's true that those points in the sky change when the carrying of the Earth is done in such a way that its axis goes to strike other and other points of the motionless celestial sphere; but our relation to them doesn't change, such that the second should rise higher for us than the first. Whoever wants one of the firmament's points answering to the Earth's poles to rise, and the other to fall, for him must walk on Earth toward the one, moving away from the other; for carrying the Earth, and us with it (as I've already said), does nothing.

Sagredo: Do me the kindness, Salviati, of letting me lay this business out quite clearly with an example — a crude one, maybe, but all the more suited to the point. Imagine, Simplicio, that you're aboard a galley, and that, standing at the stern, you've trained a quadrant or some other astronomical instrument on the top of the foremast, as though you wanted to take its elevation, which was, let's say, 40 degrees. There's no doubt that if you walk along the gangway toward the mast some 25 or 30 paces, and then train the same instrument again on the same masthead, you'll find its elevation to be greater — grown, let's say, by 10 degrees. But if, instead of walking those 25 or 30 paces toward the mast, you stayed put at the stern and made the whole galley move that way, do you believe that, by the voyage it had made of those 25 or 30 paces, the elevation of the foremast would show itself increased to you by 10 degrees?

Simplicio: I believe, and understand, that it wouldn't gain so much as a single hair's breadth by a voyage of a thousand or a hundred thousand miles, let alone of 30 paces. But I do believe that if, in sighting along the top of the foremast, a fixed star happened to lie in the same line, then — I mean — keeping the quadrant fixed, after sailing 60 miles toward the star, the sight would strike the tip of the foremast just as before, but no longer the star, which would have risen a degree for me.

Sagredo: But surely you don't believe that the line of sight would fail to strike that point of the starry sphere which answers to the line of the masthead?

Simplicio: No, that it wouldn't; but the point would have changed, and would lie below the star first observed.

Sagredo: That's exactly how it is. But just as, in this example, what answers to the elevation of the masthead is not the star, but the point of the firmament that lies in line with the eye and the top of the mast, so in the case we're illustrating, what in the firmament answers to the pole of the Earth is not a star or any other fixed thing of the firmament, but that point at which the terrestrial axis, prolonged straight out that far, comes to end — a point that isn't fixed, but obeys the changes the earthly pole might make. And therefore Tycho, or the others who brought this objection, ought to have said that, given such a motion of the Earth (if it were true), one should detect and observe some difference in the rising and falling not of the pole, but of some fixed star toward that quarter which answers to our pole.

Simplicio: Now I understand perfectly the confusion these people fell into; but it still doesn't rob me of the force — which seems to me very great — of the argument brought on the other side, once it's referred to the change of the stars, and no longer of the pole. For if the galley's motion, of only 60 miles, makes a fixed star rise for me by a degree, how could a like change — and a far greater one, too — not come to me when the galley is carried toward that same star over as much space as the diameter of the great orb, which you say is twice the distance from the Earth to the Sun?

Sagredo: Here, Simplicio, there's another confusion — one you really do understand, only it doesn't occur to you to understand it; and I'll try to remind you of it. So tell me: suppose that, after adjusting your quadrant on a fixed star and finding, for instance, its elevation to be 40 degrees, you — without shifting from your place — tilted the arm of the quadrant so that the star stayed raised above that line of sight; would you say, on that account, that the star had gained a greater elevation?

Simplicio: Certainly not, because the change was made in the instrument, not in the observer — who would have had to change place by moving toward the star.

Sagredo: But when you're sailing or walking over the surface of the Earth, would you say that no change at all was made in that same quadrant — that it always kept the same elevation with respect to the sky — so long as you yourself didn't tilt it, but left it standing in its first setting?

Simplicio: Let me think about it a moment. I'd say without a doubt that it wouldn't keep it, because the journey I make isn't over a flat plane, but over the curved surface of the terrestrial globe, which step by step changes its inclination with respect to the sky, and consequently makes the instrument — which keeps its setting upon that surface — change too.

Sagredo: Very well put; and you also grasp that the larger and larger that circle was, over which you moved, the more miles you'd have to walk to make that star rise by that one degree for you; and finally, that when the motion toward the star was along a straight line, you'd have to move even farther than along the circumference of any circle, however enormous.

Salviati: Yes, because in the end the circumference of an infinite circle and a straight line are the same thing.

Sagredo: Oh, that I don't understand — nor do I think Simplicio understands it either; and there must be some hidden mystery underneath, because we know that Salviati never speaks at random, nor brings out a paradox that doesn't turn on some far-from-trivial idea. So, in due place and time, I'll remind you to explain this business of the straight line being the same as the circumference of the infinite circle — but for now I don't want us to interrupt the discussion we have in hand. And getting back to the point, I'd have Simplicio consider that the approaching and withdrawing the Earth does to that fixed star which is near the pole is done as if along a straight line — namely the diameter of the great orb; so that to try to gauge the rising and falling of the polar star by the motion along that diameter as though it were the motion over the Earth's own tiny little circle is a great sign of small understanding.

Simplicio: But we're still stuck in the same difficulties, since not even that slight difference which there ought to be is seen to be there; and if this is nil, then we have to confess that the annual motion along the great orb, attributed to the Earth, is nil as well.

Sagredo: Now here I'll let Salviati carry on — for it seems to me he wasn't dismissing as nil the rising or falling of the polar star, or of some other fixed star, even though it's grasped by no one, and even though Copernicus himself set it down not, I'd say, as nil, but as unobservable for its smallness.

Salviati: I've already said above that I don't believe anyone has set himself to observe whether, at different times of the year, any change can be detected in the fixed stars that might depend on the annual motion of the Earth; and I added, moreover, that I doubt whether anyone has even properly understood what the changes are, and among which stars they ought to appear. So it's well that we go through this point carefully. Finding it written only in general terms — that the annual motion of the Earth along the great orb ought not to be admitted, because it's not likely that no apparent change would be seen in the fixed stars on account of it — and then not hearing said what in particular such apparent changes should be, and in which stars, makes me very reasonably reckon that those who rest on that general pronouncement have not understood, nor perhaps even tried to understand, how the business of these changes actually works, nor what the things are that they say ought to be seen. And what moves me to judge so is knowing that the annual motion attributed by Copernicus to the Earth, when it has to become perceptible in the starry sphere, doesn't have to produce an apparent change equally with respect to all the stars; rather, that appearance must be greater in some, smaller in others, smaller still in others, and finally, in others, absolutely nil — however enormous the circle of this annual motion is supposed to be. Now the changes that ought to be seen are of two kinds: one is the stars' changing their apparent magnitude, and the other, their varying their meridian altitudes — from which follows, in consequence, the changing of their risings and settings, and of their distances from the point overhead, and so on.

Sagredo: It seems to me I see a whole skein of these turnings being got ready for me — God grant I'm ever able to disentangle myself from it; for, to confess my failing to you, Salviati, I've thought about this now and then, and could never find the loose end of the thread — and I don't mean so much about this that concerns the fixed stars, as about another, more terrible business that you've brought back to my mind by mentioning these meridian altitudes, rising latitudes, and distances from the point overhead, and so on. And my brain's tangle comes from what I'll tell you now. Copernicus places the starry sphere motionless, and the Sun at its center, likewise motionless; therefore every change that appears to us to occur in the Sun or in the fixed stars must necessarily belong to the Earth — that is, to us. But the Sun rises and falls in our meridian through an enormous arc, of nearly 47 degrees, and through arcs greater and greater still it varies the breadth of its risings and settings on the slanting horizons. Now how can the Earth ever tilt and rise so notably toward the Sun, and not at all toward the fixed stars — or by so little that it's an imperceptible thing? This is the knot that has never once been able to pass through my comb; and if you'll untie it for me, I'll think you more than an Alexander.

Salviati: These are difficulties worthy of Sagredo's wit; and the doubt is such that even Copernicus himself almost despaired of being able to explain it in a way that would make it intelligible — which is plain both from his own confession of its obscurity, and from his falling back on it twice, in two different ways, to explain it. And I frankly confess that I didn't grasp his explanation until, by another and different method, quite plain and clear, I made it intelligible to myself — though not without a long and laborious application of mind.

Simplicio: Aristotle saw this same difficulty and used it to refute certain ancients who wanted the Earth to be a planet: against them he argues that, if that were so, then it too, like the other planets, would have to have more than one motion, from which would follow this variation in the risings and settings of the fixed stars, and in their meridian altitudes as well. And since he raised the difficulty and didn't resolve it, it must be — if not impossible — at least hard to solve.

Salviati: The size and force of the knot make its untying all the finer and more admirable; but I don't promise it to you for today, and I beg you to excuse me until tomorrow. For now, we'll go on considering and explaining those changes and differences that ought to be detected in the fixed stars on account of the annual motion, just as we were saying — and in explaining them there come to be set out certain preparatory points for the untying of the greatest difficulty. Now, taking up again the two motions attributed to the Earth (and I say two, because the third isn't really a motion at all, as I'll explain in its place), that is, the annual and the daily: the first is to be understood as made by the center of the Earth along the circumference of the great orb — that is, of a greatest circle described in the plane of the ecliptic, fixed and unchanging; the other, the daily, is made by the globe of the Earth on itself, about its own center and its own axis — not upright, but tilted to the plane of the ecliptic, with an inclination of about 23 and a half degrees. This inclination is maintained all year long, and — what is most of all to be noted — it always keeps toward the same part of the sky, so that the axis of the daily motion stays perpetually parallel to itself. Thus, if we imagine that axis prolonged out to the fixed stars, then while the center of the Earth circles the whole ecliptic in a year, that same axis describes the surface of an oblique cylinder, which has for one of its bases the said yearly circle, and for the other a like circle imaginarily described by its extremity — or, if you like, its pole — among the fixed stars. And this cylinder is oblique to the plane of the ecliptic according to the inclination of the axis that describes it, which we've said is 23 and a half degrees; and this inclination, keeping perpetually the same (except that over many thousands of years it makes some very slight change, which matters nothing to the present business), causes the terrestrial globe never to tilt any further or to rise, but to keep itself unchanging. From which it follows that, as regards the changes to be observed in the fixed stars depending on the annual motion alone, the same will happen to any point whatever of the Earth's surface as to the very center of the Earth; and so in the present explanations we'll make use of the center, as of any point of the surface. And for easier understanding of the whole, we'll draw the line-figures; and first let's mark, in the plane of the ecliptic, the circle ANBO, and let's take the points A, B to be the extremes toward the north and toward the south — that is, the beginning of Cancer and of Capricorn — and let's prolong the diameter AB indefinitely through D and C toward the starry sphere. I say now, first, that none of the fixed stars set in the ecliptic will ever, by any change made by the Earth through that plane of the ecliptic, vary its elevation, but will always be seen in the same plane; but the Earth will indeed approach it and withdraw from it by as much space as the diameter of the great orb. This is plainly seen in the figure: for whether the Earth is at point A or at B, the star G is always seen along the same line ABC; but the distance BC has become smaller than CA by the whole diameter BA. The most, then, that can be detected in the star C — and in any other set in the ecliptic — is the increased or diminished apparent magnitude, from the Earth's approaching or withdrawing.

Sagredo: Hold on a moment, please, because I feel some scruple or other bothering me, and it's this. That the star C is seen along the same line A B C whether the Earth is at A or at B — this I understand very well; and moreover I grasp that the same would happen from all the points of the line A B, while the Earth passed from A to B along that line. But since it passes, as is supposed, along the arc A N B, it's a plain thing that when it's at point N — and at any other point except the two, A and B — it will be seen no longer along the line A B, but along other and other lines. So that if showing itself along different lines must cause an apparent change, some difference will have to be detected. What's more, I'll say — with that philosophical freedom which among friends who are philosophers ought to be allowed — it seems to me that you, contradicting yourself, are now denying what this very day you declared to us, to our wonder, to be a most true and important thing: I mean what happens with the planets, and in particular the three outer ones, which, being continually in the ecliptic or very near it, not only show themselves now near to us and now most remote, but so irregular in their otherwise regular motions that they sometimes appear to us stationary, and sometimes, by many degrees, retrograde — and all for no other cause than the annual motion of the Earth.

Salviati: Even though a thousand proofs have made me certain of Sagredo's shrewdness, I wanted, with this further trial, to reassure myself all the more of what I can promise myself from his wit — and all for my own benefit, since when my propositions can stand firm to the hammer, or to the assayer's cupel, of his judgment, I can be sure they're of good alloy under any test. I say, then, that I had deliberately disguised that objection — yet not with any intent to deceive you and persuade you of some falsehood, as could have happened if the instance I disguised, and you passed over, had really been such as it shows itself in appearance, that is, truly strong and conclusive. But it isn't such; on the contrary, I now suspect that you, to test me, are pretending not to recognize its nullity. But in this particular I mean to be craftier than you, by dragging out of your mouth by force what you were artfully trying to hide from us: so tell me, what is it by which you recognize the stations and retrogradations of the planets deriving from the annual motion — an effect so great that at least some trace of a like effect ought to be seen in the stars of the ecliptic?

Sagredo: Your question contains two queries, to which I must reply: the first concerns the charge you lay on me, of being a dissembler; the other is about what may appear in the stars, and so on. As to the first, I'll say — with your leave — that it's not true I pretended not to understand the nullity of that instance; and to reassure you of it, I tell you now that I understand that nullity perfectly well.

Salviati: But I don't understand how it can be that you weren't speaking dissemblingly when you said you didn't understand that particular fallacy — which you now confess you understand perfectly well.

Sagredo: The very confession that I understand it can assure you I wasn't dissembling when I said I didn't understand it; for if I'd wanted, and did want, to dissemble, who could stop me from continuing in the same pretense, still denying that I understood the fallacy? I say, then, that I didn't understand it then, but that I grasp it well now — thanks to your having woken up my intellect, first by telling me resolutely that it's nil, and then by beginning to question me so broadly, what that thing was by which I recognized the stations and retrogradations of the planets. And since this is recognized from the comparison one makes of them with the fixed stars — in relation to which their motions are seen to vary, now westward and now eastward, and sometimes to stay as if motionless — and since above the starry sphere there's no other, immensely more remote and still visible to us, with which we could compare our fixed stars, therefore we can detect no trace in the fixed stars answering to what appears to us in the planets. This, I think, is just what you meant to draw out of my mouth.

Salviati: That it is — with the added bonus of your very subtle wit. And if with a little hint I opened your mind, you with another make me recall that it's not altogether impossible that something might, at some time, be found observable among the fixed stars, by which one could make out in which of them the annual revolution resides — so that they too, no less than the planets and the Sun itself, might be willing to appear in court to bear witness to such a motion in the Earth's favor. For I don't believe that the stars are strewn over a spherical surface, all equally distant from a single center; rather I reckon that their distances from us are so various that some of them may be 2 and 3 times more remote than certain others. So that if there were found, with the telescope, some very tiny star quite close to one of the larger ones — and that larger one were therefore very high up — it might happen that some perceptible change would occur between them, answering to that of the outer planets. And so much be said for now about the particular of the stars set in the ecliptic. Let's come now to the fixed stars set outside the ecliptic, and let's imagine a greatest circle erect to the plane of the ecliptic — say, for example, a circle that in the starry sphere answers to the colure of the solstices — and let's mark it CEH, which will at the same time turn out to be a meridian; and in it let's take a star outside the ecliptic, such as E would be. Now this one, at the Earth's motion, will indeed vary its elevation; for from the Earth at A it will be seen along the ray AE, with the elevation of the angle EAC; but from the Earth placed at B it will be seen along the ray BE, with the elevation of the angle EBC, greater than the other EAC, since that one is exterior and this interior and opposite, in the triangle EAB. The distance of the star E from the ecliptic will thus be seen changed; and likewise its altitude in the meridian will have become greater in the position B than in the place A, according as the angle EBC exceeds the angle EAC, which is the size of the angle AEB. For, the side AB of the triangle EAB being prolonged to C, the exterior angle EBC (being equal to the two interior and opposite ones, E and A) exceeds A itself by the size of the angle E. And if we take another star in the same meridian, more remote from the ecliptic — such as, for instance, the star H would be — the difference in its being seen from the two places A and B will be greater still, according as the angle AHB becomes greater than the other, E; which angle will keep growing, the farther the observed star is from the ecliptic, until finally the greatest change will appear in that star which was set in the very pole of the ecliptic — as, for total understanding, we can demonstrate thus. Let the diameter of the great orb be AB, whose center is G, and let it be understood as prolonged out to the starry sphere at the points D, C; and from the center G let the axis of the ecliptic GF be raised up to the same sphere, in which let there be understood described a meridian DFC, which will be erect to the plane of the ecliptic; and taking in the arc FC any points you please, H, E, as places of fixed stars, let the lines FA, FB, AH, HG, HB, AE, GE, BE be joined, so that the angle of the difference — or let's say the parallax — of the star set at the pole F is AFB, that of the star set at H is the angle AHB, and that of the star at E is the angle AEB. I say that the angle of the difference of the polar star F is the greatest, and, of the others, the one nearer the greatest is greater than the more remote — that is, that the angle F is greater than the angle H, and this greater than the angle E. Let there be understood a circle described about the triangle FAB; and because the angle F is acute (its base AB being smaller than the diameter DC of the semicircle DFC), it will be set in the larger portion of the circumscribed circle cut off by the base AB; and because that AB is divided in half and at right angles by FG, the center of the circumscribed circle will be on the line FG: let it be the point I. And because, of the lines drawn from the point G — which is not the center — to the circumference of the circumscribed circle, the greatest is the one that passes through the center, GF will be greater than any other drawn from the point G to the circumference of that same circle; and therefore that circumference will cut the line GH (which is equal to the line GF), and cutting GH it will also cut AH: let it cut it at L, and let the line LB be joined. The two angles AFB, ALB will therefore be equal, being in the same portion of the circumscribed circle; but ALB, being exterior, is greater than the interior H: therefore the angle F is greater than the angle H. And by the same method we'll demonstrate that the angle H is greater than the angle E, because the center of the circle described about the triangle AHB is on the perpendicular GF, to which the line GH is nearer than GE, and therefore its circumference cuts GE, and also AE: whence the point is manifest. Let's conclude, then, that the difference of appearance (which, by a term proper to the art, we may call the parallax of the fixed stars) is greater and smaller according as the observed stars are more or less near the pole of the ecliptic; so that finally, of the stars that are in the ecliptic itself, that difference is reduced to nothing. As for the Earth's approaching or withdrawing from the stars by this motion: from those that are in the ecliptic it approaches and withdraws by as much as the whole diameter of the great orb, as we saw just now; but from the stars around the pole of the ecliptic that approach or withdrawal is almost nil, and for the others this difference grows greater according as they are nearer the ecliptic. We can, in the third place, understand how that difference of apparent position grows greater or smaller according as the observed star is nearer to us or more remote; for, if we mark another meridian less distant from the Earth — such as this DFI would be — a star set at F and seen along the same ray AFE, the Earth being at A, when it was then observed from the Earth at B, would be seen along the ray BF, and would make the angle of the difference, that is BFA, greater than the other first one AEB, being exterior to the triangle BFE.

Sagredo: I've listened to your argument with great pleasure — and profit, too; and to make sure I've followed it correctly, I'll sum up the conclusions in a few words. It seems to me you've explained that there are two distinct kinds of appearance we could observe in the fixed stars by means of the Earth's annual motion. One is the change in their apparent magnitudes, as we, carried along by the Earth, draw nearer to them or farther away. The other — which likewise depends on that same approaching and receding — is their showing up in the same meridian sometimes higher and sometimes lower. What's more, you tell me (and I understand it perfectly) that neither of these changes happens equally in all the stars, but more in some, less in others, and not at all in still others. The approach and recession by which a given star should look now larger and now smaller is imperceptible — practically nil — in the stars near the pole of the ecliptic, but greatest in the stars sitting right on the ecliptic, and middling in those in between; while the reverse holds for the other difference, that is, the raising and lowering is nil in the stars on the ecliptic, greatest in those near the pole of the ecliptic, and middling in the ones between. Beyond that, both of these differences are more noticeable in stars that happen to be closer, less noticeable in the farther ones, and finally, in the extremely distant ones, they would vanish altogether. That's my part of it; what remains, as I see it, is to satisfy Simplicio, who I don't think will readily agree to wave off as imperceptible such differences — differences springing from so vast a movement of the Earth, and from a change that carries the Earth to places twice as far apart from each other as we are from the Sun.

Simplicio: To speak freely, I do feel a great reluctance to grant that the distance of the fixed stars has to be so enormous that in them the differences we've been describing must be completely undetectable.

Salviati: Don't give yourself up entirely to despair, Simplicio — there may still be some easing of your difficulties. First of all, that the apparent size of the stars isn't seen to change noticeably shouldn't strike you as at all improbable, once you notice how badly people's estimation deceives them in this sort of thing, especially when they're looking at bright objects. Take yourself, for example, gazing at a lit torch 200 paces off: if it came 3 or 4 arm-lengths closer, would you claim to notice it, because it looked bigger? I certainly wouldn't notice, not even if it came 20 or 30 closer. In fact I've sometimes happened to watch a light like that at such a distance and couldn't make up my mind whether it was coming toward me or moving away, when in reality it was drawing nearer. But what of it? If that same approaching and receding (I mean twice the distance from the Sun to us) is almost totally imperceptible in the star of Saturn, and barely observable in Jupiter, what must it be in the fixed stars, which I don't think you'd be unwilling to place at twice the distance of Saturn? In Mars, which by coming closer to us —

Simplicio: Don't trouble yourself any further on this particular point; I'm already convinced that what's been said about the unchanged apparent size of the fixed stars could very well be the case. But what shall we say about the other difficulty — the one that arises from our not detecting any variation in the change of position?

Salviati: We may say something that could set you at ease on this side too. And to come straight to it: wouldn't you be satisfied if we actually did observe in the stars those changes which you think ought to be seen if the annual motion belonged to the Earth?

Simplicio: I'd be satisfied beyond any doubt, as far as this particular point is concerned.

Salviati: I'd like you to say that if such a difference were observed, nothing would be left that could cast doubt on the Earth's mobility, seeing that no other explanation could be assigned to such an appearance. But even supposing it doesn't show up perceptibly, that still doesn't remove the mobility, nor does it necessarily prove immobility, since it may be (as Copernicus maintains) that the immense distance of the starry sphere makes such tiny appearances impossible to observe. And those appearances, as I've already said, may be that up to now they've never even been looked for — or, if looked for, not in the way they should be, that is, with the exactness that such minute precision would require. That exactness is hard to come by, partly through the shortcomings of astronomical instruments, which are subject to many distortions, and partly through the fault of those who handle them with less care than they ought. A necessarily conclusive proof of how little such observations are to be trusted is the disagreement we find among astronomers in assigning the positions — I won't say of new stars and comets, but of the fixed stars themselves, right down to the polar altitudes, where more often than not they're at odds with one another by many arcminutes. And to tell the truth, who, working with a quadrant or a sextant whose side is at most 3 or 4 arm-lengths long, could be sure — in the seating of the plumb line, or in the cut of the sight-vane — not to be off by two or three arcminutes, which around its circumference will be no wider than a grain of millet? On top of that, it's nearly impossible for the instrument to be built and kept with absolute accuracy. Ptolemy himself shows distrust of an armillary instrument built by Archimedes for catching the Sun's entrance into the equinoctial.

Simplicio: But if the instruments are so suspect and the observations so doubtful, how can we ever put ourselves on secure ground and free ourselves from error? I'd heard great things preached about Tycho's instruments, made at immense expense, and about his singular diligence in his observations.

Salviati: All of that I grant you; but neither the one nor the other is enough to make us secure in a matter of such importance. I want us to make use of instruments far, far larger than Tycho's — supremely exact, and made at hardly any expense at all — whose side is 4, 6, 20, 30, or 50 miles, so that a degree comes out a mile wide, an arcminute 50 arm-lengths, and an arcsecond a little under an arm-length. In short, we can have them, without spending a thing, of any size we please. Staying at a villa of mine near Florence, I plainly observed the Sun's arrival at, and departure from, the summer solstice: one evening, as it set, it slipped behind a crag of the Pietrapana mountains about 60 miles off, leaving a thin sliver of itself uncovered toward the north, a sliver whose width was not the hundredth part of the Sun's diameter; and the next evening, at a similar setting, it again showed a similar uncovered sliver, but noticeably thinner — a sure sign that it had begun to pull away from the tropic. The Sun's backward shift from the first observation to the second certainly didn't amount to so much as one arcsecond on the horizon; and the observation, made with a superb telescope that magnifies the Sun's disc more than a thousand times, comes out both easy and delightful. Now, with instruments like these I want us to make our observations of the fixed stars, using one of those in which the change ought to be most conspicuous — namely, as already stated, the ones most remote from the ecliptic. Among these the Lyre, a very bright star near the pole of the ecliptic, would be well suited for very northern countries, worked in the manner I'll describe shortly; but I'll use a different star. I've already scouted out a spot quite well suited for such an observation. The place is an open plain, above which, toward the north, there rises a very lofty mountain, on whose summit is built a small church, set from west toward east, so that the ridge of its roof can cut the meridian of some dwelling down in the plain at right angles. I mean to fix a little beam parallel to that ridge, or crest of the roof, and about an arm-length away from it. Once that's set, I'll look in the plain for the spot from which one of the stars of the Wain — the Big Dipper — as it crosses the meridian, comes to be hidden behind the beam I've placed there; or else, if the beam isn't thick enough to hide the star, I'll find the spot from which that same beam is seen to cut the star's disc in half — an effect that a superb telescope brings out exquisitely. And if the spot from which this occurs happens to have some dwelling on it, so much the better; if not, I'll have a stake driven firmly into the ground, with a fixed mark to show where the eye should be placed each time one wants to repeat the observation. The first of these observations I'll make around the summer solstice, and then go on from month to month, or whenever I please, until the other solstice; with this observation one could detect the raising and lowering of the star, however slight it might be.
