And if, in carrying it out, we should manage to make out any change at all, what a great gain there would be for astronomy! For by such means, besides assuring ourselves of the annual motion, we could come to know the size and distance of that very star.

Sagredo: I follow the whole procedure perfectly, and the operation seems to me so easy, and so well suited to what's needed, that one might very reasonably believe Copernicus himself, or some other astronomer, had already carried it out.

Salviati: It seems to me quite the opposite, because it isn't likely that, if anyone had tried it, he wouldn't have made some mention of the outcome — whether it turned out in favor of the one opinion or the other. Besides, neither for this nor for any other purpose does one find that anyone made use of such a way of observing — which, moreover, could scarcely be carried out at all without an exact telescope.

Sagredo: I'm completely satisfied with what you've said. But since we still have plenty of time before nightfall, if you want me to pass the evening in peace, do me the kindness of laying out those problems whose explanation you asked, a little while ago, to put off until tomorrow. Give us back the leave you were granted — cash it in now — and setting all other discussions aside, come and explain to us how, once we grant the Earth the motions Copernicus assigns it, and hold the Sun and the fixed stars still, these same phenomena can follow — the Sun's rising and sinking in the sky, the changing of the seasons, the inequality of days and nights, and so on — in exactly the way they are so easily grasped in the Ptolemaic system.

Salviati: Nothing Signor Sagredo asks can be, or ought to be, refused. The delay I asked for had no other purpose than to give me time to arrange in my mind the premises needed for a full and open account of how the phenomena you name follow in the Copernican arrangement just as they do in the Ptolemaic — indeed, with far greater ease and simplicity in the one than in the other. From that it will be plain that this hypothesis is as easy for nature to carry out as it is hard for the intellect to grasp. Even so, I hope that by using a different way of laying it out than the one Copernicus used, I can also make it a good deal less obscure to follow. To do that I'll put forward a few assumptions that are self-evident and obvious in themselves, and they are these:

First. Suppose the Earth, a spherical body, turns about its own axis and poles: then each point marked on its surface traces out the circumference of a circle, larger or smaller according as the point is farther from or nearer to the poles. Of these circles, the largest is the one traced by a point equally distant from the two poles; and all these circles are parallel to one another — and "parallels" is what we'll call them.

Second. Since the Earth is spherical in shape and opaque in substance, it is continually lit by the Sun over half its surface, while the other half stays dark. And since the line that divides the lit part from the dark is a great circle, we'll call it the boundary circle of the light.

Third. When the boundary circle of the light passes through the Earth's poles, it cuts (being a great circle) all the parallels into equal parts. But when it does not pass through the poles, it cuts them all into unequal parts, except for the middle circle alone, which, being itself a great circle, is still cut into equal parts.

Fourth. As the Earth turns about its own poles, the lengths of the days and the nights are determined by the arcs of the parallels cut off by the boundary circle of the light: the arc that lies in the lit hemisphere sets the length of the day, and the remainder is the length of the night.

With these things laid down, and for a clearer grasp of what remains to be said, let's draw a figure. First we'll mark the circumference of a circle to stand for the great orb — the Earth's annual path — drawn in the plane of the ecliptic, and we'll divide it into four equal parts with two diameters, Capricorn, Cancer, Libra, and Aries, which at the same time will stand for the four cardinal points, that is, the two solstices and the two equinoxes. At the center of this circle we'll mark the Sun, O, fixed and unmoving. Now, about the four points Capricorn, Cancer, Libra, and Aries, taken as centers, let's draw four equal circles to stand for the Earth, set at those places at different times of year; and in the space of one year its center travels the whole circumference Capricorn, Aries, Cancer, and Libra, moving from west to east — that is, in the order of the signs. It's already clear that while the Earth is at Capricorn, the Sun will appear at Cancer; and as the Earth moves along the arc from Capricorn to Aries, the Sun will appear to move along the arc from Cancer to Libra, and in short to run through the zodiac in the order of the signs over the course of a year. And so this first premise alone accounts, without dispute, for the Sun's apparent annual motion along the ecliptic.

Now, coming to the other motion — the Earth's daily turning on itself — we have to fix its poles and its axis. This axis is to be understood as not standing upright at right angles to the plane of the ecliptic — that is, not parallel to the axis of the great orb — but leaning away from the right angle by about 23 and a half degrees, with its north pole tilted toward the axis of the great orb, while the Earth's center sits at the solstice point of Capricorn. Taking the terrestrial globe, then, to have its center at the point Capricorn, we'll mark its poles and its axis AB, tilted 23 and a half degrees from the perpendicular over the diameter Capricorn–Cancer, so that the angle at A over Capricorn–Cancer comes to be the complement of a quadrant, that is, 66 and a half degrees; and this tilt is to be understood as unchanging. The upper pole A we'll take to be the north, and the other, B, the south. Now imagine the Earth turning on itself about the axis AB in twenty-four hours, again from west to east: then every point marked on its surface traces circles parallel to one another. In this first position of the Earth, we'll mark the greatest of them, CD, and the two that lie 23 and a half degrees from it — EF above and GN below — and the two outermost, IK and LM, lying the same distance from the poles A and B. And just as we've marked these five, so we can imagine countless others, parallel to these, traced by the countless points of the Earth's surface. Now imagine the Earth, by the annual motion of its center, carried into the other places already marked, but passing through them under this law: that its own axis AB not only never changes its tilt with respect to the plane of the ecliptic, but also never changes its direction at all, so that, always keeping parallel to itself, it points continually toward the same part of the universe — of the firmament, we might say. If we imagine it extended that far, its uppermost tip would trace a circle parallel and equal to the great orb Libra–Capricorn–Aries–Cancer, like the upper base of a cylinder described by the axis itself in the annual motion over the lower base Libra–Capricorn–Aries–Cancer. So, given this unchanging tilt, we'll draw these other three figures about the centers Aries, Cancer, and Libra, alike in every respect to the first one drawn about the center Capricorn.

Now let's look at the first figure of the Earth. In it, since the axis AB leans 23 and a half degrees from the perpendicular over the diameter Capricorn–Cancer, toward the Sun O, and since the arc AI is likewise 23 and a half degrees, the Sun's light will illuminate the hemisphere of the terrestrial globe turned toward the Sun (of which we see half here), divided from the dark part by the boundary of the light, IM. By this boundary the parallel CD, being a great circle, is divided into equal parts, but all the others into unequal parts, since the boundary of the light IM does not pass through their poles A and B. The parallel IK, together with all those drawn inside it and nearer the pole A, will remain wholly in the lit part; while, on the other side, the opposite ones toward the pole B, contained within the parallel LM, will remain in the dark. Moreover, since arc AI equals arc FD, and arc AF is shared between them, the two arcs IKF and AFD will be equal, each of them a quadrant. And because the whole arc IFM is a semicircle, the arc MF will be a quadrant, equal to the other, FKI. And so the Sun O will, in this state of the Earth, be directly overhead for anyone at the point F. But by the daily rotation about the fixed axis AB, all the points of the parallel EF pass through this same point F; and so on that day the Sun at noon will be directly overhead for everyone living on the parallel EF, and in its apparent motion it will seem to them to trace the circle we call the Tropic of Cancer. But to those living on all the parallels that lie above EF, toward the north pole A, the Sun sinks from their overhead point toward the south; and, on the other hand, to everyone living on the parallels below EF, toward the equinoctial CD and the south pole B, the noon Sun stands higher than their overhead point, toward the north pole A. It can be seen, next, how of all the parallels only the greatest, CD, is cut into equal parts by the boundary of the light IM, while the others, both below and above that greatest circle, are all cut into unequal parts. And of the upper ones, the daytime arcs — those of the part of the Earth's surface lit by the Sun — are larger than the nighttime arcs, which remain in the dark; and the opposite happens with the rest, which lie below the greatest circle CD toward the pole B, whose daytime arcs are smaller than their nighttime arcs. It can also be plainly seen that the differences between these arcs keep growing as the parallels come nearer the poles, until the parallel IK stays entirely in the lit part, and its inhabitants have a day of twenty-four hours with no night; while, on the contrary, the parallel LM, staying entirely in the dark, has a night of twenty-four hours with no day.

Now let's come to the third figure of the Earth, set with its center at the point Cancer, from which the Sun appears to be at the first point of Capricorn. It is at once plain that, because the axis AB has not changed its tilt but has kept parallel to itself, the aspect and situation of the Earth are exactly the same as in the first figure — except that the hemisphere lit by the Sun in the first is now in the dark, and the one that was dark in the first position is now lit. So what happened before about the differences between days and nights — about which were longer or shorter — now happens in reverse. First we see that where, in the first figure, the circle IK was wholly in the light, it is now wholly in the dark, and the opposite circle LM, which before was wholly dark, is now wholly in the light. On the parallels between the greatest circle CD and the pole A, the daytime arcs are now smaller than the nighttime ones, where before it was the reverse; and likewise on the others toward the pole B, the daytime arcs are now larger than the nighttime ones, the opposite of what happened in the other state of the Earth. Now we see the Sun standing directly overhead for the inhabitants of the tropic GN, and sunk toward the south for those on the parallel EF by the whole arc ECG, that is, 47 degrees — in short, it has passed from one tropic to the other, crossing the equinoctial, rising and sinking on the meridian through that span of 47 degrees. And all this change comes not from the Earth's tilting or rising, but on the contrary from its never tilting or rising at all — in short, from its keeping always the same posture with respect to the universe, merely circling the Sun, which sits in the middle of the very plane along which the Earth moves round it in a circle by the annual motion. And here we should note a marvelous thing: that just as the Earth's keeping its axis pointed in the same direction toward the universe — toward the highest sphere of the fixed stars, we might say — makes the Sun appear to us to rise and sink through so great a span, namely 47 degrees, while the fixed stars neither rise nor sink at all, so, on the contrary, if that same axis of the Earth kept continually the same tilt toward the Sun — toward the axis of the zodiac, we might say — no change would appear in the Sun as to its rising and sinking, so that the inhabitants of any given place would always have the same differences of days and nights and the same arrangement of seasons — some always winter, others always summer, others always spring, and so on — but, on the other hand, a very great change would appear in the fixed stars as to their rising and sinking for us, amounting to those same 47 degrees. To understand this, let's go back and consider the Earth's state in the first figure, where we see the axis AB, with its upper pole A, tilting toward the Sun. But in the third figure, that same axis, having kept the same direction toward the highest sphere by staying parallel to itself, no longer tilts toward the Sun with its upper pole A, but on the contrary has swung 47 degrees away from its first state and tilts toward the opposite side. So, to restore the same tilt of that pole A toward the Sun, we'd have to carry the terrestrial globe around the circumference ACBD, shifting it those same 47 degrees toward E; and by that many degrees any fixed star at all, observed on the meridian, would appear to have risen or sunk.

Now let's come to the explanation of what remains, and consider the Earth set in the fourth figure, that is, with its center at the first point of Libra, so that the Sun will appear at the beginning of Aries. In the first figure the Earth's axis is understood to lean over the diameter Capricorn–Cancer, and so to lie in the very plane that, cutting the plane of the great orb along the line Capricorn–Cancer, stands upright and perpendicular to it. Carried into the fourth figure, and kept, as we've always said, parallel to itself, it comes to lie in a plane that is likewise upright to the surface of the great orb and parallel to the plane that cuts that surface at right angles along the diameter Capricorn–Cancer. And so the line running from the Sun's center to the Earth's center — which is the line O–Libra — will be perpendicular to the axis BA. But the same line from the Sun's center to the Earth's center is always perpendicular to the boundary circle of the light as well; therefore this same circle will pass through the poles A and B in the fourth figure, and the axis AB will lie in its plane. But a great circle passing through the poles of the parallels divides them all into equal parts; therefore the arcs IK, EF, CD, GN, and LM will all be semicircles, and the lit hemisphere will be the one facing us and the Sun, and the boundary of the light will be that same circle ACBD. With the Earth in this place, it makes an equinox for all its inhabitants. And the same happens in the second figure, where the Earth, having its lit hemisphere toward the Sun, shows us the other, dark one with its nighttime arcs, which are likewise all semicircles; and so here too there is an equinox. And finally, since the line drawn from the Sun's center to the Earth's center is perpendicular to the axis AB — to which the greatest of the parallel circles, CD, is likewise upright — that same line O–Libra will necessarily pass through the very plane of the parallel CD, cutting its circumference at the midpoint of the daytime arc CD; and so the Sun will be directly overhead for whoever is found at that point of cutting. But carried by the Earth's daily turning, all the inhabitants of that parallel pass through it; therefore all of them, on that day, will have the noon Sun over their overhead point, while meanwhile, to everyone living on the Earth, the Sun will appear to trace the greatest parallel, called the equinoctial. Furthermore, since with the Earth at either solstice point one of the polar circles IK, LM stays wholly in the light and the other wholly in the dark, but when the Earth is at the equinox points half of each of those polar circles is found in the light and the rest in the dark, it shouldn't be hard to understand how, as the Earth passes, say, from Cancer (where the parallel IK is wholly in the dark) into Leo, a part of the parallel IK toward the point I begins to enter the light, and the boundary of the light IM begins to draw back toward the poles A and B, cutting the circle ACBD no longer at I and M, but at two other points falling between the ends I, A and M, B of the arcs IA and MB — so that the inhabitants of the circle IK begin to enjoy daylight, and those of the circle LM begin to feel the night. And there you have it: with two utterly simple motions, taking place in times proportioned to their sizes and not contrary to one another — indeed made, like all the other motions of the moving bodies of the world, from west to east — assigned to the terrestrial globe, we have given adequate reasons for all those same appearances. To save them by keeping the Earth still, one is forced instead — giving up that symmetry we see between the speeds and the sizes of the moving bodies — to grant one vast sphere, above all the others, an incomprehensible speed, while the other, smaller spheres move very slowly; and further to make that motion run contrary to theirs; and, to pile improbability on improbability, to make all the lower spheres be swept along, against their own tendency, by that topmost one. Here I leave it to your own judgment to decide which is the more likely.

Sagredo: For my part, as far as my own understanding goes, I see no small difference between the simplicity and ease of producing these effects by the means assigned in this new arrangement, and the multiplicity, confusion, and difficulty found in the old, commonly received one. For if the universe were ordered according to that multiplicity, we'd have to strike out of philosophy many axioms commonly accepted by all philosophers: that nature does not multiply things without necessity; that she uses the easiest and simplest means in producing her effects; that she does nothing in vain; and others like them. I confess I've never heard anything more admirable than this, nor can I believe that the human intellect has ever penetrated a subtler line of thought. I don't know how it strikes Signor Simplicio.

Simplicio: These, if I'm to speak my mind freely, seem to me some of those geometric subtleties that Aristotle faults Plato for, when he charges him with drifting away from sound philosophizing through too much study of geometry. And I've known and heard the very greatest Peripatetic philosophers advise their pupils against the study of mathematics, on the grounds that it makes the mind quibbling and unfit for good philosophizing — a program diametrically opposed to Plato's, who would admit no one to philosophy who had not first mastered geometry.

Salviati: I applaud these Peripatetics of yours for their advice to steer their pupils away from the study of geometry, since there's no art better suited for exposing their fallacies. But notice how different they are from the mathematical philosophers, who much prefer dealing with people well versed in the common Peripatetic philosophy than with those who lack that knowledge — for lacking it, they can't compare one doctrine with another. But setting that aside, tell me, please: what extravagances, or overstrained subtleties, make this Copernican arrangement less applaudable to you?

Simplicio: To be honest, I haven't fully grasped it — perhaps because I don't have ready to hand the explanations Ptolemy gives for these same effects: I mean the stations and retrogradations of the planets, their drawing nearer and moving farther off, the lengthening and shortening of the days, the changing of the seasons, and so on. But setting aside the consequences that follow from the first assumptions, I feel real difficulties — and no small ones — in those assumptions themselves; and when the assumptions are knocked down, they bring the whole edifice crashing down with them. Now, since the entire Copernican machine seems to me to rest on shaky foundations — for it leans on the mobility of the Earth — once that is taken away, there's no need to go on to any further disputes. And to take it away, Aristotle's axiom seems to me more than sufficient: that of a simple body only a single simple motion can be natural. Yet here the Earth, a simple body, is assigned three motions — if not four — and very different ones at that. For besides the straight motion downward toward the center, which, being heavy, it can't be denied, they credit it with a circular motion in a great circle around the Sun once a year, and with a spin on its own axis every twenty-four hours; and then — the most outlandish of all, and perhaps that's why you passed over it in silence — yet another turning about its own center, opposite to the first, the twenty-four-hour one, and completed once a year. To this my mind feels the very greatest resistance.

Salviati: As for the downward motion, we've already settled that it belongs in no way to the terrestrial globe as a whole, which has never moved with such a motion and never will; it belongs (if it belongs to anything) to the parts, as they rejoin their whole. As for the annual and the daily motions, since these both run the same way, they're perfectly compatible — just as, if we let a ball roll down a sloping surface, it will, in coming down of its own accord, also turn on itself. And now for the third motion Copernicus assigns to it, the yearly turn on its own axis whose sole purpose is to keep its axis tilted and pointing toward the same region of the firmament — here I'll tell you something well worth the greatest consideration. Namely, that so far from there being any resistance or difficulty in it (even though it runs contrary to the other yearly motion), this turn belongs, most naturally and without any moving cause at all, to any body whatsoever that is suspended and balanced: for if such a body is carried around the circumference of a circle, it immediately, and all by itself, takes on a turning about its own center, contrary to the one that carries it around, and of just such a speed that both complete one revolution in precisely the same time. You can see this remarkable experiment — and how neatly it fits our case — by putting a ball to float in a basin of water and holding the basin in your hands: if you turn yourself around on the soles of your feet, you'll see the ball at once begin to turn on itself with a motion contrary to the basin's, and finish its revolution just when the basin finishes its own. Now, what is the Earth but a globe hung and balanced in thin, yielding air, which, carried around once a year along the circumference of a great circle, must surely take on — with no other mover — a yearly turning about its own center, contrary to that other, likewise yearly, motion? You'll see this effect. But if you then look more carefully, you'll realize it isn't a real thing at all, but a mere appearance; and what looks to you like a turning-on-itself is in fact a not-moving, a keeping of the whole utterly unchanged with respect to everything outside you and the basin that stays still. For if you mark some spot on that ball and note which part of the wall of the room you're in — or of the countryside, or of the sky — it faces, you'll see that spot, as the basin and you turn, keep facing that same part the whole time; but compared with the basin and with yourself, which are the ones moving, it will certainly appear to change direction, and with a motion contrary to yours and the basin's it will seem to seek out every point along the basin's circuit. So that it's truer to say that you and the basin turn around the motionless ball than that the ball turns inside the basin. In just this way the Earth, hung and balanced on the circumference of the great orb, and set so that one of its marks — say, its north pole — faces toward some star or other region of the firmament, keeps itself always pointed toward that same region, even as it's carried by the annual motion around the circumference of that great orb. This alone is enough to make the wonder cease and to remove every difficulty. But what will Simplicio say if, to this needing-no-cooperating-cause, we add a marvelous power built into the terrestrial globe itself — a power of facing, with certain fixed parts of it, toward certain fixed parts of the firmament? I'm speaking of the magnetic power, shared out most constantly by every last piece of lodestone. And if every tiniest particle of that stone has such a power in it, who could doubt that the same power resides, on a far grander scale, in this whole earthly globe, which abounds in such material — and that perhaps the globe itself, in its inner and primary substance, is nothing but one immense mass of lodestone?

Simplicio: So you're one of those who subscribe to the magnetic philosophy of William Gilbert?

Salviati: I certainly am, and I believe I have as my companions everyone who has read his book with attention and checked his experiments for themselves. Nor would I give up hope that what happened to me in this matter might happen to you as well — provided a curiosity like mine, and a recognition that endless things in nature remain unknown to human minds, were to free you from bondage to this or that particular writer on natural things, loosen the reins on your reasoning, and soften the stubbornness and reluctance of your judgment, so that it wouldn't refuse, now and then, to lend an ear to voices it had never heard before. But — if I may use the word — the faint-heartedness of ordinary minds has reached such a point that they not only blindly give away, indeed pay as tribute, their own assent to everything they find written by those authors whom their teachers held up to them in the first infancy of their studies, but they refuse even to listen to — let alone examine — any new proposition or problem whatsoever, though it has not only never been refuted but never so much as examined or considered by their authors. One such question is precisely this: to investigate what is the true, proper, primary, inner, and general matter and substance of this earthly globe of ours. And although it never crossed the mind of Aristotle, or of anyone before Gilbert, to ask whether it might be lodestone — much less did Aristotle or anyone else ever refute such an opinion — nonetheless I've run into plenty of people who, at the very first mention of it, like a horse shying at a shadow, have shrunk back and dodged discussing it, dismissing the idea as an empty fantasy, indeed as sheer madness. And perhaps Gilbert's book would never have come into my hands if a Peripatetic philosopher of great reputation hadn't made me a present of it — to keep his own library safe from the contagion, I imagine.

Simplicio: I, who freely admit I've been one of these ordinary minds — and only in these last few days, since I was granted leave to take part in your discussions, do I recognize that I've stepped a little off the well-worn, popular roads — still don't feel myself so far raised up that the roughness of this new and fantastic opinion doesn't strike me as very hard and difficult to get over.

Salviati: If what Gilbert writes is true, then it isn't an opinion but a subject of science; it isn't something new but as old as the Earth itself; and (being true) it can't be harsh or hard, but smooth and perfectly easy. And I, whenever you please, will let you feel with your own hands how you frighten yourself with your own shadow, recoiling in horror from something that holds nothing dreadful in it at all — like a small child afraid of the witches' sabbath, knowing nothing of it but the name, which, apart from the name, is nothing.

Simplicio: I'll be glad to be enlightened and drawn out of my error.

Salviati: Then answer the questions I'll put to you. And first, tell me whether you believe that this globe of ours, which we live on and call Earth, consists of one single, simple material, or is rather a mixture of materials different from one another.

Simplicio: I see it as made up of very different substances and bodies. First of all, as to its largest components, I see water and earth, utterly unlike each other.

Salviati: Let's set the seas and the other waters aside for now and consider the solid parts. Tell me: do they seem to you all one and the same thing, or rather different things?

Simplicio: As to appearance, I see them as different. There are vast plains of barren sand, and others of rich, fruitful soil; there are countless mountains, sterile and rugged, full of hard rocks and stones of the most varied kinds — porphyry, alabaster, jasper, and a thousand thousand other sorts of marble; there are the enormous mines of metals of so many species; and, in short, so many different materials that a whole day wouldn't be enough merely to count them.

Salviati: Now, of all these different materials, do you believe that equal portions of each go into making up this great mass? Or that among them all there's one part that far surpasses the rest and is, as it were, the chief matter and substance of the vast bulk?

Simplicio: I believe the stones, marbles, metals, gems, and all the other varied materials are really like jewels and outward, surface ornaments of the primary globe, which in bulk I think immeasurably exceeds all these other things.

Salviati: And this chief, vast bulk — of which the things you've named are, so to speak, mere outgrowths and ornaments — what material do you believe it's made of?

Simplicio: I think it's the simple, or less impure, element of earth.

Salviati: But what do you mean by earth? Perhaps this stuff that's spread over the fields, that's broken up with spades and plows, where the grain is sown and the fruit trees planted, where great forests spring up of their own accord, and which, in short, is the dwelling place of all the animals and the womb of all the plants?

Simplicio: That, I'd say, is the primary substance of this globe of ours.

Salviati: Now that doesn't seem to me well said. For this earth that's broken up, sown, and made fruitful is one part — and a very thin one — of the globe's surface, going down only a short way compared with the distance to the center. And experience shows us that you don't dig far down before you come upon materials quite different from this outer rind: firmer ones, no good for growing plants. Besides, the innermost parts, pressed as they are by the enormous weights bearing down on them from above, must in all likelihood be packed together and as hard as the hardest crag. Add to this that fruitfulness would have been given in vain to materials that were never going to bear fruit anyway, but only to lie buried forever in the deep and gloomy abysses of the Earth.

Simplicio: And who assures us that the innermost parts, near the center, are barren? Perhaps they too have their own products — things unknown to us.

Salviati: You, as well as anyone, could be certain of this — since you can readily grasp that if the integral bodies of the universe are produced solely for the benefit of the human race, then this one above all the rest must be destined for the sole conveniences of us who live on it. But what benefit could we ever draw from materials so hidden and remote from us that we'll never be able to make any use of them? So the inner substance of this globe of ours can't be a crumbling, loose, incoherent material like this surface stuff we call earth; it has to be a body extremely dense and solid — in short, a rock of the hardest kind. And if it must be such, what reason should make you any more reluctant to believe it's lodestone than porphyry, or jasper, or some other hard marble? Perhaps if Gilbert had written that this globe is made inside of grey freestone, or of chalcedony, the paradox would have struck you as less outrageous?

Simplicio: That the inner parts of this globe are more compressed, and therefore more packed and solid — and more and more so the deeper down they lie — I grant, and so does Aristotle. But that they degenerate, and are anything other than earth of the same sort as this surface earth, I hear nothing that forces me to grant.

Salviati: I haven't taken up this discussion in order to prove to you demonstratively that the primary and real substance of this globe of ours is lodestone, but only to show you that there's no reason to be any more reluctant to grant that it's lodestone than that it's some other material. And you, if you'll think it over carefully, will find it not at all improbable that a single, bare, arbitrary name has moved people to believe it's made of earth. And that name is this: from the start we've commonly used the word earth to mean both the material that's plowed and sown and to name this whole globe of ours. If its name had instead been taken from stone — as it could just as well have been, no less than from earth — then to say that its primary substance was stone would surely have met with no reluctance or contradiction from anyone. And this is all the more likely because I hold it for certain that, if you could strip the bark off this great globe — peeling away a layer a thousand or two thousand arm-lengths thick — and then separate the stones from the earth, the heap of rock would be far, far greater than the heap of fertile soil. As for the arguments that would prove conclusively, in fact, that this globe of ours is lodestone, I've offered you none, nor is this the time to offer them — all the more since at your leisure you can find them in Gilbert. Only, to fire you up to read him, I'll lay out for you with a little comparison of my own the method he follows in his philosophizing. I know you're perfectly aware how much the knowledge of a thing's properties contributes to the investigation of its substance and essence. So I want you to take pains to inform yourself well of the many properties that are found in lodestone alone, and in no other stone or body: as, for instance, its attracting iron; its conferring on iron, merely by its presence, that same power; and likewise its imparting to iron the property of facing toward the poles, just as it holds that property in itself. And beyond this, see for yourself, by trial, how there resides in it the power to give the magnetic needle not only the settling of itself along a meridian toward the poles with a horizontal motion (a property known long ago), but a newly observed property of dipping — for when it's balanced beneath a meridian marked out on a little sphere of lodestone, it dips, I say, to definite degrees, more or less, according as the needle is held nearer to or farther from the pole; until, right over the pole, it plants itself upright and perpendicular, whereas over the middle parts it lies parallel to the axis. What's more, take pains to test how, since the power of attracting iron is far more vigorous toward the poles than around the middle parts, that force is markedly stronger in one pole than in the other — and this in every piece of lodestone, the stronger pole of which is the one that faces south. Note next that in a small lodestone this southern pole, the more powerful of the two, becomes the weaker whenever it has to hold up iron in the presence of the north pole of another, much larger lodestone. And, not to make a long speech of it, satisfy yourself by experiment of these and many other properties described by Gilbert, all of which so belong to lodestone alone that not one of them attaches to any other material. Now tell me, Simplicio: suppose you were offered a thousand pieces of different materials, each of them covered and wrapped in a cloth that hid it, and you were asked to guess, without uncovering them, the material of each from outward signs; and suppose that, in trying, you came upon one that plainly showed all the properties you already knew reside in lodestone and in no other material — what would you judge the essence of that body to be? Would you say it might be a piece of ebony, or alabaster, or tin?

Simplicio: I'd say, without the slightest hesitation, that it was a piece of lodestone.

Salviati: Then say so outright: under this covering and rind of earth, of stones, of metals, of water, and so on, there hides a great lodestone — since, for anyone who takes the trouble to observe them, all those very same properties are found in it that are seen to belong to a genuine, bare globe of lodestone. Why, if you saw nothing but that business of the dipping needle, which, carried around the Earth, tips more and more as it nears the north pole and dips less toward the equinoctial, under which it finally comes into balance — that alone ought to persuade the most stubborn judgment. I say nothing of that other marvelous effect plainly seen in every piece of lodestone: that for us, dwellers in the northern hemisphere, the southern pole of the lodestone is the stronger of the two, and the difference shows up the greater the farther one moves from the equinoctial; while under the equinoctial both parts are of equal force, but notably weaker; and in the southern regions, far from the equinoctial, nature switches, and the part that was the weaker for us gains the upper hand over the other. And all this squares with what we see a little piece of lodestone do in the presence of a large one, whose power, prevailing over the smaller, makes it obey; and depending on whether it's held on this or that side of the big stone's equinoctial, it undergoes the very same changes I've described every lodestone undergoing when carried this side or that of the Earth's equinoctial.

Sagredo: I was persuaded at my first reading of Gilbert's book; and, having come across a piece of the most excellent lodestone, I spent a long time making many observations, all of them worthy of the utmost wonder. But above all I find astounding the one about increasing its power to hold up iron so enormously by arming it in the way that same author teaches. By arming that piece of mine, I multiplied its force eightfold: whereas unarmed it could barely hold up nine ounces of iron, armed it held more than six pounds. And perhaps you'll have seen this very piece in the Gallery of your Most Serene Grand Duke (to whom I gave it up), holding up two little iron anchors.

Salviati: I saw it many times, and with great wonder — until an even greater astonishment was handed to me by a small piece that our friend the Academician has in his keeping. It weighs no more than six ounces, and unarmed it barely holds up two ounces; but armed — fitted with an iron cap — it holds up a hundred and sixty, so that it comes to support eighty times more armed than unarmed, and to bear a weight twenty-six times greater than its own. That is a marvel far greater than any Gilbert had been able to meet with, for he writes that he could never find a lodestone able to hold up four times its own weight.

Sagredo: This stone seems to me to open up a vast field for the human mind to philosophize in. I have turned it over in my own thoughts a thousand times — how can it be that it gives to the iron that arms it a force so much greater than its own? — and in the end I find nothing that sets me at rest; nor do I get much sense out of what Gilbert writes on this particular point. I don't know whether the same happens to you.

Salviati: I praise, admire, and envy this author to the highest degree, for having had so stupendous a conception fall into his mind about a thing that countless sublime intellects had handled, and none had noticed. He seems to me worthy of the greatest praise, too, for the many new and true observations he made — to the shame of so many lying and empty authors who write not only what they know, but everything they hear from the foolish crowd, without troubling to make sure of it by experiment, perhaps so as not to shrink their books. What I would have wished in Gilbert is that he had been a somewhat greater mathematician, and in particular well grounded in geometry; for the practice of it would have made him less ready to accept as conclusive demonstrations the reasons he offers as the true causes of the true conclusions he himself observed. Those reasons (speaking freely) do not knot and bind with the force that must belong, beyond all doubt, to reasons drawn from natural, necessary, and eternal conclusions. And I don't doubt that as time goes on this new science will be perfected, with further new observations, and still more with true and necessary demonstrations. Yet that should take nothing from the glory of the first observer. I esteem no less — indeed I admire far more — the first inventor of the lyre (even though one must suppose the instrument was very crudely built, and more crudely played) than the hundred other artists who in later centuries brought that craft to great refinement. And it seems to me quite reasonable that antiquity numbered the first inventors of the noble arts among the gods, since we see that the common run of human minds has so little curiosity, and cares so little for rare and refined things, that even when they see and hear them exquisitely practiced by masters, they are not moved to want to learn them. Now think whether brains of that sort would ever have set themselves to investigate the making of the lyre, or the invention of music, enticed by the whistling of the dry sinews of a tortoise, or the strokes of four hammers. To fasten upon great inventions, spurred by the tiniest beginnings, and to judge that beneath a first, childish appearance marvelous arts may lie hidden — that is not for ordinary minds; those are the conceptions and thoughts of superhuman spirits. Now, to answer your question: I say that I too have long thought about what the cause might be of this tenacious and powerful joining that we see form between the one iron that arms the lodestone and the other that clings to it. And first I made sure that the virtue and force of the stone is not increased in the least by being armed, since it neither attracts from any greater distance, nor holds up any more firmly an iron between which and the armature there is placed a very thin paper — down to a single leaf of beaten gold. On the contrary, with such a thing between, it holds more iron bare than armed. So there is no change in the virtue, and yet there is a new result in the effect; and since a new effect must have a new cause, I looked for what novelty is introduced in the act of holding with the armature, and could find no other change than in the different touching: for where before iron touched lodestone, now iron touches iron. So one must necessarily conclude that the different touchings are the cause of the difference in the effects. And this difference between the contacts, I can see no way for it to come from anything other than this: that the substance of iron is of parts finer, purer, and more tightly packed than those of the lodestone, which are coarser, less pure, and looser. From which it follows that the surfaces of the two irons that are to touch, so long as they are exquisitely flattened, polished, and burnished, join together so exactly that all the infinite points of the one meet the infinite points of the other, so that the filaments (so to speak) linking the two irons are far more numerous than those linking lodestone to iron — because the substance of the lodestone is more porous and less pure, which means that not all the points and filaments of the iron's surface find, in the surface of the lodestone, counterparts to unite with. And that the substance of iron (especially the well-purified kind, like the finest steel) is of parts far denser, finer, and purer than the matter of the lodestone, is shown by the fact that its edge can be brought to an extreme thinness, like the edge of a razor — something the edge of a piece of lodestone could never come near. The impurity of the lodestone, and its being mixed with other kinds of stone, is first seen plainly in the color of certain little specks, mostly whitish; and then in this: present to it a needle hanging from a thread, and over such little stones the needle cannot settle, but, drawn by the parts surrounding them, seems to flee them and jump onto the lodestone next to them. And since some of these foreign parts are, by their size, quite visible, we may believe that others in great abundance — too small to be seen — are scattered through the whole mass. What I say (namely, that the multitude of touchings made between iron and iron is the cause of so firm a joining) is confirmed by an experiment. It is this: if we present the sharp point of a needle to the armature of the lodestone, it will attach no more strongly than to the same lodestone bare — which can come from nothing but the two touchings being equal, that is, both being of a single point. But there's more. Take a needle and lay it on the lodestone so that one of its ends sticks out a little, and present a nail to that end: the needle will at once attach to it, so that as you draw the nail back, the needle is left hanging, held by its two ends to the lodestone and to the iron; and drawing the nail still further will pull the needle off the lodestone — provided the eye of the needle is the end joined to the nail, and the point is the end on the lodestone. But if the eye is toward the lodestone, then when you take the nail away, the needle stays stuck to the lodestone. And this (in my judgment) for no other reason than that, the needle being thicker toward the eye, it touches at many more points than the very sharp point does.

Sagredo: The whole argument has seemed to me very conclusive, and these experiments with the needle make it little inferior to a mathematical demonstration. And I frankly confess that in all of magnetic philosophy I have neither heard nor read anything else that accounts, with like force, for any other of its many marvelous behaviors — of which, if we had the causes explained with such clarity, I don't know what sweeter food our intellect could desire.

Salviati: In investigating the reasons for conclusions unknown to us, one must have the luck to steer the argument, from the very start, onto the road of truth. When a man sets out along that road, it readily happens that he meets one true proposition after another — known to be true whether by reasoning or by experience — and from their certainty the truth of his own gains force and evidence. That is exactly what happened to me with the present problem. Wishing to make sure, by some further check, whether the reason I had worked out was true — namely, that the substance of the lodestone is really far less continuous than that of iron or steel — I had one face of that very piece of lodestone that was once yours flattened by the craftsmen who work in the Gallery of the Grand Duke, my lord, and then polished and burnished as much as possible. And there, to my satisfaction, I touched with my hand what I was after. For there came to light many specks of a color different from the rest, but as bright and lustrous as any dense hard stone; the rest of the field was smooth, but only to the touch, being not at all shiny — rather, as if clouded by a haze. That dull part was the substance of the lodestone; the bright part, of other stones mixed into it — as one could plainly tell by bringing the flattened face over iron filings, which in great abundance jumped to the lodestone, but not a single grain to those specks. And the specks were many: some as large as a quarter of a fingernail, others somewhat smaller, very many quite small, and the barely visible ones almost past counting. So I was assured that my idea had been perfectly true, when I first judged that the substance of the lodestone must be not solid and closed, but porous — or better, spongy — yet with this difference: where a sponge holds air or water in its cavities and cells, the lodestone has its own filled with very hard, heavy stone, as the exquisite luster they take on shows us. And so, as I said at the start, when you apply the surface of the iron to the surface of the lodestone, the least particles of the iron — though more continuous, perhaps, than those of any other body (as its taking a finer polish than any other material shows) — meet pure lodestone not all of them, but few; and the contacts being few, the attachment is weak. But because the armature of the lodestone, besides touching a great part of the stone's surface, also clothes itself in the virtue of the neighboring parts even where they are not touched, then — that face of it being exactly flattened, and the matching face of the iron to be held being likewise well flattened — the touching is made of countless least particles, if not perhaps of the infinite points of both surfaces, so that the attachment turns out extremely strong. This point, of flattening the surfaces of the irons that are to touch, Gilbert did not notice; on the contrary, he makes his irons rounded, so that their contact is small, and the result is that far weaker is the grip with which those irons hold together.

Sagredo: As I said just now, the reason you have assigned leaves me hardly less satisfied than if it were a pure geometric demonstration; and since this is a physical problem, I judge that Simplicio too will find himself satisfied, so far as natural science allows — for he knows that in it one must not look for geometric proof.

Simplicio: It truly seems to me that Salviati, with a fine roundabout of words, has explained the cause of this effect so clearly that any middling mind, even one that is no scientist, could take it in. But we, keeping within the terms of the discipline, trace the cause of these and similar natural effects to sympathy, which is a certain agreement and mutual appetite arising between things that are alike in quality — just as, on the other hand, that hatred and enmity by which other things naturally flee one another and hold each other in horror, we call antipathy.

Sagredo: And so, with these two names, one manages to account for a great number of the happenings and effects that we see, not without wonder, produced in nature. But this way of philosophizing seems to me to have great sympathy with a certain manner of painting a friend of mine used to practice. On the canvas he would write in chalk: "Here I want the fountain to be, with Diana and her nymphs; here, some greyhounds; in this corner I want a hunter, with the head of a stag; the rest, countryside, wood, and little hills." The rest he then left to the painter to fill in with colors — and so he convinced himself that he had himself painted the tale of Actaeon, having put in nothing of his own but the names. But where have we brought ourselves, with so long a digression, against the rules we had already laid down? I've all but forgotten what the matter was that we were treating when we strayed into this magnetic talk — and yet I did have some point or other in mind to make about it.

Salviati: We were on the verge of demonstrating that the third motion Copernicus assigns to the Earth is not really a motion at all, but a rest — a keeping of itself unchangeably aimed, with its fixed parts, toward the same fixed parts of the universe; that is, a perpetual keeping of the axis of its daily rotation parallel to itself and turned toward certain fixed stars. This most constant state, we were saying, belongs naturally to every body balanced and suspended in a fluid, yielding medium, which, though carried around, does not change its direction with respect to external things, but only seems to turn upon itself with respect to whatever is carrying it, and to the vessel in which it is carried. Then, to this simple and natural behavior, we added the magnetic virtue, by which the terrestrial globe could all the more firmly hold itself unchangeable, and so on.

Sagredo: Now it all comes back to me. What was passing through my mind then, and what I wanted to bring out, was a certain thought about the difficulty and objection Simplicio had raised against the Earth's mobility — the one drawn from the multiplicity of motions, which cannot be attributed to a simple body, since, in Aristotle's teaching, only one single, simple motion can be natural to it. And what I wanted to put forward was precisely the lodestone, to which we plainly see three motions naturally belong. The first, toward the center of the Earth, as a heavy body. The second is the horizontal circular motion by which it restores and keeps its axis toward fixed parts of the universe. The third is this one, newly discovered by Gilbert: the tilting of its axis — lying in the plane of a meridian — toward the surface of the Earth, and this more or less according to how far it is from the equinoctial, beneath which its axis stays parallel to the Earth's axis. Besides these three, it is perhaps not unlikely that it may have a fourth: turning about its own axis, whenever it were balanced and suspended in air or some other fluid, yielding medium, so that all external and accidental hindrances were taken away — and to this thought Gilbert himself seems to give his applause. So you see, Simplicio, how shaky Aristotle's axiom is left.

Simplicio: This not only fails to wound the maxim, it isn't even aimed its way — since he speaks of a simple body and of what may naturally belong to it, while you bring up what happens to a mixed one. Nor are you saying anything new within Aristotle's teaching, since he too grants a compound motion to mixed bodies, and so on.

Sagredo: Hold on a moment, Simplicio, and answer the questions I'll put to you. You say the lodestone is not a simple body, but a mixed one: now I ask you, which are the simple bodies that mix together to compose the lodestone?

Simplicio: I couldn't tell you the ingredients, nor the exact proportions, but it's enough that they are elementary bodies.

Sagredo: That's enough for me as well. And of these simple, elementary bodies, which are their natural motions?

Simplicio: They are the two simple straight ones, sursum et deorsum.

Sagredo: Tell me next: do you believe that the motion which will remain natural to such a mixed body must be one that can result from compounding the two simple natural motions of the simple component bodies — or that it might also be a motion impossible to compound out of those?

Simplicio: I believe it will move with the motion resulting from the compounding of the motions of the simple component bodies, and that it is impossible for it to move with a motion that cannot be compounded from these.

Sagredo: But, Simplicio, with two simple straight motions you will never compound a circular motion — and circular are the two or three different motions the lodestone has. See, then, into what tight corners ill-founded principles lead us — or, better put, badly drawn conclusions from good principles. For now you are forced to say that the lodestone is a mixture composed of elementary and of celestial substances, if you want to maintain that straight motion belongs to the elements alone, and circular motion to the celestial bodies. So, if you want to philosophize more safely, say instead that of the integral bodies of the universe, those that are by nature mobile all move in circles, and that the lodestone, therefore, being part of the true, primary, integral substance of our globe, keeps that same nature. And notice this fallacy of yours: you call the lodestone a mixed body, and the terrestrial globe a simple one — when the globe is plainly a hundred thousand times more composite, since, besides containing a thousand and one materials utterly different from one another, it contains a great quantity of this very thing you call mixed, I mean the lodestone. This seems to me the same as if someone called bread a mixed body, and called simple that olla podrida — that Spanish hodgepodge — into which there also goes no small amount of bread, along with a hundred different sundries. A marvelous thing indeed seems to me, among others, this business of the Peripatetics. They grant (nor can they deny it) that our terrestrial globe is in fact a compound of endless different materials; they grant, next, that the motion of compound bodies must be compound; the motions that can be compounded are the straight and the circular, since the two straight ones, being contrary, are incompatible with each other; they affirm that the pure element of earth is nowhere to be found; they confess that it has never moved with any local motion. And then they want to place in nature that body which is not to be found, and make it move with a motion it has never performed and never will; while to the body that is, and always has been, they deny the very motion they first granted must naturally belong to it.

Salviati: Please, Sagredo, let's not wear ourselves out any further over these particulars — the more so since you know our aim has never been to settle firmly, or to accept as true, this or that opinion, but only to lay out, for our own enjoyment, the reasons and answers that can be brought forward on either side. And Simplicio gives this reply to ransom his Peripatetics. So let's leave the verdict hanging, and the decision in the hands of those who know more than we do. And since it seems to me that we've discoursed at quite enough length, over these three days, about the system of the universe, it will be time now to come to the greatest matter of all, the one that first set our conversations going: I mean the ebb and flow of the sea, whose cause, it seems, may quite probably be traced to the motions of the Earth. But that, if you please, we'll save for the following day. Meanwhile, so as not to let it slip my mind, let me tell you a certain point on which I would not have wished Gilbert to lend an ear — I mean his allowing that if a little sphere of lodestone could be perfectly balanced, it would spin upon itself; for there's no reason why it should. For if the whole terrestrial globe has it by nature to turn about its own center in twenty-four hours, and all its parts must do the same — I mean, turn, together with their whole, about the center of it in twenty-four hours — then they already do so, in effect, while, resting upon the Earth, they go around together with it. To assign them a rotation about their own center would be to give them a second motion, quite different from the first, so that they'd then have two: the turning about their whole's center in twenty-four hours, and the turning about their own. Now this second one is arbitrary, and there is no reason to bring it in. If, when a piece of lodestone is detached from the whole natural mass, it were robbed of following that mass — as it did while joined to it — so that it were left deprived of turning about the universal center of the terrestrial globe, then one might, perhaps, with somewhat more probability, believe that it would take on a new spin about its own particular center. But if it — no less when separated than when joined — still keeps up its first, eternal, natural course, why want to saddle another new one onto it?

Sagredo: I follow you perfectly, and it reminds me of an argument very much like this one in its emptiness, put forward by certain writers of textbooks on the sphere — among them, if I remember rightly, Sacrobosco. To prove that the element of water shapes itself, together with the Earth, into a spherical surface, so that the two of them make up this globe of ours, he writes that we have conclusive proof of it in seeing the tiniest particles of water take on a round shape — as we can see every day in droplets of dew, and on the leaves of many plants. And since, by the well-worn axiom "The same reasoning holds for the whole as for the parts," the parts crave that shape, it must be that the same shape belongs to the whole element. And really it seems to me quite an unseemly thing that men like this don't notice a bit of foolishness that's all too plain to see: they fail to consider that, if their reasoning were sound, it wouldn't just be the tiny drops, but any larger quantity of water whatsoever, once separated from the whole element, that would draw itself up into a ball — which is not at all what we see. What we can see with our senses, and grasp with our minds, is this: the element of water, loving to shape itself into a sphere around the common center of gravity toward which all heavy bodies tend (which is the center of the terrestrial globe), is in this followed by all its parts, just as the axiom says; so that all the surfaces of the seas, of the lakes, of the ponds — in short, of all the bodies of water held in their basins — spread themselves out in a spherical figure, but a sphere whose center is the center of the terrestrial globe, and they do not make little spheres of their own.

Salviati: The error really is childish, and if it were nobody's but Sacrobosco's, I'd forgive it him readily; but having to pardon it in his commentators too, and in other great men, and even in Ptolemy himself, I can't do it without some blush for their reputation. But it's time to take our leave, the hour being late by now — so that we can be here tomorrow, as usual, for the final conclusion of everything we've discussed.

Simplicio: Please, gentlemen, allow me to bring my mind back to some quiet — right now I find it much tossed about by a certain point Salviati has just touched on — so that afterward, once the waves have been smoothed, I can take in your speculations more distinctly; for images don't imprint themselves well on a rippling mirror, as the Latin poet gracefully put it:

. . . nuper me in littore vidi cum placidum ventis staret mare — I lately caught sight of myself at the shore, when the sea lay calm, stilled by the winds.

Salviati: You're quite right — so tell us your doubts.

Simplicio: A moment ago you dispatched, as equally dull-witted, both those who deny the Earth the daily rotation — because they don't find themselves carried off to Persia or Japan by it — and those who oppose the annual motion, out of the repugnance they feel at having to admit that the enormous, ponderous bulk of the terrestrial globe could lift itself up on high and then sink down low, as it would have to do while wheeling around the Sun with that motion. And I — not blushing to be counted among these fools — feel that same repugnance in my own head, at least as to this second point raised against the annual motion; all the more when I see how much resistance is put up against being moved even along a level surface, and I don't mean a mountain but a stone that's only a small chip off some alpine crag. So, not despising objections of this sort, I beg you to resolve them — and not only for my sake, but for others, to whom they seem utterly conclusive; because I find it very hard for anyone, however simple, to recognize and confess his own simplicity, moved by nothing more than hearing himself called simple.

Sagredo: On the contrary — the simpler he is, the more impossible it'll be to persuade him of his failing. And this gives me occasion to reflect that it's worth resolving this objection, and others of the same kind, not only to satisfy Simplicio but for another reason no less important: for you can see that there's no shortage of men, thoroughly versed in the common philosophy and in other sciences, who — for want of astronomy, or of mathematics, or of whatever other discipline it is that sharpens the mind for penetrating the truth — remain persuaded by discourses as empty as these. And so the plight of poor Copernicus seems to me to deserve our pity: he can't be sure that the judging of his doctrines won't happen to fall into the hands of people who, unable to grasp his most subtle reasonings (which are therefore hard to understand), but already persuaded by such empty appearances that they're false, go about preaching them as false and mistaken. So, when such people can't be brought to understand the more abstruse arguments, it's worth taking pains that they at least recognize the emptiness of these others — and from that recognition may come a more measured judgment and condemnation of the doctrine they now hold to be in error. Let me put forward, then, two more objections — but against the daily rotation — which not long ago I heard raised by persons of great learning; and afterward we'll come to the annual motion. The first was this: if it were true that the Sun and the other stars don't rise above the eastern horizon, but rather that the eastern part of the Earth drops down beneath them while they stay put, then within a few hours the mountains lying to the east, tilting downward through the turning of the terrestrial globe, would have to reach such a state that, where a little before you had to walk uphill to reach their ridge, you'd afterward have to go downhill to get up there. The other was this: if the daily motion belonged to the Earth, it would have to be so fast that a man standing at the bottom of a well could see a star directly overhead only for a single instant — able to see it only for that very brief time in which it crosses the 2 or 3 braccia of the Earth's circumference that make up the width of the well. Yet experience shows that the apparent passage of such a star, in crossing the well, takes up quite a long time; which is a necessary proof that the mouth of the well is not moving with anything like the fury the daily turning would require — and consequently that the Earth is standing still.

Simplicio: Of these two arguments, the second really does seem quite conclusive to me; but as for the first, I think I could get myself out of it on my own. For I consider that it comes to the same thing whether the terrestrial globe, turning on its own center, carries a mountain toward the east, or whether — the globe staying still — the mountain were torn up by the roots and dragged across the Earth; and carrying the mountain over the surface of the Earth is, so far as I can see, no different an operation from taking a ship across the surface of the sea. So that, whenever the mountain objection held good, it would follow just the same that, as the ship kept up its voyage and had drawn many degrees away from our ports, we'd have to climb its mast no longer by going up, but by moving along the flat and then even going down — which doesn't happen. Nor have I ever heard any sailor, even among those who've sailed clear around the globe, report any difference in that operation, or in any other task done aboard ship, on account of the vessel's being in one part of the world rather than another.

Salviati: You reason very well. And if it had ever crossed the mind of the author of that objection to consider that his neighboring mountain, set to his east, would — when the terrestrial globe turned — be carried in 2 hours by that motion to the place where, say, Mount Olympus or Mount Carmel now stands, he'd have realized that by his own way of arguing he was forced to believe and confess that, to reach the peak of those mountains, one must de facto go downhill. These are the sort of brains fit to deny the antipodes, on the grounds that you can't walk with your head hanging down and your feet stuck to the ceiling; men who, from true ideas — even ones they understand perfectly — still can't manage to draw the easiest solutions to their own doubts. I mean that they understand perfectly well that to gravitate and to descend is to tend toward the center of the terrestrial globe, and that to rise is to move away from it; and then they get lost when it comes to understanding that our antipodes have no trouble at all supporting themselves and walking, because they do exactly as we do — that is, they keep the soles of their feet toward the center of the Earth and their heads toward the sky.

Sagredo: And yet we know that men of lofty wit in other fields have been dazzled by matters like these; which all the more confirms what I was just saying — that it's good to clear away every objection, even the very weakest. So let's have an answer to those men of the well, too.

Salviati: This second argument does have, on the face of it, a certain something more than merely plausible; and yet I'm firmly convinced that if we could question the very man it occurred to — get him to explain himself better by stating exactly what the effect is that ought to follow, and that he thinks doesn't follow, once the daily turning is granted to be the Earth's — I believe, I say, that he'd get himself just as tangled up in laying out his difficulty and its consequences as he would in thinking his way out of it.

Simplicio: If I'm to tell the truth, I'm quite sure that's how it would go — because I too find myself, right now, in that same confusion. The argument seems to grip, at first apprehension; but on the other hand I see, as if through fog, that if the reasoning were carried through properly, that immense speed of travel we'd have to detect in the star if the motion belonged to the Earth would have to be detected in it just as much — indeed far more — if the motion belonged to the star itself, since it would have to be many thousands of times faster in the star than in the Earth. And on the other hand, having to lose sight of the star for the mere crossing of the well's mouth — which would be 2 or 3 braccia across, while the well, along with the Earth, travels no less than 2,000,000 in an hour — seems as though it would have to be something so momentary that it couldn't even be perceived; and yet from the bottom of that same well the star is seen for quite a long stretch of time. So I find myself wanting to be brought clear on this business.

Salviati: Now I'm confirmed all the more in believing the objection's author was confused, seeing that you too, Simplicio, only shadow forth — and don't really possess — what you're trying to say; which I gather chiefly from your leaving out a distinction that is a most crucial point in this whole matter. So tell me: in performing this experiment — this passage of a star over the mouth of the well, I mean — do you make any difference according to whether the well is deeper or shallower, that is, according to whether the observer is farther from the mouth or nearer to it? Because I haven't heard you make anything of that.

Simplicio: To tell the truth, I hadn't turned my thoughts to it; but your question wakes up my mind, and suggests to me that such a distinction must be absolutely necessary. And I'm already beginning to see that, in fixing the time of that passage, the depth of the well may perhaps make no less difference than its width.

Salviati: Indeed, I rather suspect the width has nothing to do with it — or next to nothing.

Simplicio: And yet it does seem to me that having to cross 10 braccia of width would take ten times as long as the passage of a single braccio; and I'm sure that a little boat 10 braccia long will pass across my line of sight sooner than a galley a hundred long.

Salviati: And still we go on clinging to that inveterate notion — that we don't move except as far as our own legs carry us. What you say, my dear Simplicio, is true when the object you see is the one moving while you stand still to watch it; but if you'll be down in the well when the well and you together are carried along by the Earth's turning, don't you see that neither in an hour, nor in a thousand, nor in all eternity will the mouth of the well pass you by? Whether the Earth is moving or not moving, whatever effect that has on you can't be detected in the mouth of the well, but only in some other object, separate from you, that doesn't share your same condition — of motion, I mean, or of rest.

Simplicio: That's all fine; but granting that I, down in the well, am carried along in company with it by the daily motion, and that the star I'm looking at is standing still — the opening of the well, which alone gives my sight a passage, being no more than three braccia out of the many millions of braccia of the rest of the Earth's surface that blocks my view — how can the time I see the star for be any perceptible fraction of the time it's hidden?

Salviati: And there you fall back into the same mix-up; you really do need someone to help you out of it. It isn't the width of the well, Simplicio, that measures how long the star is in view — for in that case you'd see it forever, since the mouth of the well gives your sight a passage forever. That measure has to be taken instead from the amount of motionless sky that stays visible to you through the opening of the well.

Simplicio: But what's uncovered to me of the sky — isn't it the same fraction of the whole celestial sphere as the mouth of the well is of the whole Earth?

Salviati: I'd like you to answer that for yourself. So tell me: is the mouth of the well always the same portion of the Earth's surface?

Simplicio: Beyond any doubt, always the same.

Salviati: And the part of the sky seen by the man down in the well — is that always the same fraction of the whole celestial sphere?

Simplicio: Now I'm beginning to clear the fog from my mind, and to understand what you hinted at a moment ago — that the depth of the well has a great deal to do with the present business. For there's no doubt that the farther the eye draws back from the mouth of the well, the smaller the part of the sky it will uncover; and that part, in consequence, will be crossed and lost from view all the sooner by the man gazing up at it from the depths of the well.

Salviati: But is there any place in the well from which one would uncover exactly that fraction of the celestial sphere which the mouth of the well is of the Earth's surface?

Simplicio: It seems to me that if the well were sunk all the way to the center of the Earth, then perhaps from there one would uncover a part of the sky that would stand to it as the well does to the Earth. But moving away from the center and rising toward the surface, one keeps uncovering an ever greater part of that sky.

Salviati: And finally, with the eye set in the plane of the well's mouth, one uncovers half the sky, or very little less — to cross which (supposing we were under the equinoctial) would take 12 hours of time.

The Fourth Day

Sagredo: I can't tell whether your return to our usual discussions really was later than usual, or whether my eagerness to hear Salviati's thoughts on so intriguing a subject only made it seem so. For a good hour I've been posted at the window, watching moment by moment for the gondola I'd sent to fetch you to come into view.

Salviati: I really do think it was your imagination, more than any lateness on our part, that stretched out the time. So as not to stretch it any further, we'd do well to come straight to the point, without more preamble, and show how nature has allowed — whether the matter in rei veritate, in actual fact, stands this way, or whether she is only playing, amusing herself at the expense of our fancies — has allowed, I say, that the very motions long ago assigned to the Earth for every purpose other than accounting for the ebb and flow of the sea should now turn out to serve that cause so exactly, and how, in return, that same ebb and flow shows up to confirm the Earth's mobility. Up to now the evidence for that mobility has been drawn from what appears in the heavens, since among the things that happen here on Earth not one had the power to settle the question either way — as we've examined at length, showing that all the terrestrial happenings by which people commonly hold the Earth to be at rest and the Sun and the firmament in motion must look exactly the same to us whether we grant the Earth its motion and those bodies their rest, or the reverse. The single element of water — being enormously vast, and not fastened and chained to the terrestrial globe the way all its solid parts are, but rather, by its fluidity, left in part its own master and free — is the one thing, among all things below the Moon, in which we can recognize some trace and sign of what the Earth is doing as to motion or rest. I, after examining over and over to myself the effects and occurrences observed in the movements of the waters — some seen, some learned from others — and after reading and hearing the great foolishness that many have put forward as the causes of such things, have felt myself drawn, and not slightly, to admit these two conclusions (granting, of course, the necessary premises): that if the terrestrial globe is motionless, the ebb and flow of the sea cannot come about naturally; and that when the same globe is granted the motions already assigned to it, the sea must be subject to an ebb and flow that matches everything observed in it.

Sagredo: That's a tremendous claim — both in itself and for everything that follows from it. All the more attentively, then, will I listen to your explanation and your proof of it.

Salviati: In questions about nature — and the one we have in hand is one of them — it's our knowledge of the effects that leads us to investigate and discover the causes. Without that knowledge, ours would be a blind man's walk — worse than blind, in fact, since a blind man at least knows where he wants to end up, while we wouldn't even know that. So before anything else we need a knowledge of the effects whose causes we're after. And of those effects, you, Sagredo, must be more fully and more reliably informed than I am. Besides having been born in Venice, and lived there a long time — where the tides are quite remarkable for their size — you've also sailed to Syria, and, being a lively and curious mind, you must have made many observations. Whereas I, who have only been able to observe for a while — and briefly at that — what happens here at this far end of the Adriatic gulf, and in our sea to the south, along the shores of the Tyrrhenian, must for many things rely on other people's reports. And those reports, being mostly not in good agreement, and so quite uncertain, can bring our speculations confusion rather than confirmation. Still, from the ones we can trust — which are also the main ones — I think I can arrive at the true and primary causes. I don't claim to be able to supply all the proper and adequate reasons for effects that might be new to me, ones I therefore couldn't have thought about beforehand. What I'm about to say I offer only as a key that opens the door to a road nobody has ever walked before, in the firm hope that minds more speculative than mine will push on and penetrate much further than I will in this first discovery of mine. And even though, in other seas remote from us, things may happen that don't happen in our Mediterranean, that won't keep the reason and cause I'll give from being true — provided it checks out and fully satisfies the things that occur in our own sea. Because in the end there has to be a single true and primary cause for effects that are of one and the same kind. So I'll tell the story of the effects I know to be real, and I'll assign the cause I believe to be the true one; and you, gentlemen, will add others known to you, beyond mine, and then we'll test whether the cause I've brought forward can satisfy those as well.

So then: there are three periods to be observed in the ebb and flow of sea water.
