The first and chief one is the great, well-known daily period, by which, at intervals of some hours, the waters rise and fall — and these intervals in the Mediterranean run mostly about six hours each, that is, six hours rising and another six falling. The second period is monthly, and seems to take its origin from the motion of the Moon — not that she introduces any other motions, but only that she alters the size of the ones just mentioned, with a notable difference according to whether she is full, or waning, or at quadrature with the Sun. The third period is yearly, and appears to depend on the Sun, again only altering the daily motions, making them, at the times of the solstices, different in size from what they are at the equinoxes.

We'll speak first of the daily period, as the chief one, on which the Moon and the Sun seem to exert only a secondary action, with their monthly and yearly alterations. Three differences are to be observed in these hourly changes. In some places the waters rise and fall without any forward movement; in others, without rising or falling, they move now toward the east and then run back toward the west; and in still others they vary both in height and in course, as happens here in Venice, where the waters rise as they come in and fall as they go out. This they do at the far ends of those gulfs that stretch from west to east and end in beaches, over which the water, as it rises, has room to spread; whereas if its course were cut off by mountains or by very high banks, there it would rise and fall without forward movement. They run and run back, without changing height, in the middle stretches — as happens most notably in the Strait of Messina, between Scylla and Charybdis, where the currents, because of the narrowness of the channel, are extremely swift. But in the more open seas, and around the islands out in the middle — such as the Balearics, Corsica, Sardinia, Elba, the part of Sicily toward Africa, Malta, Candia, and so on — the changes in height are very small, though the currents are quite notable, and especially so where the sea narrows between the islands, or between them and the mainland.

Now these effects alone, true and certain as they are, seem to me — even if nothing else were seen — quite likely to persuade anyone willing to stay within natural limits to grant the mobility of the Earth. For to hold the basin of the Mediterranean fixed, and yet have the water it contains do what it does, is beyond my imagination — and perhaps beyond anyone else's who looks past the surface and digs into the question.

Simplicio: These occurrences, Salviati, aren't new. They're extremely ancient, observed by countless people, and many have racked their brains to give a reason for them — one this reason, another that. Not many miles from here there's a great Peripatetic who brings forward a cause freshly fished out of a certain passage in Aristotle that his interpreters hadn't properly noticed. From that passage he gathers that the true cause of these movements comes from nothing but the differing depths of the seas: the waters of the greater depths, being larger in quantity and therefore heavier, drive out the waters of the shallower places, which, once raised up, then want to descend; and from this continual struggle comes the ebb and flow. Then there are the many who refer it to the Moon, saying she has a special dominion over water. And most recently a certain prelate has published a little treatise in which he says the Moon, wandering across the sky, draws and lifts toward herself a heap of water that keeps following after her, so that the sea is always high in whatever part lies beneath the Moon. And since, even when she's below the horizon, the rising still returns, he says the only way to save the effect is to hold that the Moon not only naturally keeps this faculty in herself, but in this case has the power to confer it on the point of the zodiac opposite her. Others — as I think you know — say that the Moon, by her mild warmth, has the power to rarefy the water, which, once rarefied, comes to rise up. Nor have we been without someone who —

Sagredo: Please, Simplicio, don't report any more of them. It doesn't seem worth spending the time to recite them, much less the words to refute them. And if you were to lend your assent to any of these trifles, or others like them, you'd be doing an injustice to your own judgment — which we know to be very well refined.

Salviati: Being a bit more phlegmatic than you, Sagredo, I'll spend fifty words for Simplicio's sake, in case he thinks there might be some probability in the things he's recounted. So here's what I say. Waters, Simplicio, whose outer surface stands higher do drive out the ones below them, the lower ones; but the waters that are greater in depth do not do this. And the higher waters, once they've driven out the lower, quickly settle and come level. This Peripatetic of yours would have to believe that all the lakes in the world that lie still, and all the seas where the ebb and flow is imperceptible, have perfectly level beds. And I was so simple-minded that I'd persuaded myself that, even if there were no other sounding, the islands rising above the waters were a pretty plain indication that the bottoms are uneven. To that prelate you might say that the Moon runs over the whole Mediterranean every day, and yet the waters don't rise except at its eastern extremities and here with us in Venice. To the people with the mild warmth strong enough to make water swell, tell them to put a fire under a cauldron full of water, keep their right hand in it until the heat raises the water by a single finger's width, and then pull it out and write about the swelling of the sea. Or at least ask them to teach you how the Moon manages to rarefy a certain part of the waters and not the rest — say, these here at Venice and not those at Ancona, Naples, or Genoa. One has to conclude that poetic minds come in two kinds: some deft and apt at inventing fables, others disposed and fitted to believing them.

Simplicio: I don't think anyone believes fables while he recognizes them as such. As for the many opinions about the causes of the ebb and flow, since I know that an effect has only a single primary and true cause, I understand perfectly well, and am certain, that at most one of them could be true, while all the rest, I know, are fabulous and false. And perhaps the true one isn't even among those brought forward so far — indeed, that's what I really believe, for it would be a strange thing if the truth could give off so little light that nothing of it showed among the darkness of so many falsehoods. But I'll say, with the freedom allowed among us, that bringing in the motion of the Earth and making it the cause of the ebb and flow seems to me, so far, a notion no less fabulous than any of the others I've heard. And if I were offered no reasons more in keeping with natural things, I'd pass without any resistance to believing this is a supernatural effect, and therefore miraculous and unfathomable to human minds — like the countless others there are that depend immediately on the omnipotent hand of God.

Salviati: You reason very prudently, and in keeping too with the doctrine of Aristotle — who, as you know, at the start of his Mechanical Questions attributes to miracle the things whose causes are hidden. But that the true cause of the ebb and flow is among the impenetrable ones — I don't think you have any greater sign of that than seeing how, among all the causes so far brought forward as the real ones, there isn't a single one by which, with whatever contrivance you employ, we can produce a like effect ourselves. For neither with the light of Moon or Sun, nor with mild heat, nor with differing depths, will anyone ever make the water contained in a motionless vessel run and run back, rise and fall, in one place and not another, by any artificial means. But if, by making the vessel move — with no contrivance at all, in the very simplest way — I can reproduce for you, point by point, all the changes observed in sea water, why would you refuse this cause and take refuge in a miracle?

Simplicio: I'll take refuge in a miracle if you don't dislodge me from it with some natural cause other than the motion of the vessels of the sea waters — because I know those vessels don't move, seeing that the whole entire terrestrial globe is naturally motionless.

Salviati: But don't you believe the terrestrial globe could be made movable supernaturally — that is, by the absolute power of God?

Simplicio: And who doubts it?

Salviati: Then, Simplicio — since to produce the ebb and flow of the sea we need to bring in a miracle — let's miraculously set the Earth in motion, and let the sea then move naturally at its motion. And this operation will be all the simpler, and I'd even say the more natural, among the miraculous ones, in that setting a globe spinning in a circle — when we see so many others moving that way — is less difficult than making an immense mass of water go forward and back, faster here and slower there, rise and fall here more and there less and elsewhere not at all, and produce all these variations in the very same vessel that contains it. Besides, these are many different miracles, and that is only one. And add to this that the miracle of making the water move drags another after it as a consequence: keeping the Earth steady against the water's impulses, which would be strong enough to make it sway now toward one side and now toward the other, unless it were held fast miraculously.

Sagredo: Please, Simplicio, let's suspend our judgment for a little while before pronouncing the new opinion Salviati means to explain to us worthless, and let's not so quickly throw it into the same bag with the old, ridiculous ones. As for the miracle, let's turn to it likewise after we've heard the reasoning that stays within natural limits — though, to speak my own mind, all the works of nature and of God look miraculous to me.

Salviati: And I judge the same. Saying that the natural cause of the ebb and flow is the movement of the Earth doesn't keep that movement from being a miraculous operation. Now, taking up our reasoning again, I repeat and affirm: it remains unknown, so far, how the waters contained within our Mediterranean gulf can make those movements we see them make, so long as that same gulf and containing vessel stays motionless. And what creates the difficulty, and makes this matter so hard to untangle, are the things I'll mention next, which are observed daily. So take note.

Here we are in Venice, where the waters are now low, the sea calm and the air still. The water begins to rise, and within five or six hours it swells ten palms and more. This rise isn't produced by the first water having rarefied; it's produced by new water that has come in — water of the same sort as the first, of the same saltiness, the same density, the same weight. Ships, Simplicio, float in it just as in the first, without sinking so much as a hair deeper; a barrel of this second water weighs not a single grain more or less than the same quantity of the other; it keeps the same coldness, not altered in the least. It is, in short, water newly and visibly come in through the cuts and mouths of the Lido. Now find me how and from where it got here. Are there perhaps chasms or channels round about, in the sea floor, through which the Earth draws in and pours back the water, breathing like some vast and boundless whale? But if that's so, how is it that in the space of six hours the water doesn't rise the same way at Ancona, at Ragusa, at Corfu, where the swelling is very small and perhaps unnoticeable? Who will find a way to pour new water into a motionless vessel, and have it rise only in one particular part of it and nowhere else? Perhaps you'll say this new water is lent to it by the Ocean, handed in through the Strait of Gibraltar? That won't remove the difficulties already mentioned; it'll bring greater ones. First, tell me what the course of that water must be, if, entering through the strait, it travels in six hours all the way to the farthest shores of the Mediterranean, a distance of two or three thousand miles, and then repasses that same distance in as much time on its way back. What would become of the ships scattered across the sea? What of those in the strait, caught in a continual headlong rush of an immense flood of waters that, entering through a channel no more than eight miles wide, has to give passage to enough water that in six hours it floods a space hundreds of miles across and thousands long? What tiger, what falcon, ever ran or flew with such speed — a speed, I mean, that would cover four hundred miles and more in an hour? There are currents, granted, along the length of the Gulf, but so slow that oared vessels outdo them, though not without loss to their own progress. Furthermore, if this water comes in through the strait, there still remains the other difficulty: how it's carried to rise so high here, in parts so remote, without first rising to a like or greater height in the parts nearer by. In short, I don't believe that either stubbornness or subtlety of wit could ever find a remedy for these difficulties, nor, in consequence, uphold against them the stability of the Earth, so long as one stays within natural limits.

Sagredo: Of this I'm now perfectly convinced, and I'm eagerly waiting to hear in what way these marvels can follow, without a hitch, from the motions already assigned to the Earth.

Salviati: For these effects to follow as consequences of the motions that naturally belong to the Earth, it is necessary not only that they meet no resistance or obstacle, but that they follow easily — and not only that they follow easily, but of necessity, so that it would be impossible for things to happen in any other way; for that is the property and condition of things natural and true. So, having established that it is impossible to account for the movements we see in the waters and at the same time keep motionless the vessel that holds them, let us go on to see whether the mobility of the container can produce the effect, under the conditions in which we observe it to occur.

Two kinds of motion can be given to a vessel, by which the water contained in it would gain the ability to run now toward one end and now toward the other, and there to rise and fall. The first would be when now one end and now the other is lowered; for then the water, running toward the tilted side, would in turn rise and fall, now on this side, now on that. But since this rising and falling is nothing other than moving away from and toward the center of the Earth, this kind of motion cannot be attributed to the hollows of the Earth itself — the vessels that hold the waters — because no matter what motion you assign to the terrestrial globe, the parts of those vessels can neither approach nor recede from its center. The other kind of motion is when the vessel moves (without tilting at all) with a forward motion that is not uniform but changes speed, now speeding up and now slowing down. From this unevenness it would follow that the water — held in the vessel, but not fixed to it like its other solid parts, indeed, by its fluidity almost separate and free and not bound to follow every change of its container — when the vessel slows down would retain part of the impetus already built up and run forward toward the leading end, where it would necessarily rise; and conversely, when new speed is added to the vessel, the water, keeping part of its own slowness and lagging somewhat behind before it grows used to the new impetus, would stay toward the trailing end, where it would rise a little. We can lay out these effects more openly, and show them plainly to the senses, with the example of one of those barges that come continually from Lizzafusina, full of fresh water for the use of the city. So let us picture such a barge coming along the Lagoon at a moderate speed, placidly carrying the water it is full of; and then, either through running aground or through some other obstacle set in its way, it is noticeably slowed. The water it holds will not, like the barge, lose the impetus already built up; keeping it, the water will run forward toward the bow, where it will rise noticeably, dropping away from the stern. But if, on the contrary, that same barge in the middle of its placid course is given a notable added burst of new speed, the water it holds, before growing used to it and keeping its own slowness, will lag behind — that is, toward the stern — where it will consequently rise, dropping away from the bow. This effect is beyond doubt and clear, and can be tried at any hour; and in it, for the moment, I want us to note three particulars. The first is that, to make the water rise at one end of the vessel, there is no need of new water, nor for it to run there leaving the other end. The second is that the water in the middle does not rise or fall noticeably — unless the barge's course were extremely fast, and the jolt or other check that stopped it very strong and sudden, in which case all the water might not only run forward but for the most part leap out of the barge; and it would do the same when, while moving slowly, it were suddenly struck by an extremely violent impetus. But when a moderate slowing or quickening overtakes a quiet motion of the barge, the middle parts (as I said) rise and fall imperceptibly, and the other parts rise less the closer they are to the middle, and more the farther off. The third is that, whereas the parts near the middle make little change in rising and falling compared with the waters at the ends, they, on the contrary, run far forward and back compared with the ends. Now, gentlemen, what the barge does with respect to the water it contains, and what the contained water does with respect to the barge that holds it, is exactly, to a hair, what the Mediterranean basin does with respect to the waters it contains, and what those waters do with respect to the Mediterranean basin that holds them. It now follows that we demonstrate how, and in what way, it is true that the Mediterranean and all other gulfs — in short, all parts of the Earth — move with a notably uneven motion, even though no motion that is not regular and uniform is assigned to the globe as a whole.

Simplicio: At first sight, to me — who am neither a mathematician nor an astronomer — this looks like a great paradox; and if it is true that, the motion of the whole being perfectly regular, the motion of the parts, while they stay always joined to their whole, can be irregular, then the paradox will destroy the axiom that states eandem esse rationem totius et partium — that the whole and its parts have one and the same character.

Salviati: I shall demonstrate my paradox, and to you, Simplicio, I'll leave the task of defending the axiom against it, or of reconciling the two; and my demonstration will be short and very easy, depending on things treated at length in our past discussions, without bringing in so much as a single syllable for the sake of the ebb and flow.

We have said there are two motions assigned to the terrestrial globe: the first, the annual, made by its center along the circumference of the great orb under the ecliptic, in the order of the signs — that is, from west to east; the other, made by the globe itself, turning about its own center in twenty-four hours, and this likewise from west to east, though about an axis somewhat tilted and not parallel to that of the annual revolution. From the compounding of these two motions, each of them uniform in itself, there results — I say — an uneven motion in the parts of the Earth. To make this more easily understood, I'll explain it by drawing the figure. First, about the center A I'll draw the circumference of the great orb, BC; on it, taking any point B, and about B as center, we'll draw this smaller circle DEFG, representing the terrestrial globe. We'll understand it to travel, with its center B, all the way around the circumference of the great orb, from west to east — that is, from the side B toward C. And beyond that, we'll understand the terrestrial globe to turn about its own center B, likewise from west to east — that is, in the succession of the points D, E, F, G — in the space of twenty-four hours. But here we must carefully note how, as a circle turns about its own center, any part of it must move at different times in contrary directions. This is clear if we consider that, while the parts of the circumference around the point D are moving to the left — that is, toward E — the opposite parts, those around F, are gaining toward the right — that is, toward G; so that when the parts at D have come to F, their motion will be contrary to what it was before, when they were at D. Moreover, in the same time that the parts at E descend, so to speak, toward F, those at G ascend toward D. Given, then, this contrariety of motions in the parts of the Earth's surface while it turns about its own center, it necessarily follows that, in coupling this daily motion with the other, annual one, there results for the parts of that terrestrial surface an absolute motion now much accelerated and now just as much retarded. This is clear if we first consider the part around D, whose absolute motion will be very fast, being born of two motions made toward the same side — that is, to the left: the first of them a part of the annual motion, common to all parts of the globe; the other belonging to the point D itself, carried likewise to the left by the daily whirl. So in this case the daily motion increases and accelerates the annual. The opposite happens to the opposite part, F, which, while it is carried by the common annual motion — together with the whole globe — toward the left, is carried by the daily turning toward the right; so that the daily motion comes to subtract from the annual, and the absolute movement resulting from the compounding of the two is greatly retarded. Around the points E and G, then, the absolute motion comes to be about equal to the simple annual, since the daily adds to it or subtracts from it nothing, or little, tending neither to the left nor to the right, but down and up. We conclude, therefore, that just as it is true that the motion of the whole globe, and of each of its parts, would be even and uniform if they moved with a single motion — whether the simple annual or the daily alone — so it is necessary that, when these two motions are mixed together, there result for the parts of the globe uneven motions, now accelerated and now retarded, through the additions or subtractions the daily rotation makes to the annual revolution. So that if it is true (as it most certainly is, and experience shows it) that the acceleration and slowing of the vessel's motion make the water it holds run and run back along its length, and rise and fall at its ends, who will make any difficulty about granting that this effect can — indeed, must of necessity — happen to the sea waters, held within their vessels, which are subject to such alterations, and above all in those that stretch lengthwise from west to east, which is the direction in which the motion of those vessels is made? Let this, then, be the principal and primary cause of the ebb and flow, without which nothing of the kind would follow. But because many and varied are the particular incidents observed in different places and times, which must depend on various other concomitant causes — though all of them must have a connection with the primary one — it is necessary to go on proposing and examining the various incidents that can be the causes of these various effects.

The first of these is that, whenever the water — thanks to a notable slowing or speeding of the motion of the vessel that holds it — has gained cause to run toward one end or the other, and has risen at one and dropped at the other, it will not stay in that state even when the primary cause ceases; but by its own weight and natural inclination to level and balance itself, it will of itself return, quickly, back again. And, being heavy and fluid, it will not only move toward equilibrium but, driven by its own impetus, will overshoot it, rising on the side where before it was lower; nor will it stop even here, but, going back once more, with ever more repeated back-and-forth surges it will show us that it will not pass at once from a speed of motion it has taken on to the loss of that motion and to a state of rest, but wants to be brought to it gradually, dying away little by little, slowly. In just the way we see a weight hanging from a cord, once it has been moved from its state of rest — that is, from the vertical — return there of itself and come to rest, but not before it has crossed it many times, this way and that, with its alternating runs and returns.

The second incident to note is that the back-and-forth surges just described are made and repeated with greater or lesser frequency — that is, in shorter or longer times — according to the different lengths of the vessels that hold the waters; so that in the shorter spaces the surges are more frequent, and rarer in the longer ones. Just as, in the same example of hanging bodies, we see the swings of those hung from longer cords to be less frequent than those of the ones hanging from shorter threads.

And here, for the third point worth knowing, it should be understood that not only the greater or lesser length of the vessel causes the water to make its surges in different times, but the greater or lesser depth does the same; and it happens that of waters held in basins of equal length but unequal depth, the one that is deeper makes its levelings in shorter times, and the surges of the shallower waters are less frequent.

Fourth, two effects the water makes in these levelings deserve to be noted and carefully observed. One is the rising and falling alternately toward one end and the other; the other is the moving and running, so to speak horizontally, forward and back. These two different motions reside differently in different parts of the water. For its end parts are the ones that rise and fall the most; those in the middle do not move up or down at all; and of the rest, step by step, those nearer the ends rise and fall proportionately more than the more distant ones. But conversely, in the other, forward-and-back progressive motion, the middle parts move a great deal, going and returning, while the waters at the very ends gain nothing of it — except insofar as, in rising, they overtop their banks and spill out of their original bed and basin; but where there is the check of banks to rein them in, they only rise and fall. Yet the middle waters do not for that reason stop running forward and back, which the other parts do too, proportionately, running more or less according as they are placed more distant from or nearer to the middle.

The fifth particular incident must be considered all the more attentively in that it is impossible for us to represent its effect by experiment and practice; and the incident is this. In the vessels we make by art, and move — like the barges named above — now faster and now slower, the acceleration and slowing are always shared in the same way by the whole vessel and by each of its parts; so that while, for example, the barge is checked in its motion, the leading part is slowed no more than the trailing one, but all share equally in the same slowing; and the same happens with acceleration — that is, when a new cause of greater speed is given to the barge, the bow and the stern accelerate in the same way. But in the immense vessels that are the very long beds of the seas — even though these too are nothing but certain hollows made in the solid body of the terrestrial globe — it marvelously happens that their ends do not increase and diminish their motion together, equally, and at the same moments of time; rather, it happens that when one of its ends finds itself, by virtue of the compounding of the two motions, the daily and the annual, greatly retarded in its speed, the other end finds itself still affected and joined with a very fast motion. This, for easier understanding, we'll explain

by taking up the figure just now drawn. In it, if we take a stretch of sea to be long, say, a quadrant — a quarter of the circle — such as the arc BC: because the parts at B are, as was explained above, in a very fast motion, through the union of the two motions, the daily and the annual, toward the same side, while the part at C is then in a retarded motion, being deprived of the advance that depends on the daily motion — if, I say, we take a gulf of sea as long as the arc BC, we shall already see how its ends move at the same time with great inequality. And the speeds would be utterly different in a stretch of sea half a circle long, placed in the position of the arc BCD, since the end B would be in a very fast motion, the other, D, in a very slow one, and the middle parts, toward C, in a moderate motion. And the shorter these stretches of sea are, the less they will share in this strange incident of having their parts, at certain hours of the day, differently affected by speed and slowness of motion. So that if, as in the first case, we see by experience that the acceleration and slowing — though shared equally by all parts of the containing vessel — are still the cause of the contained water's running forward and back, what must we suppose ought to happen in a vessel so marvelously arranged that slowing and speeding of motion are contributed to its parts very unequally? Certainly we can say nothing else but that a greater and more marvelous cause of commotions in the water — and stranger ones — must be found there. And though it may seem impossible to many that, in artificial machines and vessels, we could experiment the effects of such an incident, nonetheless it is not entirely impossible; and I have the design of a machine in which, in particular, the effect of these marvelous compoundings of motions can be discerned. But as far as concerns the present subject, what you have been able to grasp so far with your imagination is enough. For my part, I understand very well that this marvelous incident must necessarily be found in the gulfs of the seas, and above all in those that stretch over great distances from west to east — that is, along the course of the terrestrial globe's motions; and since it is in a certain way unthinkable and without parallel among the motions we can produce, so I find it not hard to believe that from it there may derive effects not imitable by our artificial experiments.

Salviati: Now that these matters are settled, it's time we turned to examining the particular phenomena — and their variety — that experience shows us in the ebb and flow of the waters. First, we should have no trouble understanding why in lakes, ponds, and even small seas there is no notable ebb and flow. There are two thoroughly conclusive reasons for this. The first is that, because the basin is short, when at different hours of the day it takes on different degrees of speed, those degrees are acquired with little difference by all its parts: the leading and the trailing parts alike — the eastern and the western — speed up and slow down in almost exactly the same way. And since this change happens gradually, and not by suddenly throwing some abrupt check and delay, or some sudden and great acceleration, against the motion of the containing vessel, the vessel and all its parts are slowly and evenly impressed with the same degrees of speed. From this uniformity it follows that the contained water, too, receives the same impressions with little stubbornness or resistance, and so gives only the faintest sign of rising or falling, of running toward one end or the other. You can see this same effect plainly in small artificial vessels, where the water within takes on the very same degrees of speed, provided the speeding up or slowing down is done in a slow and uniform proportion. But in arms of the sea that stretch a great distance from east to west, the acceleration or slowing is far more notable and uneven, since one of its ends will be in a much-retarded motion while the other is still moving very fast. The second cause is the reciprocal swinging of the water, arising from the impetus it, too, would have taken on from the motion of its container — a swinging whose oscillations, as we noted, are very frequent in small vessels. From this it results that, although the cause residing in the Earth's motions contributes motion to the water only once every twelve hours — since only once a day does the motion of the containing vessels reach its greatest slowing and its greatest speeding — nevertheless that other, second cause, depending on the weight of the water as it seeks to return to equilibrium, and having, according to the shortness of the vessel, its swings of one hour, or two, or three, and so on: this second cause, mixing with the first (which by itself, in small vessels, already stays very slight), renders it altogether imperceptible. For before the disturbance produced by the primary cause — the one with periods of twelve hours — has finished being impressed, along comes the other, secondary one, running counter to it, depending on the water's own weight, which, according to the shortness and depth of the vessel, has the time of its swings at one, two, three, or four hours, and so on; and, running counter to the first, it upsets and displaces it, without letting it reach the top, or even the middle, of its movement. And from this opposition the evidence of ebb and flow is wholly annihilated, or greatly obscured. I say nothing of the continual changes in the air, which, by troubling the water, would keep us from ever being sure of a very slight rise or fall of half a finger's breadth, or less, that might really reside in gulfs and basins of water no longer than a degree or two.

I come, in the second place, to resolving the puzzle of how — given that the primary principle contains no cause for stirring the waters except once every twelve hours (once by the greatest speed of motion, and once by the greatest slowness) — the period of ebb and flow nonetheless commonly appears to be one of six hours. To this the answer is that such a determination can in no way come from the primary cause alone; the secondary ones must be brought in — namely, the greater or lesser length of the vessels, and the greater or lesser depth of the waters they contain. These causes, though they have no action at all in the movements of the waters (that action belonging to the primary cause alone, without which nothing of ebb and flow would follow), nonetheless have the chief say in determining the times of the swings — so powerful a say that the primary cause must remain subject to them. So the six-hour period is no more proper or natural than those of other intervals of time; it is simply, perhaps, the most observed, being the one that fits our Mediterranean, which for long ages was the only sea people sailed. And even there this period is not observed in all its parts, since in certain more confined places — such as the Hellespont and the Aegean — the periods are much shorter, and very different from one another besides. This variety and its causes were beyond Aristotle; and some say that, after long observing them from certain rocks of Negroponte, he was driven to despair, threw himself into the sea, and deliberately drowned.

In the third place, we shall have very ready to hand the reason why a certain sea, though very long — such as the Red Sea — is nonetheless almost entirely free of ebb and flow. This happens because its length does not run from east to west, but rather crosswise, from the southeast toward the northwest. But since the Earth's movements are from west to east, the impulses of the waters always strike along the meridians, and do not move from parallel to parallel. Hence in seas that stretch crosswise toward the poles, and are narrow the other way, there remains no cause for ebb and flow except through the sharing of some other sea they communicate with, one that is subject to large movements.

In the fourth place, we shall very easily grasp the reason why the ebb and flow are greatest — in the rising and falling of the waters — at the far ends of gulfs, and least in the middle parts. Daily experience shows this here in Venice, set at the far end of the Adriatic, where the difference commonly amounts to five or six feet; whereas in places of the Mediterranean distant from the ends, the change is very slight — as in the islands of Corsica and Sardinia, and on the shores of Rome and Livorno, where it does not exceed half a foot. We shall also grasp how, conversely, where the risings and fallings are small, the currents' runnings-out and runnings-back are large. It is an easy matter, I say, to understand the cause of these phenomena, since we have plain confirmations of them in every sort of vessel we build by hand, in which these same effects are seen to follow naturally when we move them with an uneven motion — now sped up, now slowed down.

Moreover, considering, in the fifth place, how the same quantity of water, moved even slowly through a spacious channel, must of necessity run with great force when it then has to pass through a narrow place, we shall have no trouble understanding the cause of the great currents in the narrow channel that separates Calabria from Sicily. For all the water that, from the breadth of the island and from the Ionian gulf, is held in the eastern part of the sea, though it descends slowly toward the west through that whole expanse, nevertheless, on being squeezed into the strait between Scylla and Charybdis, drops swiftly and makes an enormous turmoil. A similar turmoil, and a far greater one, is understood to occur between Africa and the great island of St. Lawrence, when the waters of the two vast seas — the Indian and the Ethiopic — that flank it must, in flowing, squeeze into a smaller channel between the island and the coast of Ethiopia. And the currents must be very great indeed in the Strait of Magellan, which links the vast Ethiopic and South oceans.

It follows now, in the sixth place, that in order to account for some more hidden and unexpected phenomena observed in this matter, we go on to make another important consideration regarding the two principal causes of ebb and flow, and then compound and mix them together. The first and simpler of the two is (as has been said many times) the fixed acceleration and slowing of the parts of the Earth, from which the waters would have a fixed period of running toward the east and returning toward the west within a span of twenty-four hours. The other is the one that depends on the water's own weight, which, once stirred by the primary cause, then seeks to return to equilibrium with repeated swings — swings not determined by a single, set time, but having as many different times as there are different lengths and depths of the basins and arms of the seas. As far as this second principle goes, some would run out and back in one hour, others in two, in four, in six, in eight, in ten, and so on. Now, if we begin to join the primary cause — which steadily keeps its period of twelve hours — with some secondary one that had its period of, say, five hours, it will happen that at certain times the primary and the secondary cause agree in making their impulses both toward the same side; and in this conjunction, this so-to-speak unanimous conspiring together, the tides will be large. At other times it will happen that the primary impulse comes in a certain way to oppose what the secondary period would bring; and in this clash, each principle taking away what the other would give, the movements of the waters will be weakened, and the sea will be reduced to a very quiet and almost motionless state. And at still other times, according as the two principles neither wholly oppose nor wholly agree with each other, there will be other variations in the increase and decrease of the tides. It can also happen that two very large seas, communicating through some narrow channel, come — through the mixing of the two principles of motion — to have the one with a cause of rising at the very time the other has a cause of opposite movement. In that case, in the channel where those seas communicate, there arise extraordinary agitations, with opposing movements, whirlpools, and most dangerous boilings — of which there are continual reports and actual firsthand experiences. From such discordant movements, depending not only on the different positions and lengths but also, in great measure, on the different depths of the communicating seas, there will arise at certain times various disturbances in the waters, irregular and beyond observation, whose causes have greatly puzzled — and still puzzle — sailors, who meet with them without seeing that no force of winds, nor any other serious change in the air, can be their cause. Of this disturbance of the air we must, in other phenomena, take great account, and reckon it a third and incidental cause, capable of greatly altering the observation of effects that depend on the primary and more essential causes. And there is no doubt that if violent winds keep blowing — from the east, for example — they will hold up the waters, preventing their ebb; so that, when the second recurrence of the flow arrives at the appointed hours, and then the third, the waters will swell greatly; and thus, held up for several days by the force of the wind, they rise higher than usual, causing extraordinary floods.

We must further (and this will be like a seventh problem) take note of another cause of movement, depending on the great abundance of water from the rivers that empty into seas that are not very vast — where, in the channels or straits that communicate with such seas, the water is seen always to run the same way, as happens in the Thracian Bosphorus below Constantinople, where the water always runs from the Black Sea toward the Propontis. For in that Black Sea, because of its smallness, the principal causes of ebb and flow have little force; but on the other hand, since very great rivers empty into it, when all that flood of water must pass and pour out through the strait, the current there is quite notable, and always toward the south. Here, moreover, we must note that this strait and channel, though quite narrow, is not subject to the disturbances that the strait of Scylla and Charybdis is. For that one has the Black Sea above it toward the north, and the Propontis and the Aegean, along with the Mediterranean beyond, toward the south — though at a long remove. But, as we have already noted, seas that run north to south, however long, are not subject to ebb and flow. Whereas the strait of Sicily lies set between parts of the Mediterranean that stretch over great distances from west to east — that is, along the run of the ebb and flow — and so in it the agitations are very great. And they would be greater at the Pillars of Hercules, if the Strait of Gibraltar were to open less; and they report those of the Strait of Magellan to be very great indeed. This is all that occurs to me for now to tell you about the causes of this first, daily period of ebb and flow, and its various phenomena. If you have anything to propose, do so, and then we can pass on to the other two periods — the monthly and the annual.

Simplicio: I don't think it can be denied that the discourse you've made proceeds very plausibly, arguing, as we put it, ex suppositione — on a supposition — that is, granting that the Earth moves with the two movements Copernicus attributes to it. But if those movements are ruled out, the whole thing remains empty and invalid; and the ruling out of that hypothesis is pointed out to us quite plainly by your own discourse. With the supposition of the two terrestrial movements you account for the ebb and flow, and then, conversely, reasoning in a circle, from the ebb and flow you draw the indication and confirmation of those very movements. And, coming to a more specific point: you say that the water, being a fluid body and not tenaciously attached to the Earth, is not compelled to obey its every motion punctually — from which you then derive its ebb and flow. Now, following in your very footsteps, I argue the opposite, and say: the air is far more tenuous and fluid than water, and less attached to the Earth's surface — to which the water, if for no other reason, clings by its weight, pressing down upon it far more than the featherlight air does. Therefore the air ought much less to follow the Earth's movements; and so, if the Earth moved in that way, we — its inhabitants, carried along by it at the same speed — ought perpetually to feel a wind from the east striking us with intolerable force. And that this must be so, experience makes us aware of daily: for if, riding post at a speed of only eight or ten miles an hour, meeting the still air with our face makes us feel a wind that strikes us no lightly, what ought our rapid course of eight hundred or a thousand miles an hour to do, against air free of that motion? Yet we feel nothing of the sort.

Salviati: To this objection, which has much of the plausible about it, I answer that it is true the air is more tenuous and lighter, and by its lightness less adherent to the Earth, than water, which is so much heavier and more corporeal. But then the conclusion you draw from these conditions is false — namely, that because of this lightness, tenuity, and lesser adherence to the Earth, the air should exempt itself, more than water does, from following the terrestrial movements, so that to us, who share those movements entirely, its disobedience would become sensible and manifest. In fact, just the opposite happens. For, if you remember rightly, the cause of the ebb and flow of the water, as we assigned it, consists in the water's not following the unevenness of the motion of its vessel, but retaining the impetus conceived earlier, without diminishing or increasing it by the precise measure by which it is increased or diminished in its vessel. Since, then, the disobedience to a fresh increase or decrease of motion consists in preserving and maintaining the impetus conceived before, that body which is more apt at such preservation will also be the better suited to display the effect that follows from that preservation. Now, how disposed water is to maintain an agitation once conceived, even after the cause that impressed it has ceased, is shown by the experience of seas mightily stirred by violent winds: their waves, even after the air has calmed and the wind died, remain in motion a long while — as the sacred poet gracefully sang, "Like the high Aegean..." and so on. And this persistence in the agitation depends on the weight of the water; for, as has been said elsewhere, light bodies are indeed far easier to set moving than heavier ones, but they are just so much less able to preserve the motion impressed on them once the moving cause has ceased. Hence the air, being in itself most tenuous and light, is very easily moved by any least force, but is also most unfit to preserve the motion once the mover has ceased. So, as for the air surrounding the terrestrial globe, I would say that by its adherence it is carried around no less than the water is — especially the part contained within basins, those basins being the plains ringed by mountains. And of this portion we can much more reasonably affirm that it is carried around, snatched along by the roughness of the Earth, than the upper air is snatched along by the celestial motion, as you Peripatetics assert.

What I have said so far seems to me a fairly adequate answer to Simplicio's objection; nevertheless, I want, with a new objection and a new reply — founded on a marvelous experience — to satisfy him more than fully, and to confirm for Sagredo the mobility of the terrestrial globe. I said that the air — and in particular that part of it which does not rise above the summits of the highest mountains — is carried around by the roughness of the Earth's surface. From this it seems to follow that if the Earth's surface were not uneven, but smooth and polished, there would be no cause to draw the air along in its company, or at least to carry it along with such uniformity. Now, the surface of this globe of ours is not all rugged and rough: there are very great, quite smooth expanses — namely, the surfaces of the vast seas — which, being also very far from the mountain ridges that ring them, do not seem able to have the power to carry the overlying air along with them. And, not carrying it, one ought in those places to feel just what follows from that.

Simplicio: This is the very difficulty I too was about to raise, and it seems to me of great force.

Salviati: You speak very well: so that, Simplicio, from not feeling in the air what would follow as a consequence if this globe of ours went around, you argue its immobility. But if this thing, which you think one should feel by necessary consequence, were in fact and by experience felt, would you accept it as a strong enough indication and argument for the mobility of that same globe?

Simplicio: In this case one mustn't argue with me alone, because if it happened, and the cause of it were hidden from me, perhaps it might be known to others.

Salviati: So with you one can never win, but always stands to lose — better, then, not to play at all. Still, so as not to leave the third player in the lurch, I'll go on. We were just saying — and I'll repeat it with something added — that the air, being a tenuous and fluid body, not firmly joined to the Earth, seemed to have no need to obey its motion, except insofar as the roughness of the Earth's surface snatches up and carries along with it a portion of air contiguous to it, a portion rising not much above the greatest heights of the mountains. And this portion of air will be all the less resistant to the terrestrial turning insofar as it is full of vapors, fumes, and exhalations — all of them materials partaking of earthly qualities, and consequently born by their nature apt for the same movements. But where the causes of motion were lacking — that is, where the surface of the globe had great flat spaces, and there was less of the admixture of earthly vapors — there the cause by which the surrounding air should wholly obey the snatching of the terrestrial turning would in part cease. So in such places, while the Earth turns toward the east, one ought continually to feel a wind striking us, blowing from the east toward the west; and such a blowing ought to be more perceptible where the whirl of the globe is faster — which would be in the places most remote from the poles and nearest the greatest circle of the daily turning. But in fact experience much applauds this philosophical reasoning: for in the wide seas, in their parts far from land and lying beneath the torrid zone — that is, enclosed between the tropics, where the earthly evaporations are also lacking — one feels a perpetual breeze moving from the east, with so constant a tenor that ships sail prosperously by its grace to the West Indies; and from those same shores, casting off from the Mexican coast, they plow with the same favor across the Pacific toward the Indies that are eastern to us but western to them. Whereas, on the contrary, voyages from there toward the east are difficult and uncertain, and can by no means be made along the same routes; instead one must hug the coast more, to find other winds — accidental and unruly, so to speak — caused by other principles, such as we who dwell among the mainland continually feel by experience. Of these generations of winds the causes are many and various, which there is no occasion to produce at present; and these accidental winds are the ones that blow indifferently from every quarter of the Earth, and that disturb the seas remote from the equinoctial and surrounded by the rough surface of the Earth — which is as much as to say, subject to those disturbances of the air that confound that primary breathing-out, which, if these accidental impediments were lacking, ought perpetually to be felt, and most of all over the sea. Now see how the effects of the water and of the air seem marvelously to agree with the celestial observations in confirming the mobility of our terrestrial globe.

Sagredo: I want, as a final seal on all this, to add one detail I think you don't know, and which also confirms the very same conclusion. You, Salviati, brought up that phenomenon sailors meet with inside the tropics — I mean the perpetual steadiness of the wind that comes to them from the east; and I've had an account of it from someone who has made that voyage many times. What's more (and here's the notable part), I gather the sailors don't call it a wind at all, but by some other name I can't call to mind just now — taken, perhaps, from its being so steady and constant that, once they've fallen in with it, they lash the shrouds and the other sail ropes and, without ever needing to touch them again, can hold their course in safety even while they sleep. Now this perpetual breeze has been recognized for what it is precisely by its blowing on and on without interruption; for if it were broken up by other winds, it would never have been noticed as a single effect distinct from the rest. From which I want to infer that it might well be that our own Mediterranean shares in some such phenomenon, only unobserved, because it is so frequently disturbed by other winds coming on. And I say this not without solid grounds — indeed, with very probable evidence, which came my way from something I had occasion to learn on the voyage I made to Syria, going as consul of our merchant community in Aleppo. It is this: a particular record and register is kept of the days on which ships depart from and arrive in the ports of Alexandria, of Alexandretta, and here at Venice; and in comparing a great many of these against one another — which I did out of my own curiosity — I found that, on average, the returns homeward, that is, the voyages from east toward west across the Mediterranean, are made in less time than the opposite runs, by a good 25 percent. So you can see that, all told, the winds out of the east are stronger than those out of the west.

Salviati: I'm glad to have learned this detail; it brings no small confirmation for the mobility of the Earth. And even though one might say that all the water of the Mediterranean runs perpetually toward the Strait — being water that has to go and discharge into the Ocean the flow of the many rivers that pour into it — I don't believe such a current could be strong enough, by itself alone, to produce so notable a difference. And that's clear enough, too, from the fact that in the Faro — the Strait of Messina — you see the water run back toward the east no less than it runs toward the west.

Sagredo: I, who unlike Simplicio have no spur to satisfy anyone but myself, am content with what has been said on this first part; so, Salviati, whenever it suits you to go on, I'm ready to listen.

Salviati: I'll do as you bid me; but I'd like to hear Simplicio's opinion too, since from his judgment I can gauge how much I might promise myself, regarding these arguments of mine, from the Peripatetic schools — if ever they reached their ears.

Simplicio: I don't want my opinion to carry weight with you, or to serve you as a guess at what others will judge, because — as I've said many times — I'm one of the least in this sort of study, and something will occur to those who have penetrated into the innermost sanctuaries of philosophy that cannot occur to me, who have (as they say) barely greeted it from the doorstep. Still, just to show some sign of life, I'll say that as for the effects you've recounted, and this last one in particular, it seems to me that without the mobility of the Earth one could give a perfectly sufficient account of them by the mobility of the heavens alone, introducing no novelty whatever except the converse of the very thing you yourself put forward. The Peripatetic schools have accepted that the element of fire, and a great part of the air as well, are carried around, following the daily rotation, from east toward west by contact with the concave of the lunar orb, as by the vessel that contains them. Now, without straying from your own footsteps, let's stipulate that the quantity of air taking part in this motion reaches down almost to the tops of the highest mountains, and would reach all the way down to the Earth if the obstacle of those same mountains didn't block it. This matches what you say — for just as you affirm that the air hemmed in by the mountain ridges is carried around by the roughness of the moving Earth, we say the converse: that the whole element of air is carried around by the motion of the heavens, except the part lying below the ridges, which is held back by the roughness of the motionless Earth. And where you said that if that roughness were taken away, the air too would lose being swept along, we can say that with the same roughness removed, all the air would go on with its motion. Hence, because the surfaces of the wide seas are smooth and polished, over them the motion of the breeze carries on, blowing perpetually from the east; and this is felt more in the regions under the equinoctial and within the tropics, where the motion of the heavens is faster. And just as this celestial motion is powerful enough to carry all the free air along with it, so we may very reasonably say that the same motion also acts on the moving water, since water is fluid and not fastened to the immobility of the Earth. And we can affirm this all the more confidently in that, by your own admission, this motion must be very small compared with the cause that produces it — a cause which, circling the whole terrestrial globe in one natural day, covers many hundreds of miles an hour, especially toward the equinoctial, whereas the currents of the open sea run at very few miles an hour. And so the westward voyages come out easy and quick not only thanks to the perpetual easterly breeze, but to the course of the waters as well — a course from which the ebb and flow might perhaps also arise, by way of the different lay of the shores, where the water, going to strike against them, can turn and run back with a contrary motion. Experience shows us as much in the course of rivers: wherever the water, in the unevenness of the banks, meets some part that juts out, or some hollow below, there the water wheels around, and you plainly see it run back the other way. So it seems to me that from the very effects by which you argue for the mobility of the Earth, and which you offer as the cause of them, one can allege a cause that concludes well enough while keeping the Earth at rest and restoring the mobility to the heavens.

Salviati: There's no denying your argument is ingenious and has a good deal of the probable about it — probable, I say, in appearance, but not in fact and reality. It has two parts. In the first, it accounts for the continuous motion of the easterly breeze, and for a like motion in the water; in the second, it tries to draw from the same source the cause of the ebb and flow. The first part has, as I said, some semblance of probability, though far less than what we take from the Earth's motion; the second is not merely improbable but flatly impossible and false. Coming to the first, where it's said that the lunar concave sweeps along the element of fire and all the air up to the tops of the highest mountains, I say, to begin with, that it's doubtful whether there even is an element of fire; but granting there is, there's grave doubt about the sphere of the Moon, as about all the others — whether there really are such solid, enormous bodies, or whether, beyond the air, there extends a continuous expanse of a substance far more tenuous and pure than our air, through which the planets go wandering, as by now a good many of these same philosophers are beginning to hold. But whichever way it is, there's no reason why fire, from the mere contact of a surface you yourself judge to be perfectly polished and smooth, should be carried around throughout its whole depth by a motion foreign to its own natural bent — as has been amply proved, and demonstrated with sound experiments, in The Assayer; setting aside the further improbability of such a motion being transfused from the most subtle fire through the far denser air, and then from the air into the water. But that a body with a rough, mountainous surface, in turning upon itself, carries with it the air right next to it, against which its prominences go striking — that is not merely probable but necessary, and one can actually see it by experiment; though even without seeing it, I don't believe there's any mind that would doubt it. As for the other part: granting that the motion of the heavens did carry the air, and the water too, that motion would still have nothing to do with the ebb and flow. For since from a single, uniform cause nothing can follow but a single, uniform effect, what we ought to observe in the water would be a continuous, uniform course from east toward west — and only in that sea which, closing on itself, encircles the whole globe. But in bounded seas, like the Mediterranean, shut off on the east, there could be no such motion; because if its water could be driven westward by the course of the heavens, it would have been left dry many centuries ago. Besides which, our water doesn't run only westward but comes back the other way, toward the east, and does so on regular schedules. And though you say, with the example of the rivers, that even if the sea's course were originally the single one from east to west, still the different lay of the shores can make part of the water surge back — this I grant you. But you must take note, my dear Simplicio, that wherever the water runs back for that reason, it runs back there perpetually, and wherever it runs straight on, it always runs the same way — that's exactly what the example of the rivers shows you. Whereas in the case of the ebb and flow, you have to find and produce a reason why, in one and the same place, the water should now run one way and now the opposite; and those effects, being contrary and unlike, you'll never be able to deduce from a uniform, constant cause. And the same thing that overthrows this notion — of the sea's motion being contributed by the daily motion of the heavens — also demolishes the notion of anyone who'd want to admit only the daily motion of the Earth and thought that by that alone he could account for the ebb and flow. For since that effect is not uniform, the cause of it absolutely must be non-uniform and changeable.

Simplicio: I've nothing to say in reply — neither of my own, given the weakness of my wit, nor from anyone else, given the novelty of the opinion. But I'd well believe that, once it were put about in the schools, there'd be no shortage of philosophers who'd know how to challenge it.

Sagredo: We'll wait, then, for such an occasion; and meanwhile, if it so please you, Salviati, let's go on ahead.

Salviati: Everything said so far belongs to the daily period of the ebb and flow. First we demonstrated, in general, its primary and universal cause, without which none of this effect would follow at all; then, moving on to the particular incidents — varied, and in a way irregular — that are observed in it, we treated the secondary and attendant causes on which they depend. Now come the other two periods, the monthly and the annual, which bring no new and different incidents beyond those already considered in the daily period, but work upon those same ones by making them greater and smaller at different points of the lunar month and at different seasons of the solar year — almost as if both the Moon and the Sun had a hand in the work and in producing these effects. And that's something my mind refuses utterly; for seeing, as I do, that this business of the seas is a local, tangible motion, worked in an immense mass of water, it can't bring itself to sign on to influences, to mild warmths, to dominances by occult qualities, and to similar empty fancies — which, tantum abest, so far from being, or possibly being, the causes of the tides, that on the contrary the tides are the cause of them: the cause, that is, of their springing up in the sort of brains fitter for chatter and show than for the study and investigation of nature's more hidden works. Such men, rather than bring themselves to utter that wise, honest, modest little word "I don't know," rush to let fall from their mouths — and from their pens too — any great absurdity you please. And the mere fact that this same Moon and this same Sun don't act — not with their light, not with motion, not with heat great or mild — upon smaller bodies of water; that indeed, to make water rise by heat, you'd have to bring it very nearly to a boil; and, in short, that we can't artificially imitate the movements of the tide by any means whatever except by moving the vessel — shouldn't all this convince everyone that the other things put forward as causes of the effect are empty fantasies, wholly alien from the truth? I say, then, that if it's true that one effect has only one primary cause, and that between the cause and the effect there's a firm, constant connection, then it necessarily follows that whenever a firm, constant change is seen in the effect, there's a firm, constant change in the cause. And because the changes that come over the tides at different parts of the year and month have their own firm, constant periods, we're forced to say that a regular change occurs, at those same times, in the primary cause of the tides. Now the change found at those times in the tides consists in nothing but their magnitude — that is, in the waters rising and falling more or less, and running with greater or smaller force. Therefore what is the primary cause of the ebb and flow must, at those determined times, increase or diminish its own strength. But we've already concluded that the primary cause of the tides is the unevenness and non-uniformity in the motion of the vessels holding the water; therefore this non-uniformity must, from time to time, correspondingly grow more uneven — must, that is, become greater and smaller. Now we should recall how this non-uniformity — the varying speed of motion of the vessels, that is, of the parts of the Earth's surface — depends on their moving with the composite motion that results from coupling the two motions, annual and daily, belonging to the whole terrestrial globe. Of these, it's the daily whirl, by now adding to and now subtracting from the annual motion, that produces the non-uniformity in the composite motion; so that in the additions and subtractions the daily whirl makes to the annual motion lies the original cause of the uneven motion of the vessels, and consequently of the ebb and flow. In such a way that, if these additions and subtractions were always made in the same proportion to the annual motion, the cause of the ebb and flow would indeed persist, but so as to act perpetually in the same manner. But we need to find the cause of these same tides being made, at different times, greater and smaller; therefore we must (if we want to keep the cause the same) find some change in these additions and subtractions that makes them more and less powerful in producing the effects that depend on them. And I don't see how such power and weakness could be brought about except by making these same additions and subtractions now greater and now smaller, so that the quickening and slowing of the composite motion happens now in a greater and now in a smaller proportion.

Sagredo: I feel myself being led very gently by the hand; and although I meet no stumbling blocks along the road, still, like a blind man, I don't see where your guidance is taking me, nor can I imagine where this journey is to end.

Salviati: Great as the difference is between my slow philosophizing and your very swift reasoning, still, on this particular point we now have in hand, I won't be surprised that the sharpness of your wit is left clouded by the high, dark fog that hides from us the goal we're walking toward. And my surprise fades when I remember how many hours, how many days — and, more than that, how many nights — I spent on this speculation, and how many times, despairing of ever reaching the end of it, I struggled, to console myself, to believe, like the unhappy Orlando, that what the testimony of so many trustworthy men set before my eyes could not, after all, be true. So don't be surprised if this once, against your custom, you don't foresee the mark; and if you are surprised, I believe the outcome — quite unexpected, as far as I can judge — will put an end to your surprise.

Sagredo: I thank God, then, that He kept such despair from carrying you off to the end the tale gives the wretched Orlando — or to the one, no less fabulously reported, they tell of Aristotle — so that neither I nor anyone else should be left robbed of the discovery of a thing as hidden as it is longed for. I beg you, then, to feed my starving hunger as soon as you can.

Salviati: Here I am, ready to satisfy you. We had got as far as finding out in what way the additions and subtractions of the Earth's whirl upon the annual motion could be made now in a greater and now in a smaller proportion — a variation which, and nothing else, could be assigned as the cause of the monthly and annual changes seen in the magnitude of the tides. Now, I observe that this proportion, between the additions and subtractions of the daily whirl and the annual motion, can be made greater and smaller in three ways. The first is by increasing and diminishing the speed of the annual motion, while keeping the additions and subtractions made by the daily whirl at the same size; for since the annual motion is about three times greater — that is, faster — than the daily motion (taken even at its great circle), if we increase it further, the additions or subtractions of the daily motion will alter it less; whereas, on the contrary, if we make it slower, it will come to be altered in a greater proportion by that same daily motion. It's like adding or taking away four degrees of speed from something moving at twenty degrees: that alters its course less than the same four degrees added or subtracted would alter something moving at only 10 degrees. The second way would be to make the additions and subtractions greater or smaller while keeping the annual motion at the same speed — as easy to grasp as it's plain that a speed of, say, 20 degrees is altered more by adding or subtracting 10 degrees than by adding or subtracting 4. The third way would be when these two are joined together, diminishing the annual motion and increasing the daily additions and subtractions. Up to here, as you see, it hasn't been hard to get; but it was laborious indeed for me to find out in what way this could be brought about in nature. Yet in the end I find that she makes marvelous use of it, and by means almost past imagining — marvelous and unimaginable to us, I mean, but not to her, who works with the utmost ease and simplicity even the things that fill our intellect with boundless wonder; and what is hardest for us to understand is easiest for her to do. Going on further, then, and having shown how the proportion between the additions and subtractions of the whirl and the annual motion can be made greater and smaller in two ways (I say two, because the third is compounded of the first two), I add that nature makes use of both; and, further, I add that if she made use of only one, one of the two periodic changes would have to be taken away. That of the monthly period would cease, if the annual movement were not altered; and if the additions and subtractions of the daily whirl kept themselves continually equal, the changes of the annual period would be lacking.

Sagredo: So the monthly variation in the tides depends on the change in the Earth's annual motion? And the yearly variation in those same tides derives from the additions and subtractions of the daily whirl? Now I find myself more confused than ever, and more hopeless of ever managing to grasp how this tangle holds together — more knotted, it seems to me, than the Gordian knot. And I envy Simplicio, from whose silence I gather that he's understood the whole of it, and is free of the confusion that so clutters my imagination.

Simplicio: I do believe, Sagredo, that you find yourself confused, and I think I even know the cause of your confusion — which, to my mind, comes from this: of the things Salviati has brought up just now, part you understand and part you don't. It's also true that I'm free of confusion, but not for the reason you suppose — that is, not because I've grasped the whole of it. Quite the opposite: it comes to me from grasping nothing at all. Confusion lies in a multitude of things, not in nothing.

Sagredo: You see, Salviati, how a few little jerks of the bridle given to Simplicio these past days have made him tame, and changed him from a jumper into a gentle palfrey. But please, without any more delay, get us both out of our trouble.

Salviati: I'll strain my clumsy powers of expression as far as they'll go; their dullness will be made up for by the sharpness of your wits. There are two phenomena whose causes we have to track down. The first concerns the differences that occur in the tides over the monthly period; the other belongs to the yearly period. First we'll speak of the monthly, then treat the yearly; and we must resolve all of it according to the foundations and hypotheses already established, without introducing any novelty — not into astronomy, nor into the universe — for the sake of the tides. Instead, let's show that of all the various phenomena we observe in them, the causes reside in things already known, and accepted as true and beyond doubt.

I say, then, that it's a true, natural — indeed necessary — thing that one and the same moving body, set turning in a circle by the same moving power, should take a longer time to complete its course along a larger circle than along a smaller one. This is a truth accepted by everyone, and confirmed by every experiment, of which I'll offer a few.

In wheel-clocks, and the large ones in particular, to regulate the timekeeping the makers fit a certain rod that turns freely in the horizontal, and at its ends they hang two lead weights; and when the clock runs too slow, merely by bringing those leads a little nearer the center of the rod they make its swings more frequent; and on the other hand, to slow it down, it's enough to draw the same weights out toward the ends, because then the swings come more seldom, and consequently the intervals of the hours grow longer. Here the moving power is the same — the counterweight; the moving bodies are the same leads; and their swings are more frequent when they're nearer the center, that is, when they move along smaller circles.

Hang equal weights from unequal cords, and, drawn aside from the vertical, let them go free: we'll see the ones on shorter cords make their swings in shorter times, as being those that move along smaller circles. But more: attach such a weight to a cord that rides over a nail fixed in the ceiling, and hold the other end of the cord in your hand; having set the hanging weight going, then, while it's making its swings, pull the end of the cord you're holding so that the weight rises. You'll see, as it lifts, the frequency of its swings increase, since they're being made continually along smaller circles.

And here I want you to note two particulars, worth knowing. One is that the swings of such a pendulum are made, with such necessity, under certain determined times, that it's utterly impossible to make it swing under any other times except by lengthening or shortening the cord — of which you can even satisfy yourself right now, by experiment: tie a stone to a string, hold the other end in your hand, and try whether, by any device whatever, you can ever manage to make it go back and forth under anything but one determined time, short of lengthening or shortening the string — which you'll see is flatly impossible.

The other particular, truly marvelous, is that the same pendulum makes its swings with the same frequency — or with so slight, so nearly imperceptible a difference — whether they're made along the largest arcs or the smallest of the same circumference. I mean that if we move the pendulum from the vertical by only one, two, or three degrees, or move it 70, 80, or even a full quarter-circle, once let go it will, in the one case and the other, make its swings with the same frequency — the first, where it has to move along an arc of 4 or 6 degrees, just as much as the second, where it has to pass through arcs of 160 degrees or more. This is seen more plainly still by hanging two equal weights from two equally long threads, then drawing one aside from the vertical by a small distance and the other by a very great one; set free, they'll go and return in the same times, the one along quite small arcs, the other along very great ones.

From which follows the conclusion of a most beautiful problem: that, given a quarter-circle (I'll sketch a little figure here on the ground) — say this one AB, set upright to the horizon so that it stands on the plane, touching at the point B — and having made an arc, with a board well planed and smooth on its concave side, bent to the curve of the circumference ADB, so that a ball, well rounded and polished, can run freely inside it (the frame of a sieve suits this experiment) — I say that, put the ball wherever you like, near or far from the lowest terminus B — say, putting it at the point C, or here at D, or at E — and let go, in equal times, or times imperceptibly different, it will arrive at the terminus B, whether it starts from C or from D or from E or from any other place whatever: a phenomenon truly marvelous.

Add another phenomenon, no less beautiful than this: that along all the chords drawn from the point B to the points C, D, E, and to any other — not only taken in the quadrant BA, but in the whole circumference of the entire circle — the same moving body will descend in absolutely equal times; so that in the same time it will descend along the whole diameter erected perpendicular over the point B as it descends along BC, even if BC subtended only a single degree or a smaller arc.

Add the other marvel, which is that the motions of falling bodies made along the arcs of the quadrant AB are made in shorter times than those made along the chords of the same arcs: so that the swiftest motion, made in the shortest time, by a body going from the point A to the terminus B, will be the one made not along the straight line AB (though it's the shortest of all the lines that can be drawn between the points A and B), but along the circumference ADB.
